topics.title: Basic Structure of Computers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Structure of Computers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, the **Basic Structure of a Computer** refers to the **fundamental Von Neumann architectural model** in which a digital computer system is organized into **five inter-operating functional units**: the **Input Unit**, **Memory Unit**, **Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)**, **Control Unit (CU)**, and **Output Unit**. The Control Unit and ALU together are commonly packaged into what is known as the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**. The system is governed by the **stored-program concept**, in which both *data* and *instructions* reside in the same memory subsystem and are accessed sequentially or via program counter addressing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term *"Computer Organization"* in this course refers to the **physical/structural view** (hardware blocks, signals, timing, and interconnects), while *"Computer Architecture"* refers to the **logical/programmer-visible view** (instruction set, registers, addressing modes).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Computer as a Smart Factory

To make the structure instantly intuitive, consider a **fully automated pizza factory**:

| Computer Block | Factory Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Unit** | Customer Order Counter | Accepts raw "ingredients" (data/program) into the system. |
| **Memory Unit** | Cold Storage Warehouse | Stores ingredients (data) and recipes (instructions) at numbered racks (addresses). |
| **ALU** | Cooking Stations | Performs the actual "chopping, mixing, baking" — arithmetic and logical operations. |
| **Control Unit** | Floor Manager / Supervisor | Reads the recipe (instruction), directs every other unit, and decides the *next* step. |
| **Output Unit** | Delivery Counter | Hands over the finished pizza (processed result) to the outside world. |
| **CPU (CU + ALU)** | The Head Chef + Supervisor | The "thinking" core of the factory. |

Just as the floor manager continuously monitors the recipe and dispatches workers, the **Control Unit** continuously *fetches*, *decodes*, and *executes* instructions — known as the **Fetch–Decode–Execute (FDE) cycle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stored-Program Concept (von Neumann, 1945):** Instructions and data share the *same* memory and the *same* bus. This is the single most important property that distinguishes a *general-purpose computer* from a *fixed-function calculator*.

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

A KTU 2024 examiner expects the following standard units and constants to be known verbatim:

- **Clock Period** $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ (measured in **seconds**, with frequency $f$ in **Hz**).
- **Clock Rate** $f$ typically measured in **MHz** or **GHz** ($1\,\text{GHz} = 10^9$ Hz).
- **Memory Addressability** usually **8 bits (1 byte)** in modern byte-addressable systems.
- **Word Length** — fixed number of bits processed by the ALU in one operation (commonly **16, 32, or 64 bits**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Functional Units — Detailed Logic Steps

### A. Input Unit
- **Why needed:** A computer operates in a digital, binary domain — but humans communicate in human-friendly formats (text, sound, image).
- **How it works:** Converts external information into binary form acceptable to the CPU/memory.
- **Examples:** Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner, Microphone, Network Card (NIC), Touchscreen controller.
- **Key metric:** *Data transfer rate* (bytes per second).

### B. Memory Unit (Primary Storage)
- **Hierarchical structure** (top = fastest, bottom = slowest):
  1. **CPU Registers** (in-CPU, sub-nanosecond).
  2. **Cache (L1/L2/L3)** (on-chip, a few nanoseconds).
  3. **Main Memory (RAM)** (off-chip, ~50–100 ns).
  4. **Secondary Storage** (HDD/SSD, milliseconds).
- **Two primary operations only:** *Read* and *Write*.
- **Random Access Memory (RAM)** is *volatile*; **ROM** is *non-volatile* and stores the **boot firmware (BIOS/UEFI)**.

### C. Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)
- Performs the actual *computation* on data fetched into CPU registers.
- **Two operation classes:**
  - **Arithmetic:** Add, Subtract, Multiply, Divide, Increment, Decrement.
  - **Logical:** AND, OR, NOT, XOR, Shift Left/Right, Rotate, Compare.
- Produces **two outputs**: a *result* and a set of **status flags** (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow, Parity) stored in a special **Status Register / PSW (Program Status Word)**.

### D. Control Unit (CU)
- The "traffic police" of the processor.
- **Generates control signals** that orchestrate data flow between the ALU, registers, memory, and I/O.
- **Inputs:** Instruction Register (IR), flags, clock, bus requests.
- **Outputs:** Read/Write signals, ALU operation selectors, register select lines.
- **Two implementation styles:** *Hardwired CU* (combinational logic) vs *Microprogrammed CU* (firmware on a control store ROM).

### E. Output Unit
- Reverses the input process: converts internal binary back into human-readable form.
- **Examples:** Monitor, Printer, Speakers, LED displays, Actuators, Network transmission.

## 2.2 Basic Operational Concepts — The FDE Cycle

The processor repeatedly performs three actions — this is the **heartbeat** of every CPU:

1. **Fetch** the next instruction from memory (address provided by the **Program Counter — PC**).
2. **Decode** the instruction in the **Instruction Register (IR)** to determine the operation and operands.
3. **Execute** the operation (may involve ALU work, memory access, or I/O), then **update the PC** to point to the next instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> After execution, the CPU checks for **interrupts** (see §2.4) before fetching the *next* instruction. Hence the modern name: **Fetch–Decode–Execute–Interrupt (FDEI) cycle**.

## 2.3 Interconnection: The System Bus

The CPU, memory, and I/O devices cannot operate in isolation — they must exchange data. The **system bus** is a *shared* set of parallel wires divided into three functional sub-buses:

| Sub-Bus | Width (typical) | Direction | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Bus** | 8 / 16 / 32 / 64 bits | **Bi-directional** | Carries the actual data values being transferred. |
| **Address Bus** | 16 / 20 / 32 / 64 bits | **Uni-directional** (CPU → others) | Carries the memory/I/O *location* being accessed. |
| **Control Bus** | ~10–20 lines | Mixed | Carries timing & command signals: $\overline{\text{RD}}$, $\overline{\text{WR}}$, $\overline{\text{MREQ}}$, $\overline{\text{IORQ}}$, $\overline{\text{BUSREQ}}$, $\overline{\text{INT}}$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum addressable memory** of a CPU with an $n$-bit address bus is $2^n$ locations. This is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

## 2.4 Interrupts — The Mechanism for Asynchronous I/O

Without interrupts, the CPU would have to *poll* devices in a busy-wait loop, wasting enormous cycles. The **interrupt mechanism** lets external devices *signal* the CPU when they need service:

1. Device raises an **interrupt request (IRQ)** line.
2. CPU finishes the *current* instruction.
3. CPU saves the **PC** (and often the **PSW**) on the **system stack**.
4. CPU branches to a predefined **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** address (from the **Interrupt Vector Table**).
5. ISR executes; on completion, **RTI (Return from Interrupt)** restores PC/PSW.
6. CPU resumes the original program.

**Types of Interrupts (high-yield for KTU):**
- *Hardware Interrupts* (external, from I/O devices).
- *Software Interrupts / Traps / Exceptions* (internal, e.g., divide-by-zero, page fault).
- *Non-maskable Interrupt (NMI)* — cannot be ignored (used for power failure, parity errors).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Pipes are escaped as `\vert` in tables to avoid breaking markdown parsing.

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Clock period from frequency | seconds |
| 2 | $2^{n}$ | Addressable memory locations with $n$-bit address bus | locations (bytes if byte-addressable) |
| 3 | $\text{CPU Time} = \text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI} \times T$ | Basic CPU execution time | seconds |
| 4 | $\text{CPU Time} = \dfrac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}}{f}$ | Same, expressed via frequency | seconds |
| 5 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{\text{Instruction Count}}{\text{Execution Time} \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS |
| 6 | $\text{MFLOPS} = \dfrac{\text{Floating-Point Operations}}{\text{Execution Time} \times 10^{6}}$ | Floating-Point throughput | MFLOPS |
| 7 | $\text{Amdahl's Law: } S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - F) + \dfrac{F}{k}}$ | Speedup when fraction $F$ is improved by factor $k$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Performance} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Execution Time}}$ | Fundamental performance relation | 1/s |
| 9 | $\text{Avg CPI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IC}_{i} \times \text{CPI}_{i} \;\big/ \;\text{Total IC}$ | Weighted average CPI across $n$ instruction classes | cycles/instruction |
| 10 | $\text{Memory Transfer Time} = \dfrac{N \times W}{B}$ | $N$ words of $W$ bytes each on bus of bandwidth $B$ bytes/s | seconds |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Von Neumann model is not just historical — it is the **blueprint for every smartphone SoC, desktop CPU, and embedded microcontroller** shipped today. Understanding the bus widths, clock rates, and interrupt architecture is essential for:
- Designing **embedded firmware** (which relies on hardware interrupts).
- **Performance engineering** of software (the *CPU Time* formula drives compiler optimization).
- Selecting the **right CPU for IoT/edge devices** (where $n$-bit address bus determines max memory map).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CPU Performance Equation

The **CPU execution time** is the single most important metric in computer organization. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Given
- $I$ = total number of **executed instructions** in a program.
- $\text{CPI}$ = average **cycles per instruction** (mean number of clock cycles each instruction consumes).
- $f$ = clock frequency in Hz.
- $T = 1/f$ = clock period in seconds.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Total clock cycles required.**

If one instruction takes, on average, $\text{CPI}$ cycles, then $I$ instructions take $I \times \text{CPI}$ cycles.

$$
C_{\text{total}} = I \times \text{CPI}
$$

*Explanation:* This is direct multiplication — cycles = instructions × cycles/instruction.

**Step 2 — Convert total cycles to wall-clock time.**

Each clock cycle lasts $T = 1/f$ seconds. So:

$$
\text{CPU Time} = C_{\text{total}} \times T = I \times \text{CPI} \times \frac{1}{f}
$$

*Explanation:* Multiply cycle count by cycle duration to get time.

**Step 3 — Final performance equation.**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{CPU Time} = \dfrac{I \times \text{CPI}}{f}\;}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark derivation** that frequently appears in university exams.

### Step 4 — Derivation of MIPS (with worked logic)

By definition, **MIPS = Million Instructions Per Second**, so:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count}}{\text{Execution Time} \times 10^{6}}
$$

Substitute the CPU Time formula and note that $\text{Execution Time} \times f = I \times \text{CPI}$:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{I}{(I \times \text{CPI} / f) \times 10^{6}} = \frac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}
$$

*Explanation:* The instruction count $I$ cancels out, proving the elegant result — **MIPS depends only on clock frequency and CPI**, not on the program size. This is why MIPS is a *hardware metric*, not a *program metric*.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem — KTU-Style 14-Mark Style

**Problem (Model):** A processor runs at **2.5 GHz**. A program contains **$2 \times 10^{8}$** dynamic instructions. The instruction mix is given below:

| Instruction Class | IC (Instruction Count) | CPI |
|---|---|---|
| ALU | $1.0 \times 10^{8}$ | 1 |
| Load | $0.5 \times 10^{8}$ | 5 |
| Store | $0.3 \times 10^{8}$ | 3 |
| Branch | $0.2 \times 10^{8}$ | 2 |

**Find:** (a) Average CPI; (b) CPU execution time; (c) MIPS rating; (d) What speedup results from making the Load CPI = 3 (a 40% improvement on loads)?

### (a) Average CPI — Detailed Steps

**Step 1.** Apply the weighted-average formula (equation #9 in the cheat sheet):

$$
\text{Avg CPI} = \frac{\sum \text{IC}_{i} \times \text{CPI}_{i}}{\sum \text{IC}_{i}}
$$

**Step 2.** Compute the numerator term-by-term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALU term}    &= (1.0 \times 10^{8}) \times 1  = 1.0  \times 10^{8} \\
\text{Load term}   &= (0.5 \times 10^{8}) \times 5  = 2.5  \times 10^{8} \\
\text{Store term}  &= (0.3 \times 10^{8}) \times 3  = 0.9  \times 10^{8} \\
\text{Branch term} &= (0.2 \times 10^{8}) \times 2  = 0.4  \times 10^{8} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Sum the numerator:

$$
\sum \text{IC}_{i} \times \text{CPI}_{i} = (1.0 + 2.5 + 0.9 + 0.4) \times 10^{8} = 4.8 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 4.** Sum the denominator (which is the same as $I = 2 \times 10^{8}$):

$$
\sum \text{IC}_{i} = (1.0 + 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2) \times 10^{8} = 2.0 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 5.** Final division:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Avg CPI} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{8}}{2.0 \times 10^{8}} = 2.4 \text{ cycles/instruction}\;}
$$

### (b) CPU Execution Time

**Step 1.** Apply equation #4 from the cheat sheet:

$$
\text{CPU Time} = \frac{I \times \text{Avg CPI}}{f} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{8} \times 2.4}{2.5 \times 10^{9}}
$$

**Step 2.** Numerator:

$$
2.0 \times 10^{8} \times 2.4 = 4.8 \times 10^{8} \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3.** Final time:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{CPU Time} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{8}}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} = 0.192 \text{ seconds}\;}
$$

### (c) MIPS Rating

**Step 1.** Apply equation #5:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{2.4 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 2.** Result:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{MIPS} = 1041.67 \text{ MIPS} \approx 1042 \text{ MIPS}\;}
$$

### (d) Speedup via Load Improvement (Amdahl's Law Application)

**Step 1.** Original Load contribution to total cycles: $2.5 \times 10^{8}$ (from part a).
**Step 2.** Total original cycles: $4.8 \times 10^{8}$.
**Step 3.** Fraction $F$ contributed by Loads:

$$
F = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{8}}{4.8 \times 10^{8}} = 0.5208
$$

**Step 4.** Improvement factor on Load: $k = \dfrac{5}{3} \approx 1.6667$ (since new CPI is 3).
**Step 5.** Apply Amdahl's Law (equation #7):

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - F) + \frac{F}{k}} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.5208) + \frac{0.5208}{1.6667}}
$$

**Step 6.** Compute the two bracket terms:

$$
(1 - F) = 0.4792, \quad \frac{F}{k} = \frac{0.5208}{1.6667} = 0.3125
$$

**Step 7.** Final:

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.4792 + 0.3125} = \frac{1}{0.7917} = 1.263
$$

**Result:** Optimizing *only* the Load instruction yields a global speedup of **~1.26×** — a classic demonstration of Amdahl's bottleneck principle.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Instruction Format vs Address Count

A frequently asked KTU 14-mark topic is the number of addresses embedded in an instruction. The table below is the **master reference**:

| Instruction Type | # Memory Addresses | # Memory Accesses per Instruction | # Registers Needed | Pros / Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **3-address** | 2 src + 1 dst = 3 | 4 (3 reads + 1 write) | 0 (mem-to-mem) | Most powerful, longest instr word. |
| **2-address** | 1 src+dst + 1 src = 2 | 3 (2 reads + 1 write) | 0 | Shorter; one operand is *overwritten*. |
| **1-address** (Accumulator) | 1 | 2 (1 read + 1 write) | 1 (ACC) | Used in early von Neumann machines. |
| **0-address** (Stack) | 0 (implied) | 0 if operands on stack; 2 stack pops + 1 push | 0 | Used in Java Virtual Machine, x87 FPU. |
| **Load/Store (RISC)** | 1 for memory op, 0 for ALU | 1 for LW/SW; 0 for register-only ALU | ≥ 32 | Modern RISC (ARM, RISC-V) — most efficient. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory access count** matters because every memory access consumes cycles. Fewer accesses = faster execution. This is a primary reason RISC architectures (Load/Store) dominate modern CPU design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram of a Von Neumann Computer

The diagram below maps the complete data-flow between the five functional units through the system bus. Every node ID is alphanumeric-prefixed per the Mermaid safeguard rules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputDevices["INPUT UNIT"]
        inKbd["KEYBOARD"]
        inMouse["MOUSE"]
        inNIC["NETWORK CARD"]
    end

    subgraph CPUCore["CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT"]
        CU["CONTROL UNIT"]
        ALU["ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT"]
        REG["REGISTER FILE"]
        IR["INSTRUCTION REGISTER"]
        PC["PROGRAM COUNTER"]
    end

    subgraph MemoryBank["MEMORY UNIT"]
        RAM["MAIN RAM"]
        ROM["ROM BOOT FIRMWARE"]
        Cache["CACHE L1 L2 L3"]
    end

    subgraph OutputDevices["OUTPUT UNIT"]
        outMon["MONITOR"]
        outPrn["PRINTER"]
        outSPK["SPEAKER"]
    end

    DataBus{{"DATA BUS BIDIRECTIONAL"}}
    AddrBus{{"ADDRESS BUS CPU TO DEVICES"}}
    CtrlBus{{"CONTROL BUS RD WR INT"}}

    InputDevices -.-> DataBus
    OutputDevices -.-> DataBus
    MemoryBank -.-> DataBus
    CPUCore -.-> DataBus

    CPUCore -.-> AddrBus
    CPUCore -.-> CtrlBus

    CU --> IR
    CU --> PC
    CU --> REG
    CU --> ALU
    IR --> CU
    PC --> CU
    REG --> ALU
    ALU --> REG
    ALU --> CU
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **dashed arrows** into the three labeled "bus" blocks represent physical parallel wire bundles (data, address, control).
- Solid arrows inside the **CPU** show internal micro-data paths between the CU, ALU, Register File, PC, and IR.
- The **Memory Unit** sits between CPU and I/O so all data can be staged through it (stored-program principle).

## 4.2 Sub-Diagram: Interrupt-Driven I/O Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPUState1["CPU EXECUTES PROGRAM"]
    CPUState2{"INTERRUPT REQUEST LINE"}
    CPUState3["FINISH CURRENT INSTRUCTION"]
    CPUState4["PUSH PC AND PSW ON STACK"]
    CPUState5["FETCH ISR ADDRESS FROM IVT"]
    CPUState6["EXECUTE INTERRUPT SERVICE ROUTINE"]
    CPUState7["RTI INSTRUCTION"]
    CPUState8["POP PC AND PSW FROM STACK"]
    CPUState9["RESUME MAIN PROGRAM"]

    CPUState1 --> CPUState2
    CPUState2 -- NO --> CPUState1
    CPUState2 -- YES --> CPUState3
    CPUState3 --> CPUState4
    CPUState4 --> CPUState5
    CPUState5 --> CPUState6
    CPUState6 --> CPUState7
    CPUState7 --> CPUState8
    CPUState8 --> CPUState9
    CPUState9 --> CPUState1
```

## 4.3 Sub-Diagram: Performance Metric Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PROGRAM EXECUTION TIME"] --> P2{"FACTOR ANALYSIS"}
    P2 --> P3["INSTRUCTION COUNT IC"]
    P2 --> P4["CYCLES PER INSTRUCTION CPI"]
    P2 --> P5["CLOCK PERIOD T = 1 BY f"]
    P4 --> P4A["INSTRUCTION MIX"]
    P4 --> P4B["MICROARCH IMPLEMENTATION"]
    P4 --> P4C["CACHE HIT RATE"]
    P5 --> P5A["FABRIC TECHNOLOGY NM"]
    P5 --> P5B["PIPELINE DEPTH"]
```

This block-level functional architecture flow shows the **three independent levers** a computer architect can tune to improve performance: *IC* (compiler), *CPI* (microarchitecture), and *f* (process technology).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q:** With a neat diagram, explain the basic functional units of a computer system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A computer system has **five functional units**:
1. **Input Unit** — accepts data/instructions (keyboard, mouse).
2. **Memory Unit** — stores data and instructions (RAM, ROM).
3. **ALU** — performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logical (AND, OR, NOT) operations.
4. **Control Unit** — directs all other units; generates timing/control signals.
5. **Output Unit** — delivers results to the user (monitor, printer).

The CU and ALU together form the **CPU**. All units communicate via the **system bus** (data + address + control lines). (3 marks for the block diagram, names, and brief function of each unit.)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q:** What is an interrupt? List its types.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **interrupt** is an *external or internal asynchronous signal* that temporarily halts the CPU's main program to execute a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. After the ISR, the CPU resumes the main program from where it stopped.

**Types:**
1. **Hardware Interrupts** — generated by external I/O devices.
2. **Software Interrupts / Traps** — generated by exceptional conditions (e.g., division by zero).
3. **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI)** — a critical hardware interrupt that *cannot* be disabled.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a basic computer system and explain the function of each unit. (7 marks)

**(b)** A program has the following instruction mix. Compute the average CPI, CPU execution time, and MIPS rating for a processor with clock rate 3 GHz. (7 marks)

| Instruction Type | IC | CPI |
|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | $1.6 \times 10^{8}$ | 1 |
| Load | $0.4 \times 10^{8}$ | 4 |
| Store | $0.3 \times 10^{8}$ | 3 |
| Branch | $0.2 \times 10^{8}$ | 2 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
Present the **five-block diagram** (same as §4.1):
- **Input Unit:** Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner — converts user data into binary.
- **Memory Unit:** RAM (volatile, working storage) and ROM (firmware); organised in addressable cells.
- **ALU:** Performs arithmetic and logic; outputs go to registers; status flags update the PSW.
- **Control Unit:** Generates $\overline{\text{RD}}$, $\overline{\text{WR}}$, clock, register-select signals; orchestrates the FDE cycle.
- **Output Unit:** Monitor, printer — converts binary back to human-readable form.
- **System Bus:** Three sub-buses (Data, Address, Control) interconnect all units. (7 marks for correct block diagram with labels and one-line functions.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Full Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Total IC.**
$$
I = (1.6 + 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.2) \times 10^{8} = 2.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ instructions}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sum \text{IC}_{i} \times \text{CPI}_{i}$ termwise:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Arith}  &= 1.6 \times 10^{8} \times 1 = 1.6 \times 10^{8} \\
\text{Load}   &= 0.4 \times 10^{8} \times 4 = 1.6 \times 10^{8} \\
\text{Store}  &= 0.3 \times 10^{8} \times 3 = 0.9 \times 10^{8} \\
\text{Branch} &= 0.2 \times 10^{8} \times 2 = 0.4 \times 10^{8} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = (1.6 + 1.6 + 0.9 + 0.4) \times 10^{8} = 4.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3 — Average CPI.**

$$
\text{CPI} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{8}}{2.5 \times 10^{8}} = 1.8
$$

**Step 4 — CPU Execution Time.**

$$
\text{CPU Time} = \frac{I \times \text{CPI}}{f} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{8} \times 1.8}{3 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{8}}{3 \times 10^{9}} = 0.15 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — MIPS Rating.**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{9}}{1.8 \times 10^{6}} = 1666.67 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**Final Answers:** Avg CPI = **1.8**, CPU Time = **0.15 s**, MIPS = **1666.67**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the stored-program concept of a computer. Compare 3-address, 2-address, 1-address, and 0-address instruction formats with examples. (7 marks)

**(b)** A processor has a clock rate of 2 GHz. A benchmark program requires $5 \times 10^{7}$ instructions to execute, with an average CPI of 3.5. Calculate the CPU execution time and MIPS rating. If we redesign the CPU so that the average CPI drops to 2.0 (without changing the clock), what is the speedup? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
- **Stored-program concept** (von Neumann, 1945): Both *instructions* and *data* are stored in the **same memory** and accessed using the **same bus**. The PC fetches the next instruction; the same memory also holds the operands. This is the foundation of general-purpose computing.
- **Comparison table** (refer §3.3 of this document for the full matrix):
  - **3-address:** e.g., `ADD R1, R2, R3` style with `ADD A, B, C` meaning A ← B + C.
  - **2-address:** `ADD A, B` meaning A ← A + B (A is both source and destination).
  - **1-address (Accumulator):** `ADD X` meaning ACC ← ACC + M[X]. Requires implicit `LOAD` and `STORE`.
  - **0-address (Stack):** `ADD` (no operand) — pops two top-of-stack values, adds, pushes result.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Full Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Original CPU Time.**

$$
\text{CPU Time}_{1} = \frac{I \times \text{CPI}_{1}}{f} = \frac{5 \times 10^{7} \times 3.5}{2 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{1.75 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{9}} = 0.0875 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Original MIPS.**

$$
\text{MIPS}_{1} = \frac{f}{\text{CPI}_{1} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{9}}{3.5 \times 10^{6}} = 571.43 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**Step 3 — New CPU Time after CPI improvement.**

$$
\text{CPU Time}_{2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{7} \times 2.0}{2 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{9}} = 0.05 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Speedup.**

$$
S = \frac{\text{CPU Time}_{1}}{\text{CPU Time}_{2}} = \frac{0.0875}{0.05} = 1.75
$$

**Final Answers:** CPU Time = **0.0875 s**, MIPS = **571.43**, Speedup = **1.75×**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Writing MIPS in Hz or CPU Time in microseconds without conversion → **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $10^{6}$ factor in MIPS** — many students write $\text{MIPS} = I / \text{Time}$ directly, getting numbers off by **six orders of magnitude** → **lose 2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up Amdahl's Law with simple speedup:** Amdahl's applies to a *fraction* of execution time; total-CPU-time speedup is just a ratio.
> 4. **Omitting the block-diagram labels** in part (a) — even a correct verbal description without a labelled diagram loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Writing the instruction format** without showing the *memory access count* — KTU expects the comparison on both count and registers used.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Five functional units:** Input, Memory, ALU, Control Unit, Output. CU + ALU = CPU.
- **Stored-program concept:** Instructions and data share *one* memory and *one* bus.
- **System bus = Data + Address + Control** sub-buses. Address bus is uni-directional; data bus is bi-directional.
- **Addressable memory** $= 2^{n}$ for an $n$-bit address bus (byte-addressable ⇒ bytes).
- **Fetch–Decode–Execute (FDE)** cycle is the CPU heartbeat; modern CPUs add an **Interrupt** step.
- **Interrupts** = asynchronous signals (Hardware, Software, NMI). They save PC/PSW on stack, branch to ISR, then RTI.
- **CPU Time** = $\dfrac{I \times \text{CPI}}{f}$ — the **master performance equation**.
- **MIPS** = $\dfrac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}$ — depends only on hardware (clock + CPI), not on program.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-F) + F/k}$ — improving a *fraction* $F$ by factor $k$ gives overall speedup $S$.
- **Instruction formats (3, 2, 1, 0 address):** more addresses ⇒ more bits per instruction but fewer memory accesses per op.
- **RISC (Load/Store) architecture** dominates modern CPUs (ARM, RISC-V) because it minimizes memory accesses.
- **Clock Period** $T = 1/f$ — higher $f$ ⇒ lower $T$ ⇒ faster clock edge transitions.
- **Average CPI** is *weighted* by instruction count, not a simple arithmetic mean.
- **Three performance levers:** reduce $I$ (compiler), reduce CPI (microarchitecture), increase $f$ (process tech).
- **Control Unit implementations:** Hardwired (fast, rigid) vs Microprogrammed (flexible, slower).

> **End of Module 1 — Basic Structure of Computers | KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functional Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Computer?

A **computer** is an electronic data processing device that accepts raw data (input) from the user, processes it under the control of a stored program (set of instructions), produces the desired output, and stores the results for future use.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the **basic functional units of a digital computer** are the fundamental hardware blocks that work together to execute instructions and produce meaningful results. The five classic functional units are:

1. **Input Unit**
2. **Memory Unit (Storage Unit)**
3. **Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)**
4. **Control Unit (CU)**
5. **Output Unit**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** = **ALU** + **Control Unit (CU)** + **Register Set**
> The CPU is the *brain* of the computer. It is often simply called the **Processor**.

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The Human Body 🍎

Think of a computer system as the **human body**:

| Computer Part | Human Body Counterpart | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Unit** (Keyboard, Mouse) | **Eyes, Ears, Skin** (Senses) | Capture raw data from the environment |
| **Memory Unit** (RAM, Hard Disk) | **Brain's memory** | Store data and instructions temporarily / permanently |
| **ALU** (Arithmetic Logic Unit) | **Brain's thinking area** (calculation & decision) | Perform math (add, subtract) and logic (AND, OR) |
| **Control Unit** (CU) | **Cerebellum / Nervous System** | Directs and coordinates all other organs |
| **Output Unit** (Monitor, Printer) | **Mouth, Hands** (expression) | Show or communicate the result to the outside world |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as your senses feed information to your brain, and your brain tells your mouth what to say — data flows from **Input → Memory → ALU → Output**, with the **Control Unit orchestrating** the entire sequence like a conductor leading an orchestra. 🎼

---

## 1.3 Detailed Definition of Each Functional Unit

### 🔹 1. Input Unit
An **input device** is used to feed raw data and instructions into the computer's memory for processing.
- Examples: **Keyboard**, **Mouse**, **Scanner**, **Microphone**, **Touchscreen**, **Barcode Reader**.
- The data is converted from a human-readable form to a **binary form (0s and 1s)** that the computer can understand.

### 🔹 2. Memory Unit (Storage Unit)
The **memory unit** stores data, instructions, intermediate results, and final output.
- **Primary Memory (Main Memory)** — *fast, volatile* (loses data on power-off). Example: **RAM (Random Access Memory)**.
- **Secondary Memory (Auxiliary Storage)** — *slow, non-volatile* (retains data permanently). Example: **Hard Disk, SSD, Optical Discs**.

### 🔹 3. Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)
The **ALU** is the computational heart of the CPU. It performs all arithmetic and logical operations.
- **Arithmetic Operations**: Add, Subtract, Multiply, Divide.
- **Logical Operations**: AND, OR, NOT, XOR, Compare, Shift, Rotate.
- The result is stored in the **Accumulator (AC)** — a special internal CPU register.

### 🔹 4. Control Unit (CU)
The **Control Unit** is the *traffic controller* of the computer. It does **not** process data itself; instead, it **directs, coordinates, and controls** all other units.
- It fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and generates the necessary **control signals** to activate the ALU, memory, and I/O devices.
- Implementation: **Hardwired CU** (using fixed logic circuits) or **Microprogrammed CU** (using microinstructions stored in a control memory).

### 🔹 5. Output Unit
An **output device** presents the processed result to the user in a human-understandable form.
- Examples: **Monitor (VFD/LCD)**, **Printer**, **Speakers**, **Plotter**.
- It converts binary data back into human-readable form.

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Memory Trick — "IMACO"** 🧠
> **I**nput → **M**emory → **A**LU → **C**ontrol → **O**utput
> Remember this sequence; examiners love asking "List the functional units in order" or "Draw the block diagram."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CPU — The Heart of the Computer

The **CPU** is the single most important chip on the motherboard. It consists of three main parts:

| Sub-Component | Full Name | Primary Job |
|---|---|---|
| **ALU** | Arithmetic Logic Unit | Performs arithmetic & logic operations |
| **CU** | Control Unit | Decodes instructions & generates control signals |
| **Register Set** | High-speed internal memory locations | Holds operands, results, and instruction addresses |

### Major CPU Registers (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Register | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Program Counter** | PC | Holds the address of the **next** instruction to be fetched |
| **Instruction Register** | IR | Holds the **currently executing** instruction |
| **Accumulator** | AC | The default register for ALU results |
| **Memory Address Register** | MAR | Holds the address of the memory location being accessed |
| **Memory Data Register (MDR)** | MBR | Holds the data being written to or read from memory |
| **Stack Pointer** | SP | Points to the top of the stack in memory |
| **Status Register / Flags** | SR | Holds condition codes (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Alert** ❗
> Questions like *"Explain the role of PC, IR, MAR, and MDR during instruction execution"* are very common in **Part B (14 marks)**. Memorize their exact roles — partial credit is often lost when students confuse MAR (address) with MDR (data).

---

## 2.2 The System Bus — The Highway of Data

A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of a set of common lines through which data, addresses, and control signals travel.

There are **three types of buses**:

| Bus Type | Direction | Width (Typical) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Bus** | **Bi-directional** | 8 / 16 / 32 / 64 bits | Carries the **actual data** between CPU, memory, and I/O |
| **Address Bus** | **Uni-directional** (CPU → others) | 16 / 20 / 32 / 64 bits | Carries the **memory address** of the location to be accessed |
| **Control Bus** | **Bi-directional** | varies | Carries **timing & control signals** (Read/Write, Interrupt, Clock) |

> [!NOTE]
> A bus with **n address lines** can address **2ⁿ unique memory locations**. This is one of the most-tested facts in KTU Module 1 problems.

---

## 2.3 Von Neumann Architecture (Stored-Program Concept)

The **Von Neumann architecture**, proposed by John von Neumann in **1945**, is the foundation of nearly all modern computers. Its key idea:

> **Both instructions and data are stored in the same memory and use the same bus.**

### Two Key Sub-Architectures of Von Neumann:

#### (a) Single-Bus Organization
- Only **one internal bus** is shared.
- All registers (PC, IR, AC, MAR, MDR) connect to this single common bus.
- **Drawback**: Only one register can drive the bus at a time, so transfers must be **serialized**.

#### (b) Multi-Bus Organization (Two / Three-Bus)
- **Two-bus** design: Two internal buses allow simultaneous data transfer, reducing steps.
- **Three-bus** design: Two source buses + one destination bus. Most registers can input/output simultaneously → **fastest** but most complex.

---

## 2.4 Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet) 📊

For solving numerical problems, you must master these formulas:

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CPU Time} = \text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI} \times \text{Clock Cycle Time}$ | IC = # of instructions, CPI = cycles per instruction | **seconds** |
| 2 | $\text{CPU Time} = \dfrac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}}{\text{Clock Rate}}$ | Clock Rate = 1 / Clock Cycle Time | **seconds** |
| 3 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{\text{Instruction Count}}{\text{Execution Time} \times 10^{6}}$ | Millions of Instructions Per Second | **MIPS** |
| 4 | $\text{MFLOPS} = \dfrac{\text{Floating-Point Operations}}{\text{Execution Time} \times 10^{6}}$ | Millions of Floating-Point Ops / Sec | **MFLOPS** |
| 5 | $\text{Clock Cycle Time} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Clock Rate}}$ | T = period, f = frequency | **seconds** |
| 6 | $\text{Average CPI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IC}_i \times \text{CPI}_i \;\Big/\; \text{IC}_{\text{total}}$ | Weighted average across instruction types | **cycles/instruction** |
| 7 | $\text{Amdahl's Speedup} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{S}}$ | $f$ = fraction enhanced, $S$ = speedup of that part | **dimensionless ratio** |
| 8 | $\text{Memory Addressable} = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = number of address bus lines | **bytes / words** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility** 🏭
> These formulas are not just for exams — chip designers at **Intel, AMD, and Apple** use them to benchmark and optimize their processors. The *Amdahl's Law* in particular is the cornerstone of parallel computing performance estimation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Basic CPU Time Calculation

### Problem Statement:
> A processor has a **clock rate of 2.5 GHz**. It executes a program with **1.2 × 10⁹ instructions**, and the **average CPI is 3.5**. Calculate the CPU execution time and MIPS rating.

---

### Step 1 — Identify Given Values
We extract the parameters from the problem statement:

- Clock Rate = **2.5 GHz** = $2.5 \times 10^{9}$ Hz
- Instruction Count (IC) = **$1.2 \times 10^{9}$** instructions
- Average CPI = **3.5** cycles/instruction

### Step 2 — Calculate Clock Cycle Time

Using the relation $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$:

$$T = \dfrac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} \text{ seconds}$$

$$T = 0.4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 4.0 \times 10^{-10} \text{ s} = \textbf{0.4 ns}$$

**Logic:** A higher frequency means a *shorter* cycle period. This is the smallest unit of time in a CPU.

### Step 3 — Calculate CPU Execution Time

Using the standard CPU Time formula:

$$\text{CPU Time} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{cycle}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{CPU Time} = (1.2 \times 10^{9}) \times 3.5 \times (4.0 \times 10^{-10})$$

$$\text{CPU Time} = 1.2 \times 3.5 \times 4.0 \times 10^{9} \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\text{CPU Time} = 16.8 \times 10^{-1} \text{ s} = \textbf{1.68 seconds}$$

**Logic:** Multiply the *number of instructions* by the *cycles needed per instruction* to get the *total cycles*, then multiply by *time per cycle* to get the *total wall-clock time*.

### Step 4 — Calculate MIPS Rating

Using the MIPS formula:

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{\text{IC}}{\text{Execution Time} \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{1.68 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{1.2}{1.68} \times 10^{3} = 0.7143 \times 10^{3} = \textbf{714.29 MIPS}$$

**Logic:** MIPS is a *throughput* metric — it tells how many *millions* of instructions complete per second. **Higher = better**.

### ✅ Final Answer Summary
- **Clock Cycle Time** = 0.4 ns
- **CPU Execution Time** = 1.68 s
- **MIPS Rating** ≈ 714.29 MIPS

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Amdahl's Law Application

### Problem Statement:
> Suppose a program spends **80%** of its time in a part of code that can be **sped up by a factor of 5** using a hardware enhancement. What is the **overall speedup** of the program?

---

### Step 1 — Identify the Variables
From the problem:
- Fraction that can be enhanced: $f = 0.80$
- Speedup of that fraction: $S = 5$
- Remaining fraction (un-enhanced): $(1 - f) = 0.20$

### Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law

The full formula is:

$$\text{Speedup}_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{S}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Values

$$\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{1}{(0.20) + \dfrac{0.80}{5}}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Denominator

$$\dfrac{0.80}{5} = 0.16$$

$$0.20 + 0.16 = 0.36$$

### Step 5 — Final Speedup

$$\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{1}{0.36} = \textbf{2.778}$$

**Logic:** Even though we made the hot portion 5× faster, the overall program only runs ~2.78× faster because the *remaining 20%* of the program is unchanged — it becomes the new bottleneck. This is the famous *"diminishing returns"* law of optimization.

> [!TIP]
> **Theoretical Limit Check** 🚧
> If we had $f = 1.0$ (i.e., 100% of the program is enhanced) and $S = 5$, the speedup would be exactly **5×**. The closer $f$ is to 1, the closer we get to the theoretical limit.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Instruction-Mix Problems

When a problem gives a *mix* of instruction types, you compute the **weighted average CPI** first, then CPU time.

### Worked Example:
> A program has the following instruction mix:

| Instruction Type | Count (ICᵢ) | CPIᵢ |
|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | $6 \times 10^{6}$ | 1 |
| Load | $3 \times 10^{6}$ | 5 |
| Store | $2 \times 10^{6}$ | 3 |
| Branch | $1 \times 10^{6}$ | 2 |

> Clock rate = **200 MHz**. Find total execution time.

#### Step 1 — Total Instructions

$$\text{IC}_{\text{total}} = (6 + 3 + 2 + 1) \times 10^{6} = 12 \times 10^{6}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Total Cycles

$$\text{Cycles} = \sum (\text{IC}_i \times \text{CPI}_i)$$

$$\text{Cycles} = (6 \times 10^{6})(1) + (3 \times 10^{6})(5) + (2 \times 10^{6})(3) + (1 \times 10^{6})(2)$$

$$\text{Cycles} = 6 + 15 + 6 + 2 = 29 \times 10^{6} \text{ cycles}$$

#### Step 3 — Clock Cycle Time

$$T = \dfrac{1}{200 \times 10^{6}} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = \textbf{5 ns}$$

#### Step 4 — CPU Time

$$\text{CPU Time} = 29 \times 10^{6} \times 5 \times 10^{-9} = \textbf{0.145 seconds}$$

#### Step 5 — MIPS

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^{6}}{0.145 \times 10^{6}} \approx \textbf{82.76 MIPS}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Computer (Von Neumann Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Devices]:::inputStyle -->|Data and Instructions| B((Central Processing Unit CPU)):::cpuStyle
    C[Output Devices]:::outputStyle -->|Processed Results| B
    B -->|Control Signals| D[Memory Unit]:::memoryStyle
    B -->|Status and Data| E[Control Unit CU]:::controlStyle
    B -->|Arithmetic and Logic Ops| F[Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU]:::aluStyle
    D -->|Stored Data| B
    E -->|Read Write Signals| D
    E -->|Operation Commands| F
    B --> G((System Bus)):::busStyle
    G -->|Address Bus 32 bits| D
    G -->|Data Bus 64 bits| D
    G -->|Control Bus| A
    G -->|Control Bus| C

    classDef inputStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cpuStyle fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef memoryStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef controlStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef aluStyle fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef busStyle fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above shows the **complete Von Neumann architecture**. Notice how the CPU is the central hub that connects to **memory** (for instructions/data) and **I/O devices** (for input/output) via the **system bus**. The CPU itself is internally split into **CU** (decides *what* to do) and **ALU** (does the *actual computation*).

---

## 4.2 Internal CPU Organization — Single-Bus Design

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BUS((Internal Common Bus 16 bits)):::busStyle
    PC[Program Counter PC]:::regStyle --> BUS
    MAR[Memory Address Register MAR]:::regStyle --> BUS
    MDR[Memory Data Register MDR]:::regStyle --> BUS
    IR[Instruction Register IR]:::regStyle --> BUS
    AC[Accumulator AC]:::regStyle --> BUS
    ALU[ALU]:::aluStyle --> BUS
    SP[Stack Pointer SP]:::regStyle --> BUS
    CTR[Control Unit CU]:::controlStyle
    CTR -.->|Control Signals| PC
    CTR -.->|Control Signals| MAR
    CTR -.->|Control Signals| MDR
    CTR -.->|Control Signals| IR
    CTR -.->|Control Signals| AC
    CTR -.->|Control Signals| ALU
    CTR -.->|Read or Write| BUS

    classDef busStyle fill:#333,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef regStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef aluStyle fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef controlStyle fill:#9370DB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **single-bus CPU organization**, every register is connected to **one** common internal bus. This means *only one register can put data on the bus at a time*. Therefore, an ALU operation (which needs **two** operands) requires **two bus cycles** to fetch the operands. This is a classic KTU Part-B sub-question.

---

## 4.3 Three-Bus CPU Organization (High-Performance Design)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Register A]:::regStyle -->|Bus A| B[ALU]:::aluStyle
    C[Register B]:::regStyle -->|Bus B| B
    B -->|Result| D[Register C or Any Destination]:::regStyle
    E[Control Unit]:::controlStyle -.-> A
    E -.-> C
    E -.-> B
    E -.-> D

    classDef regStyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef aluStyle fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef controlStyle fill:#9370DB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

**Key Advantage:** In the **three-bus** design, **two source registers** can drive Bus A and Bus B **simultaneously**, and the ALU output is captured in one cycle. This makes it the **fastest internal design** but at the cost of **more bus wires and hardware complexity**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> *List and briefly explain the basic functional units of a digital computer.*

#### ✅ Model Answer:
A digital computer consists of **five basic functional units**:

1. **Input Unit** — Accepts raw data and instructions from the user via devices like keyboard and mouse, and converts them into a binary form.
2. **Memory Unit** — Stores data, instructions, intermediate results, and final output. It includes **primary memory (RAM)** for fast temporary storage and **secondary memory (Hard Disk, SSD)** for permanent storage.
3. **Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)** — Performs all arithmetic operations (add, subtract, multiply, divide) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, compare).
4. **Control Unit (CU)** — Directs and coordinates the operations of all other units by fetching, decoding, and generating timing/control signals. It does not process data itself.
5. **Output Unit** — Delivers the processed result to the user via devices like monitors, printers, or speakers, converting binary output back to a human-readable form.

The **CPU = ALU + CU + Register Set**, and acts as the brain that coordinates all other units.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall** ⚠️
> Do **not** write *"The CPU is a functional unit"* without defining the CPU's components. KTU examiners expect the **CPU to be explained as a sub-system of the main five units**, not as a sixth unit.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> *Explain the functions of the Program Counter (PC) and Instruction Register (IR).*

#### ✅ Model Answer:
- **Program Counter (PC)**: It is a special CPU register that **holds the address of the next instruction** to be fetched from memory. After each instruction fetch, the PC is **automatically incremented** (e.g., PC ← PC + 1 for word-addressable memory) so that it points to the subsequent instruction. The PC is the *program flow controller*.

- **Instruction Register (IR)**: It is a CPU register that **holds the currently executing instruction** that has been fetched from memory. While the CU decodes the opcode bits in the IR, the rest of the bits (operand/address fields) are used by the CPU to perform the operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall** ⚠️
> Many students write *"PC stores the current instruction"* and *"IR stores the next instruction"* — this is **exactly the opposite** of the truth. Memorize: **PC = next, IR = current**.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> *(a) Draw the block diagram of a basic computer and explain the functions of each functional unit. (7 Marks)*
> *(b) Describe the various types of system buses with neat diagrams and explain the role of address bus width in determining memory size. (7 Marks)*

---

#### **Part A(a) — Block Diagram of a Basic Computer (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A digital computer's block diagram consists of the **CPU**, **Memory Unit**, **Input Unit**, and **Output Unit**, all interconnected via the **system bus**.

**Step 1 — CPU (Central Processing Unit)**:
The CPU is the brain of the computer. It has three sub-parts:
- **ALU** — performs arithmetic (add, subtract) and logical (AND, OR) operations.
- **Control Unit (CU)** — fetches, decodes, and executes instructions by generating control signals.
- **Register Set** — high-speed storage inside the CPU (PC, IR, AC, MAR, MDR, SP).

**Step 2 — Memory Unit**:
- **Primary Memory (RAM)**: fast, volatile, used to hold active programs and data.
- **Secondary Memory (HDD/SSD)**: slow, non-volatile, used for permanent storage.
- The CPU reads/writes to memory via **MAR** (address) and **MDR** (data).

**Step 3 — Input Unit**:
- Devices like keyboard, mouse, scanner.
- Converts user-supplied data (text, clicks) into **binary (0/1)** signals.

**Step 4 — Output Unit**:
- Devices like monitor, printer.
- Converts binary data back to **human-readable** form (text, images, sound).

**Step 5 — System Bus**:
- A shared communication highway consisting of:
  - **Data Bus** (carries the actual data),
  - **Address Bus** (carries memory locations),
  - **Control Bus** (carries timing/read-write signals).

> [!NOTE]
> Always include the **bus** lines in your block diagram — failing to do so costs 1–2 marks in KTU valuation.

---

#### **Part A(b) — System Buses and Address Bus Width (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **bus** is a set of conducting wires that provides a communication path between the CPU, memory, and I/O devices.

**Step 1 — Types of Buses**:

| Bus Type | Function | Direction | Width (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Bus** | Carries actual data being transferred | **Bi-directional** | 8 / 16 / 32 / 64 bits |
| **Address Bus** | Carries the memory address of the location to be accessed | **Uni-directional (CPU → Memory)** | 16 / 20 / 32 / 64 bits |
| **Control Bus** | Carries control & timing signals (Read, Write, Interrupt, Clock) | **Bi-directional** | varies |

**Step 2 — Role of Address Bus Width**:
If the address bus has **n lines**, the CPU can uniquely identify $2^{n}$ memory locations.

- Example: A **16-bit address bus** → $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ locations = **64 KB** memory.
- Example: A **32-bit address bus** → $2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ locations = **4 GB** memory.
- Example: A **64-bit address bus** → $2^{64} = 1.844 \times 10^{19}$ locations ≈ **16 EB (Exabytes)**.

**Step 3 — Data Bus Width**:
The data bus width determines **how many bits can be transferred in one bus cycle**. A 64-bit data bus transfers 8 bytes per cycle, doubling throughput over a 32-bit bus.

**Step 4 — Control Bus Signals**:
Common control signals include:
- **MRDC** (Memory Read Control)
- **MWTC** (Memory Write Control)
- **IORC** / **IOWC** (I/O Read/Write Control)
- **INTR** (Interrupt Request)
- **CLK** (System Clock)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall** ⚠️
> Students often confuse the **direction** of the buses. The address bus is **unidirectional** because only the CPU *generates* addresses. The data bus is **bidirectional** because data flows both ways (read and write). Losing this distinction costs 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**
> *(a) Explain the different CPU registers and their functions with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)*
> *(b) A processor has a clock rate of 3 GHz. A program contains 2 × 10⁸ instructions. The average CPI is 4. Calculate (i) CPU execution time, (ii) MIPS rating, and (iii) clock cycle time. (7 Marks)*

---

#### **Part B(a) — CPU Registers and Their Functions (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

CPU registers are **high-speed storage elements** inside the processor that are used for **temporary data storage and control** during instruction execution.

**Step 1 — Basic CPU Internal Diagram**:

```
[PC] →→→  ┐
[MAR]→→→  ├─→  Internal Bus  →→→ [ALU] →→→ [AC]
[IR]  →→→  │                      ↑
[MDR]→→→  ┘                      │
                                  │
[SP]  →→→  ┘                    [CU]
```

**Step 2 — Functions of Key Registers**:

| Register | Function |
|---|---|
| **PC (Program Counter)** | Holds the address of the **next** instruction to be fetched. |
| **IR (Instruction Register)** | Holds the **currently executing** instruction fetched from memory. |
| **AC (Accumulator)** | Default register to hold one of ALU operands and store the result. |
| **MAR (Memory Address Register)** | Holds the **address** of the memory location being accessed. |
| **MDR / MBR (Memory Data/Buffer Register)** | Holds the **data** to be written to or read from memory. |
| **SP (Stack Pointer)** | Points to the top of the **stack** in memory. |
| **Status / Flags Register** | Holds **condition codes** (Zero flag, Carry flag, Sign flag, Overflow flag). |

**Step 3 — Use during Instruction Execution**:
1. **Fetch Phase**: PC's content is loaded into MAR → memory read → instruction comes into MDR → transferred to IR → PC is incremented.
2. **Decode Phase**: CU decodes the opcode in IR.
3. **Execute Phase**: Operands loaded into AC → ALU performs operation → result stored back in AC.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall** ⚠️
> Do not write *"MAR stores the data"* or *"MDR stores the address"*. KTU is strict: **MAR = address, MDR = data**. Confusing them is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks on this question.

---

#### **Part B(b) — Performance Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Given Parameters**:
- Clock Rate (f) = **3 GHz** = $3 \times 10^{9}$ Hz
- Instruction Count (IC) = **$2 \times 10^{8}$**
- Average CPI = **4** cycles/instruction

**Step 2 — (i) Calculate the Clock Cycle Time**:

$$T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{3 \times 10^{9}} = 0.3333 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 0.333 \text{ ns} \; (3.33 \times 10^{-10} \text{ s})}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Calculate the CPU Execution Time**:

$$\text{CPU Time} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T$$

$$\text{CPU Time} = (2 \times 10^{8}) \times 4 \times (3.33 \times 10^{-10})$$

$$\text{CPU Time} = 8 \times 3.33 \times 10^{8} \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\text{CPU Time} = 26.64 \times 10^{-2} = \boxed{0.2664 \text{ seconds}}$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Calculate the MIPS Rating**:

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{\text{IC}}{\text{Execution Time} \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{8}}{0.2664 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{200}{0.2664}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MIPS} \approx 750.75 \text{ MIPS}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification (Alternate Method)**:
We can cross-check CPU Time using:

$$\text{CPU Time} = \dfrac{\text{IC} \times \text{CPI}}{\text{Clock Rate}} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{8}) \times 4}{3 \times 10^{9}} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{8}}{3 \times 10^{9}} = 0.2667 \text{ s} \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall** ⚠️
> The most common mistake is **forgetting to multiply by $10^{6}$** in the MIPS denominator. Always double-check the units — MIPS is *millions* of instructions per second, so the denominator *must* have a $10^{6}$.

---

## 🔥 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> 📌 **Use this as your last-minute revision checklist before entering the exam hall!**

- ✅ The **five functional units** of a computer are: **Input, Memory, ALU, Control, Output**. Use the mnemonic **"IMACO"**.
- ✅ The **CPU = ALU + CU + Registers**. It is *not* a sixth functional unit — it is a subsystem of the memory-processor complex.
- ✅ **PC** holds the *next* instruction's address; **IR** holds the *current* instruction. Never confuse the two.
- ✅ **MAR** holds the *address*; **MDR** holds the *data* of a memory operation. Never confuse the two.
- ✅ Three bus types: **Data (bi-directional)**, **Address (uni-directional, CPU → outside)**, **Control (bi-directional)**.
- ✅ An **n-bit address bus** can address $2^{n}$ unique memory locations.
- ✅ A **single-bus CPU** has 1 common bus → ALU needs **2 cycles** for two operands. A **three-bus CPU** needs only **1 cycle** — fastest.
- ✅ **CPU Time = IC × CPI × Clock Cycle Time** is the single most important performance formula. Use it whenever asked for execution time.
- ✅ **MIPS** measures *throughput* (million instructions per second). **CPU Time** measures *latency* (how long to finish).
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law** shows that speedup is limited by the *un-enhanced* portion — there is no "free lunch" in performance optimization.
- ✅ The **Von Neumann bottleneck** is the single shared bus between CPU and memory — modern computers use **caches** to overcome it.

> **Good luck with your KTU Exam! 🎓✨**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Operational Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Operational Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Basic Operational Concepts** in Computer Organization refer to the fundamental principles governing the interaction between a computer's three primary functional units — the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, the **Main Memory (MM)**, and the **Input/Output (I/O) devices** — coordinated by a shared communication backbone called the **System Bus**. These concepts describe how instructions are fetched, decoded, executed, and how data is moved between the processor, memory, and peripheral devices under both program control and interrupt-driven mechanisms.

The term **operational** emphasizes the *dynamic* behavior of the machine: the flow of addresses, data, and control signals during every clock cycle. The KTU 2024 Scheme frames this topic under the broader umbrella of single-processor architecture, with a strong emphasis on the **bus-based interconnection model**, the **interrupt mechanism**, the **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** paradigm, and the **performance metrics** (CPI, MIPS, CPU Time, Speedup) used to quantify machine behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The 2024 scheme categorizes the following under "Basic Operational Concepts":
> 1. Functional units of a computer
> 2. System bus and bus arbitration
> 3. Interrupts and interrupt service routines
> 4. Direct Memory Access (DMA)
> 5. Performance metrics: CPI, MIPS, CPU execution time, Amdahl's Law
> 6. Multi-module memory and interleaving

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen. Here is the mapping:

| Kitchen Element | Computer Equivalent | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Head Chef** | **CPU (Processor)** | Decides what to cook, follows recipes, controls the process |
| **Walk-in Pantry** | **Main Memory (RAM)** | Stores raw ingredients (data) and recipes (instructions) |
| **Serving Counter** | **I/O Devices** | Interface with customers (users) — takes orders, serves food |
| **Waiter's Path** | **System Bus** | Carries orders (addresses), ingredients (data), and signals (control) |
| **Order Bell** | **Interrupt Line** | A loud alert that interrupts the chef to handle a priority task |

In this analogy:
- The **chef (CPU)** never leaves the kitchen — he sends runners (bus signals) to fetch items from the **pantry (memory)**.
- When a customer rings the **bell (interrupt)**, the chef pauses his current recipe, services the customer, then resumes.
- For a *bulk order* (DMA), the chef hires a helper to transfer crates directly from the supplier's truck to the pantry without involving the chef.

This analogy makes it intuitively clear why **buses** are the lifelines of the system, why **interrupts** are essential for responsiveness, and why **DMA** exists to offload the CPU from bulk data movement.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

The following standard values are expected to be memorized for KTU 2024 examinations:

- **Clock Rate ($f$)**: Measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, typically **MHz** or **GHz** for modern processors.
- **Clock Cycle Time ($T$)**: $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$, measured in **seconds** (or nanoseconds for high-speed CPUs).
- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)**: A dimensionless ratio, typically between **1 and 10** for RISC/CISC processors.
- **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second)**: A performance rate metric, unit is **$10^6$ instructions/sec**.
- **Bus Width ($n$)**: Number of parallel data/address lines, typically **8, 16, 32, or 64** bits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Three-Bus Model**: Every KTU 2024 board question on this topic assumes the canonical three-bus architecture (Address Bus, Data Bus, Control Bus) unless explicitly stated otherwise. Always draw a clean, labeled block diagram before any solution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Functional Units — A Structured Breakdown

### 2.1.1 The Central Processing Unit (CPU)
The CPU is the *brain* of the computer. It contains:
- **Control Unit (CU)**: Generates the timing and control signals that orchestrate every operation.
- **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**: Performs all arithmetic ($+$, $-$, $\times$, $\div$) and logical (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, shift) operations.
- **Register File**: A set of high-speed on-chip storage locations. KTU 2024 explicitly highlights:
  - **Program Counter (PC)** — Holds the address of the *next* instruction to be fetched.
  - **Instruction Register (IR)** — Holds the *currently executing* instruction.
  - **Memory Address Register (MAR)** — Holds the address for the next memory access.
  - **Memory Data Register (MDR)** — Temporarily holds data being transferred to/from memory.
  - **Accumulator (AC)** — Default operand and result register in many architectures.
  - **Stack Pointer (SP)** — Points to the top of the system stack.

**Why these registers?** The CPU cannot talk to memory directly without first placing the address in the MAR — the MAR is essentially the *envelope* in which the request is mailed. The MDR is the *truck* that carries the payload. The PC is the *cursor* in your editor.

### 2.1.2 Main Memory (Primary Storage)
Main memory is **volatile** (loses contents on power-off), organized as a linear array of addressable words. Each location has a unique address.

- **Word Size** = number of bits read/written in one memory operation (typically 32 or 64 bits).
- **Address Space Size** = $2^n$ locations, where $n$ is the number of address lines.

**Why it is called "primary"**: It is the only large, fast storage the CPU can address directly. Secondary storage (HDDs, SSDs) must first transfer data *into* main memory before the CPU can process it.

### 2.1.3 Input/Output Subsystem
I/O devices include keyboards, displays, disks, network cards, etc. They are connected to the CPU/memory via **I/O interfaces** (controllers). Two addressing schemes exist:
1. **Memory-Mapped I/O**: I/O devices are treated as memory locations; the same instructions (load/store) are used.
2. **Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped I/O)**: Separate I/O address space; special IN/OUT instructions are needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 board questions frequently test **memory-mapped vs. isolated I/O** as a 7-mark Part B question. Always compare both with a small comparison table.

## 2.2 The System Bus — Communication Backbone

A **bus** is a shared communication pathway. Three bus types exist:

| Bus Type | Direction | Width (Typical) | Carries |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Address Bus** | CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory/I/O | 16, 20, 24, 32 bits | Memory or I/O address |
| **Data Bus** | Bidirectional | 8, 16, 32, 64 bits | Actual data being transferred |
| **Control Bus** | Bidirectional | $\sim$10–20 lines | Read/Write, Interrupt, Clock, Reset signals |

### 2.2.1 Bus Arbitration
When multiple devices want to use the bus simultaneously, an **arbiter** decides who goes first. Two common schemes:
- **Daisy Chain Arbitration** — Simple, priority hardwired by physical position.
- **Centralized Parallel Arbitration** — Faster, uses a priority encoder.

**Why it matters**: Without arbitration, two devices could drive the data bus simultaneously, causing **bus contention** — a short circuit that can damage hardware.

## 2.3 Interrupts — The Asynchronous Alert System

An **interrupt** is an *external* (or sometimes *internal*, called *traps/exceptions*) signal that temporarily suspends the CPU's current execution, forces it to jump to a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, and then resume the original program.

### 2.3.1 The Interrupt Handling Cycle
1. **Device raises interrupt request** on the interrupt line.
2. CPU **finishes the current instruction**.
3. CPU saves the **PC** (and sometimes other registers) onto the **stack**.
4. CPU loads the ISR's address from the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** into the PC.
5. The **ISR executes**.
6. Upon ISR completion, the **return-from-interrupt (RTI/IRET)** instruction pops the saved PC back.
7. CPU **resumes** the original program.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why save the PC?** Because the ISR will itself use the PC to fetch its own instructions, and the main program's location would otherwise be lost. Think of it as bookmarking your novel before answering the doorbell.

### 2.3.2 Types of Interrupts
- **Hardware Interrupts**: External (I/O ready, timer, power failure).
- **Software Interrupts / Traps**: Internal (division by zero, invalid opcode, system call).
- **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI)**: Cannot be ignored (e.g., power failure).
- **Maskable Interrupt**: Can be disabled by setting the interrupt enable flag.

## 2.4 Direct Memory Access (DMA)

**DMA** allows I/O devices to transfer data *directly* to/from main memory **without CPU intervention**, thereby freeing the CPU to do other work.

- The DMA controller takes control of the bus from the CPU using a signal called **HOLD** (or **BUSREQ**).
- The CPU grants the bus using **HLDA** (Hold Acknowledge).
- The DMA controller performs the data transfer, then releases the bus.

**Why DMA?** Consider reading 1 GB from a disk. Without DMA, the CPU would have to fetch every single byte — a massive waste of compute cycles. With DMA, the CPU sets up the DMA controller (source, destination, count) and walks away to do other useful work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Favorite**: The three DMA transfer modes — **Burst Mode**, **Cycle Stealing Mode**, and **Transparent Mode** — are tested almost every year. Memorize the trade-off: Burst is fastest but blocks the CPU; Cycle Stealing is slower but interleaves with CPU work; Transparent is invisible to CPU but only works if CPU is slow enough.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | $T$ = cycle time, $f$ = clock frequency | Relate CPU speed to clock period |
| 2 | $\text{CPU Time} = \text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI} \times T$ | Time taken to execute a program | Core performance metric |
| 3 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{\text{Instruction Count}}{\text{Execution Time} \times 10^6} = \dfrac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}$ | Million instructions per second | Quick performance comparison |
| 4 | $\text{AMAT} = T_{\text{hit}} + \text{Miss Rate} \times T_{\text{miss}}$ | Average memory access time | Cache performance analysis |
| 5 | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}}$ | Improvement ratio from enhancement | Quantify optimization gains |
| 6 | $\text{Amdahl's Law}: S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | $f$ = fraction enhanced, $k$ = speedup of enhancement | Theoretical limit of optimization |
| 7 | $\text{Bus Bandwidth} = \dfrac{\text{Bus Width} \times f}{8}$ bytes/sec | Data transfer rate of bus | I/O performance analysis |
| 8 | $\text{Memory Capacity} = 2^n \times w$ | $n$ = address lines, $w$ = word size | Addressable memory size |
| 9 | $\text{Interleaving Speedup} = \dfrac{1}{n}\left(1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{1}{1+i\cdot r}\right)^{-1}$ | $n$ = modules, $r$ = ratio of access to transfer | Multi-bank memory performance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Pipe Usage in Tables**: All absolute-value and divisibility notations in this sheet use $\vert$ or $\mid$ explicitly inside the formula column to prevent breaking the markdown table parser. In LaTeX-rendered answers, prefer $\lvert x \rvert$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The CPU Performance Equation (KTU High-Priority)

The CPU execution time for a program depends on three factors: how many instructions it has, how many cycles each takes, and how long each cycle is.

**Starting Point (Definition):**
The total number of clock cycles required to execute a program is the sum over all instruction types of (count of that type $\times$ CPI of that type).

$$\text{Total Cycles} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( C_i \times \text{CPI}_i \right)$$

where $C_i$ is the count of instructions of type $i$, and $\text{CPI}_i$ is the average cycles per instruction of type $i$.

**Step 1: Compute the Average CPI**

The average CPI across all instruction types is the total cycles divided by the total instruction count.

$$\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\text{Total Cycles}}{\text{Instruction Count}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \times \text{CPI}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the Basic Time Equation**

CPU Time equals Total Cycles multiplied by the cycle period $T = 1/f$.

$$\text{CPU Time} = \text{Total Cycles} \times T = \text{Total Cycles} \times \frac{1}{f}$$

**Step 3: Final Compact Form**

Multiplying out the instruction count and the average CPI:

$$\text{CPU Time} = \text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} \times T = \frac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}}}{f}$$

**Interpretation**: To make the CPU faster, you must reduce *any one* of the three factors — fewer instructions (better compiler/ISA), lower CPI (better pipeline), or shorter cycle time (faster clock).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: MIPS Rating

MIPS is the **M**illion **I**nstructions **P**er **S**econd. It is the rate of instruction execution.

**Starting Point (Definition):**
$$\text{Instruction Rate} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count}}{\text{Execution Time}} \quad \text{(in instructions/second)}$$

**Step 1: Convert to Millions**

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count}}{\text{Execution Time} \times 10^6}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Execution Time from §3.1**

Using $\text{Execution Time} = \dfrac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}}}{f}$:

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count}}{\left(\frac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}}}{f}\right) \times 10^6} = \frac{f}{\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} \times 10^6}$$

**Engineering Insight**: A higher clock frequency alone does **not** guarantee higher MIPS — if the new architecture has a higher CPI, MIPS can actually *drop*. This is why KTU 2024 questions often ask students to *critique* MIPS as a metric.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Amdahl's Law (Theoretical Speedup Limit)

**Scenario**: A fraction $f$ of a program's execution time is enhanced by a factor of $k$. What is the overall speedup $S$?

**Step 1: Identify the Old Execution Time**
Let the old execution time be $T_{\text{old}}$. By definition:

$$T_{\text{old}} = T_{\text{enhanced}} + T_{\text{unchanged}} = f \cdot T_{\text{old}} + (1-f) \cdot T_{\text{old}}$$

The enhanced portion takes time $f \cdot T_{\text{old}}$, and the unchanged portion takes $(1-f) \cdot T_{\text{old}}$.

**Step 2: Apply the Enhancement to the Enhanced Portion**
The enhanced portion is now $k$ times faster, so its new time is $\dfrac{f \cdot T_{\text{old}}}{k}$. The unchanged portion remains $(1-f) \cdot T_{\text{old}}$.

**Step 3: Sum to Get the New Execution Time**

$$T_{\text{new}} = (1-f) \cdot T_{\text{old}} + \frac{f \cdot T_{\text{old}}}{k}$$

**Step 4: Form the Speedup Ratio**

$$S = \frac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}} = \frac{T_{\text{old}}}{(1-f) \cdot T_{\text{old}} + \frac{f \cdot T_{\text{old}}}{k}}$$

Canceling $T_{\text{old}}$ from numerator and denominator:

$$S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{k}}$$

**Limiting Case (As $k \to \infty$)**:
$$\lim_{k \to \infty} S = \frac{1}{1-f}$$

This is the **theoretical maximum speedup** — no matter how much you enhance the part $f$, the *unchanged* part $(1-f)$ forms an irreducible bottleneck. This is why improving 50% of a program by 1000$\times$ gives only a 2$\times$ speedup.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem (Dec 2023 style)**: A program runs in **100 seconds** on a computer. Of this, **40%** is spent in floating-point operations. We enhance the floating-point unit and make it run **5 times faster**. What is the new total execution time?

**Given**:
$T_{\text{old}} = 100$ s, $f = 0.40$, $k = 5$.

**Solution**:

The enhanced portion (FP ops) takes $0.40 \times 100 = 40$ s.
The unchanged portion takes $0.60 \times 100 = 60$ s.

After enhancement, the FP portion takes $40 / 5 = 8$ s.
The new total time is:

$$T_{\text{new}} = 60 + 8 = 68 \text{ seconds}$$

The speedup is:

$$S = \frac{100}{68} \approx 1.47$$

**Amdahl's check**:
$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.40) + \frac{0.40}{5}} = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.08} = \frac{1}{0.68} \approx 1.47 \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Between Data Transfer Schemes

| Feature | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CPU Involvement** | 100% (busy wait) | Moderate (only on interrupt) | Minimal (only setup/teardown) |
| **Speed for Bulk Transfer** | Very slow | Moderate | Fastest |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Best Use Case** | Simple, infrequent I/O | Keyboard, mouse, low-bandwidth I/O | Disk, network, high-bandwidth I/O |
| **CPU Wasted Time** | High | Low | Very low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Computer System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU with Control Unit and ALU]
    MEM[Main Memory RAM and ROM]
    IO1[Keyboard Input Device]
    IO2[Display Output Device]
    IO3[Disk Storage I-O Device]
    BUS[(System Bus Address Data Control)]

    CPU <--> BUS
    MEM <--> BUS
    IO1 <--> BUS
    IO2 <--> BUS
    IO3 <--> BUS

    classDef cpuClass fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef memClass fill:#A0D8A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ioClass fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef busClass fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class CPU cpuClass
    class MEM memClass
    class IO1,IO2,IO3 ioClass
    class BUS busClass
```

## 4.2 Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU executes current instruction] --> B{Interrupt request received?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Finish current instruction]
    C --> D[Save PC and status flags on stack]
    D --> E[Load ISR address from Interrupt Vector Table]
    E --> F[Execute Interrupt Service Routine]
    F --> G{More interrupts pending?}
    G -- Yes --> F
    G -- No --> H[Execute Return from Interrupt IRET]
    H --> I[Restore PC and status from stack]
    I --> A
```

## 4.3 DMA Transfer Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU
    participant DMAC as DMA Controller
    participant MEM as Main Memory
    participant IO as I-O Device

    CPU->>DMAC: Set source, destination, count
    CPU->>DMAC: Initiate transfer
    CPU->>CPU: Continue other useful work
    DMAC->>CPU: Request bus via HOLD
    CPU->>DMAC: Grant bus via HLDA
    DMAC->>IO: Read data block
    IO->>DMAC: Data word
    DMAC->>MEM: Write data word
    Note over DMAC,MEM: Repeat for entire block
    DMAC->>CPU: Release bus and send interrupt
    CPU->>DMAC: Process completion status
```

## 4.4 Multi-Module Memory Organization with Interleaving

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ADDR[Address Decoder] --> M0[Memory Module 0]
    ADDR --> M1[Memory Module 1]
    ADDR --> M2[Memory Module 2]
    ADDR --> M3[Memory Module 3]
    M0 --> MUX[Multiplexer]
    M1 --> MUX
    M2 --> MUX
    M3 --> MUX
    MUX --> CPU[CPU Data Bus]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interleaving Strategy**: Consecutive addresses are spread across modules in a *round-robin* fashion. If module $M_i$ is busy servicing word $i$, the CPU can immediately issue a request for word $i+1$ to module $M_{i+1}$ in the next cycle. This is called **n-way interleaving**.

## 4.5 Processor-Internal Register Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REG[Register File]
        PC[Program Counter PC]
        IR[Instruction Register IR]
        MAR[Memory Address Register MAR]
        MDR[Memory Data Register MDR]
        AC[Accumulator AC]
        SP[Stack Pointer SP]
    end

    PC --> ADDR_BUS[Address Bus Driver]
    MAR --> ADDR_BUS
    MDR --> DATA_BUS[Data Bus Interface]
    IR --> DECODER[Instruction Decoder]
    DECODER --> CU[Control Unit]
    CU --> ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    AC --> ALU
    ALU --> AC
    SP --> STACK[System Stack in Memory]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between memory-mapped I/O and isolated I/O. List any two advantages of memory-mapped I/O.**

**Model Answer**:

| Feature | Memory-Mapped I/O | Isolated I/O |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Address Space | I/O devices share memory address space | Separate I/O address space |
| Instructions | Uses standard load/store instructions | Requires special IN/OUT instructions |
| Address Lines | Full width of address bus | Limited to fewer lines |
| Flexibility | High — can do arithmetic on I/O data | Lower — limited instruction set |
| Memory Reduction | Reduces available memory space | Full memory available |

**Two Advantages of Memory-Mapped I/O**:
1. A larger set of instructions (all memory-reference instructions) can be used to manipulate I/O data — for example, you can ADD the value of an input port to a memory location directly.
2. No need for separate I/O control lines; existing memory control signals (Memory Read / Memory Write) suffice.

*(1 mark for differentiation table, 2 marks for listing and explaining two advantages.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the three transfer modes of a DMA controller. Which one provides the highest throughput and why?**

**Model Answer**:
