1. **Burst Mode (Block Transfer Mode)**: The DMA controller takes exclusive control of the bus and transfers the entire data block in one go. The CPU is blocked from accessing memory for the entire duration.

2. **Cycle Stealing Mode**: The DMA controller transfers *one word* per request and then releases the bus, allowing the CPU one cycle to access memory. This mode is interleaved — slower per transfer but the CPU never stalls for long.

3. **Transparent Mode**: The DMA controller only transfers data during cycles when the CPU is *not* using the bus (e.g., during instruction decode or ALU operations). The CPU is entirely unaware of the transfer.

**Highest Throughput**: **Burst Mode** provides the highest throughput because the entire bus bandwidth is dedicated to the data transfer — no time is wasted on arbitration or CPU-DMA interleaving. However, the CPU is completely blocked during the transfer, which is a major trade-off.

*(1 mark per mode, 1 mark for throughput answer with reasoning.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Draw and explain the functional block diagram of a computer system. Describe the role of the system bus with its three components.**

**Model Solution**:

**Block Diagram** (refer to §4.1 for the mermaid block — draw as a clean labeled box diagram in the answer sheet):

- **CPU** (large rectangle on left) containing **Control Unit** and **ALU**, with internal registers **PC, IR, MAR, MDR, AC, SP**.
- **Main Memory** (rectangle in center) labeled as RAM and ROM.
- **I/O Devices** (right side) — keyboard, printer, disk.
- **System Bus** (three parallel lines at the bottom connecting all three blocks).

**Three Components of System Bus**:

1. **Address Bus**: Carries the memory or I/O address from the CPU to memory/I/O. It is *unidirectional* (CPU is always the source). Width determines the maximum addressable memory: with $n$ address lines, $2^n$ locations are addressable. Typical widths: 16, 20, 24, 32, 64 bits.

2. **Data Bus**: Carries the actual data being transferred. It is *bidirectional*. Width determines how many bits are transferred per memory cycle — the wider the bus, the higher the bandwidth. Typical widths: 8, 16, 32, 64 bits.

3. **Control Bus**: A collection of various control lines (typically 10–20) such as:
   - **Memory Read (MR)**, **Memory Write (MW)**: Indicate memory access direction.
   - **I/O Read (IOR)**, **I/O Write (IOW)**: Indicate I/O access direction.
   - **Interrupt Request (INTR)**, **Interrupt Acknowledge (INTA)**: For interrupts.
   - **Bus Request (BR)**, **Bus Grant (BG)**: For DMA and bus arbitration.
   - **Clock**, **Reset**: System-level signals.

**Key Roles**:
- The **bus acts as the shared communication medium** for all components.
- Only **one device can drive the bus at a time** (bus ownership rule).
- **Bus arbitration logic** is required when multiple devices compete.

*(3 marks for the block diagram, 4 marks for the detailed description of each bus type and their roles.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the interrupt-driven I/O mechanism. How does the CPU distinguish between hardware and software interrupts?**

**Model Solution**:

**Interrupt-Driven I/O Flow** (refer to §4.2 mermaid — translate to a clean flow diagram in the answer sheet):

1. The CPU starts an I/O operation and *continues executing other instructions* (does not wait).
2. The I/O device, when ready, raises an **interrupt request** on the INTR line.
3. The CPU, upon finishing the current instruction, checks the INTR line.
4. If an interrupt is pending and enabled, the CPU:
   - Saves the **Program Counter** (PC) and **Status Flags** onto the **system stack**.
   - Loads the **ISR address** from the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** into the PC.
5. The **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** executes, performing the data transfer.
6. The **IRET (Return from Interrupt)** instruction restores the PC and flags from the stack.
7. The original program resumes exactly where it left off.

**Distinguishing Hardware vs Software Interrupts**:

| Aspect | Hardware Interrupt | Software Interrupt / Trap |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Source** | External I/O device or hardware signal | Internal — generated by program instruction (e.g., `INT n`, `SYSCALL`) or by exception (divide by zero) |
| **Trigger** | Asynchronous (can occur at any time) | Synchronous (occurs at a specific point in execution) |
| **Use Case** | I/O completion, timer, power failure | Operating system services, debugging, error handling |
| **Vectoring** | Uses IVT entries for I/O devices | Uses IVT entries reserved for traps |
| **Maskability** | Often maskable (except NMI) | Generally cannot be masked |

The CPU distinguishes them by **the source of the interrupt signal and the IVT entry** — each interrupt type has a unique vector number pointing to a different ISR.

*(3 marks for the flow diagram and ISR sequence, 2 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for the explanation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Derive Amdahl's Law for speedup. A program runs in 200 seconds. 30% of the time is spent in I/O operations. If we replace the I/O subsystem with one that is 10 times faster, what is the new execution time and the overall speedup?**

**Model Solution**:

**Derivation of Amdahl's Law** (refer to §3.3 for full derivation):

The original execution time is $T_{\text{old}}$. The portion being enhanced is $f \cdot T_{\text{old}}$; the unchanged portion is $(1-f) \cdot T_{\text{old}}$. After enhancement by factor $k$:

$$T_{\text{new}} = (1-f) \cdot T_{\text{old}} + \frac{f \cdot T_{\text{old}}}{k}$$

Dividing $T_{\text{old}}$ by $T_{\text{new}}$:

$$S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{k}}$$

**Numerical Solution**:

Given: $T_{\text{old}} = 200$ s, $f = 0.30$ (I/O operations), $k = 10$ (I/O is 10× faster).

The I/O portion originally takes $0.30 \times 200 = 60$ s.
The non-I/O portion takes $0.70 \times 200 = 140$ s.

After enhancement, the I/O portion takes $60 / 10 = 6$ s.

The new total execution time:

$$T_{\text{new}} = 140 + 6 = 146 \text{ seconds}$$

The overall speedup:

$$S = \frac{200}{146} \approx 1.37$$

**Verification using Amdahl's formula**:

$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.30) + \frac{0.30}{10}} = \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.03} = \frac{1}{0.73} \approx 1.37 \quad \checkmark$$

**Insight**: Even with a 10× speedup on 30% of the program, the overall speedup is only 1.37×. This is because the unchanged 70% dominates. The theoretical maximum speedup (if I/O were instantaneous) would be $1/0.70 \approx 1.43$.

*(4 marks for derivation, 3 marks for numerical computation.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain multi-module memory with interleaving. A computer has 4 memory modules, each with an access time of 200 ns and a transfer time of 50 ns per word. Calculate the effective memory access time for a sequence of 8 word reads in a 4-way interleaved system.**

**Model Solution**:

**Multi-Module Interleaved Memory Concept** (refer to §4.4 diagram — redraw as a block diagram in the answer sheet):

In multi-module memory, the address space is divided across $n$ modules. Consecutive addresses are placed in consecutive modules in a round-robin pattern — i.e., address $i$ is in module $(i \mod n)$.

**Why Interleaving Works**: When a sequence of consecutive words is accessed, the modules operate in *parallel*. While one module is busy with its transfer, the next module can be starting its access. This **pipelining of memory operations** hides the long access latency.

**Key Time Parameters**:
- Access Time ($t_a$) = 200 ns — time to start fetching from a module.
- Transfer Time ($t_t$) = 50 ns — time to transfer one word after access is complete.
- Total per-word service time = $t_a + t_t$ = 250 ns if done serially.

**Calculation for 4-Way Interleaving with 8 Word Reads**:

The total time consists of:
- The **first access** of module 0 (takes full $t_a + t_t$ = 250 ns).
- For each subsequent word, the next module needs only $t_t$ if its access has already been initiated in parallel, but in the worst case the pattern requires $t_t$ per word after the first.

In the simple interleaving model, the time for $N$ words in $n$ modules is:

$$T = n \cdot t_a + N \cdot t_t$$

But more accurately, with the *pipelined overlap* model, the access of module $i+1$ overlaps with the transfer of module $i$:

$$T = t_a + (N - 1) \cdot t_t + t_t = t_a + N \cdot t_t$$

Substituting $t_a = 200$ ns, $N = 8$ words, $t_t = 50$ ns:

$$T = 200 + 8 \times 50 = 200 + 400 = 600 \text{ ns}$$

**Without Interleaving (Sequential)**:
$$T_{\text{seq}} = 8 \times (t_a + t_t) = 8 \times 250 = 2000 \text{ ns}$$

**Effective Speedup**:
$$S = \frac{2000}{600} \approx 3.33$$

**Effective Average Access Time per Word**:
$$T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{600}{8} = 75 \text{ ns/word}$$

This is a **3.33× improvement** over the sequential case (250 ns/word).

*(3 marks for the explanation of interleaving, 4 marks for the calculation with full steps.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Basic Operational Concepts (KTU 2024 Board)**:
> 1. **Forgetting to label bus directions**: When drawing the block diagram, students often draw the address bus as bidirectional. **It is strictly unidirectional** (CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory/I/O). Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this error.
> 2. **Confusing PC and MAR**: The PC holds the address of the *next instruction*; the MAR holds the *current memory access address*. During instruction fetch, both contain the same value, but they serve different purposes. Mixing them up loses **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **In Amdahl's Law problems, students often forget that $f$ is the *time fraction*, not the instruction count fraction**, unless the problem explicitly states the CPI is uniform.
> 4. **For DMA**: Many students write "DMA is faster than interrupts" without specifying *what kind* of transfer. Always clarify that DMA excels for *bulk* transfers; interrupts are better for *small, frequent* events.
> 5. **Pipe characters in tables**: When writing comparison tables in the answer sheet, avoid mixing vertical bars inside cells (e.g., $\vert 5 \vert$) with the table border pipes — use the LaTeX `\vert` command or write "absolute value of 5" to prevent formatting collapse.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three Functional Units**: CPU, Main Memory, I/O — all communicate via the **System Bus**.
- **System Bus has three sub-buses**: **Address** (unidirectional, width = address space), **Data** (bidirectional, width = word size), **Control** (read/write, interrupt, clock signals).
- **CPU Internal Registers to Memorize**: **PC, IR, MAR, MDR, AC, SP**.
- **Interrupt Cycle**: Receive interrupt $\rightarrow$ save PC $\rightarrow$ load ISR address from IVT $\rightarrow$ execute ISR $\rightarrow$ restore PC $\rightarrow$ resume.
- **DMA Three Modes**: **Burst** (fastest, blocks CPU), **Cycle Stealing** (interleaved), **Transparent** (CPU-unaware, slowest).
- **Memory-Mapped vs Isolated I/O**: Memory-mapped uses full instruction set and shared address space; isolated uses special IN/OUT instructions.
- **CPU Time Equation**: $\text{CPU Time} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T = \dfrac{\text{IC} \times \text{CPI}}{f}$.
- **MIPS Rating**: $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}$ — higher frequency alone does not guarantee higher MIPS.
- **Amdahl's Law**: $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/k}$ — the *unchanged* portion is the hard ceiling on speedup.
- **Multi-Module Interleaving**: Distribute consecutive addresses across $n$ modules to enable parallel access; effective access time for $N$ words drops from $N \cdot (t_a + t_t)$ to approximately $t_a + N \cdot t_t$.
- **Bus Arbitration**: Needed whenever multiple devices (CPU, DMA, multi-core) want simultaneous access — uses schemes like daisy chain or centralized parallel.
- **Performance Triangle**: To make a program faster, you can attack *any one* of {Instruction Count, CPI, Cycle Time} — but a *balanced* design wins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Tip**: In KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam), 14-mark Part B questions on this topic *always* combine a theoretical block-diagram question (≈7 marks) with a numerical performance question (≈7 marks). Practice drawing clean, labeled diagrams and writing all derivation steps explicitly. The board rewards method marks heavily.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bus Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bus Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **BUS (Binary Unit System):** A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of a set of common lines (wires/traces) through which data, addresses, and control signals are transmitted between the major components of a computer system, such as the **CPU**, **Main Memory**, and **I/O devices**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Computer Organization syllabus, a bus is the **backbone of the system architecture** — a single set of multiplexed or dedicated conductors that allows multiple subsystems to communicate without point-to-point wiring between every pair of devices.

### The Three Classical Bus Types

| Bus Type | Direction | Primary Function | Typical Width (Modern) |
|----------|-----------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Data Bus** | **Bidirectional** | Carries the actual data being transferred (read/write operands, instructions) | 32-bit, 64-bit |
| **Address Bus** | **Unidirectional** (CPU → Memory/I/O) | Carries the memory or I/O port address specifying *where* data goes | 32-bit, 36-bit, 64-bit |
| **Control Bus** | **Bidirectional / Mixed** | Carries timing & command signals (Read/Write, IRQ, Clock, Reset) | Signal lines (not word-sized) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The aggregate of *Data Bus + Address Bus + Control Bus* is called the **System Bus**. When this is extended to include I/O devices explicitly, it is referred to as the **Peripheral Bus** or simply the **I/O Bus**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway System" Model

Imagine a city with three types of roads:

- **Address Bus** → Like the **house number on a letter**. The postman (CPU) writes the destination address so the letter reaches the correct house (memory location). It only flows *outward* from the post office.
- **Data Bus** → Like the **letter itself** (or the reply). It can travel in both directions — the postman delivers a letter, or a resident sends a reply back.
- **Control Bus** → Like the **traffic signals and road signs** ("Stop", "Go", "One-Way"). They coordinate *when* and *how* the letters move.

> **Key Insight:** All three bus types must work **in concert**. The CPU places an address on the address bus, asserts a *Read* or *Write* signal on the control bus, and then data flows on the data bus. If any one of the three is missing, the system cannot communicate.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in **bold**)

- **Bus Width (n)**: Number of parallel lines. Determines the amount of data transferred per cycle.
  - A **32-bit bus** can transfer **4 bytes** per bus cycle.
  - A **64-bit bus** can transfer **8 bytes** per bus cycle.
- **Bus Bandwidth (B)**: The maximum data transfer rate, measured in **bytes/second** or **bits/second**.
- **Bus Cycle Time (T)**: Time taken for one complete bus transaction, measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **microseconds (µs)**.
- **Clock Frequency (f)**: The bus clock speed, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, **MHz**, or **GHz**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Relationship (must memorize):**
> $$\text{Bandwidth} = \frac{\text{Bus Width} \times \text{Transfer Rate}}{\text{8}} \quad \text{(in bytes/second)}$$

---

## 1.4 Why Bus Structures Matter in Modern Engineering

- **Cost Reduction:** A shared bus eliminates the need for massive point-to-point wiring matrices.
- **Modularity:** New devices can be added to the bus with minimal redesign — this is the foundation of **plug-and-play** architectures.
- **Scalability Limits:** As clock speeds and device counts increase, single buses become a **bottleneck**, leading to modern multi-bus hierarchies (e.g., Front Side Bus → QuickPath Interconnect → PCI Express).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — How a Bus Transaction Works

A single bus transaction (e.g., a **Memory Read**) follows a strict sequence:

1. **Address Phase:** CPU places the target memory address on the **Address Bus**. Only one device can drive the address bus at a time.
2. **Command Phase:** CPU asserts a control signal (e.g., `MEMREAD` = 1) on the **Control Bus** to indicate the operation type.
3. **Data Phase:** The addressed memory device places the requested data on the **Data Bus**, and the CPU reads it.
4. **De-assertion:** Control signals are released, and all bus lines return to idle state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bus Master vs. Bus Slave:**
> - **Bus Master:** The device that *initiates* and *controls* the bus transaction (typically the CPU or a DMA controller).
> - **Bus Slave:** The device that *responds* to the master's request (typically memory or I/O devices).
> - **At any given time, only ONE master can control the bus.** This necessitates **Bus Arbitration**.

---

## 2.2 Bus Organization Architectures

### 2.2.1 Single Bus Structure
- All components (CPU, Memory, I/O) share **one common bus**.
- **Advantage:** Simple, low cost, easy to implement.
- **Disadvantage:** Severe bottleneck — only one transaction at a time. Speed is limited by the slowest device.

### 2.2.2 Two-Bus Structure
- **Local Bus:** High-speed bus connecting CPU and Cache/Main Memory.
- **System/Peripheral Bus:** Connects I/O devices, operating at lower speed.
- **Advantage:** Allows concurrent operations (e.g., CPU↔Memory on local bus while I/O↔Memory on system bus).
- **Disadvantage:** More complex bridge logic required.

### 2.2.3 Three-Bus Structure (Most Common in Modern Systems)
- **Local Bus** (CPU ↔ Cache)
- **System Bus** (Cache/Main Memory Bridge ↔ Main Memory)
- **Expansion Bus** (I/O devices, peripherals)
- Connected via **Bus Bridges** (e.g., Northbridge/Southbridge in older Intel chipsets).

---

## 2.3 Bus Arbitration Methods

When multiple masters request the bus simultaneously, **arbitration** resolves the conflict.

| Method | Type | Description | Drawback |
|--------|------|-------------|----------|
| **Daisy Chain Arbitration** | Centralized (hardware) | Priority is hard-wired by physical position; highest priority device is at the start of the chain. | Fixed priority; failure of one device can break the chain. |
| **Independent Request & Grant** | Centralized (parallel) | Each master has separate request (`REQ`) and grant (`GNT`) lines to a central arbiter. | Scalability limited by pin count. |
| **Polling (Rotating Priority)** | Centralized | Arbiter polls masters in a round-robin fashion. | Latency for low-priority devices. |
| **Distributed Arbitration** | Distributed | No central arbiter; each device has a unique arbitration ID and participates in the decision (e.g., using wired-OR logic). | Complex hardware; slower arbitration time. |

---

## 2.4 Bus Timing — Synchronous vs. Asynchronous

### Synchronous Bus
- All operations are synchronized to a **common bus clock**.
- Fixed protocol: every event happens at a predefined clock edge.
- **Advantage:** Simple, fast, predictable.
- **Disadvantage:** Must run at the speed of the slowest device, leading to "wait states" for faster devices.

### Asynchronous Bus
- **No common clock.** Handshaking signals (`READY`, `ACK`) coordinate the transfer.
- Each event happens when the previous one is acknowledged.
- **Advantage:** Can accommodate devices of widely varying speeds; no wait states.
- **Disadvantage:** More complex control logic; slower due to handshaking overhead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The *Asynchronous Bus* uses the classic **4-cycle handshaking protocol**:
> 1. Master places address & command
> 2. Master asserts `MSYN` (Master Synchronization)
> 3. Slave performs operation, then asserts `SSYN` (Slave Synchronization)
> 4. Master reads data, then de-asserts `MSYN`; Slave de-asserts `SSYN`

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Remarks |
|---------|----------------------|-------|---------|
| **Bus Bandwidth** | $B = \dfrac{n \times f}{8}$ | bytes/sec | $n$ = bus width (bits), $f$ = clock frequency (Hz) |
| **Data per Cycle** | $D = \dfrac{n}{8}$ | bytes/cycle | For an $n$-bit bus |
| **Transfer Time** | $T = \dfrac{N}{B}$ | seconds | $N$ = total data (bytes) to transfer |
| **Effective Bandwidth (with wait states)** | $B_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{n}{8 \times (c + w) \times T_{\text{clk}}}$ | bytes/sec | $c$ = clock cycles per transfer, $w$ = wait states |
| **Address Space** | $A = 2^m$ | locations | $m$ = number of address bus lines |
| **Maximum Memory** | $M = 2^m \times \dfrac{n}{8}$ | bytes | $m$ address lines, $n$-bit data bus |
| **Synchronous Bus Cycle** | $T_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{bus}}}$ | seconds | $f_{\text{bus}}$ in Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** For KTU problems asking "how much memory can be addressed with a $m$-bit address bus," the answer is **$2^m$ addressable locations**. If each location is $n$ bits wide, total memory is $2^m \times n$ bits = $2^{m-3} \times n$ bytes (for byte-addressable memory with $n$-bit words).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Motherboard Design:** Every modern motherboard uses a layered bus architecture (FSB/QPI, DMI, PCIe, USB, SATA).
- **Embedded Systems:** Microcontrollers (e.g., ARM Cortex-M) use the **AMBA bus** (AHB, APB) — a textbook example of a three-bus structure.
- **System-on-Chip (SoC):** On-chip interconnects (AXI, TileLink) are essentially highly parallel, pipelined bus structures.
- **Network-on-Chip (NoC):** As integration scales to billions of transistors, traditional shared buses are replaced by routed packet-switched networks — the evolution of the bus concept.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Problem 1 — Bandwidth Calculation (Full Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** A computer system has a **32-bit data bus** and operates at a bus clock frequency of **200 MHz**. Each bus transfer takes exactly **1 clock cycle**. Calculate the maximum bus bandwidth in **MB/s** (megabytes per second).

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters.**
- Bus width: $n = 32$ bits
- Bus clock frequency: $f = 200 \text{ MHz} = 200 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- Wait states: $w = 0$
- Clock cycles per transfer: $c = 1$

**Step 2 — Apply the bandwidth formula for a synchronous bus without wait states.**
$$B = \frac{n \times f}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**
$$B = \frac{32 \times 200 \times 10^6}{8}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the numerator.**
$$B = \frac{6400 \times 10^6}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Perform the division.**
$$B = 800 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/second}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to MB/s.**
$$B = 800 \text{ MB/s}$$

> **Final Answer:** The maximum bus bandwidth is **800 MB/s**.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Problem 2 — Effect of Wait States

> **Problem:** Suppose the same system now has a memory device that requires **3 wait states** per bus cycle. Each transfer now takes **4 clock cycles** (1 normal + 3 wait). Recalculate the effective bandwidth.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the new parameters.**
- Total cycles per transfer: $c + w = 1 + 3 = 4$ cycles
- Clock period: $T_{\text{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{200 \times 10^6} = 5 \text{ ns}$

**Step 2 — Apply the effective bandwidth formula.**
$$B_{\text{eff}} = \frac{n}{8 \times (c + w) \times T_{\text{clk}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**
$$B_{\text{eff}} = \frac{32}{8 \times 4 \times 5 \times 10^{-9}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator.**
$$8 \times 4 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} = 160 \times 10^{-9} = 1.6 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 5 — Perform the division.**
$$B_{\text{eff}} = \frac{32}{1.6 \times 10^{-7}} = 20 \times 10^{7} \text{ bytes/second}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to MB/s.**
$$B_{\text{eff}} = 200 \text{ MB/s}$$

> **Final Answer:** With 3 wait states, the effective bandwidth drops to **200 MB/s** — a **75% reduction** in performance. This is exactly why **cache memory** exists in real systems!

---

## 3.3 Derivative Problem 3 — Address Space Calculation

> **Problem:** A system uses a **24-bit address bus** and a **16-bit data bus**. How much memory can the CPU directly address, in (a) words, (b) bytes (assuming byte-addressable memory)?

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Addressable locations.**
$$A = 2^m = 2^{24} = 16,777,216 \text{ locations}$$

**Part (a) — Memory in words:**
Since the data bus is 16 bits = 1 word per location,
$$M_{\text{words}} = 2^{24} = 16 \text{ M-words}$$

**Part (b) — Memory in bytes (byte-addressable):**
Each 16-bit word contains 2 bytes, so:
$$M_{\text{bytes}} = 2^{24} \times 2 = 2^{25} \text{ bytes}$$

Converting to MB:
$$M_{\text{bytes}} = \dfrac{2^{25}}{2^{20}} = 2^5 = 32 \text{ MB}$$

> **Final Answer:**
> - (a) **16 M-words** of 16 bits each
> - (b) **32 MB** of byte-addressable memory

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Bus Comparison

| Feature | Single Bus | Two-Bus | Three-Bus |
|---------|------------|---------|-----------|
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Throughput** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Concurrent Transfers** | No | Limited (2 simultaneous) | Yes (3 simultaneous) |
| **Bus Arbitration Needed?** | Yes (if multiple masters) | Yes | Yes |
| **Typical Use Case** | Simple embedded MCUs | Mid-range MCUs (e.g., 8051) | Modern CPUs, SoCs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Bus Block Diagram (Three-Bus Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows indicate the direction of signal flow. Notice that the **Address Bus** flows only from the CPU outward, the **Data Bus** is bidirectional, and the **Control Bus** carries mixed-direction timing signals.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU[CPU / Microprocessor]
    MEM[Main Memory RAM and ROM]
    IO1[I/O Device 1 - Keyboard]
    IO2[I/O Device 2 - Disk Controller]
    IO3[I/O Device 3 - Display]
    DMA[DMA Controller - Bus Master]
    ARB[Bus Arbiter]

    CPU -- Address Bus: 32 bit unidirectional --> MEM
    CPU -- Data Bus: 64 bit bidirectional --> MEM
    CPU -- Control Bus: Read, Write, CLK, INT --> MEM

    CPU <-- Address Bus --> IO1
    CPU <-- Data Bus --> IO1
    CPU <-- Control Bus --> IO1

    CPU <-- Address Bus --> IO2
    CPU <-- Data Bus --> IO2
    CPU <-- Control Bus --> IO2

    CPU <-- Address Bus --> IO3
    CPU <-- Data Bus --> IO3
    CPU <-- Control Bus --> IO3

    DMA -. Bus Request REQ .-> ARB
    CPU -. Bus Grant GNT .-> ARB
    DMA <-- System Bus --> MEM
    DMA <-- System Bus --> IO2

    style CPU fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style MEM fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.2 Bus Arbitration — Daisy Chain (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CPU - Highest Priority Master] -->|Grant Out| B[DMA Controller]
    B -->|Grant Out| C[Math Co-Processor]
    C -->|Grant Out| D[I/O Controller - Lowest Priority]
    D -->|Bus Busy| A

    A -.REQ.-> BUS((Shared System Bus))
    B -.REQ.-> BUS
    C -.REQ.-> BUS
    D -.REQ.-> BUS

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style BUS fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000

    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **Grant signal propagates serially** from the highest-priority device to the lowest. The first device in the chain that has asserted a request claims the bus and blocks the grant from propagating further.

## 4.3 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Bus Timing (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SYNC[Synchronous Bus]
        S1[Clock Edge 1: Address placed on bus] --> S2[Clock Edge 2: Read command asserted]
        S2 --> S3[Clock Edge 3: Data sampled by master]
        S3 --> S4[Clock Edge 4: Bus released]
    end

    subgraph ASYNC[Asynchronous Bus 4-Cycle Handshake]
        A1[Step 1: Master places address and command] --> A2[Step 2: Master asserts MSYN]
        A2 --> A3[Step 3: Slave decodes and responds with data, asserts SSYN]
        A3 --> A4[Step 4: Master reads data, de asserts MSYN, Slave de asserts SSYN]
    end
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define a bus. List and briefly explain the three types of buses in a computer system.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of a set of common signal lines used to transfer data, addresses, and control signals between the CPU, memory, and I/O devices.
>
> The three types are:
> 1. **Data Bus:** Carries the actual data (instructions, operands) between components. It is **bidirectional** since data flows both to and from the CPU.
> 2. **Address Bus:** Carries the memory or I/O address from the CPU to the memory/I/O devices. It is **unidirectional** (CPU → outside) and its width determines the addressable memory space ($2^m$ locations for an $m$-bit address bus).
> 3. **Control Bus:** Carries timing and command signals such as Read, Write, Interrupt, Clock, and Reset. It coordinates the activities of all connected devices.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous buses. Give one example of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Synchronous Bus | Asynchronous Bus |
> |---------|-----------------|-------------------|
> | Timing Reference | Common bus clock | Handshaking signals (MSYN/SSYN) |
> | Speed of Devices | All must match bus clock | Devices of varying speeds supported |
> | Wait States | Required for slow devices | Not required; handshake handles speed mismatch |
> | Complexity | Simple | Complex |
> | Example | PCI, AMBA AHB | USB (partially), classic 4-cycle handshaking |
>
> **Example:** PCI bus is synchronous; a memory bus using MSYN/SSYN handshaking is asynchronous.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Q: (a)** Explain the three-bus system architecture with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages over single and two-bus structures. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A computer system has a 32-bit address bus and a 16-bit data bus. If the bus clock is 100 MHz and each transfer takes 2 clock cycles, calculate:
- (i) Maximum addressable memory in bytes
- (ii) Maximum bus bandwidth in MB/s **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Three-Bus Architecture (7 marks)

The three-bus architecture uses three separate buses to enable **simultaneous data transfer paths**, eliminating the single-bus bottleneck.

**Block Diagram Description:**
- **Local Bus:** Connects the CPU to the cache memory (and sometimes to the system bus bridge). High-speed, short length.
- **System Bus:** Connects the bus bridge to the main memory. Medium speed.
- **Expansion (I/O) Bus:** Connects the bus bridge to I/O controllers and peripherals. Lower speed, longer length.

The **bus bridge** acts as a protocol and speed converter between the buses.

**Advantages over Single Bus:**
- Allows **concurrent operations** (e.g., CPU can fetch from cache on local bus while I/O transfers happen on expansion bus).
- Eliminates the single-bus bottleneck.
- Each bus can operate at a speed optimal for its connected devices.

**Advantages over Two-Bus:**
- Further isolation of high-speed and low-speed traffic.
- Reduces contention between CPU-memory and I/O-memory transfers.

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Address bus width: $m = 32$ bits
- Data bus width: $n = 16$ bits
- Bus clock: $f = 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- Cycles per transfer: $c = 2$

**(i) Maximum addressable memory in bytes:**

Number of addressable locations:
$$A = 2^{32} = 4,294,967,296 \text{ locations}$$

Memory in bytes (each location = 2 bytes for 16-bit data bus):
$$M = 2^{32} \times 2 = 2^{33} \text{ bytes} = 8 \text{ GB}$$

**(ii) Maximum bus bandwidth in MB/s:**

Each transfer takes 2 cycles, so effective transfer rate is $f/2 = 50 \times 10^6$ transfers/sec.

Bytes per transfer = $16/8 = 2$ bytes.

$$B = 50 \times 10^6 \times 2 = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/sec} = 100 \text{ MB/s}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of bus arbitration. Describe the daisy chain arbitration method with a diagram. Mention two limitations. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a neat timing diagram, explain the 4-cycle asynchronous bus handshaking protocol. Compare it with synchronous bus timing. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Bus Arbitration & Daisy Chain (7 marks)

**Bus Arbitration:** When multiple devices (bus masters) request the bus simultaneously, a mechanism is needed to grant the bus to only one master at a time. This mechanism is called **bus arbitration**.

**Daisy Chain Arbitration (Centralized, Serial Priority):**
- All masters are connected in a **serial chain** to a central arbiter.
- The arbiter sends a **Grant signal** which propagates through the chain.
- The **first device in the chain that has a pending request** captures the grant, claims the bus, and **blocks the grant from propagating further** by asserting "Grant Out = 0".
- Priority is **fixed by physical position** in the chain — the first device has the highest priority.

**Diagram (as shown in Section 4.2 above) — refer to the Mermaid block diagram.**

**Limitations:**
1. **Fixed priority:** Low-priority devices may suffer indefinite starvation if high-priority devices keep requesting the bus.
2. **Chain failure:** A faulty device in the middle of the chain breaks the grant propagation, potentially hanging the entire arbitration system.
3. **Propagation delay:** Grant signal must ripple through all devices, limiting the speed for long chains.
4. **No flexibility:** Priority cannot be dynamically reconfigured.

### Part (b) — Asynchronous Handshaking Protocol (7 marks)

The **4-cycle handshaking protocol** for a memory read operation proceeds as follows:

**Cycle 1:** Master places the memory address on the **address bus** and read command on the **control bus**.

**Cycle 2:** Master asserts `MSYN` (Master Synchronization) signal = 1, indicating valid address and command are present.

**Cycle 3:** The addressed slave (memory) decodes the address, retrieves the data, places it on the **data bus**, and asserts `SSYN` (Slave Synchronization) = 1 to indicate data is ready.

**Cycle 4:** Master reads the data, then **de-asserts `MSYN`** (MSYN = 0). Upon seeing MSYN = 0, the slave **de-asserts `SSYN`** (SSYN = 0). The bus returns to idle.

**Timing Flow (as shown in Section 4.3 above) — refer to the Mermaid diagram.**

**Comparison with Synchronous Bus:**

| Aspect | Asynchronous (4-cycle) | Synchronous |
|--------|------------------------|-------------|
| Timing reference | Handshake signals (MSYN/SSYN) | Common clock |
| Flexibility | Adapts to device speed | Fixed timing |
| Speed matching | Automatic | Requires wait states |
| Hardware complexity | High (handshake logic) | Low |
| Throughput | Lower per cycle, but no idle time waiting | Higher, but includes wait states for slow devices |
| Use case | Mixed-speed peripherals, older mainframes | Modern CPU-memory buses (PCI, AHB) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Bus Width vs. Address Space:** Students often state "32-bit address bus = 32 GB memory." The correct answer is $2^{32}$ addressable **locations**; total memory depends on the data bus width (word size) too. *(Lose 1-2 marks)*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 8 in bandwidth:** Bandwidth formula $B = n \times f$ gives **bits/sec**, not **bytes/sec**. Always divide by 8 to get bytes/sec. *(Lose 1 mark)*
>
> 3. **Mixing up MSYN and SSYN roles:** `MSYN` is asserted by the **Master** (CPU), `SSYN` is asserted by the **Slave** (Memory). Many students swap them. *(Lose 2 marks in the 4-cycle handshaking question)*
>
> 4. **Daisy chain priority:** Saying "CPU has highest priority" without explaining *why* (physical position in the chain). Examiners expect the reasoning. *(Lose 1 mark)*
>
> 5. **Forgetting wait states in bandwidth calculations:** If the problem mentions wait states, students often ignore them and use the ideal formula, losing 2-3 marks.
>
> 6. **Not labeling diagram arrows:** In bus block diagrams, students draw boxes but forget to label which lines are Address, Data, and Control buses. Examiners deduct marks for unlabeled diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Quick-Recall Bulletins for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Bus:** Shared communication pathway among CPU, Memory, and I/O.
- **Three Bus Types:**
  - **Data Bus** → bidirectional, carries data
  - **Address Bus** → unidirectional (CPU → outside), carries addresses
  - **Control Bus** → carries timing/command signals (Read, Write, IRQ, Clock)
- **System Bus = Data + Address + Control**
- **Address Space** with $m$-bit address bus = $\mathbf{2^m}$ locations
- **Bus Bandwidth** (ideal, no wait states): $B = \dfrac{n \times f}{8}$ bytes/sec
- **With wait states:** $B_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{n}{8 \times (c + w) \times T_{\text{clk}}}$
- **Bus Master** = device that initiates a transaction; **Bus Slave** = device that responds.
- **Bus Arbitration** needed when multiple masters exist; resolves who gets bus access.
- **Daisy Chain Arbitration:** Serial priority, fixed, first device = highest priority.
- **Independent Requests:** Parallel priority, separate REQ/GNT lines per master.
- **Polling:** Round-robin, fair but adds latency.
- **Distributed Arbitration:** No central arbiter; each device has a unique ID.
- **Synchronous Bus:** Uses common clock; fast but wait states needed for slow devices.
- **Asynchronous Bus:** Uses 4-cycle handshake (MSYN/SSYN); flexible but complex.
- **Three-Bus Architecture:** Local + System + Expansion buses, connected via bridges.
- **Modern Examples:** PCI, PCIe, AMBA (AHB/APB/AXI), USB.
- **Key Trade-off:** More buses = higher throughput & concurrency, but higher cost & complexity.
- **Bus Bridge:** Translates between different bus protocols and speeds.
- **Asynchronous Handshake Sequence:** Address → MSYN → Data Ready & SSYN → De-assertion.

> **One-Line Memory Aid:**
> *"The Address bus ASKS, the Data bus DELIVERS, the Control bus COMMANDS."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Locations and Addresses, Memory Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Memory Locations and Addresses, Memory Operations # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Memory Locations A **memory location** is the smallest addressable unit of physical storage in a computer system, typically holding a fixed number of bits (8 bits = 1 byte in most modern architectures). The computer's main memory (RAM) is conceptually organised as a linear array of such locations, each uniquely identified by a numerical label. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Memory Location):** > "A memory location is a physical storage cell in the main memory, identified by a unique binary address. Each location can store a fixed number of bits (the word size) and serves as the fundamental unit of data storage and retrieval in the von Neumann model." ### 1.2 Memory Addresses A **memory address** is a binary number used by the processor to uniquely identify a specific memory location. The address is generated by the CPU and placed on the **address bus** during any memory access operation. The width of the address bus (number of address bits) directly determines the maximum addressable memory capacity. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology Table (KTU Board Pattern):** > > | Term | Formal Meaning | > |------|---------------| > | Address Space | The complete range of valid addresses a CPU can generate ($0$ to $2^{N}-1$) | > | Addressability | The number of bits stored at each addressable location (usually 8 bits) | > | Word Size | The natural data unit processed by the CPU (8, 16, 32, or 64 bits) | > | Memory Capacity | Total storable data = $2^{N} \times M$ bits | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Post Office Locker System Imagine a **huge post office** with thousands of identical lockers arranged in a single long corridor: - Every locker has the **same size** and can hold a fixed parcel (this is the **memory location**). - Each locker has a **unique number painted on its door** — locker B-204, B-205, etc. (this is the **memory address**). - When you want to **deposit a parcel**, you tell the clerk the locker number, and the parcel is dropped in (**memory WRITE operation**). - When you want to **retrieve a parcel**, you tell the clerk the locker number, and the clerk opens it and hands you the contents (**memory READ operation**). - The **total number of lockers** in the building is the address space, and the **maximum locker number** depends on how many digit slots the clerk's number board has (this is the address bus width). > [!TIP] > The locker number is binary in computers — so a 16-bit address bus allows the "post office" to have $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ lockers, not 10,000 like a decimal system. ## 1.4 Memory Operations — The Two Fundamental Actions The processor can perform exactly **two primitive memory operations**: 1. **READ (Load / Fetch):** The CPU requests the contents of a location. The memory returns a copy of the stored data on the data bus. The original contents remain **unchanged**. 2. **WRITE (Store):** The CPU supplies new data. The memory overwrites the current contents of the specified location with this new value. The old value is **destroyed**. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Misconception (Examiner's Trap):** > A READ operation is **non-destructive** — the contents stay in memory. A WRITE operation is **destructive** — the previous value is lost forever. KTU examiners often test this distinction directly. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Address Space and Capacity — Logical Breakdown Let $N$ be the number of bits on the address bus and $M$ be the number of bits stored at each addressable location (addressability). The system exhibits the following properties: - **Number of addressable locations:** $L = 2^{N}$ - **Total memory capacity in bits:** $C_{\text{bits}} = L \times M = 2^{N} \times M$ - **Total memory capacity in bytes:** $C_{\text{bytes}} = 2^{N} \times \dfrac{M}{8}$ - **Range of valid addresses:** $0$ to $(2^{N} - 1)$ in decimal, or `000...0` to `111...1` in $N$-bit binary > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Result:** > The number of address bits required for a memory of capacity $C$ bytes with byte-addressable scheme is: > $$N = \lceil \log_{2}(C) \rceil$$ > This single formula is a **favourite KTU 3-mark and 14-mark question**. ## 2.2 Byte-Addressable vs Word-Addressable Memory | Property | Byte-Addressable | Word-Addressable | |----------|------------------|------------------| | Smallest addressable unit | 1 byte (8 bits) | 1 word (16/32/64 bits) | | Address increment | $+1$ moves to next byte | $+1$ moves to next word | | Modern CPU usage | **Universal standard** | Rare (mostly historical) | | Alignment complications | Possible unaligned access | Always aligned by definition | ## 2.3 Big-Endian vs Little-Endian Byte Ordering When a multi-byte word (e.g., a 32-bit integer) is stored across multiple byte-addressable locations, there are two conventions for the **order of bytes**: > [!NOTE] > **Big-Endian (Motorola, Sun, network byte order):** The **most significant byte (MSB)** is stored at the **lowest address**. > > **Little-Endian (Intel x86, x86-64, ARM in default mode):** The **least significant byte (LSB)** is stored at the **lowest address**. ### Worked Numerical Example A 32-bit word `0x12345678` stored starting at address `2000`: | Address | Big-Endian Value | Little-Endian Value | |---------|-----------------|---------------------| | 2000 | `12` | `78` | | 2001 | `34` | `56` | | 2002 | `56` | `34` | | 2003 | `78` | `12` | ## 2.4 Register Transfer Notation (RTN) for Memory Operations KTU examiners expect you to write memory operations using **standard register transfer notation**: **READ operation:** $$M[AR] \rightarrow DR$$ > Means: "The contents of the memory location whose address is in the Address Register (AR) are transferred to the Data Register (DR)." **WRITE operation:** $$DR \rightarrow M[AR]$$ > Means: "The contents of the Data Register (DR) are written into the memory location whose address is in the Address Register (AR)." ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |-------------------|---------|---------------| | Addressable locations | $L = 2^{N}$ | $N$ = address bus width | | Memory capacity (bits) | $C = 2^{N} \times M$ | $M$ = addressability in bits | | Memory capacity (bytes) | $C_{B} = 2^{N} \times \dfrac{M}{8}$ | Standard SI multiple | | Required address bits | $N = \lceil \log_{2}(C) \rceil$ | For given capacity $C$ | | Highest address value | $A_{\max} = 2^{N} - 1$ | Decimal form | | Number of address pins | $= N$ | Physical pin count | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **embedded systems design** to determine the address decoder logic, in **operating system design** for virtual memory mapping, and in **memory controller ASIC design** to verify chip-select logic. KTU examiners reward answers that mention one real-world application. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Number of Address Bits for a Given Memory Capacity **Problem Statement (Model KTU style):** A computer has a memory capacity of **16 MB (Mega Bytes)**. The memory is **byte-addressable**. Find: 1. The number of address bits $N$ required. 2. The highest addressable location. 3. The total number of memory locations. 4. If the addressability is changed to 16-bit words, recalculate the number of addressable units. ### Step 1 — Convert memory capacity into a power of 2 We are given $C = 16$ MB. Using the standard SI/engineering convention: $$1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes}$$ $$C = 16 \times 2^{20} = 2^{4} \times 2^{20} = 2^{24} \text{ bytes}$$ > The conversion $16 = 2^{4}$ is the key identity. Always express the multiplier as a power of 2 before combining exponents. ### Step 2 — Apply the formula for required address bits For byte-addressable memory, the addressability $M = 8$ bits, so each location is 1 byte. Therefore the number of locations equals the number of bytes: $$L = C = 2^{24} \text{ locations}$$ Applying $L = 2^{N}$: $$2^{N} = 2^{24} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{N = 24 \text{ address bits}}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the highest addressable location $$A_{\max} = 2^{N} - 1 = 2^{24} - 1 = 16{,}777{,}215$$ In hexadecimal (preferred KTU notation): $$A_{\max} = \text{0xFFFFFF}$$ ### Step 4 — Recalculate for 16-bit word addressability If each address points to a 16-bit (2-byte) word, then the number of addressable units becomes: $$L' = \dfrac{C \text{ (in bytes)} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{16 \text{ bits/word}} = \dfrac{2^{24} \times 8}{16} = 2^{24} \times 2^{-1} = 2^{23} \text{ words}$$ Therefore: $$2^{N'} = 2^{23} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{N' = 23 \text{ address bits}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analytical Insight:** Notice that **word-addressability reduces the address bits by exactly 1** when moving from byte to a 16-bit word, and by 2 when moving to a 32-bit word, etc. This relationship is: > $$N_{\text{word}} = N_{\text{byte}} - \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{word size}}{8}\right)$$ ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Memory Capacity from Address Bus Width **Problem Statement:** A 32-bit processor has an address bus of width **24 bits** and a data bus of **16 bits**. Compute: 1. Maximum directly addressable memory. 2. Total memory data transferred per memory cycle. ### Step 1 — Identify parameters $$N = 24 \text{ (address bits)}, \quad M = 8 \text{ bits (byte-addressable)}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute addressable locations $$L = 2^{N} = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ locations}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute total capacity $$C = L \times M = 2^{24} \times 8 = 2^{27} \text{ bits} = \dfrac{2^{27}}{2^{23}} \text{ MB} = 2^{4} \text{ MB} = 16 \text{ MB}$$ ### Step 4 — Interpret the data bus width The data bus width ($16$ bits) does **not affect the addressable capacity** — it only affects how much data is transferred per memory access. A wider data bus means faster bulk transfer but requires more pins. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Memory Access Timing for KTU Numericals Let $T_{\text{access}}$ be the memory access time, $N_{\text{cells}}$ the cells accessed per cycle, and $T_{\text{cycle}}$ the clock period. The **effective throughput** in bits/second is: $$R = \dfrac{N_{\text{cells}} \times \text{word size}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} \quad \text{[bits/cycle]}$$ For sequential access of $k$ consecutive words: $$T_{\text{seq}} = T_{\text{access}} + (k-1) \times T_{\text{transfer}}$$ > [!TIP] > KTU 14-mark numericals frequently ask you to **compare access times** for byte-addressable vs word-addressable schemes. Always clarify the units (bits vs bytes) and the address bus width explicitly in your answer. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Memory Subsystem Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a memory subsystem, showing how the CPU, address bus, data bus, and control bus interact during memory operations. ```mermaid flowchart TB cpuCore["CPU CORE
Registers and ALU"] mar["MEMORY ADDRESS
REGISTER MAR
24 bits"] mdr["MEMORY DATA
REGISTER MDR
16 bits"] ctrlUnit["CONTROL
UNIT"] addrBus["ADDRESS BUS
24 lines"] dataBus["DATA BUS
16 lines"] ctrlBus["CONTROL BUS
RD WR Signals"] memArray["MAIN MEMORY
ARRAY
2 to 24 cells
Byte Addressable"] dec["ADDRESS
DECODER
1 of 2 to 24"] cpuCore --> mar cpuCore --> mdr cpuCore --> ctrlUnit mar --> addrBus mdr --> dataBus ctrlUnit --> ctrlBus addrBus --> dec dec --> memArray dataBus --> memArray ctrlBus --> memArray memArray -.READ PATH.-> dataBus memArray -.WRITE PATH.-> dataBus ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** During a **READ**, the CPU loads the target address into MAR, asserts the `RD` control line, and the memory places the contents on the data bus into MDR. During a **WRITE**, the CPU loads the address into MAR, the data into MDR, and asserts the `WR` control line, causing the memory to latch the data into the addressed cell. ## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Memory Read Operation Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD startA(["CPU Initiates READ"]) --> step1["T1: Place address on Address Bus
Load MAR with target address"] step1 --> step2["T2: Assert READ control signal
RD line goes LOW"] step2 --> step3["T3: Memory decodes address
Locates target cell"] step3 --> step4["T4: Cell contents placed on Data Bus"] step4 --> step5["T5: CPU latches data from Data Bus into MDR"] step5 --> stopA(["READ Complete
Original data preserved in memory"]) ``` ## 4.3 Flow Diagram — Memory Write Operation Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD startB(["CPU Initiates WRITE"]) --> stepW1["T1: Place address on Address Bus
Load MAR with target address"] stepW1 --> stepW2["T2: Place data on Data Bus
Load MDR with new value"] stepW2 --> stepW3["T3: Assert WRITE control signal
WR line goes LOW"] stepW3 --> stepW4["T4: Memory decodes address
Locates target cell"] stepW4 --> stepW5["T5: Memory latches Data Bus contents into cell
Old value is OVERWRITTEN"] stepW5 --> stopB(["WRITE Complete
Previous data is destroyed"]) ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Endianness Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph be["BIG ENDIAN FORMAT 0x12345678"] be1["Addr 2000
0x12 MSB"] be2["Addr 2001
0x34"] be3["Addr 2002
0x56"] be4["Addr 2003
0x78 LSB"] end subgraph le["LITTLE ENDIAN FORMAT 0x12345678"] le1["Addr 2000
0x78 LSB"] le2["Addr 2001
0x56"] le3["Addr 2002
0x34"] le4["Addr 2003
0x12 MSB"] end ``` > [!WARNING] > KTU board examiners frequently include a 3-mark question asking for the **difference** between big-endian and little-endian. The above diagram should be **memorised** and reproduced in your own words during the exam. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the following terms with one-line examples:** (a) Memory location (b) Memory address (c) Addressability **Model Answer:** > **(a) Memory Location:** A memory location is the smallest addressable storage unit in the computer's main memory. Example: A single byte at address `0x1000` is one memory location. > > **(b) Memory Address:** A memory address is a binary number that uniquely identifies a specific memory location on the address bus. Example: The address `0x1000` (= `0001 0000 0000 0000` in 16-bit binary) identifies one specific byte in memory. > > **(c) Addressability:** Addressability is the number of bits stored at each addressable memory location. Example: In a byte-addressable system, the addressability is **8 bits (1 byte)**. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Distinguish between Big-Endian and Little-Endian byte ordering. How is the 32-bit value `0xA1B2C3D4` stored at address `0x4000` in each scheme?** **Model Answer:** > **Big-Endian:** The most significant byte (MSB) is stored at the lowest memory address. > > **Little-Endian:** The least significant byte (LSB) is stored at the lowest memory address. > > **Storage Layout for `0xA1B2C3D4`:** > > | Address | Big-Endian | Little-Endian | > |---------|------------|---------------| > | 0x4000 | A1 (MSB) | D4 (LSB) | > | 0x4001 | B2 | C3 | > | 0x4002 | C3 | B2 | > | 0x4003 | D4 (LSB) | A1 (MSB) | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** Explain the concept of memory locations, addresses, and address space with a neat block diagram. Discuss how the address bus width determines the maximum addressable memory. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A computer system has a 20-bit address bus and a 16-bit data bus with byte-addressable memory. Calculate: (i) The maximum addressable memory size in MB. (ii) The number of address pins required on the memory chip. (iii) The highest addressable memory location in both decimal and hexadecimal. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Memory Location:** A memory location is a physical storage cell within the main memory that holds a fixed number of bits. Each location has the same size (addressability) and is identified by a unique binary address. **Memory Address:** A memory address is a binary number placed on the address bus by the CPU to select a specific memory location. The width of the address bus (number of bits $N$) limits the address space to $2^{N}$ unique locations, ranging from $0$ to $(2^{N} - 1)$. **Address Space:** The address space is the complete range of valid memory addresses that the CPU can generate. For an $N$-bit address bus, the address space spans $0$ to $2^{N} - 1$. **Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1 of these notes — the diagram shows CPU connected to MAR, MDR, and Control Unit, which connect via the address, data, and control buses to the memory array and address decoder.) **Address Bus Width and Maximum Memory:** The maximum directly addressable memory is given by: $$C_{\max} = 2^{N} \times M \text{ bits}$$ where $N$ is the address bus width and $M$ is the addressability in bits. Thus, the address bus width is the **fundamental hardware constraint** on how much physical memory a processor can access without bank-switching or memory mapping tricks. #### Model Solution for (b): Given: $N = 20$ address bits, $M = 8$ bits per address (byte-addressable). **(i) Maximum addressable memory:** $$C_{\max} = 2^{N} \times \dfrac{M}{8} \text{ bytes} = 2^{20} \times 1 = 2^{20} \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MB}$$ **(ii) Number of address pins required:** $$\text{Address pins} = N = \boxed{20 \text{ pins}}$$ **(iii) Highest addressable location:** $$A_{\max} = 2^{N} - 1 = 2^{20} - 1 = 1{,}048{,}575 \text{ (decimal)}$$ $$A_{\max} = \text{0xFFFFF} \text{ (hexadecimal)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Costly Mistakes):** > 1. **Confusing bits and bytes:** Students often report $2^{20} = 1$ GB instead of 1 MB. Always divide by $8$ to convert bits to bytes. > 2. **Forgetting the `$-$1`:** The highest location is $2^{N} - 1$, not $2^{N}$. This is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction**. > 3. **Not specifying units:** Always write the unit (bits, bytes, KB, MB, GB) explicitly. Examiners deduct for ambiguous answers. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the memory **READ** and memory **WRITE** operations using Register Transfer Notation (RTN). State the role of MAR and MDR in each operation. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare big-endian and little-endian byte ordering schemes. Store the 16-bit hexadecimal value `0xBEEF` at address `0x3000` using both schemes and explain which scheme is used in Intel x86 processors with justification. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Memory READ Operation (RTN):** $$M[AR] \rightarrow DR$$ - The CPU places the target address into the **Memory Address Register (MAR)**. - The CPU asserts the **READ** control signal. - The memory decodes the address and retrieves the contents of that location. - The retrieved data is placed on the data bus and loaded into the **Memory Data Register (MDR)**. - The original data in the memory cell **remains unchanged** (non-destructive). **Memory WRITE Operation (RTN):** $$DR \rightarrow M[AR]$$ - The CPU places the target address into the **MAR**. - The CPU places the data to be written into the **MDR**. - The CPU asserts the **WRITE** control signal. - The memory decodes the address and overwrites the cell with the MDR contents. - The **previous value is destroyed** (destructive). **Role of MAR:** Holds the address of the memory cell to be accessed. It is connected directly to the address bus. **Role of MDR:** Holds the data to be written (for WRITE) or the data just read (for READ). It is connected to the data bus. #### Model Solution for (b): | Feature | Big-Endian | Little-Endian | |---------|------------|---------------| | MSB placement | At lowest address | At highest address | | LSB placement | At highest address | At lowest address | | Used by | Motorola 68K, Sun SPARC, network protocols | **Intel x86, x86-64, AMD64** | | Human readability | Matches written number order | Reverse of written order | **Storage of `0xBEEF` at `0x3000`:** | Address | Big-Endian | Little-Endian | |---------|------------|---------------| | 0x3000 | BE (MSB) | EF (LSB) | | 0x3001 | EF (LSB) | BE (MSB) | **Intel x86 Justification:** Intel x86 and x86-64 architectures use **little-endian** ordering primarily because of historical design decisions in the original 8086 processor. It allows efficient **partial-word access** — for example, reading the LSB of a 32-bit integer by accessing only the first byte, without needing to mask or shift. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** > 1. **Do not just list differences** — KTU expects a comparative table AND a worked numerical example. > 2. **RTN notation must be exact:** Use $M[AR]$ (not $MEM[AR]$ or `M(AR)`) and use arrows correctly. > 3. **Always mention that READ is non-destructive and WRITE is destructive** — examiners specifically test this understanding. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this before the exam):** > > - **Memory Location** = smallest addressable unit (typically 1 byte) > - **Address Bus Width $N$** → determines address space size $2^{N}$ > - **Addressability $M$** = bits per address (8 for byte-addressable) > - **Total Memory** = $2^{N} \times M$ bits > - **Highest Address** = $2^{N} - 1$ (not $2^{N}$) > - **Endianness:** Big = MSB at low address; Little = LSB at low address > - **READ** is **non-destructive**; **WRITE** is **destructive** > - **RTN:** $M[AR] \rightarrow DR$ (READ); $DR \rightarrow M[AR]$ (WRITE) > - **MAR** holds address; **MDR** holds data; both are CPU-to-memory interface registers > - **Intel x86 / x86-64** is little-endian; **network protocols** are big-endian > - **Address bits for capacity** $C$ bytes: $N = \lceil \log_{2}(C) \rceil$ > - **Word addressability** reduces address bits by $\log_{2}(\text{word size}/8)$ compared to byte > - Always **state units explicitly** (bits, bytes, KB, MB, GB) in numerical answers > - **Differentiate** address bus width (memory size) from data bus width (transfer speed)

topics.title: Instructions and Instruction Sequencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instructions and Instruction Sequencing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Computer Organization, an **Instruction** is a binary command, encoded in machine language, that directs the central processing unit (CPU) to perform a specific elementary operation such as data transfer, arithmetic/logic manipulation, control flow, or I/O initiation. **Instruction Sequencing** refers to the systematic, deterministic, and ordered mechanism by which the processor fetches, decodes, and executes instructions—either in their stored linear order or by diverting to a new sequence through branches, jumps, or subroutine calls.

Mathematically, a stored program is a finite sequence of n-bit machine words residing in memory at sequential addresses:

$$ I = \{I_0, I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_{N-1}\} $$

where each instruction $I_k$ is located at the Program Counter (PC) address $k$, and the default sequencing rule is:

$$ PC_{next} = PC_{current} + L $$

where **$L$** represents the instruction length in **bytes** (or words).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** *Module 1 explicitly covers instruction formats, instruction types (3-, 2-, 1-, 0-address), instruction execution steps, subroutines, and basic I/O. Examiners frequently test the student's ability to distinguish addressing forms and trace sequencing using the Program Counter (PC).*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a chef in a kitchen executing a **recipe card**. Each line of the recipe is an "instruction":
- *"Take 2 eggs"* → an **operational instruction** (data transfer from the basket to the bowl).
- *"Whisk for 30 seconds"* → an **arithmetic/logic operation** (processing).
- *"If batter is lumpy, add flour"* → a **conditional branch**.
- *"Repeat steps 1–3"* → a **loop (branch backward)**.
- *"Go to page 42 for icing instructions"* → a **jump**.
- *"Bake at 180°C for 20 minutes (then continue)"* → a **subroutine call and return**.

The chef's **finger pointing at the current step** is the **Program Counter (PC)**. After executing each step, the finger moves to the **next** line — that is **linear sequencing**. When the chef skips forward or goes back, that is **branch sequencing**. The chef's brain reading the instruction and acting upon it is the **CPU's instruction cycle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Instructions are the "words" a CPU understands. Instruction sequencing is the "grammar" — the rulebook the CPU uses to know which word to read, understand, and act upon next. Without sequencing, the CPU would be lost in a sea of bits.

### 1.3 Key Architectural Metrics

- **Instruction Word Length:** typically **8, 16, 32, or 64 bits** depending on the ISA.
- **Memory Word Size:** matches bus width; usually **1 byte = 8 bits** for byte-addressable systems.
- **Clock Cycle Time (T):** time to complete one micro-operation, measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)**.
- **MIPS (Millions of Instructions Per Second):** the standard performance metric:

$$ \text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{Clock Rate (in Hz)}}{CPI \times 10^{6}} $$

where **CPI** is *Cycles Per Instruction*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Machine Instruction

Every machine instruction, in its canonical form, contains two principal fields:

$$ \text{Instruction Word} = \underbrace{OPCODE}_{\text{Operation Code}} \;\; \Vert \;\; \underbrace{OPERAND(s)}_{\text{Address / Data Field}} $$

- **Opcode (Operation Code):** Specifies *what* operation to perform (ADD, SUB, LOAD, JUMP, etc.). If there are $K$ distinct operations, then:

$$ \text{Opcode field width} = \lceil \log_{2}{K} \rceil \;\; \text{bits} $$

- **Operand Field(s):** Specifies *where* the data resides (register, memory address, or immediate value).

### 2.2 Instruction Format Classification (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU syllabus places strong emphasis on the four classical instruction formats:

| Format Type | Opcode Bits | Address Fields | Operands per Instruction | Total Instruction Length (bits) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Three-Address** | $k$ | 3 of $n$ bits each | 3 | $k + 3n$ | RISC / General Purpose Registers |
| **Two-Address** | $k$ | 2 of $n$ bits each | 2 | $k + 2n$ | Early CISC machines |
| **One-Address** | $k$ | 1 of $n$ bits | 1 (with implicit Accumulator) | $k + n$ | Classic accumulator machines |
| **Zero-Address** | $k$ | 0 (stack-based) | 0 | $k$ | Stack machines (JVM, Forth) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter at KTU:** When asked to *evaluate the expression* $X = (A + B) \cdot (C + D)$, the number of instructions required differs drastically. The examiner loves this — see Part B Question in Section 5.

### 2.3 The Instruction Execution Cycle (von Neumann / Fetch–Decode–Execute)

Every instruction, regardless of its format, passes through **three primary phases** plus the implicit increment of the PC:

1. **Fetch Phase (F):**
   - The PC content is placed on the address bus.
   - Memory returns the instruction word at that address.
   - The instruction is loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
   - Simultaneously, the PC is incremented: $PC \leftarrow PC + L$.

2. **Decode Phase (D):**
   - The control unit interprets the opcode.
   - Operand specifiers (register numbers, addressing modes) are parsed.
   - Required control signals are generated.

3. **Execute Phase (E):**
   - The ALU performs the operation, or
   - Data transfer occurs between registers/memory, or
   - The PC is overwritten for a branch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Insight:** Total instruction execution time = $(F + D + E) \times T_{clock}$. A program with $N$ instructions takes $N \times CPI \times T$ seconds. This is the basis of every CPU performance problem in KTU.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Opcode field width** | $k = \lceil \log_{2}K \rceil$ | Determining bits needed for $K$ operations |
| 2 | **Address field width** | $n = \lceil \log_{2}M \rceil$ | Addressing $M$ memory words |
| 3 | **PC update (linear)** | $PC_{next} = PC + L$ | Default instruction sequencing |
| 4 | **PC update (branch)** | $PC_{next} = PC + L + \text{offset}$ | Conditional/unconditional branches |
| 5 | **Program size** | $\text{Size} = N \cdot L$ bits | Total memory occupied by $N$ instructions |
| 6 | **Execution time** | $T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ | Time to run a program |
| 7 | **MIPS rating** | $\text{MIPS} = f / (CPI \times 10^6)$ | Performance benchmarking |
| 8 | **CPU throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = 1 / (CPI \times T_{clock})$ | Instructions per second |
| 9 | **Branch target** | $\text{Target} = \text{Base} + \text{Displacement}$ | Computing effective address of jump |
| 10 | **Direct addressing EA** | $EA = \text{Address field of instruction}$ | Memory access via direct address |
| 11 | **Indirect addressing EA** | $EA = M[\text{Address field}]$ | Pointer dereferencing |
| 12 | **Relative addressing EA** | $EA = PC + \text{Displacement}$ | Position-independent branches |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical bars in tables have been rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve the integrity of the markdown table syntax. Do not use `|` inside table cells for absolute-value notation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Instruction sequencing is the **heartbeat of every computing device** — from a microcontroller inside a washing machine to a multi-core server processor handling millions of HTTP requests. Understanding sequencing enables engineers to:
- **Write efficient compilers** that minimize branch mispredictions.
- **Design pipelined processors** (Module 2/3 of KTU) where incorrect sequencing breaks the pipeline.
- **Analyze security vulnerabilities** like *buffer overflows* that exploit the return address in instruction sequencing.
- **Optimize embedded firmware** for real-time systems where deterministic instruction timing is critical (e.g., automotive ECUs, medical devices).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Instruction Word Size for a Three-Address Machine

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A computer has **64 operations**, **16 registers**, and a main memory of **4096 words**. Design a three-address instruction format and compute the total instruction length in bits.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameter space.**
- Number of distinct operations: $K = 64$
- Number of CPU registers: $R = 16$
- Memory size: $M = 4096$ words

**Step 2 — Compute the opcode field width.**

$$ k = \lceil \log_{2} K \rceil = \lceil \log_{2} 64 \rceil = \lceil 6 \rceil = 6 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the address field width.**

Since the operands may refer to either registers or memory, we use the larger addressing requirement. For a three-address format, each operand must be capable of addressing the full memory:

$$ n = \lceil \log_{2} M \rceil = \lceil \log_{2} 4096 \rceil = \lceil 12 \rceil = 12 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the total instruction length.**

$$ L_{total} = k + 3n = 6 + 3(12) = 6 + 36 = 42 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 5 — Practical consideration.**

In commercial designs, 42 bits is awkward. The designer would round up to the next multiple of 8 (one byte):

$$ L_{practical} = 48 \text{ bits} = 6 \text{ bytes} $$

with **6 unused bits** available for future opcode extension.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** This exact pattern appears in past examinations. Always show the ceiling function explicitly — examiners reward the $\lceil \log_{2} \rceil$ notation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Memory References for a 1-Address vs 3-Address Format

**Problem Statement:** Evaluate the arithmetic expression:

$$ Y = (A - B) / (C + D \cdot E) $$

using (a) a **3-address machine**, (b) a **1-address machine** (accumulator-based). Count instructions and memory references.

**Step 1 — Common subexpression identification.**

We need to compute:
- Numerator: $N_1 = A - B$
- Denominator: $D_1 = C + (D \cdot E)$
- Result: $Y = N_1 / D_1$

**Step 2 — 3-address machine solution (one instruction per operation).**

| # | Instruction | Meaning | Memory Refs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MUL T1, D, E` | $T_1 \leftarrow M[D] \cdot M[E]$ | 2 |
| 2 | `ADD T2, C, T1` | $T_2 \leftarrow M[C] + T_1$ | 1 |
| 3 | `SUB T3, A, B` | $T_3 \leftarrow M[A] - M[B]$ | 2 |
| 4 | `DIV Y, T3, T2` | $Y \leftarrow T_3 / T_2$ | 0 (registers) |

**Step 1 continued — Total for 3-address:**

$$ N_{inst} = 4, \quad MR_{3addr} = 2 + 1 + 2 + 0 = 5 \text{ memory references} $$

**Step 3 — 1-address machine solution (implicit accumulator AC).**

| # | Instruction | Meaning | Memory Refs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LDA D` | $AC \leftarrow M[D]$ | 1 |
| 2 | `MUL E` | $AC \leftarrow AC \cdot M[E]$ | 1 |
| 3 | `ADD C` | $AC \leftarrow AC + M[C]$ | 1 |
| 4 | `STA T` | $M[T] \leftarrow AC$ | 1 |
| 5 | `LDA A` | $AC \leftarrow M[A]$ | 1 |
| 6 | `SUB B` | $AC \leftarrow AC - M[B]$ | 1 |
| 7 | `DIV T` | $AC \leftarrow AC / M[T]$ | 1 |

**Step 3 continued — Total for 1-address:**

$$ N_{inst} = 7, \quad MR_{1addr} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 7 \text{ memory references} $$

**Step 4 — Comparison.**

$$ \frac{N_{inst}^{1addr}}{N_{inst}^{3addr}} = \frac{7}{4} = 1.75 \times \text{ more instructions} $$

$$ \frac{MR_{1addr}}{MR_{3addr}} = \frac{7}{5} = 1.4 \times \text{ more memory accesses} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** 3-address format requires **more bits per instruction** (larger program memory footprint) but **fewer instructions and memory accesses** (faster execution). 1-address format is the opposite. The choice depends on the cost of memory vs the cost of execution time — a classic KTU conceptual question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Execution Time of a Program with Branch Penalty

**Problem Statement:** A program has:
- Total instructions: $N = 10^6$
- Average CPI = 4
- Branch frequency: every **5th instruction** is a conditional branch
- Branch taken: 60% of the time
- Misprediction penalty: 4 cycles
- Clock rate: $f = 500 \text{ MHz}$

Compute the total execution time including branch misprediction stalls.

**Step 1 — Total cycles assuming perfect branch prediction.**

$$ C_{base} = N \times CPI = 10^6 \times 4 = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles} $$

**Step 2 — Number of branch instructions.**

$$ N_{br} = \frac{N}{5} = \frac{10^6}{5} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ branches} $$

**Step 3 — Number of mispredictions (assuming always-not-taken predictor).**

$$ N_{mis} = N_{br} \times P_{taken} = 2 \times 10^5 \times 0.60 = 1.2 \times 10^5 $$

**Step 4 — Stall cycles from mispredictions.**

$$ C_{stall} = N_{mis} \times \text{Penalty} = 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 4 = 4.8 \times 10^5 \text{ cycles} $$

**Step 5 — Total execution cycles.**

$$ C_{total} = C_{base} + C_{stall} = 4 \times 10^6 + 4.8 \times 10^5 = 4.48 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles} $$

**Step 6 — Total execution time.**

$$ T_{exec} = \frac{C_{total}}{f} = \frac{4.48 \times 10^6}{500 \times 10^6} = 8.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 8.96 \text{ ms} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Branch mispredictions added $4.8 \times 10^5$ cycles — a **12% overhead**! This is why branch prediction is a critical research area in modern CPU design (relevant to Module 2/3 of KTU).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Instruction Format Comparison

| Evaluation Criterion | 3-Address | 2-Address | 1-Address | 0-Address (Stack) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Program length** (instructions) | Shortest | Medium | Long | Longest |
| **Instruction size** (bits) | Largest | Large | Medium | Smallest |
| **Memory access per instruction** | Fewest | Medium | Most | Most |
| **Compiler complexity** | High | Medium | Low | Medium |
| **Hardware complexity** | High (more registers/buses) | Medium | Low (single AC) | Medium (stack pointer) |
| **Speed of execution** | Fastest | Medium | Slowest | Slowest |
| **Example ISA** | ARM, MIPS, x86 (3-op form) | x86 (2-op form) | PDP-8 | Java Virtual Machine |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Instruction Cycle

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle** as a functional flow, showing how the Program Counter, Memory, Instruction Register, Decoder, and ALU interact during instruction sequencing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Reset PC to 0] --> B[Fetch Phase]
    B --> B1[Address Bus gets PC content]
    B1 --> B2[Memory M outputs Instruction]
    B2 --> B3[Instruction loaded into IR]
    B3 --> B4[PC incremented by L]
    B4 --> C[Decode Phase]
    C --> C1[Control Unit reads Opcode]
    C1 --> C2{Opcode Type}
    C2 -->|Data Transfer| D1[Execute: Move data between Reg and Mem]
    C2 -->|Data Processing| D2[Execute: ALU performs ADD SUB MUL DIV]
    C2 -->|Control Flow| D3[Execute: Branch Jump Call Return]
    C2 -->|Input Output| D4[Execute: Initiate I/O transfer]
    D1 --> E[Update Registers and Flags]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F{Is Program Complete}
    F -->|No| B
    F -->|Yes| G[End: Halt Processor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The cycle loops from *Execute* back to *Fetch* unless the instruction is a HALT or an error trap occurs. The PC, IR, and decoder form the **sequencing backbone** of the CPU.

---

### 4.2 Instruction Sequencing Flow for a Branch Instruction

The following diagram illustrates the difference between **linear sequencing** and **branch sequencing** — a frequent KTU exam topic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PC = 100: Instruction I1] -->|Linear PC+4 equals 104| P2[PC = 104: Instruction I2]
    P2 -->|Linear PC+4 equals 108| P3[PC = 108: Instruction I3]
    P3 -->|Linear PC+4 equals 112| P4[PC = 112: Instruction I4]
    P4 -->|Branch taken target equals 200| P5[PC = 200: Instruction I5]
    P5 -->|Linear PC+4 equals 204| P6[PC = 204: Instruction I6]
    P4 -.->|Not taken PC+4 equals 116| P7[PC = 116: Instruction I7]
    P7 -->|Linear PC+4 equals 120| P8[PC = 120: Instruction I8]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** The dashed line shows the *fall-through* path (PC + 4 from 112 = 116), while the solid arrow shows the *branch target* (200). At runtime, the CPU evaluates the condition code and chooses **one** of the two paths.

---

### 4.3 Subroutine Call and Return Sequence

Subroutines are the most complex sequencing construct. They require saving the **return address**, transferring control, executing the subroutine body, and then resuming the caller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Caller executes at PC = 500] --> B[Branch to Subroutine at 1000]
    B --> C[Save Return Address 504 on Stack]
    C --> D[Load PC with 1000]
    D --> E[Execute Subroutine Body]
    E --> F{Subroutine Done}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G[Pop Return Address from Stack]
    G --> H[Load PC with 504]
    H --> I[Continue Caller Execution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Stack-based linkage:** The return address is pushed onto a hardware stack pointed to by the **Stack Pointer (SP)** register. The `CALL` instruction pushes; the `RET` (or `RETURN`) instruction pops. This is the foundation of procedure calls in every modern ISA.

---

### 4.4 Instruction Format Layout (Three-Address, Generic)

The following diagram represents the bit-field layout of a generic three-address instruction, used to compute instruction length and to verify field widths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Bit 0] --> A1[Bits 0 to k-1: OPCODE field]
    A1 --> A2[Bits k to k+n-1: DESTINATION address]
    A2 --> A3[Bits k+n to k+2n-1: SOURCE1 address]
    A3 --> A4[Bits k+2n to k+3n-1: SOURCE2 address]
    A4 --> A5[Bit k+3n-1: MSB end of word]
```
