**Numerical Example (extending Derivation 1):**

$$ \text{Width breakdown} = \underbrace{6}_{\text{opcode}} + \underbrace{12}_{D_1} + \underbrace{12}_{S_1} + \underbrace{12}_{S_2} = 42 \text{ bits} $$

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an instruction format. Differentiate between three-address and one-address instruction formats.**

**Model Answer:**

An **instruction format** is the binary layout of an instruction word, comprising the opcode field and one or more operand/address fields. The format defines how the bits are partitioned to specify the operation and its operands.

| Aspect | Three-Address Format | One-Address Format |
|---|---|---|
| **Operand fields** | 3 (destination + 2 sources) | 1 (with implicit accumulator) |
| **Instruction length** | Longer (k + 3n bits) | Shorter (k + n bits) |
| **Program length** | Shorter (fewer instructions) | Longer (more instructions) |
| **Example machine** | MIPS, ARM | PDP-8, early microcontrollers |

> **Word limit target:** 80–100 words for a 3-mark answer; use the table to compress comparison.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the Program Counter (PC) in instruction sequencing?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Program Counter (PC)** is a special-purpose CPU register that holds the **memory address of the next instruction to be fetched**. After each fetch, the PC is automatically incremented by the instruction length $L$, producing linear sequencing. For branch, jump, call, or return instructions, the PC is loaded with a new address, overriding linear sequencing. Thus, the PC is the **sequencing register** that controls the order of instruction execution.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** A computer has 32 operations, 64 registers, and a memory of 8192 words. Design a **three-address instruction format** and compute the total instruction length. If a 2-address format is used instead, what is the new instruction length?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute field widths.**

- Opcode width: $k = \lceil \log_2 32 \rceil = 5$ bits
- Register address: $\lceil \log_2 64 \rceil = 6$ bits
- Memory address: $\lceil \log_2 8192 \rceil = 13$ bits
- For operands referencing memory: use $n = 13$ bits per address.

**Step 2 — Three-address format length.**

$$ L_{3addr} = k + 3n = 5 + 3(13) = 5 + 39 = 44 \text{ bits} $$

Rounded to nearest byte: **48 bits (6 bytes)**.

**Step 3 — Two-address format length.**

$$ L_{2addr} = k + 2n = 5 + 2(13) = 5 + 26 = 31 \text{ bits} $$

Rounded to nearest byte: **32 bits (4 bytes)**.

**Step 4 — Comparison.**

$$ \Delta L = L_{3addr} - L_{2addr} = 44 - 31 = 13 \text{ bits} $$

The 3-address format is **13 bits longer** but executes in fewer instructions overall.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the **Fetch–Decode–Execute** cycle with a neat block diagram. What happens to the PC during each phase?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fetch Phase.**
- $MAR \leftarrow PC$ (address placed on memory bus)
- $IR \leftarrow M[MAR]$ (instruction fetched into Instruction Register)
- $PC \leftarrow PC + L$ (PC incremented to point to the next instruction)

**Step 2 — Decode Phase.**
- The opcode field of the IR is sent to the Control Unit.
- The Control Unit identifies the operation type and the addressing modes.
- Control signals are generated for the Execute phase.

**Step 3 — Execute Phase.**
- For data transfer: copy data between registers/memory.
- For ALU operations: route operands to the ALU, perform the computation, store the result.
- For control operations: the PC is overwritten with the branch target address, overriding the next-instruction address set during Fetch.

**Step 4 — PC behavior summary.**

| Phase | PC State |
|---|---|
| Start of Fetch | Holds address of current instruction |
| End of Fetch | Holds address of next instruction (PC + L) |
| Decode | Unchanged |
| Execute (no branch) | Unchanged — next fetch uses PC + L |
| Execute (branch) | Overwritten with branch target |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** A program consists of **200,000 instructions** with an average CPI of **3.5** on a processor with a clock period of **2 ns**. Compute the (i) total execution time, (ii) MIPS rating, and (iii) throughput in instructions per second. If **20%** of instructions are branches and **50%** of branches are mispredicted with a **3-cycle penalty**, what is the new execution time?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Base execution time.**

$$ T_{base} = N \times CPI \times T_{clock} = 200{,}000 \times 3.5 \times 2 \text{ ns} $$

$$ T_{base} = 1{,}400{,}000 \text{ ns} = 1.4 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 2 — MIPS rating.**

$$ \text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^6} = \frac{1/(2\text{ns})}{3.5 \times 10^6} = \frac{500 \times 10^6}{3.5 \times 10^6} = 142.86 \text{ MIPS} $$

**Step 3 — Throughput.**

$$ \text{Throughput} = \frac{1}{CPI \times T_{clock}} = \frac{1}{3.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.43 \times 10^8 \text{ inst/s} $$

**Step 4 — Branch misprediction overhead.**

- Branch instructions: $0.20 \times 200{,}000 = 40{,}000$
- Mispredicted: $0.50 \times 40{,}000 = 20{,}000$
- Stall cycles: $20{,}000 \times 3 = 60{,}000$ cycles
- Stall time: $60{,}000 \times 2 \text{ ns} = 120{,}000 \text{ ns} = 0.12 \text{ ms}$

**Step 5 — New total execution time.**

$$ T_{new} = T_{base} + T_{stall} = 1.4 \text{ ms} + 0.12 \text{ ms} = 1.52 \text{ ms} $$

$$ \text{Overhead} = \frac{0.12}{1.4} \times 100\% = 8.57\% $$

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Explain **subroutine call and return** using a stack. How is the return address preserved? What happens if the stack overflows during a deeply nested call?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Call instruction sequence.**
- The CALL instruction has two effects:
  1. $M[SP] \leftarrow PC_{return}$; $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$ (push return address onto stack).
  2. $PC \leftarrow \text{Target Address}$ (transfer control to subroutine entry).

**Step 2 — Return instruction sequence.**
- $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$; $PC \leftarrow M[SP]$ (pop return address from stack and jump back).

**Step 3 — Why a stack?**
- The stack enables **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** handling of nested calls.
- Each CALL pushes a new return address; each RET pops the most recent one.
- The hardware **Stack Pointer (SP)** tracks the top of the stack.

**Step 4 — Stack overflow scenario.**
- If the subroutine calls another subroutine, multiple return addresses accumulate.
- With deeply nested recursion, the stack grows downward (toward lower memory addresses in the conventional model).
- **Stack overflow** occurs when the stack collides with the program's data or code region, potentially overwriting critical data — a security vulnerability known as a **stack buffer overflow** (CVE exploits like the famous *Code Red* worm).

**Step 5 — Mitigation.**
- Operating systems allocate fixed stack frames per process.
- Compilers insert *stack canaries* and *guard pages* to detect overflow.
- Hardware supports *stack limit registers* (e.g., ARM's SPLIM).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function:** In format-design questions, writing $\log_2 64 = 6$ without the $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ wrapper loses a half-mark. Always use $\lceil \log_2 K \rceil$.
> 2. **Mixing up PC increment timing:** Students often write "$PC \leftarrow PC + 1$" universally. The increment is **PC + L**, where L is the instruction length in bytes. A 32-bit instruction on a byte-addressable machine uses L = 4.
> 3. **Branch target calculation:** For relative branches, the offset is added to the **already-incremented PC** (the address of the *next* instruction), not the address of the *branch* instruction. Examiners deduct heavily for this.
> 4. **Stack direction confusion:** Always state explicitly whether your stack grows toward **lower** (decrementing SP) or **higher** (incrementing SP) addresses. Inconsistent notation loses marks.
> 5. **Skipping units:** $T_{clock} = 2$ ns vs $T_{clock} = 2$ μs changes the MIPS rating by **1000×**. Always carry units through every calculation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Instructions and Instruction Sequencing":**

- **Instruction Word** = Opcode + Operand(s); use $\lceil \log_2 \rceil$ for field widths.
- **Three-address** format is shortest in program length but largest per instruction; **zero-address** is the opposite.
- The **Program Counter (PC)** is the master sequencing register — it always points to the next instruction to fetch.
- **Linear sequencing:** $PC \leftarrow PC + L$ after every fetch.
- **Branch sequencing:** $PC \leftarrow \text{Target Address}$ overrides the linear increment.
- The **Fetch–Decode–Execute** cycle is universal: $F \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow (PC_{update}) \rightarrow F$.
- **Subroutines** use a stack to save and restore the return address ($CALL$ pushes, $RET$ pops).
- **Performance formula:** $T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times T_{clock}$; lower CPI = faster execution.
- **Branch misprediction penalty** is a major source of pipeline stalls — covered in detail in Module 2/3.
- **RISC vs CISC:** RISC machines prefer **three-address register-based** formats; CISC machines often use **two-address or one-address memory-based** formats.
- Always specify the **addressing mode** when computing the **Effective Address (EA)** — KTU examiners reward clarity.
- Total execution cycles = Base cycles + Stall cycles (from cache misses, branch mispredictions, hazards).
- The **MIPS** metric is misleading for programs with widely varying instruction mixes — prefer *execution time* as the universal metric.
- **Byte-addressable** vs **word-addressable** memory changes the PC increment: L = 1 byte for byte-addressable, L = word size for word-addressable.
- For **relative addressing**, displacement is added to $PC + L$ (the *updated* PC), not the original PC.

> **End of Notes — Module 1: Instructions and Instruction Sequencing**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addressing Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Addressing Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Addressing Modes** are the different techniques (or rules) used by the **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** to specify the **location of an operand** (the data on which an operation is to be performed) inside a CPU instruction. They define *how* the **Effective Address (EA)** of an operand is calculated from the address fields present in the machine instruction, including constants, register contents, and memory locations.

In the **Fetch-Decode-Execute (FDE) cycle**, once an instruction is fetched, the Control Unit must resolve the location of its operand(s) before the ALU can execute the operation. The method used for this resolution is the **addressing mode**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The question *"Explain different addressing modes with examples"* is a **guaranteed high-weightage question** in Part B (14 marks). Students must be able to draw the **EA calculation diagram** for each mode and list its **advantages/disadvantages** in tabular form to score full marks.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "How to Tell a Friend Where to Meet You"

Imagine you are telling your friend, **Ravi**, how to reach a coffee shop. You can use several strategies:

| Your Instruction Style | Addressing Mode Equivalent |
|---|---|
| *"Meet me at Shop No. **47**, MG Road"* — exact, hardcoded location | **Immediate / Direct Addressing** |
| *"Meet me at the shop written on **this paper**"* — pointer to the address | **Indirect Addressing** |
| *"Meet me where my **GPS device** (register) says I am"* — pointer in a small notepad | **Register Indirect Addressing** |
| *"Go to **Cochin** and then ask for **3rd shop on the left**"* — base + offset | **Indexed / Base Register Addressing** |
| *"From the **gate**, walk **15 steps** forward"* — relative displacement | **Relative Addressing** |
| *"Meet me at my **home (PC stack)**"* — LIFO location | **Stack Addressing** |

> **Core Takeaway:** Just as there are multiple ways to describe a destination, there are multiple ways for a CPU to locate an operand. Each mode has a trade-off between **speed**, **flexibility**, and **memory reference cost**.

## 1.3 Why Addressing Modes Matter (Standard Metrics)

- The number of addressing modes supported by an ISA typically ranges from **5 to 20**.
- A typical **RISC** (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) processor supports **3 to 5** modes (e.g., Register, Immediate, Register Indirect, PC-relative, Displacement).
- A typical **CISC** (Complex Instruction Set Computer) processor supports **12 to 24** modes (e.g., x86 supports 24+ modes).
- **Performance Metric:** *Memory References per Operand* — measured as **0, 1, or 2** depending on the mode.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Addressing Modes

Addressing modes are broadly classified into two categories:

1. **Data-Transfer / Operand-Addressing Modes** — used to locate data operands.
2. **Branch / Control-Flow Addressing Modes** — used to specify the target of a jump/branch instruction (PC-relative, Direct Jump, Indirect Jump).

## 2.2 Detailed List of Addressing Modes

### (A) Immediate Addressing
- The operand **value itself** is embedded inside the instruction.
- **Effective Address (EA) = Operand field of the instruction.**
- No memory reference is required to fetch the operand.
- **Use:** Loading constants, initializing registers.

### (B) Direct (Absolute) Addressing
- The instruction contains the **full memory address** of the operand.
- **EA = Address field of the instruction.**
- Requires **one memory reference**.
- **Drawback:** Limited address range (instruction size restricts it).

### (C) Register Addressing
- The operand is contained in a **CPU register** specified by the instruction.
- **EA = Register (no memory access).**
- **Fastest mode** — zero memory references.
- **Use:** Internal CPU operations, ALU operations.

### (D) Register Indirect Addressing
- The instruction specifies a **register** that **contains the memory address** of the operand.
- **EA = [Register] = Content of the register.**
- Requires **one memory reference** to fetch the operand.
- **Analogy:** "The register holds a house number; go to that house."

### (E) Indirect (Memory Indirect) Addressing
- The instruction's address field points to a **memory location that contains the address** of the operand.
- **EA = [Address field] = Content of memory location.**
- Requires **two memory references** (slowest).
- **Use:** Pointers, dynamic data structures.

### (F) Displacement Addressing
- The **EA = Base Value + Offset (Displacement).**
- Subdivided into:
  - **Relative Addressing** — Base = **Program Counter (PC)**.
  - **Base-Register Addressing** — Base = a designated base register.
  - **Indexed Addressing** — Base = Index Register (IX); operand often comes from a table/array.

### (G) Auto-Increment / Auto-Decrement Addressing
- Special case of Register Indirect.
- **Auto-Increment:** After use, the register is automatically incremented by operand size.
- **Auto-Decrement:** Before use, the register is automatically decremented.
- **Use:** Sequential access of arrays, stack operations (LIFO).

### (H) Stack Addressing
- Operand is implicitly on the **Top of Stack (TOS)**, pointed to by the **Stack Pointer (SP)** register.
- **EA = [SP].**
- Used in PUSH, POP, CALL, RET instructions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Addressing Mode | Effective Address (EA) Formula | Memory Refs | Typical Operand Size | CISC / RISC Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Immediate | $\text{EA} = \text{Operand field itself}$ | **0** | Constant $\vert$ Varies | Both |
| 2 | Direct (Absolute) | $\text{EA} = \text{Address field A}$ | **1** | Memory[$\text{A}$] | CISC |
| 3 | Register | $\text{EA} = \text{Register } R$ | **0** | $R$ content | Both |
| 4 | Register Indirect | $\text{EA} = \vert R \vert$ | **1** | Memory[$\vert R \vert$] | Both |
| 5 | Memory Indirect | $\text{EA} = \vert \vert A \vert \vert$ | **2** | Memory[Memory[$\text{A}$]] | CISC |
| 6 | Displacement (Indexed) | $\text{EA} = \vert IX \vert + \text{Offset}$ | **1** | Memory[$\vert IX \vert + \text{Offset}$] | Both |
| 7 | Relative | $\text{EA} = \vert PC \vert + \text{Offset}$ | **1** | Branch target | Both |
| 8 | Base-Register | $\text{EA} = \vert BR \vert + \text{Offset}$ | **1** | Memory[$\vert BR \vert + \text{Offset}$] | CISC |
| 9 | Auto-Increment | $\text{EA} = \vert R \vert$, then $R \leftarrow R + d$ | **1** | Sequential data | CISC (e.g., PDP-11) |
| 10 | Auto-Decrement | $R \leftarrow R - d$, then $\text{EA} = \vert R \vert$ | **1** | Stack (LIFO) | CISC |
| 11 | Stack (Implicit) | $\text{EA} = \vert SP \vert$ | **1** (TOS) | TOS content | Both |

> **Legend:** $\vert x \vert$ denotes the **content of** register/memory location $x$. $d$ = operand data size in bytes.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Compilers choose the most efficient addressing mode for each high-level language construct (e.g., arrays use indexed, pointers use register indirect, constants use immediate).
- **Operating Systems:** Base-register addressing enables **memory protection** and **relocation** of processes in virtual memory.
- **Embedded Systems:** RISC processors (ARM, RISC-V) use a small set of addressing modes to keep instruction decoding simple and power-efficient.
- **Branching:** PC-relative addressing is essential for **position-independent code (PIC)** used in shared libraries and modern OS kernels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Generalised EA Calculation — The Master Equation

For a generic addressing mode, the Effective Address is computed by the Control Unit using:

$$
\text{EA} = f(\text{Register Set}, \text{Address Field}, \text{PC}, \text{Offset})
$$

This is the **fundamental derivation** from which all specific modes are derived.

---

### Derivation 1: Effective Address of Indexed Addressing

**Given:** A machine with a 16-bit address field and a 16-bit Index Register (IX) holding value $0x2000$. The instruction is `MOV A, [IX + 0x40]`.

**Step 1 — Identify the components:**

$$
\text{IX} = 0x2000, \quad \text{Offset} = 0x40
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to decimal for clarity:**

$$
\text{IX}_{\text{dec}} = 8192, \quad \text{Offset}_{\text{dec}} = 64
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\text{EA} = \vert \text{IX} \vert + \text{Offset}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\text{EA} = 8192 + 64
$$

**Step 5 — Final result in decimal and hex:**

$$
\text{EA}_{\text{dec}} = 8256 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{EA}_{\text{hex}} = 0x2040
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** The operand is fetched from memory location $0x2040$. This required **1 memory reference**.

---

### Derivation 2: Effective Address of Relative (PC-Relative) Branch

**Given:** A branch instruction is at memory address $\text{PC} = 0x1000$, with a signed 8-bit offset $= 0x1A$ (positive branch).

**Step 1 — Identify components:**

$$
\text{PC} = 0x1000, \quad \text{Offset} = 0x1A
$$

**Step 2 — Convert offset to decimal:**

$$
\text{Offset}_{\text{dec}} = 26
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the relative addressing formula:**

$$
\text{EA} = \vert \text{PC} \vert + \text{Offset}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and compute:**

$$
\text{EA}_{\text{dec}} = 4096 + 26
$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$
\text{EA}_{\text{hex}} = 0x101A
$$

**Step 6 — Note on PC increment:** In most ISAs, by the time the offset is added, the PC has already been incremented to $\text{PC} + 1$ (i.e., $0x1001$). So the *actual* branch target becomes:

$$
\text{Target} = 0x1001 + 0x001A = 0x101B
$$

This **+1 or +2 offset correction** is a common KTU exam trap.

---

### Derivation 3: Auto-Increment Pointer Traversal

**Given:** A 16-bit machine, data size $d = 2$ bytes. Register $R1 = 0x3000$ (start of an array). Execute `LOAD R2, (R1)+` (Auto-Increment).

**Step 1 — Initial state of $R1$:**

$$
R1 = 0x3000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute EA before increment:**

$$
\text{EA} = \vert R1 \vert = 0x3000
$$

**Step 3 — Operand at $\text{EA} = 0x3000$ is loaded into $R2$:**

$$
R2 \leftarrow \text{Memory}[0x3000]
$$

**Step 4 — Auto-increment $R1$ by data size $d = 2$:**

$$
R1 \leftarrow R1 + d = 0x3000 + 0x0002 = 0x3002
$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for next element:** Next `LOAD R2, (R1)+` will fetch from $0x3002$, then increment $R1$ to $0x3004$, and so on. This produces **sequential array access with a single instruction**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Examiner's View)

| Mode | Speed (Cycles) | Flexibility | Memory Access | Best For | Worst For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Immediate | **1 (Fastest)** | Lowest | 0 | Constants | Large/Changing data |
| Register | **1 (Fastest)** | Medium | 0 | ALU ops | External data |
| Register Indirect | 2 | High | 1 | Pointers | Static constants |
| Direct | 2 | Medium | 1 | Static globals | Relocatable code |
| Displacement (Indexed) | 2 | **Highest** | 1 | Arrays, structures | Single scalars |
| Memory Indirect | **3 (Slowest)** | High | 2 | Multi-level pointers | Real-time systems |
| Stack (Implicit) | 2 | Low | 1 | Subroutines, recursion | Random access |

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Rule of Thumb:** Each additional memory reference adds **1 to 3 clock cycles** of latency. Register and Immediate modes are therefore **preferred in tight CPU loops**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Addressing Modes (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ADDRESSING MODES"] --> B["DATA ADDRESSING MODES"]
    A --> C["BRANCH / CONTROL ADDRESSING MODES"]

    B --> B1["IMMEDIATE: Operand is constant"]
    B --> B2["DIRECT: EA equals address field"]
    B --> B3["REGISTER: Operand in CPU register"]
    B --> B4["REGISTER INDIRECT: Register holds pointer"]
    B --> B5["MEMORY INDIRECT: Memory holds pointer"]
    B --> B6["DISPLACEMENT: EA equals base plus offset"]
    B --> B7["STACK: EA equals top of stack pointer"]

    B6 --> B6a["RELATIVE: Base is PC"]
    B6 --> B6b["BASE REGISTER: Base is general register"]
    B6 --> B6c["INDEXED: Base is index register"]
    B4 --> B4a["AUTO INCREMENT: Pointer advances after use"]
    B4 --> B4b["AUTO DECREMENT: Pointer retreats before use"]

    C --> C1["PC RELATIVE: Branch target equals PC plus offset"]
    C --> C2["DIRECT JUMP: Target is absolute address"]
    C --> C3["INDIRECT JUMP: Target is read from register or memory"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B6 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 EA Calculation Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["INSTRUCTION FETCH UNIT\nbrings instruction to IR"] --> node2["DECODE UNIT\nreads addressing mode bits"]
    node2 --> node3["ADDRESS GENERATION UNIT\ncomputes Effective Address"]
    node3 --> node4["MEMORY ACCESS UNIT\nfetches operand from EA"]
    node4 --> node5["ALU EXECUTES OPERATION\non fetched operand"]

    node2 -.mode 1 immediate.-> node5
    node3 -.mode 2 register.-> node5
    node4 -.mode 3 plus.-> node5

    style node1 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000
    style node2 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000
    style node3 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000
    style node4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style node5 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Diagrammatic EA Computation Examples (ASCII Schematic)

```
(I)  IMMEDIATE              (II)  DIRECT
Instruction:  [OP | 0x25]    Instruction:  [OP | 0x500]
Operand     =  0x25          EA            =  0x500
                              Mem[0x500]   =  operand

(III) REGISTER               (IV)  REGISTER INDIRECT
Instruction: [OP | R2]       Instruction: [OP | R1]
Operand     = R2 content     EA            = [R1] = 0x300
                              Mem[0x300]   =  operand

(V)   INDEXED                (VI)  RELATIVE
Instruction: [OP | IX, Off]  Instruction: [OP | Offset]
EA = [IX] + Off              EA = [PC] + Offset
Mem[EA]    = operand         Branch jumps to EA

(VII) AUTO-INCREMENT
Instruction: [OP | (R1)+]
Step 1:  EA     = [R1]
Step 2:  fetch  Mem[EA]
Step 3:  R1     = R1 + d        (post-increment)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Set B]**
**Define addressing mode. List any four data addressing modes used in a typical CPU.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Addressing Mode:** The technique by which the CPU specifies/identifies the location of an operand of an instruction. It defines the rule used to compute the **Effective Address (EA)** of the operand from the bits present in the instruction.

**Four data addressing modes:**

1. **Immediate Addressing** — Operand value is embedded inside the instruction.
2. **Direct Addressing** — Instruction contains the absolute memory address of the operand.
3. **Register Addressing** — Operand is present in a CPU register.
4. **Register Indirect Addressing** — Register contains the memory address of the operand.

> **Examiner Note:** 1 mark for definition, 0.5 mark each for the four modes.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Set A]**
**Distinguish between absolute addressing mode and relative addressing mode.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Absolute (Direct) Addressing | Relative Addressing |
|---|---|---|
| Base value | Fixed memory address (constant) | Current value of **PC** |
| Effective Address | $\text{EA} = \text{Address field A}$ | $\text{EA} = \vert \text{PC} \vert + \text{Offset}$ |
| Use case | Static global variables | Branch/jump instructions |
| Code Relocatable? | **No** (address is hardcoded) | **Yes** (position-independent) |
| Typical size of address field | Large (full memory range) | Small (e.g., 8 or 16 bits) |

> **Examiner Note:** Tabular format fetches full 3 marks. Avoid writing only one or two points.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, candidates answer **one** full question of 14 marks, usually containing two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **OPTION A: Question A (14 Marks)**
### **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the following addressing modes with suitable diagrams and EA calculation:
(i) Direct Addressing, (ii) Register Indirect Addressing, (iii) Indexed Addressing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 16-bit machine has a memory of 64 KB. An instruction of size 2 bytes is stored at location $0x2000$. The instruction uses **relative addressing** with an 8-bit signed offset of $0x1E$. Calculate the effective address of the branch target. Justify whether it is a **forward** or **backward** branch.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Addressing Mode Explanations [7 Marks]**

**(i) Direct Addressing [2 Marks]**
- The address field of the instruction **directly gives** the memory location of the operand.
- **EA = Address field (A)**
- Example: `LOAD A, 5000` → Operand is fetched from memory location $5000$.
- **Memory references:** 1
- **Disadvantage:** Address field size is limited by instruction word length.

**(ii) Register Indirect Addressing [2 Marks]**
- The instruction specifies a register. The **content of the register** is the memory address of the operand.
- **EA = $\vert R \vert$** (content of register R)
- Example: `LOAD R2, (R1)` where R1 = $0x4000$ → Operand fetched from memory[$0x4000$].
- **Memory references:** 1
- **Use:** Implementing pointers in high-level languages.

**(iii) Indexed Addressing [2 Marks]**
- **EA = $\vert \text{IX} \vert + \text{Offset}$**, where IX is an index register.
- Commonly used to access **arrays** and **tables** in memory.
- Example: To read the 5th element of an array starting at $0x1000$ with each element = 4 bytes, set $\text{IX} = 0x1000$, Offset $= 5 \times 4 = 20 = 0x14$.
- **EA = $0x1000 + 0x14 = 0x1014$.**
- **Memory references:** 1
- **Note:** Sometimes a constant base is supplied by the instruction and IX provides the variable offset.

**Diagram (Mandatory for 1 Mark):**
```
Instruction:  [OP | Address Field = A]
                    |
                    v
              Memory Location A  ---->  Operand
```

#### **Part (b) — Effective Address Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- Machine is 16-bit → memory address size = 16 bits.
- Memory size = 64 KB (confirms 16-bit addressing).
- Instruction is at $\text{PC}_{\text{start}} = 0x2000$.
- Instruction size = 2 bytes.
- Addressing mode = **Relative (PC-relative)**.
- Offset = $0x1E$ (8-bit signed).

**Step 1 — Convert offset to decimal:**

$$
\text{Offset}_{\text{dec}} = 1 \times 16 + 14 = 30
$$

Since the most significant bit (MSB) of $0x1E$ is $0$ (because $0x1E < 0x80$), the offset is **positive**.

**Step 2 — Account for PC increment after fetch:**

After the CPU fetches the 2-byte instruction, the PC is auto-incremented by the instruction size.

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{updated}} = 0x2000 + 0x0002 = 0x2002
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the relative addressing formula:**

$$
\text{EA} = \text{PC}_{\text{updated}} + \text{Offset}
$$

$$
\text{EA} = 0x2002 + 0x001E
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final EA:**

$$
\text{EA} = 0x2020
$$

**Step 5 — Forward/Backward Justification:**

Since the offset is **positive** ($+0x1E$), the branch moves to a **higher memory address** than the current PC. This is a **FORWARD branch**.

**Final Answer:** $\text{EA} = 0x2020$, and the branch is a **forward branch**.

---

### **OPTION B: Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
### **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the working of **Auto-Increment** and **Auto-Decrement** addressing modes. Give one example for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An instruction `ADD R3, (R1)+` is executed on a 16-bit machine (data size = 2 bytes). Initially, $R1 = 0x4000$ and $R3 = 0x0010$. The memory content at $0x4000$ is $0x0025$. Calculate:
(i) The value of $R3$ after execution.
(ii) The new value of $R1$ after execution.
(iii) The total number of memory references involved.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Auto-Increment and Auto-Decrement [7 Marks]**

**Auto-Increment Addressing [3.5 Marks]**
- Variant of register indirect addressing.
- After the operand is fetched, the register is **automatically incremented** by the operand size $d$.
- **Working:** $\text{EA} = \vert R \vert$, then $R \leftarrow R + d$.
- **Use:** Sequential traversal of arrays (forward direction).
- **Example:** `LOAD R2, (R1)+` fetches operand from address $\vert R1 \vert$, then increments $R1$ by data size.

**Diagram:**
```
Initial:   R1 = 0x3000
Fetch:     EA = [R1] = 0x3000  -->  operand loaded
Update:    R1 = 0x3000 + 2 = 0x3002
```

**Auto-Decrement Addressing [3.5 Marks]**
- Variant of register indirect addressing.
- The register is **automatically decremented first**, then the operand is fetched from the new address.
- **Working:** $R \leftarrow R - d$, then $\text{EA} = \vert R \vert$.
- **Use:** Stack operations (LIFO push), reverse traversal of arrays.
- **Example:** `STORE R2, -(R1)` decrements $R1$ by 2, then stores R2 at the new address.

**Diagram:**
```
Initial:   R1 = 0x3000
Decrement: R1 = 0x3000 - 2 = 0x2FFE
Fetch/Store: EA = [R1] = 0x2FFE  -->  operand accessed
```

---

#### **Part (b) — Instruction Execution Analysis [7 Marks]**

**Given Instruction:** `ADD R3, (R1)+`
**Initial State:** $R1 = 0x4000$, $R3 = 0x0010$, $d = 2$ bytes.
**Memory Content:** $\text{Memory}[0x4000] = 0x0025$.

**Step 1 — Decode the instruction:**
- Destination register = R3.
- Source operand is at address pointed to by R1 using **auto-increment** mode.

**Step 2 — Compute Effective Address:**

$$
\text{EA} = \vert R1 \vert = 0x4000
$$

**Step 3 — Fetch the operand from memory [Memory Reference #1]:**

$$
\text{Operand} = \text{Memory}[0x4000] = 0x0025
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the ADD operation:**

$$
R3_{\text{new}} = R3_{\text{old}} + \text{Operand}
$$

$$
R3_{\text{new}} = 0x0010 + 0x0025 = 0x0035
$$

**Step 5 — Auto-Increment $R1$ by $d = 2$ bytes:**

$$
R1_{\text{new}} = R1_{\text{old}} + d = 0x4000 + 0x0002 = 0x4002
$$

**Step 6 — Total Memory References:**
- **1** memory reference was used (to fetch the operand from $0x4000$).
- Auto-increment happens in the register file → **0 additional memory references**.

**Final Answers:**

(i) $R3 = 0x0035$ (decimal: 53)
(ii) $R1 = 0x4002$
(iii) Total memory references = **1**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Forgetting PC auto-increment in Relative Addressing:** A common mistake is computing $\text{EA} = 0x2000 + 0x1E = 0x201E$ **without** adding the instruction size. Always add the instruction length to PC *before* applying the offset. **Penalty: −2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing Auto-Increment order:** Students often increment $R1$ *before* fetching the operand. The correct order is **fetch first, then increment.** **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 3. **Omitting the EA computation diagram:** Many students write the formula but skip the diagram. The diagram is worth **at least 1.5 marks** in any 7-mark sub-part.
> 4. **Sign confusion in relative offset:** A negative offset (MSB = 1) represents a **backward branch**, not a positive offset. Convert $0xFE = -2$ (in 2's complement) before applying the formula.
> 5. **Counting memory references incorrectly:** For `ADD R3, (R1)+`, students sometimes count 2 memory references (for R1 update). The auto-increment happens in the register, **not** memory.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Addressing Modes (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Addressing mode = rule for finding the operand's location.
- **Master EA Equation:** $\text{EA} = f(\text{Registers}, \text{Address Field}, \text{PC}, \text{Offset})$
- **Fastest Modes (0 memory refs):** Immediate, Register.
- **1 Memory Reference Modes:** Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed, Base-Register, Relative, Auto-Increment, Auto-Decrement, Stack.
- **Slowest Mode (2 memory refs):** Memory Indirect.
- **Relative Addressing** → uses PC as base → must add instruction size to PC first.
- **Indexed Addressing** → uses IX (Index Register) as base → ideal for arrays.
- **Base-Register Addressing** → uses BR (Base Register) → ideal for relocatable code and OS memory protection.
- **Auto-Increment:** fetch first, then $R \leftarrow R + d$. (Used in arrays, forward traversal.)
- **Auto-Decrement:** decrement first, then fetch. (Used in stack PUSH, reverse traversal.)
- **Stack Addressing:** EA is implicit (= SP); used in PUSH, POP, CALL, RET.
- **RISC philosophy:** 3–5 modes only (Register, Immediate, Register Indirect, PC-relative, Displacement).
- **CISC philosophy:** 12–24 modes (e.g., x86 supports 24+).
- **Performance Rule:** Each memory reference adds **1 to 3 clock cycles** of latency.
- **Use case shortcuts:**
  - **Constants** → Immediate
  - **Local variables** → Register or Register Indirect
  - **Arrays / Tables** → Indexed
  - **Branches** → PC-Relative
  - **Pointers** → Register Indirect
  - **System calls / Recursion** → Stack
- **Common KTU traps:** PC auto-increment, sign of relative offset, auto-increment order, counting memory references, missing diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Processing Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Processing Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Basic Processing Unit (BPU)**: The **Basic Processing Unit** is the fundamental computational engine of a computer system, comprising the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, the **Register File**, the **Control Unit (CU)**, and the **Interconnection Bus Structure**, all of which cooperate to fetch, decode, and execute machine-level instructions stored in main memory. It is the **datapath + control** view of a CPU as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme textbook (Hamacher / Mano's classic organization).

The **processing unit** performs all computational, logical, and data-movement operations on operands retrieved from memory or registers. The operations performed on data stored in registers are termed **Microoperations** — the atomic, indivisible actions that constitute a single machine instruction.

The four canonical microoperation classes studied in Module 1 are:

1. **Register Transfer Microoperations** – move data between registers.
2. **Arithmetic Microoperations** – addition, subtraction, increment, decrement.
3. **Logic Microoperations** – AND, OR, XOR, NOT, complement.
4. **Shift Microoperations** – logical shift, circular shift, arithmetic shift.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the CPU as a Bank Teller's Counter**:
>
> - The **Teller** = ALU (performs the actual calculation on cash/cheque).
> - The **Cash Drawer with slots** = Register File (temporary fast storage of denominations, customer ID, transaction slip).
> - The **Manager behind the glass** = Control Unit (reads the cheque, decides what action to take, signals the teller).
> - The **Conveyor Belt** = Internal Bus (carries money, slips, and ID cards back and forth).
> - The **Customer Window (memory)** = External Memory Interface.
>
> Every cheque (instruction) follows a strict sequence: pick it up from the drawer (fetch), read the amount (decode), tell the teller what to do (control signals), teller counts (execute), and the result is filed back (store). The Basic Processing Unit is exactly this *coordinated office* operating in nanosecond time-frames.

### 1.3 High-Yield Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know Elements (Frequently asked in ESE):**
> - Internal bus organization: **Single Bus**, **Two-Bus**, **Three-Bus** structures.
> - Standard register set: **PC, AR, DR, AC, IR, TR, INPR, OUTR, E (Carry), S(0), S(1)**.
> - **Common Bus System** with multiplexers and tri-state buffers.
> - Complete **Fetch–Decode–Execute** sequencing with timing signals.
> - Control word format for combined microoperations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Components of the Basic Processing Unit

The BPU is structurally decomposed into three cooperating subsystems:

| Subsystem | Functional Role | Critical Registers / Blocks |
|-----------|----------------|------------------------------|
| **Datapath** | Performs arithmetic, logic, and shift operations; routes data | ALU, Shifter, Register File |
| **Register Section** | Provides high-speed operand storage inside the CPU | PC, AR, DR, AC, IR, TR |
| **Control Unit** | Generates timing & control signals (microoperation sequencer) | Instruction decoder, Timing generator, Control Logic |
| **Bus Subsystem** | Physically connects registers to the ALU and to memory | Common Bus (MUX-based or 3-state buffer based) |

### 2.2 The Register Set — Definitions Required for KTU

> [!NOTE]
> Every register is a **fast on-chip n-bit storage cell** accessed within one clock pulse.

- **PC (Program Counter)** – holds the address of the **next** instruction to be fetched.
- **AR (Address Register)** – holds the memory address used for the current read/write.
- **DR (Data Register / Memory Data Register, MDR)** – temporary buffer for data being read from/written to memory.
- **AC (Accumulator)** – principal register for ALU operations; holds one operand and the result.
- **IR (Instruction Register)** – holds the **opcode** of the currently executing instruction.
- **TR (Temporary Register)** – holds an intermediate operand (e.g., second operand for ALU).
- **INPR / OUTR** – Input/Output registers (for serial I/O in Mano-style CPU).
- **E (Carry Flip-Flop)** – single-bit overflow / carry-out register.
- **S0, S1 (Status / Selector bits)** – selector for ALU function in 1-bit design.

### 2.3 Internal Bus Organization

#### 2.3.1 Single Common Bus (Mano's Model)
A single set of **n-bit parallel lines** shared by **all** registers. Selection of source/destination register is performed by **multiplexers** or by **tri-state bus drivers** with a `SEL` line per register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constraint**: In a single-bus CPU, only **one transfer per clock cycle** is possible (the bus is a **shared contention resource**). The ALU then needs a separate **AC → ALU input path** and the result is moved back to AC.

#### 2.3.2 Two-Bus Internal Organization
- **Bus A** (source) and **Bus B** (destination).
- ALU inputs come from both buses; output goes to a chosen destination register.
- Allows `R3 ← R1 + R2` to be completed in **one micro-operation cycle** (single step).

#### 2.3.3 Three-Bus Internal Organization
- **Bus A**, **Bus B**, **Bus C**.
- ALU input from A and B; output to C.
- An instruction of the form `R3 ← R1 op R2` requires **only one micro-step**, since all three transfers occur in parallel.
- Highest hardware cost, highest throughput — used in modern pipelined processors.

### 2.4 Microoperations — The Heart of BPU Functioning

**Register Transfer Microoperation**
A symbolic notation `R2 ← R1` denotes that the contents of register `R1` are copied into `R2` while `R1` is preserved (a *non-destructive* read, *destructive* write on R2).

**Arithmetic Microoperations (Symbol Table)**

| Operation | Symbolic Form | Description |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| Add | $R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2$ | Bitwise binary addition with carry |
| Subtract | $R3 \leftarrow R1 - R2$ | Realized as $R1 + \overline{R2} + 1$ |
| Increment | $R2 \leftarrow R2 + 1$ | Single operand add |
| Decrement | $R2 \leftarrow R2 - 1$ | Single operand subtract |
| Add with Carry | $R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2 + E$ | Includes carry flag E |
| Negate (2's complement) | $R2 \leftarrow \overline{R2} + 1$ | |

**Logic Microoperations (Bitwise)**

| Operation | Symbol | Truth-Table Pattern |
|-----------|--------|---------------------|
| AND | $\wedge$ | `F=1` only if both bits = 1 |
| OR | $\vee$ | `F=1` if at least one bit = 1 |
| XOR | $\oplus$ | `F=1` if bits differ |
| NOT (Complement) | $\overline{A}$ | Bit inversion |
| NAND / NOR | $\barwedge$, $\barvee$ | Composed forms |

**Shift Microoperations**

- `R ← shl R` : shift left, fill LSB with 0, MSB → E.
- `R ← shr R` : shift right, fill MSB with 0, LSB → E.
- `R ← cil R` : circular left (rotate).
- `R ← cir R` : circular right.
- `R ← ashl R` : arithmetic shift left (same as shl).
- `R ← ashr R` : arithmetic shift right (sign-bit replicated, MSB preserved).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the symbols $\vert$ (absolute) — never `|` — to keep table syntax intact.

| # | Equation / Symbol | Meaning | Units / Domain |
|---|-------------------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $T_{cycle} = T_{fetch} + T_{decode} + T_{execute}$ | Total instruction time | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $CPI = \dfrac{\sum_i N_i \cdot C_i}{N}$ | Average cycles per instruction | cycles / instr |
| 3 | $T_{CPU} = N \cdot CPI \cdot T_{clock}$ | CPU execution time | seconds |
| 4 | $R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2 + E$ | Add with carry (1-bit) | bit-vector |
| 5 | $R3 \leftarrow R1 + \overline{R2} + 1$ | Subtraction via 2's complement | bit-vector |
| 6 | $S_{out} = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} a_i \cdot m_i$ | Sum-of-Products selector | Boolean |
| 7 | $\vert X \vert = \sqrt{X \cdot X}$ | Magnitude (Mano abs. value unit) | numeric |
| 8 | $N = 2^{n}$ | Addressable words for n-bit address | words |
| 9 | $MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \cdot 10^{6}}$ | Million instructions per second | MIPS |
| 10 | $P_{3:0}^{n+1} = P^{n} + 1 \pmod{16}$ | 4-bit up-counter recurrence | mod 16 |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Processor Design Verification**: The microoperation table is the *blueprint* for the **RTL (Register Transfer Level)** description fed to hardware synthesis tools (Verilog/VHDL).
- **Performance Engineering**: The datapath width (n = 8/16/32/64 bits) and bus structure determine instruction-level parallelism. A **3-bus datapath** is the conceptual ancestor of every modern **superscalar pipeline** (Intel Core, ARM Cortex).
- **Embedded Controllers**: Even today's microcontrollers (8051, AVR, PIC) descend directly from the single-bus, accumulator-based Mano CPU model.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Two's-Complement Subtraction from a Single Adder

> **Goal**: Show that subtraction can be implemented with one adder plus inverters and a carry-in of 1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A - B &= A - B + 0 \\
      &= A + (-B) \\
      &= A + (\overline{B} + 1) \pmod{2^{n}} \quad &\text{(two's complement definition)} \\
      &= A + \overline{B} + 1 \pmod{2^{n}} \quad &\text{(associativity of addition)} \\
      &\Rightarrow \text{Adder receives } A, \overline{B}, \text{ and } C_{in}=1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation**: This is why the **Arithmetic Unit** in KTU's reference CPU (Mano Fig. 8-13) needs only **one adder**, **one set of XOR gates** to conditionally invert B, and a single control line `SUB` that simultaneously drives the inverter and forces `C_{in}=1`.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Common-Bus Selection Using Multiplexers

Consider an n-bit register file with **k registers**. To place any one of them on the common bus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} M_i \cdot R_i \\
  &= M_0 R_0 \;\vert\; M_1 R_1 \;\vert\; \cdots \;\vert\; M_{k-1} R_{k-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $M_i$ is the binary select code for register $R_i$. **Exactly one** $M_i$ may equal 1 at a time, so a binary-to-1-hot decoder is used in practice.

For $k = 8$ registers we need a **3-to-8 decoder**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_2 S_1 S_0 &\rightarrow \text{selected register} \\
000 &\rightarrow R0,\; 001 \rightarrow R1,\; \ldots,\; 111 \rightarrow R7
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Instruction Cycle Timing (Fetch Phase)

A typical **fetch** sequence for the KTU 1-bus accumulator CPU:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &: AR \leftarrow PC \\
T_1 &: DR \leftarrow M[AR], \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1 \\
T_2 &: IR \leftarrow DR[opcode], \quad AR \leftarrow DR[address]
\end{aligned}
$$

Each $T_i$ is one **clock cycle** (one microoperation). The fetch therefore consumes **3 clock cycles** for memory-reference instructions.

**Decode phase** is implicit at $T_2$ when `IR ← DR[opcode]` is performed; the opcode feeds the control logic.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Execution of a Memory-Reference Instruction (ADD)

For an instruction of the form `ADD X` (where `X` is a memory address):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 &: DR \leftarrow M[AR] \\
T_4 &: AC \leftarrow AC + DR, \quad \text{SC} \leftarrow 0 \;(\text{reset sequence counter})
\end{aligned}
$$

Total time for the complete ADD instruction = $5T$ (3 fetch + 2 execute).

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Shift-Right with Carry Bit (SHR Operation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &\leftarrow \text{shr} \; R \\
\text{bit}_i^{new} &= \text{bit}_{i+1}^{old}, \quad i = 0,1,\ldots,n-2 \\
\text{bit}_{n-1}^{new} &= 0 \\
E &= \text{bit}_{0}^{old}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the least significant bit "falls off" and lands in the carry flag `E` — this is how single-bit shifts propagate the discarded bit, a frequent KTU ask.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operand Availability by Bus Structure

| Instruction Form | Single Bus (cycles) | Two Bus (cycles) | Three Bus (cycles) |
|------------------|--------------------|------------------|--------------------|
| `R2 ← R1` | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| `R3 ← R1 + R2` | 3 (TR ← R1; AC ← R2; R3 ← AC+TR) | 2 | 1 |
| `R3 ← R1 + R2 + R3` | 4 | 3 | 1 (with multi-input ALU) |
| Shift `R2` | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| Memory read `DR ← M[AR]` | 2 (AR, then DR) | 2 | 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip**: When asked "design a 3-bus CPU for *X*", explicitly state the **microoperations for each bus** in separate $T_i$ columns. Marks are awarded for the *parallel* use of buses, not for serial single-bus solutions.

### 3.7 Derivation 6 — Instruction Format (Mano's Reference CPU)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{15 14 13 12 11 10 9 8 \quad 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0} \\
\underbrace{I \quad\;\, opcode}_{3 \text{ bits}} \quad \underbrace{address}_{12 \text{ bits}} \\
I = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Memory-Reference (MRI)} \\
I = 111 \Rightarrow \text{Register-Reference (RRI)} \\
I = 011 \Rightarrow \text{Input–Output / IRI}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of branch**: A direct-memory-reference instruction is identified by `I = 0` (bits 15-13). The remaining 13 bits encode the address.

### 3.8 Worked Numeric Example

> **Problem**: In an 8-bit KTU-style CPU, AC = 0110 0011, E = 0, and we execute `AC ← AC + E`. What is the new AC and E?
>
> **Solution**:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> AC + E &= 01100011_2 + 0 \\
>        &= 01100011_2 \\
> \text{New AC} &= 01100011_2 = 99_{10} \\
> \text{New E}   &= 0 \text{ (no carry-out generated)}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of the Basic Processing Unit (Mano-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL UNIT"]
        cdec["Instruction Decoder"]
        tgen["Timing Generator"]
        cseq["Control Sequencer"]
    end

    subgraph REG["REGISTER SET"]
        PC[("PC")]
        AR[("AR")]
        IR[("IR")]
        DR[("DR")]
        AC[("AC")]
        TR[("TR")]
        Ebit[("E")]
    end

    subgraph ALU["ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT"]
        adder["Adder / Subtracter"]
        logic["Logic Unit"]
        shift["Shifter"]
    end

    BUS{{"INTERNAL COMMON BUS"}}

    PC --> BUS
    AR --> BUS
    IR --> BUS
    DR --> BUS
    AC --> BUS
    TR --> BUS

    BUS --> adder
    BUS --> logic
    BUS --> shift

    adder --> AC
    logic --> AC
    shift --> AC
    adder --> Ebit

    cseq --> tgen
    IR --> cdec
    cdec --> cseq
    cseq --> PC
    cseq --> AR
    cseq --> DR
    cseq --> AC
    cseq --> TR
```

### 4.2 Three-Bus CPU Organization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[("R1")] -->|Bus A| ALU["ALU"]
    R2[("R2")] -->|Bus B| ALU
    ALU -->|Bus C| R3[("R3")]
    R1 -->|Bus A| R4[("R4")]
    R2 -->|Bus B| R5[("R5")]
    SHIFT["Shifter"] -->|Bus C| R6[("R6")]
    R1 -->|Bus A| SHIFT
    R2 -->|Bus B| SHIFT
```

### 4.3 Instruction Cycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Fetch
    Fetch --> Decode: T0-T2 complete, IR has opcode
    Decode --> Execute: opcode decoded, control signals issued
    Execute --> Fetch: SC reset to 0, next instr.
    Decode --> Interrupt: I/O or trap detected
    Interrupt --> Fetch: handler returns
    Execute --> Halt: HLT instruction
    Halt --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Microoperation Flow for a Typical Memory-Reference ADD Instruction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA["T0: AR <- PC"] --> stepB["T1: DR <- M AR, PC <- PC+1"]
    stepB --> stepC["T2: IR <- DR opcode, AR <- DR address"]
    stepC --> stepD["T3: DR <- M AR"]
    stepD --> stepE["T4: AC <- AC + DR, SC <- 0"]
    stepE --> stepF["Next Instruction Fetch"]
```

### 4.5 Control Word Format

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cw["CONTROL WORD (n bits)"]
    cw --> f1["F1 (3 bits): ALU function select"]
    cw --> f2["F2 (3 bits): Shift select"]
    cw --> f3["F3 (3 bits): Destination register"]
    cw --> f4["F4 (3 bits): Source register"]
    cw --> f5["CD: Control of memory R/W"]
    cw --> f6["BR: Branch field"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** (substitute for physical schematic):
> The diagram above captures the **register-to-ALU data flow**, the **control path** from the decoder to the timing generator, and the **feedback loop** that re-fetches the next instruction. This is the canonical Mano CPU block diagram rendered as a process-flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define microoperation. List and briefly explain the four types of microoperations performed by the basic processing unit.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks)**:
> A *microoperation* is an elementary operation performed on information stored in registers during one clock pulse. The four types are:
> 1. **Register Transfer Microoperations** — transfer of data between registers (`R2 ← R1`).
> 2. **Arithmetic Microoperations** — addition, subtraction, increment, decrement (`R3 ← R1 + R2`).
> 3. **Logic Microoperations** — bitwise AND, OR, XOR, NOT (`R3 ← R1 ∧ R2`).
> 4. **Shift Microoperations** — shift left, shift right, circular shift, arithmetic shift (`R2 ← shl R2`).
>
> *(1 mark definition + 2 marks listing + brief explanation.)*

---

**Q.A2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supple Series]`
**What is the role of a common bus system in a single-bus CPU? How is register selection performed?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks)**:
> The common bus is a single set of n-bit lines shared by all registers, allowing only **one transfer per clock**. Selection of source register is done by a **multiplexer** or a set of **tri-state buffers** controlled by select lines from the control unit. The selected register's output is enabled while all others are in high-impedance `Z`. Destination register is selected by a separate decoder-driven `LD` (load) line.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks) — Single-Bus CPU Organization

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **A.** (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the **organization of a basic processing unit** with a **single common bus**. Clearly show all registers, the ALU, and the bus. *(7 marks)*
>
> **A.** (b) Show the complete **fetch and execute sequence** for a *Memory-Reference ADD instruction* using register transfer statements. Assume Mano's reference CPU. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — A(a)

The single-bus CPU consists of:

- **Registers** connected to a common bus via multiplexers: `PC, AR, IR, DR, AC, TR, INPR, OUTR, E`.
- **ALU** with inputs from `AC` and the bus (or from `TR` and `AC`); output back to `AC`.
- **Control Unit** decoding the IR opcode and generating timing signals $T_0, T_1, T_2, \ldots$
- **Memory interface** through `AR` (address) and `DR` (data).

Because only one register can drive the bus at a time, multi-operand operations need a **temporary register** (TR) to hold the second operand.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bus drivers} &: \text{tri-state buffers, one per register} \\
\text{Bus select}  &: \text{3-to-8 decoder driven by control word field } S_2 S_1 S_0
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Block diagram shown in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

#### Model Solution — A(b) — Microoperation Sequence for `ADD X`

| Step | Microoperation | Description |
|------|---------------|-------------|
| $T_0$ | $AR \leftarrow PC$ | Load address of instruction. |
| $T_1$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | Read instruction, increment PC. |
| $T_2$ | $IR \leftarrow DR[15..13]$, $AR \leftarrow DR[12..0]$ | Decode opcode; prepare operand address. |
| $T_3$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | Fetch operand. |
| $T_4$ | $AC \leftarrow AC + DR$, $SC \leftarrow 0$ | Perform add, reset sequence counter. |

**Total cycles for ADD = 5.** This matches the KTU reference table.

---

### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks) — Three-Bus CPU & ALU Design (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
>
> **B.** (a) Compare **single-bus, two-bus, and three-bus** internal organizations of a CPU. Show the **micro-operation sequence** for the instruction `R3 ← R1 + R2` in all three organizations. *(7 marks)*
>
> **B.** (b) Design a **1-bit ALU slice** that can perform **AND, OR, ADD, and SUBTRACT** operations. Show the control inputs and the resulting output expressions. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — B(a) — Bus Comparison

| Feature | Single Bus | Two Bus | Three Bus |
|---------|-----------|---------|-----------|
| Internal buses | 1 | 2 (A,B) | 3 (A,B,C) |
| ALU input from | Bus + AC | Bus A, Bus B | Bus A, Bus B |
| ALU output to | AC | Bus C → dest | Bus C → dest |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Speed for `R3 ← R1+R2` | 3 steps | 2 steps | 1 step |

**Microoperation sequences for `R3 ← R1 + R2`:**

- **Single Bus**:
  $$
  T_0: TR \leftarrow R1, \quad T_1: AC \leftarrow R2, \quad T_2: R3 \leftarrow AC + TR
  $$

- **Two Bus**:
  $$
  T_0: R3 \leftarrow \text{ALU}(A=R1, B=R2), \quad T_1: \text{(no-op or return)}
  $$

- **Three Bus**:
  $$
  T_0: R3 \leftarrow \text{ALU}(A=R1, B=R2) \;\; \text{(single-step parallel transfer)}
  $$

#### Model Solution — B(b) — 1-bit ALU Slice Design

The 1-bit ALU slice has:

- **Data inputs**: $A$, $B$ (and $C_{in}$ for arithmetic).
- **Operation Select**: $S_1 S_0$ (2 bits) → 4 operations.
- **Output**: $F$, carry-out $C_{out}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 S_0 &= 00 \rightarrow F = A \wedge B \\
S_1 S_0 &= 01 \rightarrow F = A \vee B \\
S_1 S_0 &= 10 \rightarrow F = A \oplus B \;\;(\text{used as sum, with } C_{in}=0) \\
S_1 S_0 &= 11 \rightarrow F = A \oplus B \oplus 1 \;\;(\text{used as diff, with } C_{in}=1) \\
C_{out} &= A \cdot B + (A \oplus B) \cdot C_{in}
\end{aligned}
$$

For subtraction, the $B$ line is XOR-ed with a `SUB` control bit to obtain $\overline{B}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A \oplus (\overline{B} \oplus SUB) \oplus C_{in} \\
  &= A \oplus B \oplus SUB \oplus C_{in} \\
  &= A \oplus \overline{B} \oplus 1 \quad \text{when } SUB=1, C_{in}=1
\end{aligned}
$$

An **n-bit ALU** is constructed by cascading **n copies** of this 1-bit slice with $C_{out,i} \rightarrow C_{in,i+1}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where You Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing PC and AR** — PC holds the address of the *next* instruction; AR holds the *current* memory address. Many students swap them in $T_0$. Marks deducted.
> 2. **Forgetting PC ← PC + 1** — Increment of PC must happen at $T_1$, *concurrently* with `DR ← M[AR]`. Failing to include both micro-operations costs 1 mark.
> 3. **No carry flag in arithmetic** — The 1-bit design *requires* tracking `E`. Writing only `AC ← AC + DR` without showing the E-update loses marks.
> 4. **Drawing single bus for a "3-bus CPU" question** — Examiner awards zero for datapath mismatch.
> 5. **Missing SC ← 0 at the end of execute** — Sequence-counter reset is mandatory to start the next fetch.
> 6. **Not specifying bus selection signals** — Always write the MUX/decoder output (e.g., $S_2 S_1 S_0 = 011$ for selecting `AC`).
> 7. **Writing generic pseudo-code** — KTU demands **register transfer notation** (`R2 ← R1`), not high-level C.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **BPU = Datapath + Control + Registers + Buses**.
- **Registers**: PC, AR, IR, DR, AC, TR, E, INPR, OUTR, S0, S1.
- **Single bus**: 1 transfer per cycle; multi-operand ops need a temporary register.
- **Two bus**: source + destination buses; ALU gets both inputs in one step.
- **Three bus**: parallel source A, source B, destination C → one-step ALU ops.
- **Microoperations**: register-transfer, arithmetic, logic, shift (4 classes).
- **Shift types**: logical (shl/shr), circular (cil/cir), arithmetic (ashr).
- **Subtraction** = add 2's complement of subtrahend; realized with one adder + XOR-inverters + $C_{in}=1$.
- **Common bus** uses MUX or tri-state buffers with a select decoder.
- **Fetch** typically 3 cycles ($T_0, T_1, T_2$); **Execute** 1–3 cycles.
- **ADD** instruction = 5 cycles (3 fetch + 2 execute).
- **1-bit ALU** built from a full adder + logic gates; cascaded for n-bit ALU.
- **Status bits**: E (carry), S0, S1 select ALU function; SC resets to 0.
- **Instruction format** in Mano CPU: 3-bit opcode (I) + 12-bit address (or opcode extension).
- **KTU expects** RTL notation, not pseudocode, in all answers.
- **Always** reset SC after the last execute cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Computer Organization — Formal Definition

**Computer Organization** refers to the operational units and their interconnections that realize the architectural specifications of a computer system. It deals with the *transparent* (programmer-invisible) hardware details such as control signals, interfaces, memory technology, bus arbitration, and data-path timing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction — Memorize This:**
> - **Computer Architecture** = Attributes visible to the programmer (instruction set, addressing modes, data types). It is an *abstract* design.
> - **Computer Organization** = Attributes related to the *transparent* implementation (control signals, interfaces, memory usage). It is a *physical* realization.

### 1.2 Von Neumann Architecture — The Conceptual Foundation

A **Von Neumann Architecture** is a stored-program computer design where both **instructions** and **data** reside in a **single, shared memory** and are accessed through a **common bus system**. The Central Processing Unit (CPU) fetches instructions sequentially, decodes them, and executes them via the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) and registers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Structural Components (Five Functional Units):**
> 1. **Input Unit** — Accepts data/commands from the outside world.
> 2. **Memory Unit** — Stores both data and program instructions (RAM/ROM).
> 3. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — Performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logical (AND, OR, NOT, XOR) operations.
> 4. **Control Unit (CU)** — Directs all operations via control signals; orchestrates fetch–decode–execute.
> 5. **Output Unit** — Returns processed results (monitor, printer, etc.).

### 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Office Worker"

> [!TIP]
> Think of a computer as a highly efficient **office clerk**:
> - **Memory** is the clerk's *filing cabinet* (where reports and instructions are stored).
> - **ALU** is the *calculator on the desk* (does math).
> - **Control Unit** is the *supervisor* (tells the clerk what to do next).
> - **Input/Output** is the *mail tray* (incoming and outgoing documents).
> - **Bus** is the *corridor* connecting the desk, cabinet, and mail tray.

In Von Neumann's design, the clerk must walk down the *same corridor* to fetch either an instruction or a data value — this creates the famous **Von Neumann Bottleneck**, where the single shared bus limits throughput.

### 1.4 The System Bus — Communication Backbone

A **Bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of parallel wires that transfer data, addresses, or control signals between components.

| Bus Type | Direction | Purpose | Width (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Bus** | Bidirectional | Carries actual data values | 32-bit / 64-bit |
| **Address Bus** | Unidirectional (CPU → Memory/I/O) | Specifies the source/destination location | 32-bit / 64-bit |
| **Control Bus** | Mixed | Timing & control signals (Read/Write, Clock, Interrupt) | 8–16 lines |

### 1.5 Number System Fundamentals

A **Number System** is a systematic way of representing numbers using a defined set of digits and a **base (radix)** $r$. The position of each digit represents a power of $r$.

> [!NOTE]
> For any number with digits $d_{n-1}\,d_{n-2}\,\ldots\,d_1\,d_0\,.\,d_{-1}\,d_{-2}\,\ldots\,d_{-m}$ in base $r$:
> $$\text{Value} = \sum_{i=-m}^{n-1} d_i \cdot r^i$$

| System | Base $r$ | Digits Used | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Binary | **2** | 0, 1 | Internal CPU representation |
| Octal | **8** | 0–7 | Compact binary shorthand |
| Decimal | **10** | 0–9 | Human interface |
| Hexadecimal | **16** | 0–9, A–F | Memory addresses, machine code |

### 1.6 Standard Constants in Computer Organization

- $1\ \text{bit} =$ binary digit
- $1\ \text{nibble} = \mathbf{4\ bits}$
- $1\ \text{byte} = \mathbf{8\ bits}$
- $1\ \text{word} = \mathbf{16/32/64\ bits}$ (architecture dependent)
- $1\ \text{KB} = 2^{10}\ \text{bytes} = \mathbf{1024\ bytes}$
- $1\ \text{MB} = 2^{20}\ \text{bytes}$
- $1\ \text{GB} = 2^{30}\ \text{bytes}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Signed Number Representation — The Three Core Schemes

Computers must represent positive *and* negative numbers. KTU tests the following three schemes exhaustively.

#### Scheme 1: Sign-Magnitude Representation
- The **MSB (Most Significant Bit)** is reserved as the sign bit: `0` = positive, `1` = negative.
- The remaining bits represent the **magnitude** in regular binary.
- Drawbacks: Two representations of zero (+0 and −0), arithmetic circuits are complex.

> **Example:** For an 8-bit system, $-25_{10} = \mathbf{1001\,1001_2}$ (sign = 1, magnitude 0011001 = 25).

#### Scheme 2: 1's Complement Representation
- For a **positive** number, the representation is the same as standard binary.
- For a **negative** number, **invert (flip) every bit** of the positive representation.
- Drawback: Still has two zeros (`00000000` and `11111111`).

> **Example:** $-25_{10}$ in 1's complement (8-bit) → start with $+25 = 00011001$ → flip bits → $\mathbf{1110\,0110_2}$.

#### Scheme 3: 2's Complement Representation *(KTU's Most Favored Scheme)*
- For a **positive** number, the representation is the same as standard binary.
- For a **negative** number, take the **1's complement** then **add 1** to the LSB.
- **Single zero** representation → simpler arithmetic hardware.

> **Example:** $-25_{10}$ in 2's complement (8-bit) → 1's complement = $11100110$ → add 1 → $\mathbf{1110\,0111_2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 2's Complement Dominates:** Addition and subtraction of signed numbers can be performed using the *same* binary addition hardware — the MSB is automatically treated as the sign bit, and overflow is detected by checking the carry into and out of the sign bit.

### 2.2 IEEE 754 Floating-Point Standard

Floating-point numbers represent real numbers in scientific notation using a fixed number of bits. The **IEEE 754** standard is universally adopted.

The general format is:
$$\text{Number} = (-1)^S \times 1.M \times 2^{(E - \text{bias})}$$

| Component | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Sign | $S$ | 0 = positive, 1 = negative (1 bit) |
| Exponent | $E$ | Biased exponent (8/11 bits) |
| Mantissa | $M$ | Fractional part (23/52 bits) |

**Common IEEE 754 Formats (KTU High-Yield):**

| Format | Total Bits | Sign | Exponent | Mantissa | Bias |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Precision** | **32** | 1 | 8 | 23 | **127** |
| **Double Precision** | **64** | 1 | 11 | 52 | **1023** |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Positional value | $V = \sum d_i \cdot r^i$ | $r$ = base |
| 2 | Decimal → Binary | Repeated division by 2 | Read remainders bottom-up |
| 3 | Binary → Decimal | $\sum d_i \cdot 2^i$ | Sum weighted bits |
| 4 | 2's complement of $N$ | $2^n - N$ | $n$ = number of bits |
| 5 | Range of $n$-bit unsigned | $0$ to $2^n - 1$ | Inclusive |
| 6 | Range of $n$-bit 2's comp | $-2^{n-1}$ to $2^{n-1} - 1$ | Asymmetric |
| 7 | Sign-Magnitude range | $-(2^{n-1} - 1)$ to $(2^{n-1} - 1)$ | Symmetric |
| 8 | Excess-$k$ bias value | $E_{stored} = e_{actual} + k$ | $k$ = bias |
| 9 | IEEE 754 exponent stored | $E_{stored} = e_{actual} + 127$ | Single precision |
| 10 | Largest value (IEEE 754) | $(2 - 2^{-23}) \times 2^{127}$ | Single precision |
| 11 | Smallest normalized +ve | $2^{-126}$ | Single precision |
| 12 | Decimal digit conversion | Multiply fraction by 2 | Read integer parts top-down |
| 13 | Hex ↔ Binary | 1 hex digit = 4 binary bits | Direct nibble substitution |
| 14 | Address space size | $2^n$ unique locations | $n$ = address bus width |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Excess-K (Biased) Representation:** The actual exponent is stored as $E_{stored} = e_{actual} + K$, where $K = 2^{k-1} - 1$ for $k$ exponent bits. This lets exponents be compared as unsigned integers, simplifying hardware.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Converting Decimal to Binary (Repeated Division)

**Problem:** Convert $45_{10}$ to binary.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } 45 \div 2 &= 22 \quad \text{remainder } \mathbf{1} \quad \text{(LSB)} \\
\text{Step 2: } 22 \div 2 &= 11 \quad \text{remainder } \mathbf{0} \\
\text{Step 3: } 11 \div 2 &= 5 \quad \text{remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
\text{Step 4: } 5 \div 2 &= 2 \quad \text{remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
\text{Step 5: } 2 \div 2 &= 1 \quad \text{remainder } \mathbf{0} \\
\text{Step 6: } 1 \div 2 &= 0 \quad \text{remainder } \mathbf{1} \quad \text{(MSB)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reading remainders bottom-up:** $45_{10} = \mathbf{101101_2}$

**Verification using positional formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
101101_2 &= 1\cdot 2^5 + 0\cdot 2^4 + 1\cdot 2^3 + 1\cdot 2^2 + 0\cdot 2^1 + 1\cdot 2^0 \\
&= 32 + 0 + 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = \mathbf{45_{10}} \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: 2's Complement of a Negative Number

**Problem:** Find the 8-bit 2's complement representation of $-87_{10}$.

**Step A:** Convert $+87$ to binary (8-bit):

$$
87 \div 2 = 43\,R\,\mathbf{1} \quad 43 \div 2 = 21\,R\,\mathbf{1} \quad 21 \div 2 = 10\,R\,\mathbf{1} \quad 10 \div 2 = 5\,R\,\mathbf{0}
$$
$$
5 \div 2 = 2\,R\,\mathbf{1} \quad 2 \div 2 = 1\,R\,\mathbf{0} \quad 1 \div 2 = 0\,R\,\mathbf{1}
$$

Read bottom-up: $+87_{10} = 01010111_2$

**Step B:** Apply 1's complement (invert every bit):
$$01010111 \rightarrow \mathbf{10101000}$$

**Step C:** Add 1 to LSB:
$$10101000 + 1 = \mathbf{10101001_2}$$

So, $-87_{10} = \mathbf{1010\,1001_2}$ in 2's complement.

**Cross-Verification using formula** $2^n - N$:

$$
2^8 - 87 = 256 - 87 = 169_{10}
$$

$$
169_{10} = 128 + 32 + 8 + 1 = \mathbf{10101001_2} \ \checkmark
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: IEEE 754 Single Precision Encoding

**Problem:** Encode $-12.625_{10}$ in IEEE 754 single precision.

**Step 1 — Sign Bit:**
The number is negative → $S = \mathbf{1}$

**Step 2 — Convert to Binary:**
Integer part: $12_{10} = 1100_2$
Fractional part: $0.625 = 0.5 + 0.125 = 2^{-1} + 2^{-3} = 0.101_2$

Combined: $12.625_{10} = 1100.101_2$

**Step 3 — Normalize (move binary point to right of leading 1):**
$$1100.101 = 1.100101 \times 2^3$$

So, mantissa $M = \mathbf{100101\ldots}$ (pad with zeros to 23 bits)
Actual exponent $e = \mathbf{3}$

**Step 4 — Biased Exponent:**
$$E_{stored} = e + 127 = 3 + 127 = 130_{10} = 10000010_2$$

**Step 5 — Assemble 32 bits:**

$$
\underbrace{\mathbf{1}}_{\text{Sign}}\ \underbrace{\mathbf{10000010}}_{\text{Exponent (8 bits)}}\ \underbrace{\mathbf{10010100000000000000000}}_{\text{Mantissa (23 bits)}}
$$

**Final 32-bit IEEE 754 representation:** $\mathbf{1100\,0001\,0100\,1010\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000}$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Signed Representations

| Property | Sign-Magnitude | 1's Complement | 2's Complement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Representation of $+0$ | $000\ldots0$ | $000\ldots0$ | $000\ldots0$ |
| Representation of $-0$ | $100\ldots0$ | $111\ldots1$ | **Does not exist** |
| Range (n bits) | $-(2^{n-1}-1)$ to $(2^{n-1}-1)$ | $-(2^{n-1}-1)$ to $(2^{n-1}-1)$ | $-2^{n-1}$ to $(2^{n-1}-1)$ |
| Hardware for subtraction | Complex | Complex | **Simple (same adder)** |
| KTU Exam Preference | Medium | Low | **Highest** |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Range Calculation for 16-bit 2's complement:**
> $$-2^{16-1} = -32768 \quad \text{to} \quad 2^{16-1} - 1 = +32767$$

### 3.5 Hexadecimal ↔ Binary Conversion Example

**Problem:** Convert $\text{F3A.2C}_{16}$ to binary.

Hex-to-binary uses 1 hex digit = 4 binary bits (nibble mapping):

| F | 3 | A | 2 | C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1111 | 0011 | 1010 | 0010 | 1100 |

**Result:** $\text{F3A.2C}_{16} = \mathbf{1111\,0011\,1010.\,0010\,1100_2}$

### 3.6 Range of Unsigned $n$-bit Number — Proof Sketch

For an $n$-bit unsigned number, every bit position represents a power of 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum} &= 000\ldots0_2 = 0 \\
\text{Maximum} &= 111\ldots1_2 = 2^{n-1} + 2^{n-2} + \ldots + 2^0 = 2^n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The sum $2^{n-1} + 2^{n-2} + \ldots + 2^0$ is a **geometric series** with sum $\frac{2^n - 1}{2 - 1} = 2^n - 1$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Computer System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InputUnit["INPUT UNIT\nKeyboard / Mouse / Scanner"] --> CPU_Bus
    OutputUnit["OUTPUT UNIT\nMonitor / Printer"]
    MemoryUnit["MEMORY UNIT\nRAM and ROM\nStores Data and Instructions"] --> CPU_Bus
    CPU_Bus{"SYSTEM BUS\nData + Address + Control"}
    CPU_Bus --> OutputUnit
    CPU_Block["CPU CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT"] --> CPU_Bus
    subgraph CPU_Block
        direction TB
        RegBank["REGISTER BANK\nPC, IR, MAR, MBR, AC"]
        ALU["ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT\nAdd Subtract AND OR"]
        CU["CONTROL UNIT\nFetch Decode Execute"]
    end
    RegBank --- ALU
    CU --- ALU
    CU --- RegBank
    CPU_Bus --- CPU_Block
```

### 4.2 Bus Architecture — Interconnection Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU"]
    MainMem["MAIN MEMORY\nRAM"]
    IO1["I/O DEVICE 1"]
    IO2["I/O DEVICE 2"]
    Disk["SECONDARY STORAGE\nHard Disk SSD"]
    DBus(["DATA BUS\nBidirectional\n32 or 64 bits"])
    ABus(["ADDRESS BUS\nUnidirectional\nFrom CPU"])
    CBus(["CONTROL BUS\nRead Write Clock\nInterrupt Signals"])
    CPU --- DBus
    CPU --- ABus
    CPU --- CBus
    MainMem --- DBus
    MainMem --- ABus
    MainMem --- CBus
    IO1 --- DBus
    IO1 --- CBus
    IO2 --- DBus
    IO2 --- CBus
    Disk --- DBus
    Disk --- CBus
```

### 4.3 Stored Program Execution Flow (Von Neumann Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["PROGRAM START"]) --> Fetch["FETCH INSTRUCTION\nMAR gets address\nMBR gets instruction\nIR stores opcode"]
    Fetch --> Decode["DECODE INSTRUCTION\nControl Unit decodes opcode\nIdentifies operation type"]
    Decode --> Operand["OPERAND FETCH\nDetermine addressing mode\nRead operands from registers or memory"]
    Operand --> Execute["EXECUTE\nALU performs operation\nOr data transfer occurs"]
    Execute --> Store["STORE RESULT\nWrite back to register or memory location"]
    Store --> Check{"PROGRAM\nCOMPLETE?"}
    Check -- NO --> Fetch
    Check -- YES --> Halt(["HALT"])
```

### 4.4 IEEE 754 Single Precision Bit Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Bit31["Bit 31\nS = 1 bit\nSign bit"]
    Bits30_23["Bits 30 to 23\nE = 8 bits\nBiased Exponent\nStored E equals e plus 127"]
    Bits22_0["Bits 22 to 0\nM = 23 bits\nFractional Mantissa\nImplied leading 1"]
    Bit31 --- Bits30_23 --- Bits22_0
```

### 4.5 Number System Conversion Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root["ANY BASE r SYSTEM"] --> Int["INTEGER PART\nRepeated DIVISION by r\nRead remainders bottom up"]
    Root --> Frac["FRACTIONAL PART\nRepeated MULTIPLICATION by r\nRead integer parts top down"]
    Int --> R1["Remainders form digits in base r"]
    Frac --> R2["Integers form digits after decimal"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1: Define Computer Organization. Differentiate it from Computer Architecture.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Computer Organization** refers to the way in which the functional units and components of a computer system are structured, interconnected, and controlled to realize its architectural design. It deals with hardware-level, *transparent* details such as bus architecture, control signal timing, memory hierarchy, and I/O mechanisms.

| Aspect | Computer Architecture | Computer Organization |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Abstract attributes visible to programmer | Hardware implementation details |
| Focus | *What* the system does | *How* it does it |
| Examples | Instruction set, addressing modes, data types | Control signals, interfaces, memory technology |
| Visibility | Transparent to the *user* | Transparent to the *programmer* hardware level |

> [!NOTE]
> A classic KTU example: The **VAX architecture** had a rich instruction set (architectural), but the **VAX 11/780** had a specific microprogrammed control unit implementation (organizational). Same architecture, different organization.

**(3 marks awarded for: 1 mark for correct definition + 1 mark for tabular comparison + 1 mark for example)**

---

#### **Question 2: Explain Von Neumann architecture with a neat block diagram. State its main limitation.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Von Neumann Architecture** is a stored-program computer design proposed by John von Neumann in 1945. Its key features are:

- Both *instructions* and *data* are stored in a **single, common memory**.
- A **single bus** is used to fetch instructions and to read/write data.
- The CPU has an **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** and a **Control Unit (CU)** with registers.
- Instructions are executed **sequentially** (one at a time) following the *fetch–decode–execute* cycle.

**Main Limitation — The Von Neumann Bottleneck:**
Since instructions and data share the *same* memory and bus, the CPU is often forced to *wait* while instructions are being fetched, because data and instructions cannot be accessed *simultaneously*. This creates a throughput bottleneck, limiting performance.

**(3 marks awarded for: 1 mark definition + 1 mark key features + 1 mark limitation)**

---

### **PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Convert the decimal number $-87.625_{10}$ into its IEEE 754 single precision floating-point representation. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the 2's complement representation scheme. Represent $+57$ and $-57$ in 8-bit 2's complement and perform the subtraction $25 - 57$ using 2's complement arithmetic. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a) — IEEE 754 Conversion of $-87.625_{10}$**

**Step 1: Sign Bit**
The number is negative → $\mathbf{S = 1}$

**Step 2: Convert to Binary**
Integer: $87 = 64 + 16 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 1010111_2$
Fraction: $0.625 = 0.5 + 0.125 = 2^{-1} + 2^{-3} = 0.101_2$

Combined: $87.625_{10} = 1010111.101_2$

**Step 3: Normalize**
$$1010111.101 = 1.010111101 \times 2^6$$

Mantissa $M = 01011110100000000000000$ (padded to 23 bits)
Actual exponent $e = 6$

**Step 4: Biased Exponent**
$$E_{stored} = 6 + 127 = 133_{10} = 10000101_2$$

**Step 5: Assemble**

| Sign (1) | Exponent (8) | Mantissa (23) |
|---|---|---|
| `1` | `10000101` | `01011110100000000000000` |
