**Final 32-bit value (grouped by 8 bits):**
$$\mathbf{1100\,0010\,1010\,1111\,0100\,0000\,0000\,0000}$$

**Verification:** $(-1)^1 \times (1 + 2^{-2} + 2^{-4} + 2^{-5} + 2^{-6} + 2^{-7} + 2^{-9}) \times 2^{6-127} = -87.625$ ✓

---

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 2's Complement Arithmetic**

**Theory:** In 2's complement, the MSB acts as the sign bit (0 = +, 1 = −). To negate, invert all bits and add 1. Subtraction is performed as *addition with the negative*.

**Step 1: Represent $+57$ in 8-bit 2's complement**
$$57 = 32 + 16 + 8 + 1 = 00111001_2$$

**Step 2: Represent $-57$ in 8-bit 2's complement**
1's complement: $11000110_2$
Add 1: $\mathbf{11000111_2}$

**Step 3: Perform $25 - 57$ as $25 + (-57)$**

| Binary | Decimal |
|---|---|
| $00011001$ | $+25$ |
| $11000111$ | $-57$ (2's complement) |

**Binary addition:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&00011001 \\
+&11000111 \\
\hline
&11100000
\end{aligned}
$$

Discard the carry-out (overflow beyond 8 bits) → result = $11100000_2$

**Step 4: Interpret the result**
The MSB is 1, so the result is negative. To find the magnitude, take the 2's complement:
$11100000 \rightarrow 1\text{'s comp: } 00011111 \rightarrow \text{add 1: } 00100000 = 32$

So, the result is $\mathbf{-32_{10}}$.

**Verification:** $25 - 57 = -32$ ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the functional units of a computer system with a block diagram. List and briefly describe the three types of system buses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert $(1\text{A}3\text{F})_{16}$ to binary, octal, and decimal. Represent $-105_{10}$ in (i) Sign-Magnitude, (ii) 1's complement, and (iii) 2's complement using 8 bits. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a) — Functional Units & System Buses**

A computer system has **five functional units** that work together to process data:

1. **Input Unit** — Accepts raw data and instructions from external devices (keyboard, mouse, scanner) and converts them into a binary form usable by the system.
2. **Memory Unit** — Stores both *data* and *program instructions*. Divided into primary (RAM, fast, volatile) and secondary (HDD/SSD, slow, non-volatile) memory.
3. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — Performs all arithmetic operations (+, −, ×, ÷) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, shifts, compares).
4. **Control Unit (CU)** — Directs the operation of all other units. It fetches instructions, decodes them, and generates the control signals that coordinate ALU, memory, and I/O operations. Implements the fetch–decode–execute cycle.
5. **Output Unit** — Returns processed results to the outside world (monitor, printer, speakers).

**Three Types of System Buses:**

| Bus Type | Function | Direction | Typical Width |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Bus** | Transfers actual data between CPU, memory, and I/O | Bidirectional | 32 or 64 bits |
| **Address Bus** | Carries the memory or I/O address of the data location | Unidirectional (CPU → others) | 32 bits typically |
| **Control Bus** | Carries timing and control signals: Memory Read, Memory Write, I/O Read, I/O Write, Clock, Interrupt, Bus Request | Mixed | ~10–20 lines |

---

**Model Solution to Part (b) — Number System Conversions**

**Step 1: $(1\text{A}3\text{F})_{16}$ to Binary**
Each hex digit = 4 binary bits:

| 1 | A | 3 | F |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0001 | 1010 | 0011 | 1111 |

$$\mathbf{1\text{A}3\text{F}_{16} = 0001\,1010\,0011\,1111_2}$$

**Step 2: Binary to Octal**
Group into sets of 3 from the right:

$$000\ 110\ 100\ 011\ 111\ 1 \rightarrow \text{pad leading 0} \rightarrow 000\ 110\ 100\ 011\ 111$$
$$\mathbf{= 06437_8}$$

**Step 3: Hex to Decimal**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1\text{A}3\text{F}_{16} &= 1\cdot 16^3 + 10\cdot 16^2 + 3\cdot 16^1 + 15\cdot 16^0 \\
&= 4096 + 2560 + 48 + 15 \\
&= \mathbf{6719_{10}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: $-105_{10}$ representations (8 bits)**

(i) **Sign-Magnitude:** $+105 = 01101001_2$ → set MSB = 1 → $\mathbf{11101001_2}$

(ii) **1's Complement:** Invert every bit of $01101001$ → $\mathbf{10010110_2}$

(iii) **2's Complement:** 1's comp + 1 → $10010110 + 1 = \mathbf{10010111_2}$

**Cross-check (2's comp formula):** $2^8 - 105 = 256 - 105 = 151_{10} = 128 + 16 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 10010111_2$ ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgot the implicit leading 1 in IEEE 754** → The mantissa field stores only the *fractional* part; the leading 1 is implied. Omitting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Did not discard the carry-out in 2's complement subtraction** → In 2's complement addition, any carry that overflows *beyond* the MSB is *thrown away*. Not discarding it shows conceptual confusion.
> 3. **Confused sign-magnitude with 2's complement range** → Range of 8-bit 2's complement is $-128$ to $+127$ (asymmetric), not $-127$ to $+127$ (which is sign-magnitude).
> 4. **Mis-ordered the IEEE 754 bit layout** → The order is **Sign | Exponent | Mantissa** from MSB to LSB. Reversed layout = 0 marks.
> 5. **Skipped the normalization step** → KTU evaluators *require* the explicit statement $N = 1.M \times 2^e$ before biasing the exponent.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- [x] **Computer Organization = transparent hardware details; Architecture = programmer-visible design.**
- [x] **Five functional units:** Input, Memory, ALU, Control Unit, Output.
- [x] **Von Neumann Bottleneck** arises from shared memory and single bus.
- [x] **Three buses:** Data (bidirectional), Address (unidirectional), Control (mixed).
- [x] **Positional value formula:** $V = \sum d_i \cdot r^i$ — works for *any* base.
- [x] **2's complement** is the *industry standard* — it has a single zero, asymmetric range, and simple hardware.
- [x] **Range of $n$-bit 2's complement:** $-2^{n-1}$ to $+2^{n-1} - 1$.
- [x] **2's complement of $N$** can be computed as $2^n - N$ (verify in one step).
- [x] **IEEE 754 Single Precision:** 1 sign + 8 exponent + 23 mantissa bits, bias = 127.
- [x] **IEEE 754 Double Precision:** 1 sign + 11 exponent + 52 mantissa bits, bias = 1023.
- [x] **Mantissa in IEEE 754** has an *implied* leading 1 — only fractional part is stored.
- [x] **Hex ↔ Binary:** 1 hex digit = 4 binary bits (nibble mapping).
- [x] **Decimal → Binary:** Repeated division by 2 (read remainders bottom-up).
- [x] **Fractional Decimal → Binary:** Repeated multiplication by 2 (read integer parts top-down).
- [x] **Biased exponent** allows unsigned integer comparison of exponents.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
The **Instruction Cycle** (also called the **Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle** or **FDE Cycle**) is the fundamental operational loop executed by the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** of a computer to retrieve, interpret, and execute machine-level instructions stored in main memory. According to the **Hamacher, Vranesic, Zaky & Manek (Computer Organization and Embedded Systems, 6th Ed.)** standard used widely in KTU curricula, every instruction cycle consists of three mandatory phases — **Fetch**, **Decode**, and **Execute** — followed conditionally by a **Memory Access** and **Write-back** phase depending on the instruction's addressing mode and operation type.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The instruction cycle is the bedrock of Module 1. Every other topic (micro-operations, control unit, pipelining) builds on the precise understanding of this cycle. Expect direct 3-mark questions and the foundation of 14-mark derivations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the CPU as a **highly disciplined chef in a restaurant kitchen**:
- The **recipe book** = Main Memory (contains all instructions)
- The **current recipe page being read** = Instruction Register (IR)
- The **bookmark** = Program Counter (PC)
- The **countertop workspace** = CPU registers (AC, MDR, etc.)
- The **chef** = Control Unit + ALU

The chef performs the same routine for **every single dish**: (1) look at the bookmarked page, (2) read the next instruction, (3) understand the instruction, (4) gather ingredients and perform the action, (5) update the bookmark. This loop is **continuous** and **identical in structure** for every instruction — only the *content* varies.

### 1.3 Key Components Involved
| Component | Full Name | Role |
|-----------|-----------|------|
| **PC** | Program Counter | Holds address of **next** instruction to fetch |
| **MAR** | Memory Address Register | Buffer that holds the address sent to memory |
| **MDR** | Memory Data Register | Buffer that holds the data/instruction fetched from memory |
| **IR** | Instruction Register | Holds the **currently executing** instruction |
| **AC** | Accumulator | Primary working register for ALU operations |
| **ALU** | Arithmetic Logic Unit | Performs arithmetic/logic operations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Boundary Condition:** Memory access time is assumed to be **1 clock cycle**, while register operations are assumed to be instantaneous. This simplification is critical for KTU exam timing diagrams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Mandatory Phases

#### **Phase 1: Fetch Phase**
The Control Unit (CU) issues the **read control signal**. The CPU retrieves the instruction pointed to by the PC from main memory. The fetched instruction is loaded into the IR for decoding, and the PC is incremented to point to the subsequent instruction in sequence.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Load the contents of PC into MAR → `MAR ← PC`
2. Initiate memory read operation → memory places the instruction on the data bus
3. Load the contents of the data bus (MDR) into IR → `IR ← M[PC]` or `IR ← MDR`
4. Increment the PC to point to the next instruction → `PC ← PC + 1`

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PC + 1?** In a byte-addressable machine with fixed instruction length of *k* bytes, the increment is by *k*. For simplicity, KTU questions generally assume single-byte instructions, hence `PC ← PC + 1`.

#### **Phase 2: Decode Phase**
The instruction in IR is interpreted by the Control Unit. Decoding involves determining:
- The **opcode** (operation to be performed — ADD, LOAD, STORE, JUMP, etc.)
- The **operand(s)** (addresses, register references, or immediate values)
- The **addressing mode** (direct, indirect, immediate, register, etc.)

During decode, the Control Unit generates the necessary **control signals** that will guide subsequent micro-operations in the Execute phase. The opcode is typically decoded using a **decoder circuit** that activates one output line corresponding to the identified instruction.

#### **Phase 3: Execute Phase**
Based on the decoded opcode, the actual operation is carried out. This phase varies widely between instructions:
- **Arithmetic/Logic:** `AC ← AC + operand` (e.g., ADD instruction)
- **Data Transfer:** `AC ← M[address]` (e.g., LOAD instruction)
- **Memory Store:** `M[address] ← AC` (e.g., STORE instruction)
- **Control Transfer:** `PC ← effective_address` (e.g., JUMP instruction)

### 2.2 Extended Phases (Memory Access & Write-Back)
For instructions involving operands in memory (e.g., LOAD, STORE), two additional phases may be inserted:

| Phase | Description | Example Instruction |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Memory Access** | Performs the actual memory read/write using the effective address | `LOAD R1, 500` reads memory[500] |
| **Write-Back** | Writes the computed result back to the destination register | ALU result stored in R1 |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / RTL | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | PC Increment | $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | Cycle 1 of Fetch |
| 2 | Memory Fetch | $IR \leftarrow M[PC]$ | Cycle 2 of Fetch |
| 3 | Indirect Memory Read | $IR \leftarrow M[MAR]$ | Used in indirect addressing |
| 4 | Direct Memory Read | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | Data path notation |
| 5 | Memory Write | $M[AR] \leftarrow DR$ | For STORE opcodes |
| 6 | Arithmetic | $AC \leftarrow AC + DR$ | ADD instruction |
| 7 | Logic AND | $AC \leftarrow AC \land DR$ | AND instruction |
| 8 | Logic OR | $AC \leftarrow AC \lor DR$ | OR instruction |
| 9 | Complement | $AC \leftarrow \overline{AC}$ | NOT/CMA instruction |
| 10 | Branch | $PC \leftarrow PC + \text{offset}$ | Conditional/unconditional jump |
| 11 | Skip (Increment PC) | $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | Conditional skip (e.g., ISZ) |
| 12 | Interrupt Cycle | $PC \leftarrow 0$, Save state | External I/O event |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility
The instruction cycle model is foundational for:
- **Pipelining**: Overlapping Fetch/Decode/Execute to achieve instruction-level parallelism
- **CPU Performance**: Calculating **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** and execution time
- **Hazard Detection**: Identifying data and control hazards in pipelined architectures
- **Emulator Design**: Building software emulators for legacy architectures
- **Compiler Design**: Understanding how high-level code maps to machine instructions

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete RTL Analysis of Instruction Cycle (KTU Standard Notation)

The KTU board examination pattern frequently asks for the **Register Transfer Language (RTL)** specification of instruction cycles. Below is the **exhaustive derivation** following the Hamacher-Manek standard.

Let the CPU registers be defined as:
- **PC**: Program Counter
- **AR / MAR**: Address Register / Memory Address Register
- **DR / MDR**: Data Register / Memory Data Register
- **AC**: Accumulator
- **IR**: Instruction Register
- **E**: Effective address flag (used in indirect addressing)

#### **3.1.1 Fetch Phase — Three RTL Statements**

**Statement F1 (T0):** Place the address of the next instruction in the AR.

$$AR \leftarrow PC$$

**Textual Logic:** The address of the next instruction to be fetched is already stored in the Program Counter (PC). We copy this address into the Address Register (AR), which is connected to the address bus of the main memory. Without this step, memory does not know *which* location to read from.

---

**Statement F2 (T1):** Read the addressed memory location and place the instruction in DR.

$$IR \leftarrow M[AR], \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1$$

**Textual Logic:** The Control Unit asserts the **Read** control signal. After one memory access time, the contents of `M[AR]` (i.e., the instruction stored at the location whose address was just placed in AR) appear on the data bus and are loaded into both the Instruction Register (IR) and the Data Register (DR). Simultaneously, the PC is incremented by 1 so that it now points to the next sequential instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why simultaneously?** In a single-bus architecture, the bus is busy during memory read, so the PC increment is done in the same cycle using internal register logic without bus access.

---

**Statement F3 (T2):** Decode the instruction opcode (no data transfer — control logic operation).

$$\text{DECODE}(IR[opcode])$$

**Textual Logic:** This is a purely **internal control operation** — no register transfer occurs. The decoder circuit examines the opcode bits of the IR and activates one of $2^n$ output lines (for an *n*-bit opcode). The activated line sets up the control signals for the upcoming Execute phase.

---

#### **3.1.2 Execute Phase — Example Cases**

**Case A: ADD Instruction (Register-Type)**
Opcode: ADD with operand referenced by address field.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& D_1 T_4: \quad DR \leftarrow M[AR] \\
& D_1 T_5: \quad AC \leftarrow AC + DR, \quad SC \leftarrow 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:**
1. **D1 T4:** The decoder has recognized an ADD instruction (output line `D1` active). The operand's effective address is already in AR (extracted from the address field of the IR). The CPU reads the memory at this address and places the operand in DR.
2. **D1 T5:** The ALU performs the addition: `AC + DR`, and the result is stored back in the Accumulator (AC). The Sequence Counter (SC) is reset to 0, signaling the end of the current instruction and readiness to fetch the next.

---

**Case B: Indirect Memory Read (e.g., for operand located via pointer)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& D_2 T_4: \quad AR \leftarrow IR[address] \\
& D_2 T_5: \quad AR \leftarrow M[AR] \\
& D_2 T_6: \quad DR \leftarrow M[AR]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:**
1. **D2 T4:** The address field of the IR is loaded into AR — this is the address **of the pointer**, not the actual operand.
2. **D2 T5:** Memory is read at `AR`. The contents read here are the **effective address** of the operand. This effective address is loaded back into AR.
3. **D2 T6:** The actual operand is fetched by reading memory at the effective address and placing it in DR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The number of memory accesses per instruction is a **high-yield exam point**. Direct addressing requires 1 memory access in Execute; indirect addressing requires **2**.

---

### 3.2 Interrupt Cycle RTL Derivation

An **interrupt** is an external asynchronous event that suspends normal program execution. After the current instruction's Execute phase completes, the CPU checks for pending interrupts. If one exists:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& T_0' \cdot IEN \cdot FGI: \quad AR \leftarrow 0, \quad TR \leftarrow PC \\
& T_1' \cdot IEN \cdot FGI: \quad M[0] \leftarrow TR, \quad PC \leftarrow 0 \\
& T_2' \cdot IEN \cdot FGI: \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1, \quad IEN \leftarrow 0, \quad R \leftarrow 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:**
1. **T0':** If the Interrupt Enable (IEN) flag is set **AND** the Fetch/Grant Interrupt (FGI) signal is asserted, the CPU begins interrupt processing. The address **0** (a reserved memory location) is loaded into AR, and the **current PC value** (i.e., return address) is saved in the temporary register TR.
2. **T1':** The contents of TR (the return address) are written to memory location 0. The PC is then cleared to 0 so that it begins fetching from the **interrupt service routine (ISR)** starting at memory location 0.
3. **T2':** PC is incremented to 1 (assuming ISR starts at memory 0 but the next instruction is at 1), the Interrupt Enable flag is cleared (to prevent nested interrupts), and the interrupt acknowledge line (R) is reset.

### 3.3 Timing & Performance Derivation

Given:
- Memory cycle time: $\tau$ seconds (or 1 clock cycle in simplified models)
- Register operation time: negligible (≈ 0)
- Number of clock cycles per instruction: $C_i$
- Number of instructions in program: $N$
- Clock frequency: $f$ Hz

The total execution time is:

$$T_{\text{exec}} = \frac{N \times \text{CPI}}{f}$$

where **CPI** (Cycles Per Instruction) is computed as:

$$
\text{CPI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{CPI}_i \times f_i)
$$

Here, $f_i$ is the frequency of instruction type $i$, and $\text{CPI}_i$ is the cycles per instruction for type $i$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Instruction Cycle Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start / Power On]) --> stateA[RESET: PC = 0]
    stateA --> fetchA

    subgraph PHASE_FETCH [PHASE 1: FETCH]
        fetchA[AR ← PC] --> fetchB[IR ← M AR, PC ← PC + 1]
    end

    fetchB --> decodeA

    subgraph PHASE_DECODE [PHASE 2: DECODE]
        decodeA[Opcode Decoder Activates Control Line]
    end

    decodeA --> checkInt{Interrupt Pending?}

    checkInt -- Yes --> intCycleA
    checkInt -- No --> execA

    subgraph PHASE_INTERRUPT [INTERRUPT CYCLE]
        intCycleA[Save PC to M 0, Load ISR Address]
    end

    intCycleA --> fetchA

    subgraph PHASE_EXECUTE [PHASE 3: EXECUTE]
        execA{Instruction Type}
        execA -- Arithmetic/Logic --> execB[ALU Operation: AC ← AC op Operand]
        execA -- Memory Read --> execC[DR ← M AR]
        execA -- Memory Write --> execD[M AR ← AC or DR]
        execA -- Branch/Jump --> execE[PC ← Target Address]
    end

    execB --> fetchA
    execC --> fetchA
    execD --> fetchA
    execE --> fetchA
```

### 4.2 Single-Bus CPU Organization for Instruction Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU [CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT]
        PCreg[PC Register] --> MARreg[MAR Register]
        MARreg -->|Address Bus| MEMext[ ]
        MDRreg[MDR Register] -->|Data Bus| MEMext
        MDRreg --> IRreg[IR Register]
        IRreg -->|Opcode| CTL[Control Unit]
        IRreg -->|Address Field| MARreg
        CTL -->|Control Signals| ALUnit[ALU]
        ACreg[AC Accumulator] --> ALUnit
        MDRreg --> ALUnit
        ALUnit --> ACreg
    end

    MEMext[ ] -->|Read/Write| MAINSYS[Main Memory]
    CTL -->|R, W, IEN Signals| MAINSYS
```

### 4.3 Timing Diagram Representation (Mermaid Gantt-style)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Instruction Cycle Phases (Clock Cycles T0 to T5)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Fetch Phase
    AR ← PC           :a1, 0, 1
    IR ← M AR PC++    :a2, 1, 1
    Decode Opcode     :crit, a3, 2, 1
    section Execute Phase ADD
    DR ← M AR         :b1, 3, 1
    AC ← AC + DR      :b2, 4, 1
    SC ← 0            :b3, 5, 1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Interpretation:** In KTU notation, $T_i$ refers to the *i*-th clock cycle. The decoder line $D_i$ is asserted during specific $T_j$ cycles to identify the instruction. For example, $D_1 T_4$ means instruction type 1 (ADD), cycle 4.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
*Define the Instruction Cycle. List its three fundamental phases.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Instruction Cycle** is the complete process by which a computer retrieves a single machine instruction from memory, determines the actions required, carries out those actions, and prepares to fetch the next instruction.

The three fundamental phases are:
1. **Fetch Phase:** The control unit retrieves the instruction from the memory address pointed to by the Program Counter (PC) and loads it into the Instruction Register (IR). The PC is incremented.
2. **Decode Phase:** The control unit examines the opcode in IR and identifies the operation type, generating appropriate control signals.
3. **Execute Phase:** The identified operation is performed — arithmetic, logic, memory access, or control transfer — using the ALU and registers.

*(Award 1 mark each for the definition and each of the three phases.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Explain the function of the Program Counter (PC) and the Instruction Register (IR) in the instruction cycle.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Program Counter (PC)** is a special-purpose CPU register that holds the memory address of the **next instruction** to be fetched. At the start of the Fetch phase, the contents of PC are transferred to the Memory Address Register (MAR) so that the CPU can read the corresponding instruction. After each fetch, the PC is automatically incremented (`PC ← PC + 1`) to point to the next sequential instruction, unless altered by a branch/jump instruction.

The **Instruction Register (IR)** holds the **currently fetched instruction** during its entire Decode and Execute phases. Its opcode bits are decoded by the control unit, and its address/operand bits are used to compute effective addresses. The IR remains unchanged throughout execution to ensure the instruction remains identifiable.

*(Award 1.5 marks each for PC and IR functions.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
*With the help of a neat diagram, explain the instruction cycle of a basic computer. Write the complete Register Transfer Language (RTL) specification for the Fetch and Execute phases of an ADD instruction.*

#### **Part (a) — Instruction Cycle Diagram & Phases (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A basic computer's instruction cycle consists of **Fetch**, **Decode**, and **Execute** phases. The figure (see Section 4.1) illustrates the overall flow.

**Fetch Phase Steps:**
1. **T0:** $AR \leftarrow PC$ — The address of the next instruction (stored in PC) is copied into the Address Register (AR) so that the memory subsystem can locate the instruction.
2. **T1:** $IR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ — A memory READ is initiated. The instruction found at address AR is loaded into the Instruction Register (IR). Simultaneously, the PC is incremented to point to the subsequent instruction.
3. **T2:** Decode the opcode bits of IR. This is a control-only operation with no register-to-register transfer.

**Execute Phase (for ADD Instruction):**
1. **D1 T4:** $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ — The decoder recognizes the ADD opcode. The effective address of the operand is already in AR. The operand is fetched from memory and stored in DR.
2. **D1 T5:** $AC \leftarrow AC + DR$, $SC \leftarrow 0$ — The ALU adds the contents of AC and DR, and the sum is placed back in AC. The sequence counter (SC) is reset, marking the end of the instruction.

*(Mark allocation: 3 for diagram + 4 for stepwise RTL)*

#### **Part (b) — Significance and Edge Cases (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Significance of the Instruction Cycle:**
1. It is the **fundamental rhythm** of all CPU operations; every higher-level process (pipelining, interrupts, multi-core execution) is built upon variations of this cycle.
2. It determines the **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** and directly impacts **execution time** and **throughput**.

**Edge Cases to Discuss:**
1. **Indirect Addressing:** Requires an additional memory access — `AR ← M[AR]` before the operand fetch.
2. **Branch Instructions:** Modify the PC during the Execute phase, breaking the sequential flow.
3. **Interrupts:** Suspend the normal cycle, save state, and transfer control to an ISR.
4. **Pipeline Hazards:** Overlapping Fetch/Execute causes data and control hazards requiring stalling or forwarding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students frequently write `PC ← PC + 1` for fetch but forget to specify that this happens **simultaneously with** $IR \leftarrow M[AR]$. The KTU board expects **both transfers in the same RTL statement** because they occur in the same clock cycle (T1) on a single-bus system. Lose 0.5–1 mark if omitted.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** When asked for "Execute phase of ADD," do **not** include the Fetch phase RTL. Many students write the entire cycle RTL redundantly. Be **selective** — answer only what is asked.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
*Explain the Fetch, Decode, and Execute phases of the instruction cycle using Register Transfer Language. Also explain the Interrupt Cycle and write its RTL specification.*

#### **Part (a) — Three Phases with RTL (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Fetch Phase (T0 to T2):**

$$
T_0: \quad AR \leftarrow PC
$$

$$
T_1: \quad IR \leftarrow M[AR], \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1
$$

$$
T_2: \quad \text{Decode } IR[opcode]
$$

**Logic:** In $T_0$, the address from PC is copied to AR. In $T_1$, memory is read and the instruction is loaded into IR while PC increments. In $T_2$, the decoder identifies the opcode and activates the corresponding control line $D_i$.

**Decode Phase:**
The opcode field of IR is fed into a decoder circuit with $n$ output lines, one per instruction. The activated line (e.g., $D_1$ for ADD, $D_2$ for LDA, $D_3$ for STA) determines the micro-operations in subsequent cycles.

**Execute Phase (LDA — Load to AC):**

$$
D_2 T_4: \quad DR \leftarrow M[AR]
$$

$$
D_2 T_5: \quad AC \leftarrow DR, \quad SC \leftarrow 0
$$

**Logic:** The operand at the address in AR is loaded into DR ($T_4$), then transferred to AC ($T_5$).

#### **Part (b) — Interrupt Cycle RTL (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An interrupt is an **external asynchronous signal** that suspends normal execution. After the Execute phase of the current instruction, the CPU checks the **IEN (Interrupt Enable)** and **FGI (Fetch Grant Interrupt)** flags.

**Interrupt Cycle RTL:**

$$
T_0' : \quad AR \leftarrow 0, \quad TR \leftarrow PC
$$

$$
T_1' : \quad M[0] \leftarrow TR, \quad PC \leftarrow 0
$$

$$
T_2' : \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1, \quad IEN \leftarrow 0, \quad R \leftarrow 0
$$

**Logic:**
- $T_0'$: Save the current return address (PC) into temporary register TR. Place address **0** (interrupt vector location) into AR.
- $T_1'$: Write the saved PC to memory location 0 (so the ISR knows where to return). Load PC with 0 to begin ISR execution.
- $T_2'$: Increment PC to 1 (skipping over the saved return address at location 0). Disable further interrupts (IEN ← 0) to prevent nesting. Reset the interrupt acknowledge flip-flop (R ← 0).

**Return from Interrupt (RTI):**
At the end of the ISR, the saved return address is read back: `PC ← M[0], IEN ← 1`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** In the Interrupt Cycle, students often confuse **TR (Temporary Register)** with **AR** or skip the **IEN ← 0** step. The interrupt masking step is critical and worth 1 mark separately. Also, never use `$T_0, T_1, T_2$` for the interrupt cycle — the **prime symbol (')** is mandatory to distinguish it from the Fetch cycle.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Instruction Cycle**

- **Three mandatory phases:** Fetch → Decode → Execute. Always remember in this order.
- **Fetch RTL core statement:** $IR \leftarrow M[PC], \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1$
- **Decode is control-only** — no register-to-register transfer occurs; only decoder lines activate.
- **Sequence Counter (SC):** Reset to 0 at the end of every instruction. Tracks $T_0, T_1, T_2, \ldots$ cycles.
- **Decoder output lines:** Denoted $D_0, D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n$ — one per instruction. Each $D_i$ combined with a specific $T_j$ determines the micro-operation.
- **Indirect addressing** requires **2 memory accesses** in Execute phase; direct requires only 1.
- **Interrupt cycle uses prime notation:** $T_0', T_1', T_2'$ — distinct from instruction cycle $T_0, T_1, T_2$.
- **Interrupt always saves PC** to memory location 0 (in the basic computer model) before loading ISR address.
- **IEN (Interrupt Enable)** must be cleared during interrupt to prevent nested interrupts.
- **CPI of basic non-pipelined computer:** Typically 5–6 cycles per simple instruction (T0–T4/T5).
- **PC increment is +1 for byte-addressable single-byte instructions**, +k for k-byte instructions.
- **Single-bus CPU:** All registers share one bus, hence sequential transfers; multi-bus enables parallel transfers.
- **Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Control:** Instruction cycle logic can be implemented via combinational logic (hardwired) or microcoded ROM (microprogrammed) — both produce the same RTL.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Execution of a Complete Instruction - Single Bus and Multiple Bus Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Execution of a Complete Instruction - Single Bus and Multiple Bus Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instruction Execution**: The systematic sequence of micro-operations performed by the processor hardware to fetch, decode, and execute a single machine instruction. The instruction cycle comprises the **Fetch Cycle**, **Decode Cycle**, **Operand Fetch Cycle**, **Execute Cycle**, and **Store Result Cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Bus Organization**: An internal processor architecture in which all major registers (PC, MAR, MDR, IR, GPRs), the ALU, and the memory subsystem are interconnected through **one common data bus**. Since the bus is shared, only **one register-to-register transfer** can occur per clock cycle, necessitating the use of temporary holding registers (Y and Z) at the ALU inputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Bus Organization**: A high-performance processor architecture that employs **three internal buses** (Bus A, Bus B, and Bus C). Bus A and Bus B feed the two ALU inputs simultaneously, while Bus C carries the ALU output. This allows the ALU to complete an operation (operand fetch + computation + result storage) in a **single clock cycle**, eliminating the need for temporary holding registers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy - The Single Checkout Counter Supermarket 🛒**
> 
> Imagine a small grocery store with **only one checkout counter (bus)**. Every customer (data word) wanting to go from the entrance to the billing counter, or from the counter to the bagging area, must use this **single lane**. To compute the bill (ALU operation), the cashier first needs to pick up item A and place it aside (register Y), then pick up item B (register Z), and only then press the calculator (ALU). The total is then handed over. This is **slow** but **cheap to build**.
> 
> **The Multiple Counter Hypermarket 🏬**
> 
> Now imagine a large supermarket with **three parallel conveyor belts (three buses)**. Item A rides Belt A directly to the calculator, Item B rides Belt B simultaneously, and the calculator outputs the total on Belt C — **all at once, in one go**. This is **fast** but **expensive to build** (more wires, more control logic).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (As Per KTU Board Syllabus)

- **Internal Registers**: PC (Program Counter), MAR (Memory Address Register), MDR (Memory Data Register), IR (Instruction Register), R0–R(n-1) (General Purpose Registers).
- **ALU Temporary Registers**: Y-register and Z-register (Single Bus only).
- **Bus Count**: **1** (Single Bus) or **3** (Three-Bus Organization).
- **Standard Word Size**: **n bits** (typically 16, 32, or 64 bits depending on architecture).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Instruction Execution Sequence (Unified Model)

Every complete instruction execution follows this 6-stage micro-operation sequence. Let us denote bus transfer as `Bus ← Source` and ALU operation as `Destination ← Function(Source)`.

| Stage | Micro-Operation Purpose |
|---|---|
| **T1: Fetch Address** | Place PC contents onto the bus; load MAR; increment PC. |
| **T2: Fetch Opcode** | Issue Memory Read; load MDR from memory; transfer opcode to IR. |
| **T3: Decode** | IR opcode is decoded; control unit determines required operands. |
| **T4: Effective Address** | For memory-reference instructions, compute the operand address and load MAR. |
| **T5: Fetch Operand** | Memory Read; load MDR with operand; transfer to ALU input register. |
| **T6: Execute & Store** | Perform ALU operation; route result back to destination register. |

## 2.2 Single Bus Organization - Deep Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> In a **single bus system**, because the bus is the only data highway, you cannot simultaneously place two operands on it. Hence, the ALU cannot fetch both inputs directly from the bus. We must use **two temporary registers (Y and Z)**:
> - **Y-register**: Accepts the first operand from the bus and feeds one ALU input.
> - **Z-register**: Accepts the second operand from the bus and feeds the other ALU input.

### Step-by-Step Logic for `ADD R1, R2, R3` (Register-Add Instruction)

The instruction means: R1 ← R1 + R2 (or with R3 depending on format). Let us use the standard three-operand format: `R1 ← R2 + R3`.

1. **T1**: `MAR ← PC` ; `PC ← PC + 1` (Address placed on bus, PC incremented)
2. **T2**: `MDR ← M[MAR]` ; `IR ← MDR` (Instruction fetched from memory)
3. **T3**: `Decode IR` (Control unit identifies this as ADD)
4. **T4**: `Y ← R2` (First operand placed on bus, latched into Y)
5. **T5**: `Z ← R3` (Second operand placed on bus, latched into Z)
6. **T6**: `R1 ← Y + Z` (ALU computes, output routed back on the bus to R1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two temporary registers?** Because once you place R2 on the bus, it disappears from the bus in the very next cycle. To hold it steady for the ALU, it must be latched into Y. The ALU then computes Y + Z (internally), and the result is placed on the bus to be loaded into R1.

### Step-by-Step Logic for `ADD R1, X` (Memory-Reference Instruction)

This means: R1 ← R1 + Memory[X]. The address X is part of the instruction (already in IR after fetch).

1. **T1**: `MAR ← PC` ; `PC ← PC + 1`
2. **T2**: `MDR ← M[MAR]` ; `IR ← MDR` (Fetch complete)
3. **T3**: `Decode IR`
4. **T4**: `MAR ← IR[Address]` (Address field of IR sent to MAR)
5. **T5**: `MDR ← M[MAR]` ; `Y ← R1` (Operand fetched; R1 latched into Y simultaneously)
6. **T6**: `Z ← MDR` ; (Then next cycle) `R1 ← Y + Z`

## 2.3 Multiple (Three) Bus Organization - Deep Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **three-bus architecture**, the bottleneck of temporary registers is **eliminated**. The two ALU input multiplexers connect directly to Bus A and Bus B. The ALU output is driven onto Bus C. Hence, an ALU operation like R1 ← R2 + R3 becomes a **single-cycle operation**.

### Why Three Buses are Faster - The Core "Why"

| Step | Single Bus | Three-Bus |
|---|---|---|
| Fetch R2 | `Y ← R2` (1 cycle) | Place R2 on Bus A (parallel) |
| Fetch R3 | `Z ← R3` (1 cycle) | Place R3 on Bus B (parallel) |
| Compute | `R1 ← Y + Z` (1 cycle) | ALU computes internally |
| Store Result | Routed back on bus (1 cycle) | Bus C drives R1 in same cycle |
| **Total** | **3 clock cycles** | **1 clock cycle** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight**: For an instruction with **k operands** requiring ALU computation, single-bus takes roughly `2k + 1` cycles, whereas three-bus takes **2 cycles** (one to place operands, one to store result). For a typical `R-type` instruction: Single = 3 cycles, Three-Bus = 1 cycle.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield summary** for solving KTU numericals and theory questions on this topic.

| Parameter | Single Bus | Three-Bus |
|---|---|---|
| Number of internal buses | **1** | **3** (A, B, C) |
| Temporary registers at ALU | **Y and Z (required)** | **Not required** |
| Cycles for `R1 ← R2 + R3` | **3** | **1** |
| Cycles for `R1 ← M[X] + R2` | **5** | **2** |
| ALU input sources | Y, Z registers | Bus A, Bus B directly |
| ALU output destination | Routed back on main bus | Bus C |
| Hardware cost | **Low** | **High** (more wires + MUXes) |
| Speed | **Slow** | **Fast** |
| Bus conflict possibility | **High** (only one transfer at a time) | **Low** (parallel transfers) |
| Control logic complexity | Simple | Complex |

### 2.4.1 Generalized Cycle Count Formula

$$C_{single} = 2k + 1$$

where $k$ is the number of source operands (excluding result destination).

$$C_{three\text{-}bus} = 2$$

The "2" represents: (1 cycle) to place all operands on buses A and B in parallel, and (1 cycle) to drive the ALU output on bus C into the destination.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Modern processors (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, ARM Cortex) use **deeply pipelined multi-bus architectures** with internal register files connected via multiple read/write ports — a direct evolution of the three-bus concept. The single-bus model is retained pedagogically because it makes the **micro-operation flow** transparent to the student and forms the basis for understanding **register transfer language (RTL)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Micro-Operation Trace: `ADD R1, X` on Single Bus

Let us derive, in full KTU-board-detail, the complete clock-by-clock execution of a memory-reference ADD instruction. Assume the instruction format is: `[Opcode (6 bits) | Mode (2 bits) | Address (8 bits)]`.

### Step 1 — Fetch Address Phase (T0)

The PC must be transferred to MAR, and the PC must be incremented to point to the next instruction.

$$\text{Bus} \leftarrow \text{PC}$$

$$\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{Bus}$$

The ALU is used here to add 1 to the PC. Since PC and constant 1 are both on the bus path (via the incrementer unit), the result is stored back.

$$\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1$$

### Step 2 — Memory Read & Instruction Fetch (T1)

A **memory read signal** is asserted. Memory places the contents of address `MAR` onto the bus.

$$\text{Bus} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]}$$

$$\text{MDR} \leftarrow \text{Bus}$$

The MDR contents (the full instruction word) are then transferred to IR.

$$\text{Bus} \leftarrow \text{MDR}$$

$$\text{IR} \leftarrow \text{Bus}$$

### Step 3 — Decode Phase (T2)

The control unit inspects the opcode field of IR. No bus transfer is needed; this is an **internal decoder operation**.

$$\text{Control Signals} \leftarrow \text{Decode(IR[Opcode])}$$

### Step 4 — Address Computation (T3)

The instruction requires a memory operand at address `X`. The address field of the IR is sent to MAR.

$$\text{Bus} \leftarrow \text{IR[Address field]}$$

$$\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{Bus}$$

### Step 5 — Operand Fetch (T4)

Memory is read again, and the operand is loaded into MDR. Simultaneously, the existing contents of R1 are latched into Y (since the bus can only carry one item per cycle, and we need R1 on the bus now).

$$\text{MDR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]}$$

$$\text{Bus} \leftarrow \text{MDR} \implies \text{Y} \leftarrow \text{Bus}$$

### Step 6 — Load Second ALU Input (T5)

Now we need to get R1 onto the bus. But wait — we already latched R1's *old* value? No, let us correct: at T4 we should have placed R1 onto the bus. Let us re-derive correctly.

**Corrected Step 5**: First latched R1 into Y (its old value preserved for the ALU).

$$\text{Bus} \leftarrow \text{R1} \implies \text{Y} \leftarrow \text{Bus}$$

**Step 6**: Then fetched the memory operand into Z.

$$\text{Bus} \leftarrow \text{MDR} \implies \text{Z} \leftarrow \text{Bus}$$

### Step 7 — ALU Computation & Store (T6)

The ALU adds Y and Z. The result must be placed on the bus and then loaded into R1.

$$\text{Bus} \leftarrow \text{Y} + \text{Z} \quad \text{(ALU drives bus)}$$

$$\text{R1} \leftarrow \text{Bus}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Clock Cycles for `ADD R1, X` on Single Bus = 7 cycles** (including the corrected latching of Y and Z). This is a frequently tested KTU numerical.

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix: `ADD R1, R2, R3` (Register-Add)

### 3.2.1 Single Bus Micro-Operations

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycle 1: } & \text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC}, \quad \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC}+1 \\
\text{Cycle 2: } & \text{MDR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]}, \quad \text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MDR} \\
\text{Cycle 3: } & \text{Decode(IR)} \\
\text{Cycle 4: } & \text{Y} \leftarrow \text{R2} \\
\text{Cycle 5: } & \text{Z} \leftarrow \text{R3} \\
\text{Cycle 6: } & \text{R1} \leftarrow \text{Y} + \text{Z} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Total: 6 cycles**

### 3.2.2 Three-Bus Micro-Operations

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycle 1: } & \text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC}, \quad \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC}+1, \quad \text{MDR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]} \\
\text{Cycle 2: } & \text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MDR} \\
\text{Cycle 3: } & \text{R1} \leftarrow \text{R2} + \text{R3} \quad \text{(Bus A} \leftarrow \text{R2, Bus B} \leftarrow \text{R3, ALU computes, Bus C} \leftarrow \text{Result)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Total: 3 cycles** (with internal pipelining, instruction fetch can overlap; net effective ALU time = **1 cycle**)

## 3.3 Speedup Derivation

The **speedup** $S$ of three-bus over single-bus for an ALU operation is:

$$S = \frac{C_{single}}{C_{three\text{-}bus}} = \frac{2k + 1}{2}$$

For $k = 2$ (typical R-type instruction):

$$S = \frac{2(2) + 1}{2} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5\times$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means the three-bus organization is **2.5 times faster** than the single-bus organization for a generic two-operand ALU operation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single Bus Organization — Block Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interconnections** of a single-bus processor. All registers, the ALU, and memory are connected to **one common bus**. The ALU has dedicated input latches (Y, Z) and a separate ALU output path that is gated onto the bus via a tri-state driver.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU[CPU Chip Boundary]
        PC1[Program Counter PC]
        MAR1[MAR]
        MDR1[MDR]
        IR1[IR]
        Yreg[Y Register]
        Zreg[Z Register]
        R0A[General Register R0]
        R1A[General Register R1]
        R2A[General Register R2]
        RnA[General Register Rn]
        ALU1[ALU Unit]
        BUS1[(Common Internal Bus)]
        MUX1[Bus Driver MUX]
    end
    MEM1[(Main Memory)]
    PC1 --> BUS1
    MAR1 --> BUS1
    MDR1 --> BUS1
    IR1 --> BUS1
    Yreg --> BUS1
    Zreg --> BUS1
    R0A --> BUS1
    R1A --> BUS1
    R2A --> BUS1
    RnA --> BUS1
    BUS1 --> Yreg
    BUS1 --> Zreg
    BUS1 --> MAR1
    BUS1 --> MDR1
    BUS1 --> IR1
    BUS1 --> R0A
    BUS1 --> R1A
    BUS1 --> R2A
    BUS1 --> RnA
    Yreg --> ALU1
    Zreg --> ALU1
    ALU1 --> MUX1
    MUX1 --> BUS1
    MAR1 --> MEM1
    MEM1 --> MDR1
```

## 4.2 Three-Bus Organization — Block Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram illustrates the **three-bus topology**. Bus A and Bus B feed the ALU directly. Bus C carries the ALU output. Each register has **two output enables** (one for Bus A, one for Bus B) and **one input enable** (from Bus C). The temporary registers Y and Z are **absent**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU3[Three Bus CPU Core]
        PC3[PC]
        MAR3[MAR]
        MDR3[MDR]
        IR3[IR]
        R0B[R0]
        R1B[R1]
        R2B[R2]
        R3B[R3]
        RnB[Rn]
        BUSA[(Bus A)]
        BUSB[(Bus B)]
        BUSC[(Bus C)]
        ALU3[ALU with Two Inputs and One Output]
    end
    MEM3[(Main Memory)]
    PC3 --output enable A--> BUSA
    PC3 --output enable B--> BUSB
    MAR3 --output enable A--> BUSA
    MDR3 --output enable A--> BUSA
    R0B --out A--> BUSA
    R1B --out A--> BUSA
    R2B --out A--> BUSA
    R3B --out A--> BUSA
    RnB --out A--> BUSA
    R0B --out B--> BUSB
    R1B --out B--> BUSB
    R2B --out B--> BUSB
    R3B --out B--> BUSB
    RnB --out B--> BUSB
    BUSA --input 1--> ALU3
    BUSB --input 2--> ALU3
    ALU3 --drive--> BUSC
    BUSC --latch--> PC3
    BUSC --latch--> MAR3
    BUSC --latch--> MDR3
    BUSC --latch--> R0B
    BUSC --latch--> R1B
    BUSC --latch--> R2B
    BUSC --latch--> R3B
    BUSC --latch--> RnB
    MAR3 --> MEM3
    MEM3 --> MDR3
```

## 4.3 Instruction Execution Control Flow (Generic)

> [!NOTE]
> The following flowchart traces the **decision tree** the control unit follows while executing any instruction on either bus organization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start of Instruction Cycle]) --> B[T1: MAR <- PC and Increment PC]
    B --> C[T2: MDR <- Memory at MAR and IR <- MDR]
    C --> D[T3: Decode Opcode Field of IR]
    D --> E{Instruction Type}
    E -- Memory Reference --> F[T4: MAR <- IR Address Field]
    F --> G[T5: MDR <- M at MAR and Y <- Source Register]
    G --> H[T6: Z <- MDR and ALU computes Y op Z]
    H --> I[T7: Destination Register <- Result on Bus]
    I --> J([End of Instruction])
    E -- Register Register --> K[T4: Y <- Source 1]
    K --> L[T5: Z <- Source 2]
    L --> M[T6: Destination <- Y op Z via ALU]
    M --> J
    E -- Branch or Jump --> N[T4: PC <- Effective Address]
    N --> J
    E -- Other Control --> O[Misc Micro operations]
    O --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between Single Bus and Three Bus internal organization of a processor. List any two advantages of Three Bus organization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Single Bus | Three Bus |
|---|---|---|
| Internal Buses | One common bus | Three buses (A, B, C) |
| Temporary Registers | Y and Z required | Not required |
| ALU Operation Time | Multiple cycles | Single cycle |

**Two Advantages of Three Bus Organization (2 marks):**
1. **Higher Speed**: ALU operations complete in a single clock cycle because both operands can be placed on Bus A and Bus B simultaneously.
2. **No temporary registers needed**: This reduces hardware complexity at the ALU inputs and improves data throughput.
3. (Bonus point) **Parallel data transfer**: Multiple register-to-register transfers can occur in the same cycle, increasing instruction-level parallelism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution**: Table (1 mark) + Two advantages (2 marks). Always present the answer in **tabular form** for easy valuation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Why are temporary registers Y and Z required in a single bus organization? Explain with the help of an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a single bus organization, **only one data transfer can occur per clock cycle** because all registers share the same bus. The ALU requires **two operands simultaneously** to perform a binary operation, but the bus can physically carry only **one operand at a time**.

**Therefore, the temporary registers Y and Z serve as latches** to hold the operands steady at the ALU inputs:
- The first operand is placed on the bus and latched into Y.
- The second operand is placed on the bus in the next cycle and latched into Z.
- The ALU then computes Y + Z (or Y op Z) and drives the result onto the bus.

**Example**: For `R1 ← R2 + R3`, the sequence is: `Y ← R2`, `Z ← R3`, `R1 ← Y + Z`. Without Y and Z, the value of R2 would vanish from the bus before the ALU could use it (1 mark each for: reason + example + explanation).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Module 1 offers an internal choice. The student must answer **either Question A or Question B**. Each question carries 7 + 7 = 14 marks distributed across two sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Draw the block diagram of a single bus organization of a computer and explain the fetch and execute cycle of the instruction `ADD R1, X` (where X is a memory address). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare single bus and three bus organization with respect to number of buses, hardware complexity, execution time, and requirement of temporary registers. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram and Execution of `ADD R1, X` (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the single-bus architecture as in Section 4.1. Label: PC, MAR, MDR, IR, R0..Rn, Y, Z, ALU, Memory, single common bus, control unit.

**Fetch Cycle (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_0: & \quad \text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC}, \quad \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1 \\
T_1: & \quad \text{MDR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]}, \quad \text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MDR} \\
T_2: & \quad \text{Decode IR[opcode]} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Execute Cycle for `ADD R1, X` (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_3: & \quad \text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{IR[address field]} \\
T_4: & \quad \text{MDR} \leftarrow \text{M[MAR]}, \quad \text{Y} \leftarrow \text{R1} \\
T_5: & \quad \text{Z} \leftarrow \text{MDR} \\
T_6: & \quad \text{R1} \leftarrow \text{Y} + \text{Z} \\
\end{aligned}$$

The final R1 contents now equal the old R1 value plus the memory operand at address X.

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1.75 marks per row)**

| Parameter | Single Bus | Three Bus |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Buses** | 1 | 3 (A, B, C) |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Low (fewer wires, simpler MUX) | High (more wires, complex MUX) |
| **Execution Time for R-type** | 3 cycles (Y, Z, then ALU store) | 1 cycle (parallel operand placement) |
| **Temporary Registers** | Y and Z mandatory | Not required |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Speed** | Slower | Faster (~2.5× for typical ALU op) |
| **Control Unit Complexity** | Simple | Complex (must arbitrate 3 buses) |
| **Power Consumption** | Lower | Higher |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the three bus organization of a processor with a neat block diagram. Show the micro-operations required to execute the instruction `R1 ← R2 + R3 × R4`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single bus processor takes 8 clock cycles to complete an instruction. If the same instruction is executed on a three-bus processor, it takes 3 clock cycles. Compute the speedup and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three Bus Organization Block Diagram and Micro-Operations (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the three-bus architecture as in Section 4.2. Label Bus A, Bus B, Bus C. Show ALU fed by Bus A and Bus B, ALU output to Bus C. Show registers R0..Rn connected to all three buses via tri-state drivers.

**Micro-Operations for `R1 ← R2 + R3 × R4` (4 marks):**

Since the three-bus ALU can accept two operands in one cycle, we must **break the expression into two sub-operations** (the ALU has only two inputs):

**Step 1 — Multiply R3 and R4 (assuming ALU supports multiplication):**

$$T_0: \quad \text{Bus A} \leftarrow \text{R3}, \quad \text{Bus B} \leftarrow \text{R4}, \quad \text{Bus C} \leftarrow \text{R3} \times \text{R4} \implies \text{Temp} \leftarrow \text{Bus C}$$

**Step 2 — Add R2 with the partial product (held in a register):**

$$T_1: \quad \text{Bus A} \leftarrow \text{R2}, \quad \text{Bus B} \leftarrow \text{Temp}, \quad \text{Bus C} \leftarrow \text{R2} + (\text{R3} \times \text{R4}) \implies \text{R1} \leftarrow \text{Bus C}$$

**Total ALU cycles = 2.** Add the 2 fetch cycles (MAR ← PC, IR ← MDR) and 1 decode cycle, giving a total of **5 cycles** for the entire instruction.

**(b) Speedup Calculation (7 marks)**

Given:
- Single bus cycles: $C_s = 8$
- Three bus cycles: $C_t = 3$

$$\text{Speedup } S = \frac{C_s}{C_t} = \frac{8}{3} \approx 2.667$$

$$\text{Percentage improvement} = \frac{S - 1}{S} \times 100\% = \frac{2.667 - 1}{2.667} \times 100\% \approx 62.5\%$$

**Comment (2 marks):** The three-bus organization is approximately **2.67 times faster** than the single-bus organization for this instruction. This dramatic speedup arises from **parallel operand fetching** — in a single bus, operands must be latched sequentially into Y and Z, whereas in a three-bus design, both ALU inputs are driven in the same cycle. The trade-off is **higher hardware cost** (more buses, more tri-state drivers, more complex control unit). This is why the three-bus model is preferred in high-performance processors and is the conceptual ancestor of modern multi-port register files.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:
> 1. **Confusing the fetch and execute cycles**: Students often skip the `PC ← PC + 1` step. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the Decode phase**: KTU examiners explicitly check for a decode cycle. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Using the bus for two simultaneous transfers in single-bus**: This is physically impossible and indicates a fundamental misunderstanding. (–2 marks)
> 4. **In three-bus, forgetting that the ALU has only 2 inputs**: An expression like `R1 ← R2 + R3 + R4` requires **2 ALU cycles**, not 1. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Not latching into Y and Z in single bus**: This is the most common omission. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 6. **Miscounting cycles**: Always re-verify with the formula $C_{single} = 2k + 1$ for ALU operations, where $k$ is the number of source operands.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This Before Every KTU Exam**

- [x] **Instruction Cycle Phases**: Fetch Address → Fetch Opcode → Decode → Effective Address → Operand Fetch → Execute → Store.
- [x] **Single Bus Uses Temporary Registers (Y, Z)** — non-negotiable for ALU operations.
- [x] **Three Bus Eliminates Y and Z** — both ALU inputs come directly from Bus A and Bus B.
- [x] **Single Bus Cycle Formula for ALU Op with k operands**: $C = 2k + 1$.
- [x] **Three Bus Cycle Formula for ALU Op**: $C = 2$ (place operands + store result).
- [x] **Speedup Ratio for k = 2 (typical R-type)**: $S = 5/2 = 2.5\times$.
- [x] **PC must be incremented** during the very first fetch cycle — do not forget this in micro-operation traces.
- [x] **Decode phase does NOT use the bus** — it is an internal control unit operation.
- [x] **Three-bus is faster but costlier** — trade-off between speed and hardware complexity.
- [x] **Modern CPUs** use a hybrid multi-bus + register file + pipeline architecture, evolved from these basic concepts.
- [x] **KTU frequently asks** for micro-operation traces of `ADD R1, X` and `R1 ← R2 + R3` — memorize both.
- [x] **Memory Read/Write** is always a 2-step process: `MAR ← Address`, then `MDR ↔ M[MAR]`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inter-Register Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inter-Register Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Inter-Register Transfer** is the fundamental operation by which binary information (data, instructions, addresses, or control words) is moved from one **processor register** to another within the Central Processing Unit (CPU), without altering the bit content of the source register. In the formal language of digital design, this operation is expressed using the **Register Transfer Language (RTL)**, where the symbol `←` denotes the transfer of the contents of the right-hand register into the left-hand register on the next active clock edge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A digital computer system is defined by its registers, the operations performed, and the data paths connecting them. The operations performed on the data stored in registers are called **micro-operations**, and the symbolic notation used to describe these micro-operations and their sequencing is called **Register Transfer Language (RTL)**."*

Mathematically, the transfer is expressed as:

$$R_2 \leftarrow R_1$$

which means: *The contents of register $R_1$ are copied into register $R_2$.* Note that the original contents of $R_1$ **remain unaltered** after the operation (it is a *non-destructive read* of $R_1$).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Office Mailroom"

Imagine a large corporate office with multiple departments (the **registers**):

- **HR Department (R0)**, **Accounts (R1)**, **Sales (R2)**, **Engineering (R3)**.
- Each department maintains its **own files and documents** (the **binary data**).
- The **Internal Mailroom** acts as the **Common Bus System**.
- A **Mail Clerk** acts as the **Multiplexer / Decoder** — picking exactly *one* department's outgoing mail and routing it to the destination department.
- A **Time Card / Stamp** (the **Clock Pulse**) ensures that mail is delivered only at scheduled intervals.

When the clerk places HR's folder into the mailroom bin and presses the "Send to Accounts" button, *the folder reaches Accounts*, **but HR still retains its original copy**. This is precisely an **inter-register transfer**: a controlled, timed, point-to-point (or bus-mediated) movement of data between storage elements, triggered by control signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The inter-register transfer is the *atomic building block* of every complex CPU operation — fetching, decoding, executing, and storing results all boil down to a sequence of these primitive register-to-register moves.

---

## 1.3 Components of a Register Transfer Statement

Every RTL statement consists of three mandatory components:

| Component | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Source Register** | $R_1$ | Holds the data to be transferred (read-only in this op) |
| **Destination Register** | $R_2$ | Receives the data on the active clock edge |
| **Control Function / Condition** | $P: R_2 \leftarrow R_1$ | Guards the transfer; transfer occurs *only if* $P = 1$ |

A conditional transfer is written in KTU-standard RTL as:

$$P: R_2 \leftarrow R_1$$

> [!TIP]
> Always read RTL statements as: *"If control condition **P** is true (logic 1), then on the next clock edge, transfer the contents of $R_1$ into $R_2$."*

---

## 1.4 Why Inter-Register Transfer is Foundational

The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly places this topic in **Module 2** because it underpins:

1. **Instruction Execution Cycles** — Every CPU instruction (ADD, MOV, LOAD) decomposes into register transfers.
2. **Microprogrammed Control Units** — Each microinstruction activates specific register-to-register moves.
3. **Datapath Design** — The physical buses, multiplexers, and tri-state buffers exist *solely* to enable these transfers.
4. **Pipelining & Hazard Resolution** — Forwarding paths and pipeline registers are advanced inter-register transfers.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often confuse **transfer** with **exchange (swap)**. A transfer is *one-way*; the source is unchanged. An exchange $\text{XCHG}$ involves a *simultaneous two-way* move and is a composite micro-operation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Register Transfer System

A complete inter-register transfer hardware system comprises four hierarchical layers:

1. **Register Bank** — A set of $n$-bit storage registers (typically $n = 8, 16, 32, 64$).
2. **Selection Logic** — Multiplexers or decoders that pick *one* source.
3. **Common Bus** — A shared multi-wire channel that carries the selected data.
4. **Control Unit Signals** — Select lines (S), Enable lines (E), and Clock (CLK) that orchestrate the transfer.

---

## 2.2 Register Transfer Language (RTL) — Formal Grammar

The KTU-recognized RTL grammar is built from a small alphabet of symbols:

$$R \leftarrow R \;\Big|\; R \leftarrow M \;\Big|\; M \leftarrow R$$

augmented with **conditional guards**, **arithmetic micro-operations**, **logic micro-operations**, and **shift micro-operations**.

### 2.2.1 Micro-Operation Taxonomy

| Category | RTL Form | Example | Hardware Realization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transfer** | $R_2 \leftarrow R_1$ | `MOV R2, R1` | Wires + clocked flip-flops |
| **Arithmetic** | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$ | `ADD R3, R1, R2` | Binary Adder |
| **Logic** | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 \oplus R_2$ | `XOR R3, R1, R2` | XOR gate array |
| **Shift** | $R_2 \leftarrow \text{shl } R_1$ | `SHL R2, R1` | Barrel shifter |
| **Memory Read** | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | `LOAD` | Data bus + memory |
| **Memory Write** | $M[AR] \leftarrow DR$ | `STORE` | Data bus + memory |

---

## 2.3 The Common Bus System — Two Canonical Designs

The KTU Module 2 syllabus **mandates** study of two methods to construct a common bus from a bank of $n$ registers:

### Design A: **Multiplexer-Based Common Bus**
- One **$n$-to-1 multiplexer** is used per bit of the bus.
- For an $n$-bit bus serving $k$ registers, total MUX size = $k \times 1$ per bit, requiring $\log_2 k$ select lines.

### Design B: **Three-State (Tri-State) Buffer Bus**
- Each register output is gated by a tri-state buffer.
- Only one buffer is *enabled* at a time; others output high-impedance ($Z$).
- Eliminates the MUX entirely; uses $\lceil \log_2 k \rceil$ select lines fed into a decoder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tri-State Logic Equation:**
> $$\text{Bus}_i = E_i \cdot R_i \;\; \text{ where } \;\; E_i = \overline{S \cdot \text{Decoder}_i}$$
> When $E_i = 0$, the buffer enters **high-impedance state (Z)**, electrically disconnecting $R_i$ from the bus.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every Module 2 KTU question paper.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Transfer | $R_2 \leftarrow R_1$ | $n$-bit parallel copy |
| 2 | Conditional Transfer | $P: R_2 \leftarrow R_1$ | $P$ is a Boolean control function |
| 3 | Simultaneous Transfer (Clock-edge aligned) | $R_2 \leftarrow R_1, \; R_1 \leftarrow R_3$ | Both occur on **same** clock edge |
| 4 | Common Bus Width (MUX-based) | $B = n$ bits, requires $k$ MUX of size $k \times 1$ | $k$ = number of registers |
| 5 | Select Lines Required | $S = \lceil \log_2 k \rceil$ | Lines for both MUX and Decoder designs |
| 6 | Tri-State Enable Logic | $E_i = 1 \;\text{iff}\; \text{Selection Code} = i$ | Otherwise output = High-Z |
| 7 | Memory Read | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | $AR$ = Address Reg, $DR$ = Data Reg |
| 8 | Memory Write | $M[AR] \leftarrow DR$ | $M$ = Memory array |
| 9 | Bus Arbitration (daisy-chain) | Priority $\propto 1 / \text{chain position}$ | Used in tri-state DMA buses |
| 10 | Bit-width of Address Bus | $A = \log_2 (\text{Memory Capacity in words})$ | Determines max addressable locations |

> [!WARNING]
> In table rows above, the absolute-value or cardinality notation uses `\vert` and `\lceil` `\rceil` — **never** the raw vertical pipe `|`, which would break Markdown table parsing.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inter-register transfer principles are not academic abstractions; they directly manifest in:

- **CPU Register Files** in ARM, RISC-V, x86 cores — physically implemented as dense arrays of flip-flops with built-in read/write MUXes.
- **GPU Shader Pipelines** — hundreds of ALUs share common data buses to access the register file.
- **FPGA Datapath Synthesis** — RTL is the *direct input* to synthesis tools (Vivado, Quartus) that generate the actual hardware.
- **DSP Processors** — Real-time filter taps are stored in registers and rotated through the ALU via inter-register transfers every clock cycle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of the two canonical KTU Module 2 bus architectures. **No step is skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Multiplexer-Based Common Bus for 4 Registers

### Problem Statement
Design a **4-bit common bus** to transfer data between **four 4-bit registers** $R_0, R_1, R_2, R_3$ using **multiplexers**. Derive the Boolean equations, draw the truth table, and state the size of the MUX required.

### Step 1: Identify Bus Parameters

- Number of registers: $k = 4$
- Bus width (bits per register): $n = 4$

Total number of bits that must be multiplexed: $4 \text{ registers} \times 4 \text{ bits} = 16 \text{ data lines}$, organized as **four 4-to-1 MUXes** (one per bit position).

### Step 2: Compute the Select Line Count

The number of select lines $S$ is the smallest integer such that $2^S \geq k$:

$$2^S \geq 4 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; S = \log_2 4 = 2$$

So we need **2 select lines**, namely $S_1$ (MSB) and $S_0$ (LSB).

### Step 3: Build the Selection Truth Table

| $S_1$ | $S_0$ | Selected Register | MUX Output |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | $R_0$ | $\text{Bus} \leftarrow R_0$ |
| 0 | 1 | $R_1$ | $\text{Bus} \leftarrow R_1$ |
| 1 | 0 | $R_2$ | $\text{Bus} \leftarrow R_2$ |
| 1 | 1 | $R_3$ | $\text{Bus} \leftarrow R_3$ |

### Step 4: Derive the Boolean Output Equation (for bit $i$ of the bus)

For bit position $i$ (where $i \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$), the bus line $\text{Bus}_i$ is given by:

$$\text{Bus}_i = \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0} \cdot R_{0i} \;+\; \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0 \cdot R_{1i} \;+\; S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0} \cdot R_{2i} \;+\; S_1 \cdot S_0 \cdot R_{3i}$$

**Explanation of each term:**

- $\overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0}$: Select code `00` — gates the $i$-th bit of $R_0$ onto the bus.
- $\overline{S_1} \cdot S_0$: Select code `01` — gates the $i$-th bit of $R_1$ onto the bus.
- $S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0}$: Select code `10` — gates the $i$-th bit of $R_2$ onto the bus.
- $S_1 \cdot S_0$: Select code `11` — gates the $i$-th bit of $R_3$ onto the bus.

The summation `+` is the Boolean **OR** operation; the product `·` is **AND**.

### Step 5: Generalize the MUX Size

For $k$ registers of $n$ bits each, the hardware cost is:

$$\text{MUX array} = n \text{ MUXes of size } (k \times 1)$$

$$\text{Total MUX inputs} = n \times k$$

For our problem: $4 \text{ MUXes} \times 4 \text{ inputs} = 16 \text{ MUX data inputs}$ (matches Step 1).

### Step 6: Write the Final RTL Control Statement

The inter-register transfer is specified declaratively in RTL as:

$$S_1 S_0 : \text{Bus} \leftarrow R_x \quad \text{(for the chosen register } x \text{)}$$

For example, selecting $R_2$ onto the bus (and then loading it into $R_0$ on the next clock) is written:

$$S_1 S_0 = 10 : \text{Bus} \leftarrow R_2$$

$$T_0: R_0 \leftarrow \text{Bus}$$

where $T_0$ is a clock-triggered control signal.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Tri-State Buffer Bus for 4 Registers

### Problem Statement
Repeat the design from 3.1 using **tri-state buffers** and a **2-to-4 decoder**.

### Step 1: Understand the Tri-State Buffer Equation

A tri-state buffer has the truth table:

| Enable $E$ | Input $I$ | Output $Y$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | X | High-Impedance (Z) |
| 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 |

Formally:

$$Y = E \cdot I$$

When $E = 0$, the output is electrically **disconnected** (Z-state) — this is what allows multiple buffers to share a common bus without contention.

### Step 2: Decoder Output Equations

A 2-to-4 line decoder produces one-hot outputs based on the select lines:

$$D_0 = \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0}, \quad D_1 = \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0, \quad D_2 = S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0}, \quad D_3 = S_1 \cdot S_0$$

For each register $R_i$, the enable line $E_i$ is wired directly to $D_i$:

$$E_i = D_i = \text{Decoder output for code } i$$

### Step 3: Bus Line Expression (per bit $i$)

For bit $i$ of the bus:

$$\text{Bus}_i = E_0 \cdot R_{0i} + E_1 \cdot R_{1i} + E_2 \cdot R_{2i} + E_3 \cdot R_{3i}$$

Because the decoder is one-hot, **exactly one** $E_i = 1$ at any time; all others are 0, so their buffers output Z and do not affect the bus.

### Step 4: Hardware Count Comparison

| Component | MUX Design | Tri-State Design |
|---|---|---|
| MUX chips | 4 (one per bit) | 0 |
| Tri-state buffers | 0 | 16 (one per bit per register) |
| Decoder | 0 | 1 (2-to-4) |
| OR-gate at bus | Embedded in MUX | Implicit (wired-OR allowed with tri-state) |
| **Total active components** | 4 MUXes | 16 Buffers + 1 Decoder |

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** Tri-state designs use more discrete gates but eliminate the OR-plane and are easier to lay out on VLSI silicon — making them the **industry standard** for wide register files.

### Step 5: Write the Conditional RTL for the Tri-State Bus

$$\text{ENABLE}_i = 1 \text{ when } S_1 S_0 = i \;(\text{binary})$$

$$R_3 \leftarrow R_1 \;\;\text{is realized as:}\;\; S_1 S_0 = 01, \; T_1: R_3 \leftarrow \text{Bus}$$

where $T_1$ is a destination-load control pulse.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Memory Transfer Operations

### Problem Statement
Show the RTL statements and the hardware signals involved in a **Memory READ** and a **Memory WRITE** operation.

### Step 3.3.1 Memory READ

The operation loads the contents of a memory location (pointed to by the Address Register $AR$) into the Data Register $DR$:

$$DR \leftarrow M[AR]$$

**Hardware signals activated (in sequence):**

1. Place the address on the address bus: $AB \leftarrow AR$
2. Assert the Memory Read control line: $\overline{MEMR} = 0$ (active low)
3. Wait for the memory access time $t_{acc}$
4. Latch the data from the data bus into $DR$: $DR \leftarrow DB$

**Combined RTL block:**

$$\overline{MEMR} \cdot T_0 : DR \leftarrow M[AR]$$

### Step 3.3.2 Memory WRITE

The operation stores the contents of $DR$ into the memory location pointed to by $AR$:

$$M[AR] \leftarrow DR$$

**Hardware signals activated (in sequence):**

1. Place the address on the address bus: $AB \leftarrow AR$
2. Place the data on the data bus: $DB \leftarrow DR$
3. Assert the Memory Write control line: $\overline{MEMW} = 0$
4. Memory latches $DB$ into location $M[AR]$ at the trailing edge of $\overline{MEMW}$

**Combined RTL block:**

$$\overline{MEMW} \cdot T_1 : M[AR] \leftarrow DR$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the directional asymmetry of the data bus:** During READ, the bus flows **Memory → CPU**; during WRITE, the bus flows **CPU → Memory**. This is why the data bus uses **bidirectional tri-state buffers** in the bus controller.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Inter-Register Transfer Architecture

The following **Mermaid block diagram** depicts the full hardware architecture for inter-register transfer using a **4-register, 4-bit MUX-based common bus system** with bidirectional memory access.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REG_BANK["4-BIT REGISTER BANK"]
        R0["R0 [3:0]"]
        R1["R1 [3:0]"]
        R2["R2 [3:0]"]
        R3["R3 [3:0]"]
    end

    subgraph MUX_ARRAY["4-TO-1 MUX ARRAY (One per bit)"]
        MUX0["MUX bit-0"]
        MUX1["MUX bit-1"]
        MUX2["MUX bit-2"]
        MUX3["MUX bit-3"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL UNIT"]
        SCTRL["Select Lines S1 S0"]
        DCTRL["Decoder 2-to-4"]
    end

    subgraph BUS["COMMON 4-BIT BUS BUS [3:0]"]
        BUSLINE["Bidirectional Data Highway"]
    end

    subgraph DEST["DESTINATION LOGIC"]
        LD["Load Enable T0 T1 T2 T3"]
        DR["Data Register DR [3:0]"]
        AR["Address Register AR [3:0]"]
    end

    subgraph MEM["MAIN MEMORY"]
        MEMBLK["M 0 to 15"]
    end

    R0 --> MUX0
    R1 --> MUX0
    R2 --> MUX0
    R3 --> MUX0
    R0 --> MUX1
    R1 --> MUX1
    R2 --> MUX1
    R3 --> MUX1
    R0 --> MUX2
    R1 --> MUX2
    R2 --> MUX2
    R3 --> MUX2
    R0 --> MUX3
    R1 --> MUX3
    R2 --> MUX3
    R3 --> MUX3

    SCTRL --> MUX0
    SCTRL --> MUX1
    SCTRL --> MUX2
    SCTRL --> MUX3
    SCTRL --> DCTRL

    DCTRL --> R0
    DCTRL --> R1
    DCTRL --> R2
    DCTRL --> R3

    MUX0 --> BUSLINE
    MUX1 --> BUSLINE
    MUX2 --> BUSLINE
    MUX3 --> BUSLINE

    BUSLINE --> DR
    BUSLINE --> LD
    DR --> AR
    AR --> MEMBLK
    MEMBLK --> DR
```

---

## 4.2 Tri-State Buffer Bus — Detailed Schematic

This diagram shows the **tri-state buffer-based common bus** for the same 4-register system. Note how the decoder replaces the MUXes, and each register's bits are gated by their own tri-state buffers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REG_GRP["REGISTER BANK 4x4 BITS"]
        R0A["R0 bit-0"]
        R0B["R0 bit-1"]
        R0C["R0 bit-2"]
        R0D["R0 bit-3"]
        R1A["R1 bit-0"]
        R1B["R1 bit-1"]
        R1C["R1 bit-2"]
        R1D["R1 bit-3"]
        R2A["R2 bit-0"]
        R2B["R2 bit-1"]
        R2C["R2 bit-2"]
        R2D["R2 bit-3"]
        R3A["R3 bit-0"]
        R3B["R3 bit-1"]
        R3C["R3 bit-2"]
        R3D["R3 bit-3"]
    end

    subgraph BUF_GRP["TRI-STATE BUFFER BANK"]
        B0A["BUF E0 bit-0"]
        B0B["BUF E0 bit-1"]
        B0C["BUF E0 bit-2"]
        B0D["BUF E0 bit-3"]
        B1A["BUF E1 bit-0"]
        B1B["BUF E1 bit-1"]
        B1C["BUF E1 bit-2"]
        B1D["BUF E1 bit-3"]
        B2A["BUF E2 bit-0"]
        B2B["BUF E2 bit-1"]
        B2C["BUF E2 bit-2"]
        B2D["BUF E2 bit-3"]
        B3A["BUF E3 bit-0"]
        B3B["BUF E3 bit-1"]
        B3C["BUF E3 bit-2"]
        B3D["BUF E3 bit-3"]
    end

    subgraph DEC_GRP["2-TO-4 DECODER"]
        DEC["DEC S1 S0"]
        E0["E0"]
        E1["E1"]
        E2["E2"]
        E3["E3"]
    end

    subgraph COMMON_BUS["COMMON BUS 4 BITS"]
        BUS_A["BUS bit-0"]
        BUS_B["BUS bit-1"]
        BUS_C["BUS bit-2"]
        BUS_D["BUS bit-3"]
    end

    R0A --> B0A --> BUS_A
    R0B --> B0B --> BUS_B
    R0C --> B0C --> BUS_C
    R0D --> B0D --> BUS_D

    R1A --> B1A --> BUS_A
    R1B --> B1B --> BUS_B
    R1C --> B1C --> BUS_C
    R1D --> B1D --> BUS_D

    R2A --> B2A --> BUS_A
    R2B --> B2B --> BUS_B
    R2C --> B2C --> BUS_C
    R2D --> B2D --> BUS_D

    R3A --> B3A --> BUS_A
    R3B --> B3B --> BUS_B
    R3C --> B3C --> BUS_C
    R3D --> B3D --> BUS_D

    DEC --> E0 --> B0A
    DEC --> E0 --> B0B
    DEC --> E0 --> B0C
    DEC --> E0 --> B0D
    DEC --> E1 --> B1A
    DEC --> E1 --> B1B
    DEC --> E1 --> B1C
    DEC --> E1 --> B1D
    DEC --> E2 --> B2A
    DEC --> E2 --> B2B
    DEC --> E2 --> B2C
    DEC --> E2 --> B2D
    DEC --> E3 --> B3A
    DEC --> E3 --> B3B
    DEC --> E3 --> B3C
    DEC --> E3 --> B3D
```

---

## 4.3 RTL Operation Flowchart — Conditional Transfer Decision

This flowchart captures the **decision logic** a control unit executes every clock cycle to handle conditional register transfers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Clock Edge Rising"] --> CHK{"Check Control Function P"}
    CHK -- "P = 0" --> NOOP["NOOP - Hold Destination Register"]
    CHK -- "P = 1" --> SRC["Identify Source Register Rs"]
    SRC --> SEL["Activate Select Lines S1 S0"]
    SEL --> BUS_STAGE["Place Rs Onto Common Bus"]
    BUS_STAGE --> LD_CHK{"Check Load Enable Tj"}
    LD_CHK -- "Tj = 0" --> NOOP
    LD_CHK -- "Tj = 1" --> LATCH["Latch Bus into Destination Rd"]
    LATCH --> DONE["Transfer Complete - Update Flags"]
    NOOP --> DONE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the flowchart:** The two-stage handshake (`P` guard → `Tj` load enable) is **exactly the KTU-exam-pleasing pattern** for conditionally writing data. Students should annotate this in design problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 🎯 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Register Transfer Language (RTL). List any four symbols used in RTL with their meanings.**

**Model Answer:**

**Register Transfer Language (RTL)** is a symbolic notation used to describe the micro-operations, data transfers, and control logic of a digital computer at the register level. It provides a concise, hardware-oriented description of how information moves between registers, memory, and the ALU.

**Four RTL Symbols:**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\leftarrow$ | Transfer of data (right to left) |
| $,$ (comma) | Simultaneous operations on the same clock edge |
| $:$ (colon) | Conditional control function guard |
| $M$ | Denotes a Memory location (array) |

*(3 marks — full distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for symbol table)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between a tri-state buffer and a multiplexer. Why are tri-state buffers preferred for wide common buses?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Multiplexer | Tri-State Buffer |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Selects one of many inputs | Either passes input or sets output to high-Z |
| Output States | Always 0 or 1 | 0, 1, or High-Impedance (Z) |
| Bus Sharing | Achieved via OR-ing MUX outputs | Achieved via wire-OR of enabled buffers |
| Cost (Wide Bus) | One large MUX per bit | One small buffer per register-bit |

**Why preferred for wide buses:** Tri-state buffers allow multiple register outputs to share a *single physical wire* without an OR-gate, drastically reducing routing congestion on the chip.
