The total time a CPU spends executing a program is a function of three parameters: the number of instructions, the average cycles per instruction, and the clock period.

**Step 1: Define the basic relationship.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= (\text{Number of Clock Cycles}) \times (\text{Duration of one Clock Cycle})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Express the number of clock cycles.**

The number of clock cycles equals the total number of instructions multiplied by the average cycles per instruction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Clock Cycles} &= IC \times CPI
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $IC$ = Instruction Count (total instructions in program)
- $CPI$ = Cycles Per Instruction (average)

**Step 3: Substitute back into Step 1.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Express clock period in terms of frequency.**

Since frequency $f$ is the reciprocal of period $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clock} &= \dfrac{1}{f_{clock}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final consolidated formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f_{clock}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** To reduce CPU execution time, you can either (a) reduce the instruction count via better compilation, (b) reduce CPI via better architecture (pipelining), or (c) increase clock frequency. Increasing frequency is the most power-expensive option.

### 3.2 Derivation: Average CPI Calculation

For a program with multiple instruction types, each with its own CPI:

**Step 1: Define the count of each instruction class.**

Let $IC_i$ be the number of instructions of class $i$, and $CPI_i$ be the cycles per instruction for that class.

**Step 2: Total cycles is the sum across all classes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cycles} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total instructions is the sum of all classes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IC_{total} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Average CPI is total cycles divided by total instructions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example: CPU Performance Comparison

**Problem (KTU Typical):** Consider two processors P1 and P2.

- **P1:** Clock rate = **3 GHz**, CPI = **1.5**
- **P2:** Clock rate = **2.5 GHz**, CPI = **1.0**

The program has **$10^9$** instructions. Find which processor is faster and by how much.

**Step 1: Compute CPU time for P1.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{P1} &= \dfrac{IC \times CPI_1}{f_1} = \dfrac{10^9 \times 1.5}{3 \times 10^9}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{P1} &= 0.5 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute CPU time for P2.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{P2} &= \dfrac{IC \times CPI_2}{f_2} = \dfrac{10^9 \times 1.0}{2.5 \times 10^9}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{P2} &= 0.4 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute speedup of P2 over P1.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup} &= \dfrac{T_{P1}}{T_{P2}} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.4} = 1.25
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** P2 is **1.25× faster** than P1, despite having a lower clock rate, because its lower CPI outweighs the frequency difference. This is a classic KTU exam result demonstrating that **CPI matters more than raw clock speed**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Control

| Parameter | Hardwired CU | Microprogrammed CU |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation** | Combinational logic gates | Microinstructions in control memory |
| **Speed** | Very Fast (direct logic) | Slower (memory access overhead) |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid (hardware redesign needed) | Highly flexible (modify microprogram) |
| **Cost** | High for complex instructions | Lower for complex instruction sets |
| **Best Suited For** | RISC processors | CISC processors |
| **Examples** | MIPS, ARM (typically) | Intel x86 microcode |
| **Control Memory Size** | Not applicable | $2^k$ words, where $k$ = control address bits |
| **Microinstruction Size** | Not applicable | Typically **32 to 64 bits** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *Hardwired = Hardware = Hard to change* ; *Microprogrammed = Memory stored = Modifiable*. The "M" and "H" letters hint at the trade-off.

### 3.5 Microinstruction Encoding Methods

#### A. Horizontal Microinstruction
- Each bit in the control field directly controls **one** control signal.
- Long microinstruction word (e.g., **48+ bits**).
- Maximum parallelism; minimum encoding logic.

#### B. Vertical Microinstruction
- A decoder interprets compact function codes.
- Short word (e.g., **8 to 16 bits**).
- Minimal parallelism; more complex decoding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K-Ternary classification:** Some textbooks also recognize **Diagonal Microinstruction** as a hybrid between horizontal and vertical.

### 3.6 Derivation: Effective Address for Addressing Modes

For memory reference instructions, the **Effective Address (EA)** is computed differently for each mode. The table below summarizes all KTU-syllabus addressing modes:

| Mode | EA Formula | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Direct | $EA = \text{Address field of instruction}$ | `LDA 2000` |
| Indirect | $EA = M[\text{Address field}]$ | `LDA @2000` |
| Register | $EA = R$ | `ADD R1` |
| Register Indirect | $EA = M[R]$ | `ADD (R1)` |
| Displacement | $EA = R + \text{Offset}$ | `ADD 100(R1)` |
| Immediate | Operand = `Address field` | `ADD #5` |
| Relative | $EA = PC + \text{Offset}$ | Branch instructions |
| Indexed | $EA = \text{Base} + \text{Index Reg}$ | `ADD 100(XR)` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** Computing the effective address of an instruction given the PC value, the addressing mode, and the offset value. Always show all arithmetic steps in base 16 (hex) or base 2 (binary) as required.

### 3.7 Microprogrammed Control Unit — Next Address Determination

The next microinstruction address is determined by:
1. **Sequential:** Next address = current address + 1 (default).
2. **Conditional Branch:** Based on a status flag (e.g., **Z, N, C**).
3. **Unconditional Jump:** To a subroutine or interrupt handler.
4. **Mapping:** Machine instruction opcode mapped to starting address of its micro-routine.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Next Address} = \begin{cases} CA + 1 & \text{if no branch} \\ \text{Target} & \text{if branch condition is true} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $CA$ = Current Address.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of a Generic Processor Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Program Counter PC] --> B[Memory Address Register MAR]
    B --> C[Main Memory]
    C --> D[Memory Data Register MDR]
    D --> E[Instruction Register IR]
    E --> F[Control Unit CU]
    F --> G[ALU]
    H[Register File] --> G
    G --> H
    F --> H
    F --> A
    I[Status Flags Z N C V] --> F
    G --> I
    C --> D
    D -.-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the **data path** (data flow). Dotted arrows show **control signals** (status flags feedback). The PC feeds the MAR to fetch the next instruction; the MDR receives it from memory; the IR holds it for decoding by the CU.

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of Three-Bus Data Path CPU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Register R1] -->|Bus A| MUXA[Mux A]
    R2[Register R2] -->|Bus B| MUXB[Mux B]
    R3[Register R3] -->|Bus C| MUXB
    R4[Register R4] -->|Bus A| MUXA
    MUXA --> ALUA[ALU Input A]
    MUXB --> ALUB[ALU Input B]
    ALUA --> ALUCORE[ALU Core]
    ALUB --> ALUCORE
    ALUCORE --> OUTBUS[Output Bus Y]
    OUTBUS --> R1
    OUTBUS --> R2
    OUTBUS --> R3
    OUTBUS --> R4
    OUTBUS --> MAR[Memory Address Register]
    CTRL[Control Unit] -.-> MUXA
    CTRL -.-> MUXB
    CTRL -.-> ALUCORE
    CTRL -.-> R1
    CTRL -.-> R2
    CTRL -.-> R3
    CTRL -.-> R4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **three-bus design** allows **two source operands and one destination** to be selected in a single clock cycle. This directly enables the **three-address instruction format** like `ADD R1, R2, R3` in a single cycle.

### 4.3 Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Control Unit — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Control Unit Design Decision] --> Q1{Instruction Set Complexity?}
    Q1 -->|Simple, Fixed, RISC| HCU[Hardwired Control Unit]
    Q1 -->|Complex, Evolving, CISC| MCU[Microprogrammed Control Unit]
    HCU --> H1[Combinational Logic Gates]
    H1 --> H2[Direct Control Signal Generation]
    H2 --> H3[Fast Execution No Memory Access]
    MCU --> M1[Control Memory CM with Microinstructions]
    M1 --> M2[Microinstruction Sequencer]
    M2 --> M3[Microprogram Counter microPC]
    M3 --> M4[Microinstruction Register MIR]
    M4 --> M5[Decoded Signals to Data Path]
    H3 --> RESULT[Control Signals to Data Path]
    M5 --> RESULT
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Control Memory (CM)** in a microprogrammed CU is typically a **Read-Only Memory (ROM)** because the microprogram is rarely changed after design.

### 4.4 Microprogrammed Control Unit — Detailed Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Instruction Register IR] --> B[Mapping Logic]
    B --> C[Starting Address of Micro Routine]
    C --> D[Micro Address Generator]
    E[Status Flags Z N C V] --> D
    D --> F[Control Memory CM]
    F --> G[Microinstruction Register MIR]
    G --> H[Control Field]
    G --> I[Condition Field]
    G --> J[Address Field]
    H --> K[Data Path Control Signals]
    I --> D
    J --> D
    F -->|Next Microinstruction| D
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** The **Condition Field** is what makes the microprogrammed CU *programmable*. Without it, execution would be strictly sequential. The condition field allows branching based on ALU status flags.

### 4.5 CPU Instruction Execution Cycle — State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Fetch
    Fetch --> Decode : Instruction in IR
    Decode --> OperandFetch : If memory operands needed
    OperandFetch --> Execute : Operands in Registers
    Decode --> Execute : If register operands
    Execute --> Store : Result computed
    Store --> InterruptCheck : Result written back
    InterruptCheck --> Fetch : No interrupt
    InterruptCheck --> HandleInterrupt : Interrupt pending
    HandleInterrupt --> Fetch : ISR complete
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** The classic **Fetch-Decode-Execute** cycle is the foundation of all CPU operations. Modern CPUs add **Operand Fetch** and **Store/Write-Back** as separate stages. Pipelining breaks these stages into overlapping sub-operations.

### 4.6 Stack Organization and Expression Evaluation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Initialize Empty Stack SP at TOP] --> B[Push A onto Stack]
    B --> C[Push B onto Stack]
    C --> D[ADD operation Pop B Pop A Push A PLUS B]
    D --> E[Push C onto Stack]
    E --> F[Push D onto Stack]
    F --> G[ADD operation Pop D Pop C Push C PLUS D]
    G --> H[MUL operation Pop result2 Pop result1 Push result1 TIMES result2]
    H --> I[POP final result into E]
    I --> J[Expression E equals A PLUS B TIMES C PLUS D evaluated]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** For expression $(A+B) \times (C+D)$, the postfix (Reverse Polish) form is `A B + C D + ×`. The stack machine requires **8 instructions** (7 PUSH/ADD/MUL + 1 POP), illustrating the **zero-address format**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] Define the Processor Unit. List its three main components and state the function of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **Processor Unit (PU)** is the component of a computer that interprets program instructions and processes data. It is the **brain of the computer system** responsible for fetching, decoding, and executing instructions.
>
> **Three main components:**
>
> 1. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — Performs arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, comparison).
> 2. **Control Unit (CU)** — Directs and coordinates all CPU activities. It fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and generates control signals to execute them.
> 3. **Register File** — A set of high-speed storage locations inside the CPU used to hold operands, intermediate results, and addresses during instruction execution.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] Compare the Hardwired Control Unit and Microprogrammed Control Unit based on speed, flexibility, and implementation.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Parameter | Hardwired CU | Microprogrammed CU |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Speed** | Faster — direct combinational logic generates control signals without memory access. | Slower — requires fetching microinstructions from control memory, adding access overhead. |
> | **Flexibility** | Inflexible — any change in instruction set requires hardware redesign and re-fabrication. | Highly flexible — instruction set changes can be implemented by modifying the microprogram in control memory. |
> | **Implementation** | Built using fixed logic gates, decoders, encoders, and flip-flops. | Implemented using a Control Memory (ROM) holding microinstructions, accessed by a microsequencer. |

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal organization of a **single-bus CPU** and discuss how data transfer between registers takes place. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Evaluate the arithmetic expression $E = (A - B) \times (C + D)$ using a **three-address, two-address, one-address (accumulator), and zero-address (stack)** instruction format. Show the sequence of instructions for each format. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **(a) Single-Bus CPU Organization (7 Marks):**
>
> In a single-bus CPU, all general-purpose registers are connected to a **common internal bus**. The ALU takes its two inputs from the bus via multiplexers. Because there is only one bus, only **one register can place data on the bus at a time**, and a data transfer between two registers requires a temporary register (Y or Z) to hold intermediate data.
>
> **Block Diagram:**
>
> ```
>  Registers:  R0   R1   R2   R3   R4   R5   R6   R7
>               |    |    |    |    |    |    |    |
>               +----+----+----+----+----+----+----+
>                              |
>                          [INTERNAL BUS]
>                              |
>                          +---+---+
>                          |  MUX  |
>                          +---+---+
>                              |
>                          Y (temp) ----+
>                                          |
>                          +-------+      |
>                          |  ALU  |<-----+
>                          +---+---+
>                              |
>                           Z (temp)
>                              |
>                        [Output Bus]
> ```
>
> **Data Transfer Example:** To perform R3 ← R5
>
> **Step 1:** Place contents of R5 on the bus → `BUS ← R5`
> **Step 2:** Load R3 from the bus → `R3 ← BUS`
>
> This requires **2 micro-operations**. To perform R3 ← R5 + R6:
>
> **Step 1:** `Y ← R5` (place R5 in temp Y)
> **Step 2:** `Z ← R6` (place R6 in temp Z)
> **Step 3:** `ALU ← Y + Z` (perform addition)
> **Step 4:** `R3 ← ALU output` (store result)
>
> This requires **4 micro-operations** and shows the **limitation** of single-bus design.
>
> **(b) Expression Evaluation in Four Formats (7 Marks):**
>
> **Given:** $E = (A - B) \times (C + D)$
>
> **Three-Address Format:**
> - `SUB R1, A, B`     → R1 ← A - B
> - `ADD R2, C, D`     → R2 ← C + D
> - `MUL E, R1, R2`    → E ← R1 × R2
>
> **Total instructions = 3**, Total code words = 3
>
> **Two-Address Format:**
> - `MOV R1, A`        → R1 ← A
> - `SUB R1, B`        → R1 ← R1 - B
> - `MOV R2, C`        → R2 ← C
> - `ADD R2, D`        → R2 ← R2 + D
> - `MUL R1, R2`       → R1 ← R1 × R2
> - `MOV E, R1`        → E ← R1
>
> **Total instructions = 6**, Total code words = 6
>
> **One-Address (Accumulator) Format:**
> - `LDA A`            → AC ← A
> - `SUB B`            → AC ← AC - B
> - `STA T`            → T ← AC
> - `LDA C`            → AC ← C
> - `ADD D`            → AC ← AC + D
> - `MUL T`            → AC ← AC × T
> - `STA E`            → E ← AC
>
> **Total instructions = 7**, Total code words = 7
>
> **Zero-Address (Stack) Format:**
> - `PUSH A`
> - `PUSH B`
> - `SUB`              → TOS ← (A - B)
> - `PUSH C`
> - `PUSH D`
> - `ADD`              → TOS ← (C + D)
> - `MUL`              → TOS ← ((A - B) × (C + D))
> - `POP E`
>
> **Total instructions = 8**, Total code words = 8

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the **microprogrammed control unit** with a neat diagram. Describe how the next microinstruction address is determined. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A program has **40% ALU instructions** (CPI = 1), **30% load/store instructions** (CPI = 5), and **30% branch instructions** (CPI = 2). The clock rate is **2 GHz**. Calculate: (i) Average CPI, (ii) CPU execution time for $10^9$ instructions, (iii) MIPS rating. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **(a) Microprogrammed Control Unit (7 Marks):**
>
> A **microprogrammed control unit** implements the control logic of the CPU by storing the control signals as **binary words called microinstructions** in a special memory called the **Control Memory (CM)**. Each machine instruction triggers a sequence of microinstructions (a *micro-routine*) from CM. This approach was pioneered by **Maurice Wilkes** in 1951.
>
> **Block Diagram:**
>
> ```
> [IR] --> [Mapping Logic] --> [Starting Address]
>                                       |
>                              [Micro Address Generator]
>                                       |
>                                [Control Memory CM]
>                                       |
>                                [MIR] ---> [Control Field] ---> Control Signals
>                                       |
>                                  [Condition Field] ---> [Next Address Logic]
>                                       |
>                                  [Address Field] ---> [Next Address]
> ```
>
> **Next Address Determination Methods:**
>
> 1. **Sequential:** When no branch is needed, the next address is the **current address + 1**. The microsequencer increments the microprogram counter (μPC) automatically.
> 2. **Conditional Branching:** The condition field tests a status flag (Z, N, C, V) from the ALU. If the condition is true, the next address is loaded from the address field of the current microinstruction. If false, sequential incrementing continues.
> 3. **Unconditional Jump:** Used for subroutine calls, interrupt handling, and instruction fetch routines. Forces a specific next address regardless of flags.
> 4. **Mapping from Machine Instruction:** When a new machine instruction is loaded into the IR, the mapping logic converts its opcode into the **starting address** of the corresponding micro-routine in CM.
>
> **Microinstruction Format:** A typical microinstruction has three fields:
> - **Control Field** (e.g., 20 bits) — directly controls data path signals
> - **Condition Field** (e.g., 4 bits) — selects which flag to test
> - **Address Field** (e.g., 8 bits) — supplies branch target address
>
> **(b) Performance Calculation (7 Marks):**
>
> **Given:**
> - ALU: 40% of IC, CPI = 1
> - Load/Store: 30% of IC, CPI = 5
> - Branch: 30% of IC, CPI = 2
> - Clock rate: $f = 2 \text{ GHz} = 2 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}$
> - Total IC: $10^9$
>
> **Step 1: Compute the weighted CPI.**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> CPI_{avg} &= (0.40 \times 1) + (0.30 \times 5) + (0.30 \times 2) \\
> CPI_{avg} &= 0.40 + 1.50 + 0.60 \\
> CPI_{avg} &= 2.50
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 2: CPU Execution Time.**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> T_{CPU} &= \dfrac{IC \times CPI_{avg}}{f} \\
> T_{CPU} &= \dfrac{10^9 \times 2.50}{2 \times 10^9} \\
> T_{CPU} &= 1.25 \text{ seconds}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 3: MIPS Rating.**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> MIPS &= \dfrac{f}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^6} \\
> MIPS &= \dfrac{2 \times 10^9}{2.50 \times 10^6} \\
> MIPS &= 800 \text{ MIPS}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Final Answer:** $CPI_{avg} = 2.50$, $T_{CPU} = 1.25 \text{ s}$, $\text{MIPS} = 800$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **MIPS formula confusion:** Some students write MIPS = $f$ / $(CPI \times 10^6)$ incorrectly. Remember the $10^6$ factor converts to millions. A frequent error: writing MIPS = $f$ / $CPI$ without the $10^6$, which gives a huge meaningless number.
> 2. **CPU time units:** Always show the answer in seconds. If you get a negative exponent, double-check whether you divided or multiplied by $10^9$.
> 3. **Microprogrammed CU:** Students often forget to mention the **mapping logic** and **microsequencer** in the diagram. Loss of 2 marks if both are missing.
> 4. **Three-bus vs Single-bus:** Do not confuse the two in the diagram. A three-bus data path needs **two multiplexers** and **three bus connections** to the registers.
> 5. **Stack evaluation:** For zero-address format, the postfix conversion must be correct. For $E = (A - B) \times (C + D)$, the postfix is `A B - C D + ×`, **not** `A B C D - + ×`.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CPU = ALU + CU + Register File** — these three components are the absolute foundation of every processor design question.
- **CPU Execution Time** = $IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ — memorize this formula; it appears in nearly every KTU performance question.
- **CPI matters more than clock speed** — a lower CPI can outperform a higher frequency, as proven in the P1 vs P2 example.
- **Three-address format** produces the most compact code (fewest instructions) but each instruction is large.
- **Zero-address (stack) format** produces the most code but each instruction is tiny.
- **Hardwired CU** = fast + rigid → best for **RISC**.
- **Microprogrammed CU** = slower + flexible → best for **CISC**.
- **Microinstruction word** has three fields: **Control**, **Condition**, **Address**.
- **Next address determination** uses sequential, conditional, unconditional, or mapping methods.
- **Effective Address (EA)** computation depends on the addressing mode — Direct, Indirect, Register, Indexed, etc.
- **MIPS = $f / (CPI \times 10^6)$** — never forget the $10^6$ factor.
- **Single-bus CPU** requires 2 micro-operations for register-to-register transfer; **three-bus** requires only 1.
- **Fetch-Decode-Execute-Store** is the canonical instruction cycle — add **Interrupt Check** for completeness.
- **Mapping logic** converts machine instruction opcode → starting address in Control Memory.
- **Status flags** (Z, N, C, V) provide feedback to the Control Unit for conditional branching.
- **Horizontal microinstructions** = one bit per control signal (fast, wide word).
- **Vertical microinstructions** = encoded fields decoded by logic (slow, narrow word).
- **Amdahl's Law** is the formula for parallel speedup: $S = 1 / ((1-f) + f/k)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Accumulator (Basic Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Accumulator (Basic Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition
An **accumulator** is a special-purpose register inside the Central Processing Unit (CPU) that serves as the **implicit source and destination** for arithmetic and logic operations. In digital circuit design, the **accumulator cell** is the fundamental 1-bit building block — a combination of a storage element (D flip-flop), an arithmetic element (full adder), and a data-selection element (multiplexer) — which can be cascaded to form an **n-bit accumulator register**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, the accumulator is positioned as the **computational heart** of the CPU data path, around which register-transfer operations are designed.

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Running Total" Counter
Imagine a **cashier's billing machine**:

| Cashier Action | Accumulator Equivalent |
|----------------|------------------------|
| Stores running total of all items | Holds current operand/result |
| Adds new item price to running total | `Q ← Q + A` (Add operation) |
| Clears total and starts fresh | `Q ← 0` (Direct load with 0) |
| Subtracts discount | `Q ← Q + Ā + 1` (2's complement subtract) |
| Displays total on screen | `Y ← Q · OUT` (Output enable) |

Just as the cashier's machine holds, updates, and displays the running sum, the accumulator holds, modifies, and outputs the current computational state of the CPU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> *"The accumulator is a register that stores the result of arithmetic-logic operations and provides one of the operands for the next operation. It is the central register of the CPU's data path, designed using a flip-flop, adder, and selection logic."*

## Key Engineering Metrics & Standards
- **Storage unit**: 1 D flip-flop per bit (rising/falling edge-triggered)
- **Combinational delay**: Full adder propagation delay (~ **2 gate delays** for sum, **2 gate delays** for carry)
- **Control encoding**: One-hot encoding (only 1 control signal HIGH at a time)
- **Cascade width**: 4-bit, 8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit accumulators are standard in KTU problems

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Accumulator"?** The name comes from its function — values *accumulate* (add up) over successive operations, just like a running total in a calculator.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a 1-Bit Accumulator Cell
A basic 1-bit accumulator cell is composed of **four logical building blocks**:

1. **D Flip-Flop** — Stores 1 bit of data (the cell's memory)
2. **Full Adder (FA)** — Performs 1-bit binary addition of input with stored value
3. **NOT Gate (Inverter)** — Generates 1's complement of external input
4. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — Selects one of multiple data sources based on control signals
5. **Output AND Gate** — Provides tri-state-like output control

## 2.2 Control Signal Architecture
The standard KTU textbook design uses **5 control inputs** (one-hot encoded):

| Control Signal | Symbol | Function |
|----------------|--------|----------|
| Direct Load | $IN_{DIRECT}$ | $Q \leftarrow A$ (load external data) |
| Load Complement | $IN_{COM}$ | $Q \leftarrow \bar{A}$ (load 1's complement) |
| Add | $IN_{ADD}$ | $Q \leftarrow Q + A + C_{in}$ (accumulate) |
| Shift Right | $IN_{DR}$ | $Q \leftarrow SR_{in}$ (bit from left neighbor) |
| Shift Left | $IN_{DL}$ | $Q \leftarrow SL_{in}$ (bit from right neighbor) |
| Output Enable | $OUT$ | Enables cell output to bus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Hot Encoding Rule:** Exactly **one** control signal is HIGH at any given time. If all are LOW → the cell **holds** its previous value (memory state).

## 2.3 Operational Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Step

**Step 1: Data sources are generated**
- $A_i$ (external input, used for direct load and as adder operand)
- $\bar{A_i}$ (generated by NOT gate, used for complement load and 2's complement)
- $S_i = A_i \oplus Q_i \oplus C_{in}$ (full-adder sum, used for add operation)
- $SR_{in}, SL_{in}$ (shifted bits from neighboring cells)

**Step 2: Each data source is "gated" by its control signal**
The AND gate between each source and the MUX acts as an enable switch.

**Step 3: OR-tree combines all gated sources into a single D input**
This implements the multiplexer function via AND-OR logic (or a true MUX IC).

**Step 4: D flip-flop captures D on the clock edge**
The flip-flop makes the operation **synchronous** — all cells update simultaneously.

**Step 5: Output gate releases data to the external bus only when $OUT = 1$**
This prevents bus contention when multiple registers share the data bus.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Component | Boolean Equation | Engineering Purpose |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Full Adder Sum** | $S_i = A_i \oplus B_i \oplus C_{in}$ | Generates 1-bit sum |
| **Full Adder Carry** | $C_{out} = A_i B_i + (A_i \oplus B_i) C_{in}$ | Propagates carry to next cell |
| **D Flip-Flop Next State** | $Q_i(t+1) = D_i$ on clock edge | Stores 1 bit of result |
| **MUX Output (D Input)** | $D_i = \sum_{k} (\text{Control}_k \cdot \text{Source}_k)$ | Selects data source |
| **Output Enable** | $Y_i = Q_i \cdot OUT$ | Tri-state bus control |
| **2's Complement Subtract** | $A - B = A + \bar{B} + 1$ | Sets $C_{in} = 1$ for subtraction |
| **Cascade Carry Rule** | $C_{out}(i) \to C_{in}(i+1)$ | Connects cells in ripple-carry |
| **One-Hot Constraint** | $\sum_{k} \text{Control}_k \leq 1$ | Only one operation active |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
The accumulator design is the **foundation of every CPU data path**:
- **Intel 8086**: Uses 16-bit accumulator (`AX` register) for arithmetic
- **ARM Cortex-M**: Has accumulator-style `R0` register for fast operations
- **DSP processors**: Use accumulator registers (e.g., TI TMS320's 40-bit `ACC`) for MAC operations
- **FPGA designs**: Accumulator cells are primitive blocks in Xilinx/Intel FPGA fabrics
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the D-Input Boolean Equation

### Step 1 — Identify the Data Sources
For a 1-bit accumulator cell $i$, the data that can be loaded into the flip-flop comes from **five possible sources**:

- Source 1: External input $A_i$ (for **Direct Load**)
- Source 2: Complement $\bar{A_i}$ (for **Load Complement**)
- Source 3: Adder sum $A_i \oplus Q_i \oplus C_{in}$ (for **Add**)
- Source 4: Shift-right input $SR_{in}$ (for **Shift Right**)
- Source 5: Shift-left input $SL_{in}$ (for **Shift Left**)

### Step 2 — Define Control Variables
Let each data source be gated by a unique control variable:

- $c_1 = IN_{DIRECT}$
- $c_2 = IN_{COM}$
- $c_3 = IN_{ADD}$
- $c_4 = IN_{DR}$
- $c_5 = IN_{DL}$

### Step 3 — AND Each Source With Its Control
Each enabled data path is implemented as an AND gate:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Path}_1 &= A_i \cdot c_1 \\
\text{Path}_2 &= \bar{A_i} \cdot c_2 \\
\text{Path}_3 &= (A_i \oplus Q_i \oplus C_{in}) \cdot c_3 \\
\text{Path}_4 &= SR_{in} \cdot c_4 \\
\text{Path}_5 &= SL_{in} \cdot c_5
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — OR All Paths Into a Single D Input
The output of all AND gates is combined via an OR tree (this is the multiplexer equivalent):

$$D_i = A_i \cdot c_1 + \bar{A_i} \cdot c_2 + (A_i \oplus Q_i \oplus C_{in}) \cdot c_3 + SR_{in} \cdot c_4 + SL_{in} \cdot c_5$$

### Step 5 — Apply the One-Hot Constraint
Since only one control signal is HIGH at a time:

$$c_1 \cdot c_2 = c_1 \cdot c_3 = \ldots = 0$$

This guarantees the OR tree produces exactly one selected source at the D input.

### Step 6 — Write the Flip-Flop Storage Equation
The D flip-flop captures the D input on the active clock edge:

$$Q_i(t+1) = D_i(t)$$

### Step 7 — Define the Output Equation
The cell output is enabled by the $OUT$ signal:

$$Y_i = Q_i \cdot OUT$$

## 3.2 Complete Operation Truth Table (1-Bit Cell)

| $IN_{DIRECT}$ | $IN_{COM}$ | $IN_{ADD}$ | $IN_{DR}$ | $IN_{DL}$ | $D_i$ Value | $Q_i(t+1)$ | Operation |
|:-------------:|:----------:|:----------:|:---------:|:---------:|:------------|:-----------|:----------|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $A_i$ | $A_i$ | **Direct Load** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $\bar{A_i}$ | $\bar{A_i}$ | **Load Complement** |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $A_i \oplus Q_i \oplus C_{in}$ | $Q_i + A_i + C_{in}$ | **Add** |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $SR_{in}$ | $SR_{in}$ | **Shift Right** |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | $SL_{in}$ | $SL_{in}$ | **Shift Left** |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 (or floating) | $Q_i$ | **Hold** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does "Hold" work?** When all control signals are LOW, every AND gate outputs 0, so $D_i = 0$. However, in practice, a feedback path from $Q_i$ is added to maintain the hold state. This is a critical design detail examiners look for.

## 3.3 Derivation of 2's Complement Subtraction

To perform $AC \leftarrow AC - B$ using the accumulator:

**Step 1:** Apply $IN_{COM}$ for one clock cycle
$$Q_i \leftarrow \bar{B_i}$$

**Step 2:** Apply $IN_{ADD}$ with $C_{in} = 1$ for the next clock cycle
$$Q_i \leftarrow Q_i + A_i + 1 = \bar{B_i} + A_i + 1 = A_i - B_i$$

**Step 3:** The two cycles together implement $AC \leftarrow AC - B$.

This is the **fundamental trick** of binary subtraction using only an adder — a KTU favorite question.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cascading n Cells

For a 4-bit accumulator (cells 0, 1, 2, 3 from LSB to MSB):

| Connection | From → To | Signal |
|------------|-----------|--------|
| Carry propagation | Cell $i$ $C_{out}$ → Cell $i+1$ $C_{in}$ | Ripple carry chain |
| Shift Right | Cell $i+1$ $Q$ → Cell $i$ $SR_{in}$ | Data flows MSB → LSB |
| Shift Left | Cell $i-1$ $Q$ → Cell $i$ $SL_{in}$ | Data flows LSB → MSB |
| Control Broadcast | Control unit → All cells | Common control bus |
| Clock | System clock → All flip-flops | Synchronous edge |
| Output Bus | All cells $Y_i$ → Common data bus | Multiplexed via $OUT$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Architecture of a 1-Bit Accumulator Cell

```mermaid
graph TD
    AINP[External Input A] --> MUXIN1[AND Gate 1]
    NGA[NOT Gate] --> COMP[Complement A-bar]
    COMP --> MUXIN2[AND Gate 2]
    FA[Full Adder A xor Q xor Cin] --> MUXIN3[AND Gate 3]
    SR[Shift Right Input] --> MUXIN4[AND Gate 4]
    SL[Shift Left Input] --> MUXIN5[AND Gate 5]
    
    MUXIN1 --> ORTREE[OR Tree MUX Output]
    MUXIN2 --> ORTREE
    MUXIN3 --> ORTREE
    MUXIN4 --> ORTREE
    MUXIN5 --> ORTREE
    
    ORTREE --> DFF[D Flip-Flop]
    CLK[System Clock] --> DFF
    DFF --> QNODE[Cell Output Q]
    
    QNODE --> FA
    QNODE --> OUTAND[Output AND Gate]
    QNODE --> COUTC[Carry Out to Next Cell]
    
    CSIG[Control Signals IN_DIRECT IN_COM IN_ADD IN_DR IN_DL] -.-> MUXIN1
    CSIG -.-> MUXIN2
    CSIG -.-> MUXIN3
    CSIG -.-> MUXIN4
    CSIG -.-> MUXIN5
    
    OEN[Output Enable OUT] --> OUTAND
    OUTAND --> YOUT[Cell Bus Output Y]
```

## 4.2 4-Bit Accumulator — Cascaded Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    CIN[Initial Carry In] --> C0
    SHIFTIN[Serial Shift Input] --> C0
    
    C0[Cell 0 LSB] -->|Cout| C1[Cell 1]
    C1 -->|Cout| C2[Cell 2]
    C2 -->|Cout| C3[Cell 3 MSB]
    C3 -->|Cout| CFINAL[Final Carry Out]
    
    BUS[Common Control Bus] -.-> C0
    BUS -.-> C1
    BUS -.-> C2
    BUS -.-> C3
    
    CLK2[System Clock] -.-> C0
    CLK2 -.-> C1
    CLK2 -.-> C2
    CLK2 -.-> C3
    
    C0 --> Y0[Y0 Output Bit 0]
    C1 --> Y1[Y1 Output Bit 1]
    C2 --> Y2[Y2 Output Bit 2]
    C3 --> Y3[Y3 Output Bit 3]
    
    EXT[External Data A 3 to 0] -.-> C0
    EXT -.-> C1
    EXT -.-> C2
    EXT -.-> C3
```

## 4.3 Data Flow During an "ADD" Operation (Conceptual Trace)

```mermaid
graph TD
    STEP1[Step 1 External input A applied to all cells] --> STEP2
    STEP2[Step 2 Full adder computes A plus Q plus Cin for each bit] --> STEP3
    STEP3[Step 3 IN_ADD control signal enables MUX to select adder output] --> STEP4
    STEP4[Step 4 D input of flip-flop receives sum bit] --> STEP5
    STEP5[Step 5 On next clock edge Q updates to new sum] --> STEP6
    STEP6[Step 6 New accumulator content equals Q plus A]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - **Diagram 1** shows *inside* a single cell — note the feedback loop from $Q$ back to the adder (essential for accumulation).
> - **Diagram 2** shows how 4 cells are wired together — the *carry chain* is the horizontal arrow, and the *control bus* (dotted) is shared by all cells.
> - **Diagram 3** is a *temporal trace* of how an ADD operation unfolds over one clock cycle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. Define an accumulator. List the basic operations performed by a 1-bit accumulator cell. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

An **accumulator** is a CPU register that stores the intermediate results of arithmetic and logic operations, and acts as the implicit operand source and destination for the ALU.

**Basic operations of a 1-bit accumulator cell:**

1. **Direct Load** — $Q \leftarrow A$ (load external data into the cell)
2. **Load Complement** — $Q \leftarrow \bar{A}$ (load the 1's complement of input)
3. **Add** — $Q \leftarrow Q + A + C_{in}$ (accumulate input to current value)
4. **Shift Right / Shift Left** — $Q \leftarrow SR_{in}$ or $SL_{in}$ (shift operations)
5. **Hold** — $Q \leftarrow Q$ (retain current value when no control is active)

*Marking Scheme: 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for any 4 operations = 3 marks*

---

### Q2. Why is a D flip-flop used in the accumulator cell instead of a latch? What is the role of the output enable signal? [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **D flip-flop** is preferred over a latch because:

1. **Edge-triggered operation** — The cell updates only on the clock edge, eliminating race conditions and ensuring synchronous behavior across all cascaded cells.
2. **Hold stability** — Between clock edges, the output $Q$ is stable, preventing glitches on the data bus.
3. **Predictable timing** — Setup and hold times are well-defined, making the design suitable for pipelined CPUs.

**Role of Output Enable ($OUT$):**
- The $OUT$ signal acts as a **tri-state control** for the cell's output buffer.
- When $OUT = 1$, the cell drives its $Q$ value onto the common data bus.
- When $OUT = 0$, the cell's output is in **high-impedance (Hi-Z)** state, allowing other registers to use the bus.
- This prevents **bus contention** in multi-register CPU architectures.

*Marking Scheme: 1.5 marks for flip-flop justification + 1.5 marks for output enable explanation*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`
**Design a 1-bit accumulator cell with the following operations: (a) Direct Load, (b) Add, (c) Shift Right, and (d) Hold. Draw the block diagram, derive the control equation for the D input, and construct the operation table.**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Component Identification [7 Marks]

**Components Required:**
- 1 × D Flip-Flop (storage)
- 1 × Full Adder (arithmetic)
- 1 × 4-to-1 Multiplexer (data selection) — implemented via AND-OR logic
- 1 × NOT gate (for complement, if needed)
- 1 × AND gate (output enable)

**Block Diagram Connections:**

| Source | → | Destination | Signal |
|--------|---|-------------|--------|
| External input $A$ | → | MUX input 0 | Direct load path |
| Full adder output $S$ | → | MUX input 1 | Add path |
| Shift-right input $SR_{in}$ | → | MUX input 2 | Shift path |
| $Q$ output (feedback) | → | MUX input 3 | Hold path |
| MUX output | → | D input of flip-flop | Storage |
| System clock | → | Flip-flop clock | Synchronization |
| $Q$ output | → | One input of full adder | Feedback for accumulation |
| $A$ | → | Other input of full adder | Operand |

#### Part (b) — Boolean Equations and Operation Table [7 Marks]

**Control Equation for D Input:**

$$D_i = A_i \cdot IN_{DIRECT} + (A_i \oplus Q_i \oplus C_{in}) \cdot IN_{ADD} + SR_{in} \cdot IN_{DR} + Q_i \cdot IN_{HOLD}$$

Where the **Hold** path uses a feedback from $Q_i$ to retain the current state.

**Output Equation:**

$$Y_i = Q_i \cdot OUT$$

**Operation Table:**

| $IN_{DIRECT}$ | $IN_{ADD}$ | $IN_{DR}$ | $IN_{HOLD}$ | $D_i$ | $Q_i(t+1)$ | Operation |
|:--------------:|:----------:|:---------:|:-----------:|:-----:|:-----------|:----------|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $A_i$ | $A_i$ | Direct Load |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $A_i \oplus Q_i \oplus C_{in}$ | $Q_i + A_i + C_{in}$ | Add |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $SR_{in}$ | $SR_{in}$ | Shift Right |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | $Q_i$ | $Q_i$ | Hold |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $Q_i$ (undefined) | Idle (avoid) |

**Cascading to n-bit accumulator:**
Connect $n$ such cells in parallel. The carry-out of cell $i$ becomes the carry-in of cell $i+1$. All cells share the same control bus and clock.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice) (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`
**With a neat block diagram, explain the design of a 4-bit accumulator. Show how the following operations are executed: (i) Addition of two 4-bit numbers, (ii) 2's complement subtraction, (iii) Logical shift left, and (iv) Direct data transfer. Derive the relevant control signals.**

#### Part (a) — 4-Bit Architecture and Block Diagram [7 Marks]

The 4-bit accumulator consists of **4 cascaded 1-bit cells** (Cell 0 = LSB, Cell 3 = MSB).

**Common Signals Shared by All Cells:**
- 4-bit external data input $A[3:0]$
- 4-bit external data input's complement $\bar{A}[3:0]$
- Common control bus: $IN_{DIRECT}, IN_{COM}, IN_{ADD}, IN_{DR}, IN_{DL}$
- Common clock signal
- Common output enable $OUT$

**Internal Inter-Cell Connections:**
- $C_{out}$ of Cell $i$ → $C_{in}$ of Cell $i+1$ (ripple carry)
- $Q$ of Cell $i+1$ → $SR_{in}$ of Cell $i$ (shift right)
- $Q$ of Cell $i-1$ → $SL_{in}$ of Cell $i$ (shift left)

#### Part (b) — Operation Execution [7 Marks]

| Operation | Control Sequence | Mathematical Result |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **(i) Addition** $AC \leftarrow AC + B$ | $IN_{ADD} = 1$; $C_{in} = 0$ | $Q \leftarrow Q + A$ |
| **(ii) 2's Complement Subtraction** $AC \leftarrow AC - B$ | Step 1: $IN_{COM} = 1$ (1 cycle); Step 2: $IN_{ADD} = 1$, $C_{in} = 1$ (next cycle) | $Q \leftarrow Q + \bar{A} + 1 = Q - A$ |
| **(iii) Logical Shift Left** $AC \leftarrow AC \ll 1$ | $IN_{DL} = 1$ for all cells simultaneously | Each $Q_i \leftarrow SL_{in,i}$ from right neighbor |
| **(iv) Direct Data Transfer** $AC \leftarrow A$ | $IN_{DIRECT} = 1$ for one cycle | $Q \leftarrow A$ |

**Detailed 2's Complement Subtraction (Key KTU Concept):**

| Clock Cycle | Control Active | Operation | Accumulator Content |
|:-----------:|:---------------|:----------|:--------------------|
| $t_0$ | $IN_{COM} = 1$ | Load 1's complement of $B$ | $AC \leftarrow \bar{B}$ |
| $t_1$ | $IN_{ADD} = 1, C_{in} = 1$ | Add $A$ to $\bar{B}$ with carry 1 | $AC \leftarrow A + \bar{B} + 1 = A - B$ |

This is the **standard micro-operation sequence** for subtraction in any accumulator-based CPU.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Carry-In for Subtraction (-2 marks)**: Many students apply $IN_{COM}$ and $IN_{ADD}$ but forget to set $C_{in} = 1$. Without it, 2's complement is incomplete. **Always remember**: Subtract = Add + 1's complement + carry-in.
>
> 2. **Confusing Shift Directions (-2 marks)**: Shift right moves data **MSB → LSB** (divide by 2). Shift left moves data **LSB → MSB** (multiply by 2). The diagram must clearly show arrow direction.
>
> 3. **No Output Enable in Diagram (-1 mark)**: A block diagram without the $OUT$ signal and the tri-state output gate is considered incomplete. Always include output control.
>
> 4. **Missing Synchronous Clock Label (-1 mark)**: The clock input to all flip-flops must be explicitly shown and labeled as "synchronous clock" in the diagram.
>
> 5. **Wrong MUX Implementation (-2 marks)**: Drawing only "MUX" as a black box without showing the AND-OR selection logic loses marks. Either show the explicit AND-OR gates or label it as "4×1 MUX with enable."
>
> 6. **Not Mentioning Hold State (-1 mark)**: When all control signals are LOW, the cell must hold its value. Forgetting this leads to an "undefined" state in the operation table.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

✅ **Accumulator = Flip-Flop + Full Adder + MUX + Output Gate**

✅ **1-bit cell stores $Q_i$; n-bit accumulator = n cascaded cells**

✅ **One-Hot Control Encoding** — exactly one control signal is HIGH at a time

✅ **D Input Equation**: $D_i = \sum (\text{Control}_k \times \text{Data Source}_k)$ (AND-OR selection)

✅ **Synchronous Design** — all flip-flops share common clock edge

✅ **Cascade Rule**: $C_{out}(i) \to C_{in}(i+1)$ for ripple-carry addition

✅ **2's Complement Subtraction** = Load Complement + Add with $C_{in} = 1$

✅ **Output Enable** $OUT$ controls tri-state bus access (prevents contention)

✅ **Shift Right** data flows MSB → LSB; **Shift Left** data flows LSB → MSB

✅ **Hold State** = all control signals LOW + feedback path from $Q$ to MUX input

✅ **Standard Operations**: Direct Load, Complement, Add, Shift Right, Shift Left, Hold

✅ **KTU Exam Tip**: Always label **all** control signals, data paths, and the clock in block diagrams for full marks.

✅ **Common Reference**: M. Morris Mano, *Digital Logic and Computer Design* — the standard KTU recommended textbook for accumulator design problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Logic Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Logic Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CONTROL LOGIC DESIGN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Control Logic Design** is the engineering process of designing the **Control Unit (CU)** — the brain of the CPU — that interprets machine instructions and generates the appropriate **micro-operations** at the correct time. The CU is responsible for issuing all the **control signals** that activate, route, and synchronize data flow across the datapath elements (registers, ALU, memory, bus) during instruction execution.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with NEP 2020 / OBE), the Control Unit is classified into two principal design philosophies:

1. **Hardwired Control Unit (HCU)** — implemented using fixed combinational logic (gates, decoders, flip-flops, PLAs).
2. **Microprogrammed Control Unit (MCU)** — implemented using a stored micro-routine in a **Control Memory (CM)** whose words are called **microinstructions**.

Each design produces a **Control Word (CW)** — a binary vector of control signals — at every clock tick.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Music Conductor

Imagine a **symphony orchestra** without a conductor. Each musician (violin, drum, trumpet) has a sheet of music, but without coordination the result is chaos.

- The **datapath** (ALU, registers, memory) = the **musicians**.
- The **control signals** = the conductor's **baton signals** (play louder, stop, change tempo).
- The **control unit** = the **conductor** reading the score (the instruction) and deciding which musician plays what at each instant.
- **Hardwired control** = a conductor who has **memorized** every score by heart (fast but inflexible).
- **Microprogrammed control** = a conductor who **reads the score** from a book on a stand (slower to turn pages, but the score can be easily edited).

### 1.3 Control Signals — The Output Language of the Control Unit

Control signals are the binary outputs that drive the datapath. They are categorised into four families:

| Signal Family | Purpose | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Register Transfers** | Enable data movement between registers | $PC_{in}$, $IR_{out}$, $Y_{in}$ |
| **ALU Operations** | Select ALU function and operands | $ADD$, $SUB$, $MUL$, $XOR$ |
| **Memory Operations** | Read/Write control for memory | $MEM_{RD}$, $MEM_{WR}$ |
| **Bus / Misc.** | Bus arbitration, interrupt handling | $BUS_{EN}$, $INT_{ACK}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical single-bus CPU (KTU 2024 syllabus benchmark) generates approximately **21 to 29 control signals** depending on instruction set complexity. The collection of all signals active during one clock period is called a **Control Word**.

### 1.4 The Central Role of Timing Signals

A **clock generator** produces a sequence of timing signals ($T_0, T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$) that partition a CPU cycle into discrete micro-steps. Each micro-step executes **one micro-operation** (a register transfer or ALU action).

> [!NOTE]
> **Micro-operation (μ-op):** A single elementary data-movement or processing operation performed in one clock cycle. The execution of one machine instruction is decomposed into a sequence of micro-operations called a **micro-routine**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Control Unit Inputs and Outputs (Generic Model)

```
                +----------------------+
   Instr.       |                      |   Control Signals
   Opcode  ---->|                      |----------------> Datapath
   Flags    --->|   CONTROL UNIT (CU)  |
   Clock     -->|                      |<---- Feedback
   T-state   -->|                      |    (Status bits)
                +----------------------+
```

### 2.2 Inputs to the Control Unit
1. **Instruction Opcode** (e.g., $I_0 \dots I_{k-1}$ from Instruction Register).
2. **Flags / Condition Codes** ($Z$, $N$, $C$, $V$) from the ALU / status register.
3. **Timing Signals** ($T_0, T_1, T_2, \dots$) from clock generator.
4. **External / Interrupt signals** ($INT$, $BUS_{REQ}$).

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Generating a Control Signal (Hardwired)

For a control signal $c_i$, we model it as a Boolean function:

$$c_i = f_1(T_j) \cdot f_2(\text{Opcode}) \cdot f_3(\text{Flags})$$

Where:
- $f_1(T_j)$ = timing gate (active in cycle $j$).
- $f_2(\text{Opcode})$ = decoding the instruction class.
- $f_3(\text{Flags})$ = conditional branch logic.

The OR of all such product terms (one per instruction cycle where $c_i$ is active) is the **canonical sum-of-products (SOP)**:

$$c_i = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \, m_k \cdot I_{\text{decoded}} \cdot T_j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **Sum-Of-Products (SOP)** equation is the heart of every KTU hardwired-control problem. A student who can derive this equation from a control sequence table gets full marks.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of control signals** | $N_c$ = count of distinct active signals in instruction set | bits |
| 2 | **Control word length** | $L_{cw} = N_c$ | bits |
| 3 | **Control memory size** | $S_{cm} = 2^{a} \times L_{cw}$ | bits |
| 4 | **Microinstruction address bits** | $a = \lceil \log_2 M \rceil$, $M$ = number of microinstructions | bits |
| 5 | **Microinstruction cycle time** | $T_{\mu} = T_{CM} + T_{decode} + T_{exec}$ | seconds |
| 6 | **Bit reduction by encoding** | $n_{\text{encoded}} = \lceil \log_2 k \rceil$ for $k$ mutually exclusive fields | bits saved per field |
| 7 | **Wilkes control word** | $L_{wilkes} = n_{\text{cond}} + n_{\text{op}} + n_{\text{addr}} + n_{\text{next}}$ | bits |
| 8 | **PLA inputs (hardwired)** | Inputs = Opcode bits + Timing bits + Flag bits | — |
| 9 | **PLA outputs** | Outputs = Control signals | — |
| 10 | **Microprogram branching** | $\text{Next Address} = \text{Base} + \text{Offset}$ or $CAR \leftarrow \mu\text{-branch field}$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary condition for Control Memory sizing:** $M$ must always be a power of 2; round up using $M = 2^{\lceil \log_2(\text{actual μ-ops})\rceil}$.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Control Logic Design

| Application Domain | Utility of Control Logic |
|---|---|
| **Processor Design (RISC-V, ARM, x86)** | Foundation of all CPU pipelines; determines critical path & clock period |
| **Embedded Controllers (8051, AVR, PIC)** | Direct application of microprogrammed control for firmware flexibility |
| **FPGA / ASIC Design** | Hardwired control mapped to LUTs and PLAs; timing closure depends on logic depth |
| **DSP Chips** | Custom control logic for SIMD/MIMD instruction dispatch |
| **Fault-Tolerant Systems** | Microprogrammed control enables runtime patching of microcode |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Design of Control Sequence for Instruction `ADD R1, R2` (Single-Bus CPU)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

> Consider a **single-bus CPU** with registers $PC$, $AR$, $IR$, $DR$, $AC$, $TR$, $R0 \dots R3$ and an ALU performing $A + B$. Registers are connected via a common internal bus. Each register has dedicated **input ($in$) and output ($out$) enable** control signals. Design the **micro-routine** (sequence of micro-operations) and the **control word** for the instruction `ADD R1, R2` (Add contents of R2 to R1 and store result in R1, assuming R1 is the accumulator).

### 3.1.2 Symbol Conventions

- `$X_{in}$` = enable write to register $X$ from bus.
- `$X_{out}$` = enable read from register $X$ onto bus.
- `$ADD$` = ALU function selector.
- `$END$` = end of micro-routine (sets $FETCH = 1$ to restart fetch cycle).

### 3.1.3 Derivation — Fetch Phase (Common to All Instructions)

The fetch phase must be performed for every instruction. We use timing signals $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3$:

**Step 1: At $T_0$** — Send PC content to Address Register.

$$AR \leftarrow PC$$

This requires the bus to carry $PC$ and $AR$ to latch it. Control signals activated: $PC_{out}$, $AR_{in}$.

**Step 2: At $T_1$** — Read memory into Data Register, increment PC.

$$DR \leftarrow M[AR]$$
$$PC \leftarrow PC + 1$$

Control signals: $READ$, $DR_{in}$, $INC_{PC}$, $ALU_{ADD}$ (the incrementer is a $+1$ adder).

**Step 3: At $T_2$** — Transfer opcode to Instruction Register.

$$IR \leftarrow DR$$

Control signals: $DR_{out}$, $IR_{in}$.

**Step 4: At $T_3$** — Decode opcode (combinational; produces a 1-of-$N$ output from instruction decoder).

Control signal: $D_0 = 1$ for `ADD` (assuming decoding done in $T_3$, signals become available for $T_4$ onward).

### 3.1.4 Derivation — Execute Phase for `ADD R1, R2`

**Step 5: At $T_4$** — Read source register $R1$ onto bus, latch in $TR$.

$$TR \leftarrow R1$$

Control signals: $R1_{out}$, $TR_{in}$.

**Step 6: At $T_5$** — Read $R2$ onto bus, perform ADD with $TR$, store in $AC$.

$$AC \leftarrow TR + R2$$

Control signals: $R2_{out}$, $ADD$, $AC_{in}$.

**Step 7: At $T_6$** — Write $AC$ back to $R1$.

$$R1 \leftarrow AC$$

Control signals: $AC_{out}$, $R1_{in}$, $END$.

### 3.1.5 Control Word Compilation

Assume 21 control signals: $\{PC_{out}, PC_{in}, MAR_{in}, READ, WRITE, DR_{out}, DR_{in}, IR_{in}, AC_{out}, AC_{in}, TR_{out}, TR_{in}, R1_{out}, R1_{in}, R2_{out}, R2_{in}, ADD, SUB, INC_{PC}, END, FETCH\}$. The control word for `ADD R1, R2` micro-routine:

| Step | Time | Active Control Signals (Control Word bits = 1) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_0$ | $PC_{out}$, $AR_{in}$ |
| 2 | $T_1$ | $READ$, $DR_{in}$, $INC_{PC}$, $ADD$ (for incrementer) |
| 3 | $T_2$ | $DR_{out}$, $IR_{in}$ |
| 4 | $T_3$ | Decoder activates (no control output) |
| 5 | $T_4$ | $R1_{out}$, $TR_{in}$ |
| 6 | $T_5$ | $R2_{out}$, $ADD$, $AC_{in}$ |
| 7 | $T_6$ | $AC_{out}$, $R1_{in}$, $END$ |

The **microprogram** is therefore a sequence of 7 control words stored in Control Memory.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Calculation of Control Word Length & Control Memory Size

### 3.2.1 Problem
A CPU has **18 distinct control signals** for its datapath. The microprogram has **64 microinstructions**. Compute:
1. Length of the control word.
2. Total size of the control memory in bits.
3. Number of address bits needed for the Control Address Register (CAR).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Control Word Length**

$$L_{cw} = N_c = 18 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Control Address Bits**

$$a = \lceil \log_2 M \rceil = \lceil \log_2 64 \rceil = \lceil 6 \rceil = 6 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3: Control Memory Size**

$$S_{cm} = 2^{a} \times L_{cw} = 2^{6} \times 18 = 64 \times 18 = 1152 \text{ bits}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The student must **show units (bits)** and **explicitly state the rounding** for $a$ to receive full marks. Many students wrongly write $M = 64$ directly without computing $a$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Hardwired Control Boolean Equation Derivation

### 3.3.1 Problem
For the `ADD R1, R2` instruction, derive the Boolean equation for control signal $TR_{in}$ in terms of timing and instruction-decoder signals.

### 3.3.2 Derivation

$TR_{in}$ is asserted in the **ADD micro-routine** at time $T_4$. Let $D_{ADD}$ be the instruction-decoder output (1 when opcode = ADD). The condition is:

$$TR_{in} = D_{ADD} \cdot T_4$$

Expanding for completeness, if multiple instructions also need $TR_{in}$ (say, SUB and AND), the SOP becomes:

$$TR_{in} = D_{ADD} \cdot T_4 + D_{SUB} \cdot T_4 + D_{AND} \cdot T_4$$

$$TR_{in} = T_4 \cdot (D_{ADD} + D_{SUB} + D_{AND})$$

This is the canonical form that will be programmed into the **PLA** (Programmable Logic Array) AND-OR array.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hardwired vs. Microprogrammed

| Parameter | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation** | Combinational logic (gates, PLA) | Control memory + CAR + next-address logic |
| **Speed** | **Very fast** (single gate delay path) | **Slower** (CM access time dominates) |
| **Flexibility** | Difficult to modify | **Easy to patch** by rewriting microcode |
| **ROM / PLA size** | Smaller for small instruction sets | Scales linearly with microinstruction count |
| **Best suited for** | RISC, fixed pipelines | CISC (VAX, x86 micro-op decode) |
| **Design effort** | High; manual SOP minimization | Medium; firmware-like development |
| **Cost of bug fix** | Re-spin silicon | Patch microcode ROM |
| **KTU exam weightage** | High (numerical + design) | High (architecture + address sequencing) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Block-Level Architecture of a Hardwired Control Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    instrReg[Instruction Register]
    flagsReg[Flags and Status Bits]
    clkGen[Clock Generator T0 T1 T2 T3]
    intCtrl[Interrupt Controller]

    instrReg -->|Opcode bits| decoder[Instruction Decoder]
    decoder -->|Decoded I0 I1 ... In| logicArray[Combinational Logic AND OR PLA]
    flagsReg -->|Z N C V| logicArray
    clkGen -->|Timing T0 T1 ... Tn| logicArray
    intCtrl -->|INT ACK| logicArray

    logicArray -->|Control Signal c1| ctrlBus1[Datapath Control Bus]
    logicArray -->|Control Signal c2| ctrlBus2[Register Enables]
    logicArray -->|Control Signal c3| ctrlBus3[ALU Function Select]
    logicArray -->|Control Signal cN| ctrlBusN[Memory RD WR]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block-Level Architecture of a Microprogrammed Control Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    car[Control Address Register CAR]
    nextAddr[Next Address Logic]
    cm[Control Memory CM ROM]
    cdr[Control Data Register CDR]
    condMux[Condition MUX]

    car -->|Address a bits| cm
    cm -->|Microinstruction Lcw bits| cdr
    cdr -->|Decode| ctrlOut[Control Signals to Datapath]
    cdr -->|Next Address field| nextAddr
    condMux -->|External flags BRANCH LOAD| nextAddr
    nextAddr -->|New address| car

    extSrc1[External Address Initial CAR load] --> car
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Sequence Counter Method (Hardwired Control)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Start Fetch]) --> t0Block[T0 AR leftarrow PC]
    t0Block --> t1Block[T1 Read Memory Increment PC]
    t1Block --> t2Block[T2 IR leftarrow DR]
    t2Block --> t3Block[T3 Decode Opcode]
    t3Block --> decChk{Instruction Type}
    decChk -->|ADD| t4Add[T4 TR leftarrow R1]
    t4Add --> t5Add[T5 AC leftarrow TR plus R2]
    t5Add --> t6Add[T6 R1 leftarrow AC END]
    decChk -->|LDA| t4Lda[T4 AC leftarrow DR]
    t4Lda --> t5Lda[T5 END]
    decChk -->|Other| t4Other[T4 Other Micro Routine]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Microinstruction Format (Horizontal vs. Vertical)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fmtH[Horizontal Microinstruction Format]
    subgraph fmtHBlock [ ]
        h1[FS Field Function Select] --- h2[CD Condition] --- h3[BR Branch] --- h4[ADF Address of Data Fetch] --- h5[ADS Address of Sequential]
    end

    fmtV[Vertical Microinstruction Format]
    subgraph fmtVBlock [ ]
        v1[OpCode 4 bits] --- v2[Field Select 2 bits] --- v3[Field Value 6 bits]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Horizontal microinstructions** have one bit per control signal → **fast** but **wide**. **Vertical microinstructions** are encoded (opcode + field) → **compact** but require an extra decode stage. KTU 2024 frequently asks students to compute bit-savings of vertical encoding.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Control Word Bit Packing Example

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cw[Control Word 21 bits]
    subgraph cwLayout [Bit Layout]
        b1[Bits 0 1 2 3 Register Select Encoded 4 bits]
        b2[Bits 4 5 6 7 ALU Op Encoded 4 bits]
        b3[Bit 8 Memory Read]
        b4[Bit 9 Memory Write]
        b5[Bit 10 Bus Source]
        b6[Bits 11 to 20 Reserved 10 bits]
    end
```
