# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Control Word. What is its significance in the design of a control unit? How is its length determined?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Control Word (CW)** is a binary pattern of all control signals asserted in a single clock cycle (or micro-step) to execute a specific micro-operation. **Significance:**
1. It uniquely identifies one micro-operation of the instruction's micro-routine.
2. It is the fundamental unit of information stored in the Control Memory of a microprogrammed CU, or generated by the PLA of a hardwired CU.
3. **Length:** $L_{cw} = N_c$, the number of distinct control signals required by the datapath. For a single-bus CPU with 21 signals, $L_{cw} = 21$ bits. (1 mark each for definition, significance, length)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Compare hardwired and microprogrammed control units on the basis of speed, flexibility, and implementation complexity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Hardwired | Microprogrammed |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Faster (gate delay) | Slower (CM access time) |
| Flexibility | Low (re-design required) | High (reprogram microcode) |
| Implementation | Combinational logic (PLA) | ROM + sequencer + CDR |
(1 mark per aspect)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Mandate (KTU ESE):** A student answers EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** from the same module.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For a single-bus CPU, design the **complete micro-routine** (sequence of micro-operations) to execute the instruction **`LDA (Load Accumulator Direct)`**, which loads the accumulator $AC$ with the contents of a memory location whose address is specified in the instruction itself. Assume fetch is common and takes 4 timing signals $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3$. List the active control signals for each micro-step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CPU has **24 control signals** and a microprogram consisting of **96 microinstructions**. Calculate: (i) length of the control word, (ii) number of address bits for the Control Address Register (CAR), and (iii) total Control Memory size in bits. Justify any rounding. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Micro-routine for `LDA addr` (7 marks)

**Instruction semantics:** $AC \leftarrow M[addr]$ where `addr` is the second word of the 2-word instruction.

**Fetch Phase (common):**

| Step | Time | Micro-operation | Active Control Signals |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_0$ | $AR \leftarrow PC$ | $PC_{out}$, $AR_{in}$ |
| 2 | $T_1$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $PC \leftarrow PC+1$ | $READ$, $DR_{in}$, $INC_{PC}$ |
| 3 | $T_2$ | $IR \leftarrow DR$ | $DR_{out}$, $IR_{in}$ |
| 4 | $T_3$ | Decode opcode (no control outputs) | $D_{LDA}$ asserted |

**Execute Phase:**

| Step | Time | Micro-operation | Active Control Signals |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | $T_4$ | $AR \leftarrow DR$ (the second word holds the address) | $DR_{out}$, $AR_{in}$ |
| 6 | $T_5$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ (read operand) | $READ$, $DR_{in}$ |
| 7 | $T_6$ | $AC \leftarrow DR$ | $DR_{out}$, $AC_{in}$, $END$ |

**Total control words in micro-routine = 7.** (1 mark per row of fetch; 1 mark per row of execute; 1 mark for `END` placement)

##### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $N_c = 24$, $M = 96$.

**Step 1: Control word length**

$$L_{cw} = N_c = 24 \text{ bits} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 2: Number of address bits for CAR**

$$a = \lceil \log_2 M \rceil = \lceil \log_2 96 \rceil = \lceil 6.585 \rceil = 7 \text{ bits} \quad (3 \text{ marks})$$

*Justification:* Although only 96 microinstructions exist, $M$ must be a power of 2, so we round up to $2^7 = 128$ locations.

**Step 3: Control Memory size**

$$S_{cm} = 2^{a} \times L_{cw} = 2^{7} \times 24 = 128 \times 24 = 3072 \text{ bits} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students compute $2^{6} \times 24 = 1536$ bits, forgetting that $96$ is **not** a power of 2. Always round **up** the address bits. (Loss: **2 marks**.)
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Failing to mention the **unit "bits"** in the final answer. (Loss: **0.5 mark**.)
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** Confusing **control memory size** with the **number of microinstructions**. These are different — microinstructions count *rows*, size is in *bits*. (Loss: **1 mark**.)

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **microprogrammed control unit**. Describe the role of the **Control Address Register (CAR)**, **Control Data Register (CDR)**, and the **next-address logic** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Boolean equation** (SOP form) for the control signal $R1_{in}$ for a CPU that needs to assert $R1_{in}$ in the following situations: (i) during execution of `ADD R1, R2` at time $T_6$, (ii) during execution of `SUB R1, R2` at time $T_6$, and (iii) during the fetch phase at time $T_0$ (PC used as a pseudo-register). Assume instruction decoder outputs are $D_{ADD}$, $D_{SUB}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Microprogrammed CU Block Diagram and Operation (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (textual reproduction for the answer script):**

> **CAR → Control Memory (ROM) → CDR → Datapath**
> **CDR → Next-Address Logic → CAR** (feedback loop with conditional multiplexer)

**Operation (stepwise, 1 mark per role):**

1. **CAR (Control Address Register):** Holds the address of the next microinstruction to be fetched from CM. Initial value is loaded from an external source (e.g., the start address corresponding to the instruction opcode). Increments or branches under next-address logic control. (1 mark)
2. **CM (Control Memory):** A ROM of $2^a \times L_{cw}$ bits, where $a$ is the number of CAR bits. Each word is one microinstruction. (1 mark)
3. **CDR (Control Data Register):** Latches the microinstruction read from CM, so the control signals are stable for the entire clock cycle while CAR is being updated for the next cycle. (1 mark)
4. **Next-Address Logic:** A combinational circuit that computes the **next value of CAR** from fields in the CDR (branch address, condition flags) and the current state. Common forms: $CAR \leftarrow CAR + 1$ (sequential), $CAR \leftarrow$ branch field (unconditional jump), $CAR \leftarrow$ branch field if condition true (conditional). (2 marks)
5. **External Address Source:** For multi-word instructions, the opcode of the decoded instruction selects the **starting micro-routine address** in CM, loaded into CAR to begin execution. (1 mark)
6. **Status / Condition Multiplexer:** Selects between **flag bits** ($Z$, $N$, $C$, $V$) and external interrupts to drive conditional branches. (1 mark)

##### Part (b) — SOP Derivation for $R1_{in}$ (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify all conditions that activate $R1_{in}$.**
- Condition A: `ADD R1, R2` active AND $T_6$ active.
- Condition B: `SUB R1, R2` active AND $T_6$ active.
- Condition C: Fetch phase — $PC_{out}$, $AR_{in}$ active (so $R1_{in}$ is **not** part of the given fetch list). The problem says "during fetch at $T_0$" — but actually $R1_{in}$ is irrelevant to fetch; the problem intends us to treat the increment path. **Re-read:** The problem states (iii) "during fetch phase at $T_0$" — the only register that gets latched in standard fetch is $AR$ (or in some designs, the PC is incremented and latched back). The student should interpret (iii) as: **the $R1_{in}$ signal is not used in fetch, so the fetch contribution is the decoder output $D_{FETCH}$ AND $T_0$ AND $R1_{in}$ (which is 0)**. Therefore the effective contribution is zero. (2 marks for interpretation)

**Step 2: Write the SOP Boolean equation.**

$$R1_{in} = D_{ADD} \cdot T_6 + D_{SUB} \cdot T_6$$

Factoring:

$$R1_{in} = T_6 \cdot (D_{ADD} + D_{SUB})$$

(2 marks for the canonical SOP, 1 mark for the factored form, 1 mark for proper use of AND/OR notation, 1 mark for stating that this will be implemented using a PLA AND-OR array)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #4:** Forgetting to **factor** the SOP — KTU awards 1 mark for the simplified form. (Loss: **1 mark**.)
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #5:** Using ambiguous notation like $R1in$ instead of $R1_{in}$ or clear subscripts. The examiner may mark "unclear" → 0.5 mark deduction.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #6:** Failing to mention that this equation maps directly to the **PLA AND-OR plane** — examiners check for the *implementation* link.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Control Logic Design (Module 3)**

- [ ] **Control Word = binary vector of all control signals for one micro-step.** Length = number of control signals.
- [ ] **Hardwired Control** = combinational logic, **fast**, **inflexible**; uses PLA / decoders / sequence counter.
- [ ] **Microprogrammed Control** = stored micro-routine in **Control Memory**; **slower**, **flexible** (microcode patchable).
- [ ] **CAR** holds next CM address; **CDR** holds current microinstruction; **Next-Address Logic** computes next CAR.
- [ ] **CM Size = $2^a \times L_{cw}$** bits, where $a = \lceil \log_2 M \rceil$ and $M$ = number of microinstructions.
- [ ] **Micro-routine** = sequence of micro-operations required to execute ONE machine instruction. Fetch phase is common to all.
- [ ] **Hardwired Boolean equation** for any control signal $c_i$ is an **SOP** of (decoded instruction × timing × flags).
- [ ] **PLA-based hardwired control** uses AND-plane (decoders) → OR-plane (control signals).
- [ ] **Horizontal microinstruction** = 1 bit per control signal (fast, wide); **Vertical** = encoded (slow, compact).
- [ ] **Wilkes Control** = early microprogrammed design with direct diode matrix; has **condition + operation + address fields**.
- [ ] Always **round up** address bits to the next power of 2.
- [ ] Always **state units** (bits, Hz, ns) in numerical answers.
- [ ] **Fetch phase** typically = 4 micro-steps: $AR \leftarrow PC$, $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $PC++$, $IR \leftarrow DR$, decode.
- [ ] Branching in microprogrammed control: $CAR \leftarrow \text{branch field}$ (if condition true).
- [ ] When in doubt, draw the **block diagram** first — it earns 1–2 marks even before the calculation begins.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardwired Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hardwired Control ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hardwired Control** is a technique for implementing the Control Unit (CU) of a CPU using a fixed, non-programmable network of **combinational logic gates, decoders, flip-flops, and sequential circuits** that directly produce the required control signals (micro-operations) for every instruction in the instruction set. In a hardwired control unit (HCU), the binary information stored in the **Instruction Register (IR)**, combined with the **system clock (timing signals T0, T1, T2, ...)** and the **status flags (Condition Codes)** from the ALU, is decoded by a permanent logic network to assert the correct sequence of control signals for each micro-operation. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEEET416 Module 3):** > The student must be able to *design* the control logic for basic instructions, derive the **Boolean control expressions**, and contrast hardwired control against **microprogrammed control** (covered next module). ### 1.2 Key Terminology - **Control Word**: A binary pattern of all control signals activated in one clock step. For an $n$-signal CU, the control word is $n$ bits wide. - **Micro-operation**: The smallest elementary data-processing action (e.g., `PC ← PC + 1`, `IR ← M[AR]`). - **Timing Signal (Tn)**: A discrete clock phase produced by a 4-bit sequence counter. KTU standard systems typically use **4 timing states T0, T1, T2, T3**. - **Control Logic**: The combinational AND-OR-NOT gate network that maps $(IR, T_n, Flags) \rightarrow Control\ Signals$. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Railway Signal Box" Imagine a **British-era railway signal box** from the 1900s, where a station master physically pulls a sequence of levers (each lever hard-wired to a specific signal light or track switch) according to a pre-decided timetable and the current position of the train. | Signal Box Element | CPU Hardwired Control Equivalent | |---|---| | Master clock (mechanical pendulum) | Master clock generator producing $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3$ | | Lever box (mechanical rods) | Combinational AND-OR logic gates | | Pre-printed train schedule | Hardwired instruction decoder (ROM-less) | | Track sensors (train on which block?) | Status flags (Z, N, C, V from ALU) | | Pulled levers → signals change | Control signals fire: `I/OR/W/MEM/PCin/PCout` … | The levers cannot be reprogrammed — to change the schedule, the entire mechanical rod network must be re-built. Similarly, to change the instruction set, the HCU gates must be redesigned. ### 1.4 Physical Metrics Worth Remembering - **Gate Delay**: Propagation delay through the combinational logic network. Typical value: **2 – 5 ns** per logic level. - **Cycle Time**: Fixed by the longest combinational path in the CU + ALU + Memory access. - **Control Word Width**: Equal to the total number of control signals. For the basic KTU reference CPU, this is **21 control signals** (Mano's classic example). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Architectural Components of a Hardwired Control Unit A hardwired control unit is built from four cooperating sub-modules: 1. **Instruction Register (IR)** — Holds the current opcode. The upper bits feed the **Instruction Decoder**; the lower bits may hold an address/operand. 2. **Instruction Decoder** — A 3-to-8 or 4-to-16 decoder that asserts exactly one output line (say $D_0 \dots D_7$) corresponding to the opcode. 3. **Timing Generator** — A 2-bit or 4-bit sequence counter (ripple/synchronous) driven by the master clock, producing sequential timing variables $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3$. 4. **Control Logic (Encoder / Gate Network)** — An AND-OR gate matrix that combines the decoder outputs, timing variables, and condition flags to produce each control signal. > [!NOTE] > **Why a 4-bit sequence counter for a CPU with multi-cycle instructions?** > Because almost every KTU-referenced CPU (Mano's basic computer, IAS-derived designs) executes each instruction in **Fetch → Decode → Execute** phases, each subdivided into micro-operations. A 4-state counter provides ample granularity for $3 \times 4 = 12$ timing slots, sufficient for memory-reference and I/O instructions. ### 2.2 Operational Logic — How a Control Signal is Generated A generic control signal $C_i$ is generated by a Boolean expression of the form: $$ C_i = f(D_j,\ T_k,\ Flags) $$ For example, to enable the memory **Read** signal during the fetch phase of every instruction: $$ \overline{MEM\_READ} = \overline{T_0} $$ which simplifies to the practical AND-OR form: $$ MEM\_READ = T_0 $$ For a conditional signal such as "load PC from ALU output if the previous ALU result was zero" (e.g., for `SKZ` — Skip if Zero): $$ PC_{in} = SKZ \cdot T_3 \cdot Z $$ where: - $SKZ$ = decoder output line for the Skip-if-Zero opcode, - $T_3$ = timing signal during the execute phase, - $Z$ = Zero flag from the ALU. ### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Design - **Why combinational logic?** Because each control signal must be deterministic, glitch-free, and synchronous with the clock — the simplest implementation is a two-level AND-OR network (sum-of-products). - **Why a sequence counter?** Because most micro-operations must occur in a **fixed order** (e.g., read memory *before* writing to a register). The counter enforces this temporal sequence without needing memory. - **Why use flags?** Because branch instructions depend on *runtime* data conditions; the flags act as the bridge between the datapath and the CU. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Equation | Meaning | Typical Value / Form | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C_i = \sum (D_j \cdot T_k \cdot Flag)$ | Generic control-signal Boolean | Sum-of-Products (SOP) | | 2 | $T_0 + T_1 + T_2 + T_3 = 1$ | Mutual exclusivity of timing signals | One-hot per cycle | | 3 | $Clock\ Period\ T_c \ge t_{ALU} + t_{CU} + t_{MEM}$ | Critical-path timing constraint | $\approx 100$–$500$ ns | | 4 | $Gate\ Levels \le 4$ | Practical AND-OR depth for speed | $\le 4$ levels | | 5 | $Control\ Word\ Width = \sum (Control\ Signals)$ | $n$-bit control bus | $21$ bits (Mano) | | 6 | $PC_{out} = D_7 \cdot T_3$ (e.g., BSA) | Example: PC-out enable for Branch-Save | Boolean product | | 7 | $DR_{in} = T_0$ | Data Register input always in T0 (fetch) | Generic fetch signal | | 8 | $Z = 1 \iff ALU_{out} = 0$ | Zero-flag definition | Status condition | | 9 | $Sequence\ Counter\ Resolution = 2^k$ | $k$-bit counter gives $2^k$ states | $k=2 \rightarrow 4$ states | | 10 | $HCU\ Speed = f(Logic\ Depth, Fan\text{-}out)$ | Faster than microprogrammed | $\sim 2$–$5\times$ faster | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Note:** When writing a Boolean expression in an exam, always express it in the **canonical SOP** form first, then minimize using a **K-map** if the syllabus demands optimization. Examiners award marks for the SOP expansion step. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Hardwired control is the de-facto implementation in: - **RISC-V cores** (e.g., SiFive E-series) — where instruction sets are simple, regular, and speed is paramount. - **Embedded microcontrollers** (ARM Cortex-M0, AVR) — small instruction sets make the HCU gate-count manageable. - **GPU shader cores** — fixed-function datapaths require ultra-fast control. - **Custom ASIC designs** — when the instruction set is frozen, the HCU is burned into silicon for maximum performance. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Worked Reference Design:** We shall derive the **Boolean control equations** for the classic Mano Basic Computer, which is the canonical KTU reference architecture. Consider the instruction set: > - `ADD` (opcode `0000` → decoder output $D_0$) > - `LDA` (opcode `0010` → decoder output $D_2$) > - `STA` (opcode `0011` → decoder output $D_3$) > - `BUN` (opcode `0100$ → $D_4$) > - `BSA` (opcode `0101$ → $D_5$) > - `ISZ` (opcode `0110$ → $D_6$) Assume a **4-state sequence counter** producing $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3$, and assume memory-reference instructions execute in three micro-cycles: $T_0$ (Fetch opcode), $T_1$ (Fetch address / Decode), $T_2, T_3$ (Execute). ### 3.1 Step 1 — Identify the Register-Transfer Language (RTL) for each instruction | Instruction | Phase | RTL Micro-operations | |---|---|---| | Fetch (All) | $T_0$ | $AR \leftarrow PC$ | | Fetch (All) | $T_0$ | $IR \leftarrow M[AR],\ PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | | Decode (All) | $T_1$ | $AR \leftarrow IR(0\text{-}11)$ | | `ADD` | $T_2$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | | `ADD` | $T_3$ | $AC \leftarrow AC + DR,\ E = 1$ (set status flags) | | `LDA` | $T_2$ | $AR \leftarrow IR(0\text{-}11)$ | | `LDA` | $T_3$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | | `LDA` | $T_4$* | $AC \leftarrow DR,\ SC \leftarrow 0$ (assumes 5-state, here we use $T_2, T_3$ only) | | `STA` | $T_2$ | $M[AR] \leftarrow AC,\ SC \leftarrow 0$ | > *For simplicity, we map `LDA` execution into $T_2$ and $T_3$ within a 4-state counter by combining the address phase and the read phase. ### 3.2 Step 2 — Translate each RTL action into a Control Signal Activation | Control Signal | Meaning | |---|---| | $PC_{out}$ | Enable PC onto common bus | | $PC_{in}$ | Load PC from common bus | | $AR_{in}$ | Load AR from common bus | | $IR_{in}$ | Load IR from common bus | | $DR_{in}$ | Load DR from common bus | | $AC_{in}$ | Load AC from common bus | | $READ$ | Memory Read | | $WRITE$ | Memory Write | | $ADD$ (ALU) | ALU performs add | | $E = 1$ | Set status flags | ### 3.3 Step 3 — Derive the Boolean Expression for `READ` (Memory-Read Signal) The memory is read: - **During the fetch phase** ($T_0$) — to fetch the opcode. - **During `ADD` and `LDA` execute phase** ($T_2$ for `ADD`, $T_3$ for `LDA`). For the simplified example: $$ READ = T_0 + D_0 \cdot T_2 + D_2 \cdot T_3 $$ **Conversion Logic:** Each term in the SOP corresponds to *one micro-cycle* in which the read signal must be high. The first term is universal (fetch); the next two are decoder-gated and timing-gated. ### 3.4 Step 4 — Derive the Boolean Expression for `WRITE` (Memory-Write Signal) Memory is written only during `STA` execution: $$ WRITE = D_3 \cdot T_2 $$ **Conversion Logic:** The `STA` opcode must be active (decoder line $D_3$ high) **and** the system must be in the execute phase $T_2$. Both conditions must be true → AND gate. ### 3.5 Step 5 — Derive the Boolean Expression for $PC_{in}$ PC is loaded from the bus: - During fetch ($T_0$): $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$. - During `BUN` execute ($T_2$): $PC \leftarrow AR$ (unconditional branch). - During `BSA` execute ($T_2$): $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ (return address save). $$ PC_{in} = T_0 + D_4 \cdot T_2 + D_5 \cdot T_2 $$ We may simplify by grouping $D_4 + D_5$: $$ PC_{in} = T_0 + (D_4 + D_5) \cdot T_2 $$ ### 3.6 Step 6 — Derive the Boolean Expression for the Branch-on-Zero Signal Assume `ISZ` is "Increment memory and Skip if Zero". The skip micro-operation is: $$ PC \leftarrow PC + 1 \quad \text{if}\ Z = 1 $$ Therefore: $$ PC_{in} = T_0 + D_6 \cdot T_3 \cdot Z $$ **Conversion Logic:** This is a 3-input AND gate combining the decoder line $D_6$ (ISZ active), the timing signal $T_3$ (execute phase), and the **runtime flag** $Z$ (condition satisfied). ### 3.7 Step 7 — Derive the Control Expression for ALU ADD in `ADD` instruction $$ ADD = D_0 \cdot T_3 $$ ### 3.8 Step 8 — Summary of the Derived Control Logic (SOP Table) | Control Signal | Boolean Expression (SOP Form) | |---|---| | $PC_{out}$ | $T_0$ | | $PC_{in}$ | $T_0 + D_4 \cdot T_2 + D_5 \cdot T_2 + D_6 \cdot T_3 \cdot Z$ | | $AR_{in}$ | $T_0 + D_5 \cdot T_2$ | | $IR_{in}$ | $T_0$ | | $DR_{in}$ | $D_0 \cdot T_2 + D_2 \cdot T_3$ | | $AC_{in}$ | $D_0 \cdot T_3 + D_2 \cdot T_3$ | | $READ$ | $T_0 + D_0 \cdot T_2 + D_2 \cdot T_3$ | | $WRITE$ | $D_3 \cdot T_2$ | | $ADD$ (ALU) | $D_0 \cdot T_3$ | | $E$ (flag set) | $D_0 \cdot T_3 + D_2 \cdot T_3$ | ### 3.9 Step 9 — Critical Path Analysis The critical path for the HCU is the longest combinational chain: $$ T_c \ge t_{Decoder} + t_{AND\text{-}gate} + t_{OR\text{-}gate} + t_{ALU} + t_{Register\ Setup} $$ For a typical 4-level AND-OR network with 5-ns gate delay: $$ T_c \ge 4 \times 5\ \text{ns} + 20\ \text{ns}\ (ALU) + 50\ \text{ns}\ (Memory) = 90\ \text{ns} $$ giving a clock frequency of $\approx 11\ \text{MHz}$, which was the operating speed of Mano's reference HCU design. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hardwired Control Unit ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUTS["INPUT BUS (FROM DATAPATH)"] IR["Instruction Register
IR 12-bit"] FL["Status Flags
Z, N, C, V"] end subgraph DECODER["INSTRUCTION DECODER (3-to-8)"] D0["D0 - ADD"] D1["D1 - AND"] D2["D2 - LDA"] D3["D3 - STA"] D4["D4 - BUN"] D5["D5 - BSA"] D6["D6 - ISZ"] D7["D7 - REG"] end subgraph TIMING["TIMING GENERATOR"] CLK["Master Clock
CLK"] SC["2-bit Sequence
Counter"] T0["T0"] T1["T1"] T2["T2"] T3["T3"] end subgraph LOGIC["CONTROL LOGIC
(AND-OR Gate Matrix)"] AND1["AND gates
combine D_i, T_k, Flags"] OR1["OR gates
collect SOP terms"] C_OUT["Control Word
21-bit output bus"] end IR --> DECODER CLK --> SC SC --> T0 SC --> T1 SC --> T2 SC --> T3 D0 --> AND1 D2 --> AND1 D3 --> AND1 D4 --> AND1 D5 --> AND1 D6 --> AND1 T0 --> AND1 T1 --> AND1 T2 --> AND1 T3 --> AND1 FL --> AND1 AND1 --> OR1 OR1 --> C_OUT ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Micro-Operation Sequence Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A([Power ON]) --> B["T0:
AR ← PC
IR ← M[AR]
PC ← PC+1"] B --> C["T1:
Decode Opcode
AR ← IR address bits"] C --> D{Opcode Type?} D -- ADD --> E1["T2: DR ← M[AR]
T3: AC ← AC + DR"] D -- LDA --> E2["T2: AR ← IR
T3: DR ← M[AR]
AC ← DR"] D -- STA --> E3["T2: M[AR] ← AC"] D -- BUN --> E4["T2: PC ← AR"] D -- BSA --> E5["T2: M[AR] ← PC
AR ← AR+1
PC ← AR"] D -- ISZ --> E6["T2: DR ← M[AR]
T3: DR ← DR+1
M[AR] ← DR
If Z=1: PC ← PC+1"] E1 --> F([Next Instruction]) E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F E5 --> F E6 --> F ``` ### 4.3 Control Word Generation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["Step 1: List all 21 control signals"] --> P2["Step 2: For each micro-operation,
determine which signals fire"] P2 --> P3["Step 3: Build SOP Boolean
C_i = SUM(D_j * T_k * Flag)"] P3 --> P4["Step 4: Minimize using
K-Map / Quine-McCluskey"] P4 --> P5["Step 5: Realize in AND-OR
or NAND-NAND network"] P5 --> P6["Step 6: Verify with timing
diagram simulation"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Block Diagram and Not Detailed Gate Schematic?** > Per the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard, a complete gate-level diagram of even a single Boolean expression like $PC_{in} = T_0 + D_4 T_2 + D_5 T_2 + D_6 T_3 Z$ requires dozens of AND/OR gate symbols, which Mermaid cannot render cleanly. The block-level schematic above is the KTU-accepted alternative. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *Define a hardwired control unit. Mention any two of its advantages over microprogrammed control.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A hardwired control unit is one in which the control logic is implemented using fixed combinational logic circuits — gates, decoders, and flip-flops — that generate the control signals directly from the instruction opcode, timing signals, and status flags. **Advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):** 1. **Faster operation:** No microinstruction fetch/decode cycle, so the control signals are produced in a single combinational delay — typically 2–3× faster than microprogrammed control. 2. **Compact silicon area for small instruction sets:** For RISC processors with ≤ 50 instructions, the HCU uses fewer transistors than a control memory + sequencer. 3. **Suitable for pipelining:** Predictable timing enables tight pipeline staging. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *List the major components of a hardwired control unit and state the function of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Instruction Register (IR):** Latches the current opcode. (0.75) 2. **Instruction Decoder:** Decodes the opcode into one-hot outputs $D_0 \dots D_n$. (0.75) 3. **Sequence Counter + Timing Generator:** Produces sequential timing variables $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3$ to enforce micro-operation order. (0.75) 4. **Control Logic (AND-OR gate matrix):** Combines decoder outputs, timing variables, and condition flags to generate each control signal. (0.75) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one full question** from a Module 3 choice of two. Both options below are provided with complete model solutions. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]* **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the design of a hardwired control unit. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design the hardwired control logic (Boolean expressions) for the following three instructions in a 4-state sequence-counter-based CPU: `ADD`, `LDA`, `STA`. Show all derivation steps. **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution — (a)** [7 marks] A hardwired control unit consists of four cooperating blocks: 1. **Instruction Register (IR)** — stores the 12-bit opcode fetched from memory. (1 mark) 2. **Instruction Decoder** — a 3-to-8 decoder that activates exactly one output line $D_i$ corresponding to the opcode. (1.5 marks) 3. **Sequence Counter (2-bit)** — driven by the master clock; produces four mutually-exclusive timing variables $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3$ such that only one is high at a time. (1.5 marks) 4. **Control Logic (AND-OR network)** — implements the Boolean equations $C_i = f(D_i, T_k, Flags)$ for every control signal. (1.5 marks) 5. **Block diagram description** (1 mark) — see Section 4.1 above for the Mermaid architecture. 6. **Working principle** (0.5 mark) — For every (opcode × timing) combination, the appropriate control signals are asserted on the bus. ##### **Model Solution — (b)** [7 marks] **Step 1 — Identify micro-operations for each instruction:** | Instruction | Cycle | Micro-operation | |---|---|---| | Fetch (all) | $T_0$ | $AR \leftarrow PC;\ IR \leftarrow M[AR];\ PC \leftarrow PC+1$ | | Decode (all) | $T_1$ | $AR \leftarrow IR(0\text{-}11)$ | | `ADD` | $T_2$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | | `ADD` | $T_3$ | $AC \leftarrow AC + DR;\ E = 1$ | | `LDA` | $T_2$ | $AR \leftarrow IR(0\text{-}11)$ | | `LDA` | $T_3$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR];\ AC \leftarrow DR;\ E = 1$ | | `STA` | $T_2$ | $M[AR] \leftarrow AC$ | **Step 2 — Map decoder outputs:** - $D_0$ = ADD, $D_2$ = LDA, $D_3$ = STA. **Step 3 — Write Boolean expressions in SOP form:** $$ PC_{out} = T_0 $$ $$ AR_{in} = T_0 + D_2 \cdot T_2 + D_3 \cdot T_2 $$ $$ IR_{in} = T_0 $$ $$ DR_{in} = D_0 \cdot T_2 + D_2 \cdot T_3 $$ $$ AC_{in} = D_0 \cdot T_3 + D_2 \cdot T_3 $$ $$ READ = T_0 + D_0 \cdot T_2 + D_2 \cdot T_3 $$ $$ WRITE = D_3 \cdot T_2 $$ $$ ADD_{ALU} = D_0 \cdot T_3 $$ $$ E = D_0 \cdot T_3 + D_2 \cdot T_3 $$ **Step 4 — Verification (1 mark):** Each expression correctly maps the active decoder line to the active timing signal. Terms are mutually consistent with the RTL of each instruction. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often write $PC_{in} = T_0 + D_2 \cdot T_2$, which is **wrong** for `LDA` because `LDA` does *not* modify the PC. PC is incremented only during fetch ($T_0$) and during conditional branches. Read the instruction semantics carefully! > > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to include the **status-flag set** signal $E$ in `ADD` and `LDA` — without $E=1$, subsequent conditional branches will fail. Loss of 1 mark is common here. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]* **(a)** Explain the role of a sequence counter in a hardwired control unit. Why is it 2-bit for a basic computer? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design the control logic for the **BSA** (Branch and Save Address) instruction. Show RTL, the required control signals, and the final Boolean expressions. **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution — (a)** [7 marks] **Role of the Sequence Counter (4 marks):** The sequence counter is a synchronous binary counter driven by the master clock. Its outputs $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3$ define the **time slots** within one instruction cycle, ensuring that micro-operations execute in a deterministic order (fetch → decode → execute). Each micro-operation is AND-gated with the appropriate $T_k$ so that it fires only in its allocated time slot. **Why 2-bit (3 marks):** 1. The basic computer executes each instruction in a maximum of 4 phases: fetch, decode, address-fetch, and execute. A 2-bit counter provides $2^2 = 4$ unique states — exactly matching this requirement. 2. Using a 2-bit counter keeps the decoder complexity low, reducing the **critical path delay** through the AND-OR matrix. 3. For longer instructions, a 3-bit (8-state) or 4-bit (16-state) counter can be cascaded without changing the fundamental design. ##### **Model Solution — (b)** [7 marks] **Step 1 — `BSA` Semantics:** - `BSA` is a subroutine call: it stores the return address in memory at the operand address, then jumps to the operand address + 1. - Opcode line: $D_5$. **Step 2 — RTL of `BSA`:** $$ T_0:\ AR \leftarrow PC,\ IR \leftarrow M[AR],\ PC \leftarrow PC + 1 $$ $$ T_1:\ AR \leftarrow IR(0\text{-}11) $$ $$ T_2:\ M[AR] \leftarrow PC,\ AR \leftarrow AR + 1 $$ $$ T_3:\ PC \leftarrow AR,\ SC \leftarrow 0 $$ **Step 3 — Boolean expressions for `BSA`:** $$ WRITE_{BSA} = D_5 \cdot T_2 $$ $$ PC_{out} = T_0 + D_5 \cdot T_2 $$ $$ AR_{in} = T_0 + D_5 \cdot T_2 + D_5 \cdot T_3 $$ $$ PC_{in} = T_0 + D_5 \cdot T_3 $$ $$ SC_{reset} = D_5 \cdot T_3 + D_2 \cdot T_3 + D_3 \cdot T_2 $$ **Step 4 — Gate-level realization (1 mark):** Each product term becomes an AND gate; the OR of all terms feeding a given control signal is realized by a multi-input OR (or wired-OR in CMOS). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** A very common error is to write $PC_{in} = D_5 \cdot T_2$ for `BSA`. This is **incorrect** — at $T_2$, we write PC *to* memory, we do *not* load PC. The new PC value is loaded only at $T_3$ ($PC \leftarrow AR$). The execution phase of `BSA` is $T_3$, not $T_2$! > > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #4:** Forgetting to reset the sequence counter ($SC \leftarrow 0$) at the end of execution. Without this reset, the CPU will continue cycling through $T_0 \rightarrow T_1 \rightarrow \dots$ indefinitely, causing the next instruction fetch to fail. Loss: 1 mark. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Five High-Frequency Mark-Deduction Points Across All Hardwired Control Questions:** > 1. **Wrong decoder-line assignment** — confusing $D_0$ with $D_3$ for opcodes. (–1 mark) > 2. **Omitting the `E` (flag-set) signal** in arithmetic instructions. (–0.5 mark) > 3. **Mixing up the timing slot** — e.g., writing $WRITE = D_3 \cdot T_3$ instead of $D_3 \cdot T_2$ for `STA`. (–1 mark) > 4. **Forgetting to reset the sequence counter** at the end of micro-routine. (–0.5 mark) > 5. **Not expressing equations in canonical SOP form** before simplifying. KTU examiners explicitly look for the SOP expansion. (–1 mark) > 6. **Including the `E` flag for unconditional instructions** like `BUN` where the ALU is not used. (–0.5 mark) --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hardwired Control** - **Definition:** Control logic built from fixed combinational gates (no microcode ROM). - **Key building blocks:** IR → Decoder ($D_i$) → AND-OR Matrix + Sequence Counter ($T_k$) + Flags → Control Word. - **Sequence counter:** 2-bit (4 states) for the basic Mano computer; cascadable to 3 or 4 bits. - **Canonical Boolean form:** Always express control signals as $C_i = \sum (D_j \cdot T_k \cdot Flag)$ first; then minimize. - **Fetch is universal:** $T_0$ always asserts $AR \leftarrow PC$, $IR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $PC \leftarrow PC+1$. (1 mark guaranteed if remembered) - **Memory `READ`** = $T_0 + (\text{any instruction needing operand from memory})$. - **Memory `WRITE`** = (store-type instruction's execute timing). - **`E` flag** must be set in every instruction that updates the AC/DR from the ALU. - **Sequence counter reset** must occur at the *last* execute micro-operation of every instruction. - **Branches depend on flags** — derived equations for `BUN`, `BSA`, `ISZ` *must* include a flag term where applicable. - **Speed advantage:** No microinstruction fetch → HCU is 2–3× faster than microprogrammed CU. - **Drawback:** Inflexible — instruction-set changes require complete redesign of the gate network. - **Used in:** RISC-V, ARM Cortex-M, GPUs, custom ASICs. - **KTU-typical control word width:** **21 bits** (Mano's classic design). - **Critical-path timing constraint:** $T_c \ge t_{decoder} + t_{gate} + t_{ALU} + t_{memory}$. - **Differentiator vs Microprogrammed CU:** HCU = hardware-only, parallel, fast, rigid; MCU = ROM-based, sequential, slower, flexible. ---

topics.title: Microprogrammed Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microprogrammed Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microprogrammed Control** is a control unit design methodology in which the control logic of a digital computer is implemented as a sequence of microinstructions stored in a dedicated **Control Memory (CM)** — a fast, usually read-only memory (ROM) — rather than being realized by discrete combinational and sequential logic gates (as in hardwired control). Each machine-level instruction is interpreted (executed) by fetching a microprogram (a routine of microinstructions) from control memory, with each microinstruction directly generating the necessary control signals to manipulate the datapath, registers, ALU, and memory for one primitive micro-operation or micro-cycle (a single clock period of the datapath).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the microprogrammed control unit is classified as a **stored-logic** control unit, in contrast to the **random logic (hardwired)** counterpart. The systematic procedure of writing microinstructions is called **Microprogramming**, a term coined by **Maurice V. Wilkes** in 1951 at the University of Cambridge.

## 1.2 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Control Word (CW)** | A binary pattern whose individual bits act as control signals for one micro-operation. |
| **Microinstruction** | One word in control memory encoding the control word plus housekeeping fields. |
| **Microprogram** | A sequence of microinstructions implementing one machine instruction. |
| **Micro-operation** | The most elementary register-transfer action performed in a single clock cycle. |
| **Firmware** | Microprograms stored permanently in ROM / PLA / EPROM. |
| **Control Memory (CM)** | High-speed memory that holds all microprograms. |
| **Control Address Register (CAR)** | Holds the address of the next microinstruction to be fetched from CM. |
| **Control Data Register (CDR)** | Latches the microinstruction just read from CM. |
| **Subroutine Register (SBR)** | Saves return addresses for microprogram subroutines. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Orchestral Conductor & The Tablature"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a large orchestra playing a symphony.** A *hardwired* conductor would have every nuance of dynamics, tempo, and entry memorized in his head and shout cues to the players with no score in front of him. A *microprogrammed* conductor instead reads a detailed **tablature (sheet music)** — note by note — telling the violins when to play, the cellos when to swell, the brass when to enter. The orchestra (datapath: ALU, registers, memory) follows whatever the sheet tells them at each beat (clock cycle).

In this analogy:
- **Datapath** = Orchestra
- **Microinstruction** = A single line/breadth of the musical score
- **Control Memory (ROM)** = The scorebook
- **CAR** = The conductor's finger on the current line
- **CDR** = The current line of music the conductor is reading
- **Microprogram** = An entire movement of the symphony
- **External Condition Flags (CC)** = Feedback from the audience / orchestra (e.g., "did the soloist finish?")

## 1.4 Microprogrammed vs Hardwired Control — At a Glance

| Attribute | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Combinational / sequential gates, flip-flops | Stored microinstructions in CM |
| Speed | **Very fast** (no memory fetch) | Slower (one or more CM accesses per clock) |
| Flexibility | Difficult to modify | Easy to alter — just rewrite CM |
| Design effort | High for complex CPUs | Systematic, structured |
| Best suited for | RISC, simple control, real-time | CISC, complex instruction sets |
| Hardware cost | High for many instructions | Lower design cost, more memory |
| Diagnosability | Hard to debug | Microprograms can be inspected |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly emphasizes that microprogrammed control trades a small amount of speed for **systematic design, regularity, and ease of change** — a critical trade-off in any computer architecture decision.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Flow — How a Microprogrammed Control Unit Works

The microprogrammed control unit operates as a strict **fetch-execute micro-cycle** within the broader machine-instruction cycle. The process can be decomposed into the following disciplined sequence:

1. **Initiate Fetch** — When a new machine instruction is loaded into the Instruction Register (IR), the upper bits of the opcode are mapped to a starting micro-address (this mapping is done by a small **Mapping / PLA Logic** block).
2. **Load CAR** — The starting address is placed into the Control Address Register (CAR).
3. **Microinstruction Fetch** — Control Memory, addressed by CAR, presents the corresponding word on its output bus; this word is latched into the **Control Data Register (CDR)** at the next clock edge.
4. **Signal Generation** — The control field of the microinstruction is decoded to activate the exact set of control signals (gates of the datapath) for the current micro-operation (e.g., `R1_out, R2_in, ADD`).
5. **Address Sequencing** — The next-address logic of the microinstruction is evaluated together with external condition flags (`CC`) and feedback signals, producing the **next CAR value** for the following cycle.
6. **Repeat** — Steps 3–5 repeat until a microinstruction with the special control bit **"END"** is encountered, signalling that the microprogram for the current machine instruction is complete.
7. **Next Machine Instruction** — Control returns to step 1, fetching the mapping for the next opcode.

## 2.2 The Microinstruction Format

A typical microinstruction (control word) is divided into **five conceptual fields** when fully described:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
F_1 & F_2 & F_3 & CD & BR \\
\hline
\end{array}
}
$$

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| $F_1, F_2, F_3$ | Three function fields encoding the *micro-operation* (e.g., `ADD R1, R2`, `SHIFT LEFT R3`, `READ MEM`, `INCREMENT PC`). |
| `CD` | Condition field (e.g., `Unconditional`, `If Z=1`, `If N=1`, `Always`). |
| `BR` | Branch field (e.g., `Next`, `Jump to address`, `Call subroutine`, `Return from subroutine`). |
| `AD` | Address field — target address if a branch/jump is taken (omitted in some formats). |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Microprogramming

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why store control in memory?** Because the control logic of a complex CPU has thousands of product terms, the boolean equations for every control signal explode combinatorially. Storing the truth table of those equations in a ROM is far simpler to design, verify, and patch than implementing the equivalent logic in gates. Maurice Wilkes recognized this in 1951: **"The control section of an automatic digital computer can be made less complex and more systematic by replacing combinational logic with a sequence of elementary control words stored in a memory."**

## 2.4 Horizontal vs Vertical Microinstruction Format

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly asks students to **distinguish between horizontal and vertical microinstruction formats**. Memorize the following table — it is high-yield for the ESE.

| Property | Horizontal | Vertical |
|---|---|---|
| Word length | **Long** (one bit per control signal — tens to hundreds of bits) | **Short** (4 to 16 bits typically) |
| Encoding | **Unencoded / direct** | **Highly encoded** (must pass through a decoder) |
| Parallelism | **High** — many signals simultaneously | **Low** — one micro-operation per cycle |
| Microinstruction count per machine instruction | Fewer (compact code) | More (longer programs) |
| Speed of decode | Fast (just a wire fan-out) | Slower (extra decoder delay) |
| Flexibility | High | Limited by encoding scheme |
| Typical use | CISC control stores, microcontrollers | CISC emulation, easy ROM packing |

**Hybrid (Diagonal) Format:** A pragmatic compromise — partially encoded fields. Most modern microprogrammed control units use a hybrid form.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize these formulas verbatim.** They are favorites of KTU valuation key-setters for Microprogrammed Control questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Control Memory (CM) capacity** | $C_{CM} = 2^{k} \times n$ bits | $k$ = number of address bits; $n$ = word length (bits) |
| 2 | **Number of microinstructions** | $N = 2^{k}$ | If $k$ address bits are available |
| 3 | **CM capacity in bytes** | $C_{CM} = 2^{k} \times \dfrac{n}{8}$ | $n$ must be divisible by 8 for clean conversion |
| 4 | **Address-generation logic delay** | $t_{seq} = t_{ROM} + t_{mux} + t_{setup}$ | $t_{ROM}$: ROM access; $t_{mux}$: MUX select; $t_{setup}$: CAR setup time |
| 5 | **Mapping function (opcode → start address)** | $A_{start} = \text{opcode} \ll s \text{ concatenated with 0s}$ | $s$ = shift amount to position opcode into CAR bits |
| 6 | **Next Address (Unconditional)** | $A_{next} = \text{CAR} + 1$ | Default: increment |
| 7 | **Next Address (Conditional Jump)** | $A_{next} = \begin{cases} A_{AD} & \text{if } CD \text{ is true and condition flag matches} \\ \text{CAR}+1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | $A_{AD}$: address field in microinstruction |
| 8 | **Subroutine Call** | $A_{next} = A_{AD}$ ; $\text{SBR} \leftarrow \text{CAR}+1$ | Saves return in SBR |
| 9 | **Return from Subroutine** | $A_{next} = \text{SBR}$ | Restores saved address |
| 10 | **Horizontal microinstruction bit count** | $n_H = \text{(number of distinct control signals)} + \text{(address bits)} + \text{(condition bits)}$ | One bit per signal |
| 11 | **Vertical microinstruction bit count** | $n_V = \lceil \log_2 M \rceil$ | $M$ = number of encoded micro-operations |
| 12 | **Wilkes control memory size** | $C = 2^{i} \times (j + k)$ bits | $i$ = micro-address bits; $j$ = control bits; $k$ = next-address bits |
| 13 | **Encoding efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{used codes}}{2^{n_V}} \times 100\%$ | Vertical format |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Microprogrammed control is the **conceptual ancestor of firmware, BIOS, microcode, and even modern CPU micro-op caches (e.g., Intel's µops)**. It is the reason a single chip design can support multiple instruction-set revisions (e.g., x86 microcode updates) without a hardware redesign. In modern contexts, microprogrammed control is also the foundation of:
- **Emulators and virtual machines** (e.g., QEMU's Tiny Code Generator).
- **Microcontroller firmware** (8051, PIC).
- **Bit-sliced microprogrammable CPUs** (AMD Am2901, Am2910 sequencer).
- **Network processors and reconfigurable logic** (P4, PISA architectures).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Control Memory Capacity

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU question gives the number of microinstructions and word length, asking for ROM size. The reverse — finding the number of addressable locations given the ROM capacity — is equally frequent. We will derive both.

**Given:**
- Word length of microinstruction: $n$ bits
- Number of addressable words in CM: $N$

**Step 1.** The CM is a ROM with $N$ words and $n$ bits per word. Each addressable word needs a unique address.

**Step 2.** The number of address bits $k$ required to address $N$ words is the smallest integer such that:

$$
2^{k} \ge N
$$

**Step 3.** Solving for $k$:

$$
k = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil
$$

**Step 4.** Total capacity of the CM in bits:

$$
C_{bits} = N \times n
$$

**Step 5.** Capacity in bytes:

$$
C_{bytes} = \frac{N \times n}{8}
$$

**Step 6.** If only $k$ address bits are actually used (some addresses may be wasted), the *usable* capacity is $2^{k} \times n$ bits, and the *utilization* is:

$$
U = \frac{N}{2^{k}} \times 100\%
$$

---

### Worked Example 3.1

> **Problem:** A microprogrammed control unit must store $N = 320$ microinstructions, each of length $n = 32$ bits. Find the minimum CM size in bits and bytes. Also compute address-bit utilization.

**Step 1.** Minimum $k$:

$$
2^{k} \ge 320
$$

Try $k = 8$: $2^{8} = 256$ → too small. Try $k = 9$: $2^{9} = 512$ → sufficient. So $k = 9$.

**Step 2.** Total CM capacity in bits:

$$
C_{bits} = 2^{9} \times 32 = 512 \times 32 = 16384 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3.** Capacity in bytes:

$$
C_{bytes} = \frac{16384}{8} = 2048 \text{ bytes} = 2 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 4.** Utilization:

$$
U = \frac{320}{512} \times 100\% = 62.5\%
$$

**Answer:** $C_{bits} = 16384$ bits, $C_{bytes} = 2$ KB, $U = 62.5\%$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Next-Address Generation for Conditional Microbranch

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "branch field" logic is the single most-asked numerical sub-part in KTU ESE Module 3 papers. The formula is short, but students often forget the *default* fall-through case.

The next address $A_{next}$ is computed as:

$$
A_{next} =
\begin{cases}
A_{AD} & \text{if branch} = \text{JUMP and } (CC \text{ true}) \\
\text{CAR} + 1 & \text{if branch} = \text{NEXT} \\
A_{AD} & \text{if branch} = \text{UNCONDITIONAL JUMP} \\
\text{SBR} & \text{if branch} = \text{RETURN}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step-by-step expansion** for the conditional case (single condition bit $CD$, single condition flag $Z$):

**Step 1.** Decode the `BR` field. Let $b_1 b_0$ be the 2-bit branch encoding:

| $b_1 b_0$ | Operation |
|---|---|
| 00 | Next (CAR+1) |
| 01 | Conditional jump on $Z=1$ |
| 10 | Conditional jump on $Z=0$ |
| 11 | Unconditional jump |

**Step 2.** The MUX selecting the next address has two data inputs:
- Input 0: $\text{CAR} + 1$ (incrementer output)
- Input 1: $A_{AD}$ (address field of microinstruction, possibly with $Z$ OR-ed into LSB)

**Step 3.** The select line $S$ of the MUX is:

$$
S = b_1 \lor (b_0 \land Z)
$$

(Or analogously for $Z=0$, depending on the encoding.)

**Step 4.** The output of the MUX feeds the input of CAR. After the clock edge:

$$
A_{next} = MUX(S, \text{CAR}+1, A_{AD})
$$

---

### Worked Example 3.2

> **Problem:** The current microinstruction in CAR is `0110 1010` (binary). The microinstruction's `BR` field is `10` (jump to address field if $Z=1$) and the address field $A_{AD} = 11010100$. The $Z$ flag is currently `0`. Find the next value of CAR.

**Step 1.** Since $Z = 0$ and `BR = 10` (jump-on-true), the condition is **false**.

**Step 2.** Default next address is the incrementer output:

$$
A_{next} = \text{CAR} + 1 = 01101010_2 + 1 = 01101011_2
$$

**Step 3.** The address field $11010100$ is *not* loaded.

**Answer:** $A_{next} = 01101011_2 = 0x6B = 107_{10}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mapping Function from Opcode to Start Address

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love this derivation. The mapping logic translates an $m$-bit opcode to a $k$-bit starting micro-address, often by zero-padding on the right.

**Given:** Opcode in IR is $m$ bits wide. CAR is $k$ bits wide, with $k > m$.

**Step 1.** The opcode is placed in the **leftmost** (most significant) $m$ bits of CAR. The remaining $k - m$ bits are **cleared to 0**.

$$
A_{start} = \text{opcode}_{m\text{-bit}} \circ \underbrace{00\cdots 0}_{(k-m)\text{ zeros}}
$$

**Step 2.** Therefore the starting address of the microprogram for that opcode is always a multiple of $2^{(k-m)}$.

**Step 3.** Each microprogram is allotted $2^{(k-m)}$ consecutive locations in CM, with the lowest address being the start.

---

### Worked Example 3.3

> **Problem:** The IR has a 4-bit opcode. The CAR is 8 bits. The opcode in IR is `1010`. Find the starting address of the corresponding microprogram.

**Step 1.** $k = 8$, $m = 4$, so the number of trailing zeros is $k - m = 4$.

**Step 2.** Build the start address:

$$
A_{start} = 1010 \, 0000_2
$$

**Step 3.** Convert to decimal:

$$
A_{start} = 128 + 32 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 160_{10}
$$

**Step 4.** Microprogram for opcode `1010` can occupy addresses $160$ through $175$ ($160 + 2^{4} - 1 = 175$).

**Answer:** Start address = $10100000_2 = 160_{10}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Comparison of Horizontal vs Vertical Word Length

**Step 1.** Suppose the datapath has $S$ distinct control signals (e.g., 28 in the Mano example CPU).

**Step 2.** Horizontal word length:

$$
n_H = S + a + c
$$

where $S$ = control bits, $a$ = address bits, $c$ = condition bits.

**Step 3.** Suppose we group the $S$ signals into $g$ groups of average size $S/g$ signals each. Each group needs $\lceil \log_2 (S/g) \rceil$ bits when encoded.

**Step 4.** Vertical word length (approximate, assuming groups are balanced and there is one decoded micro-operation per group):

$$
n_V = \sum_{i=1}^{g} \left\lceil \log_2 \left( \frac{S}{g} \right) \right\rceil + a + c
$$

**Step 5.** Encoding compression ratio:

$$
\rho = \frac{n_V}{n_H} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 6.** Vertical microinstructions execute **slower** because the decoder adds a delay of $t_{dec}$ per group, but vertical packing allows larger programs to fit in the same CM.

---

### Worked Example 3.4

> **Problem:** A datapath has $S = 28$ control signals. The control unit uses (a) horizontal, (b) vertical format with $g = 4$ groups. Address and condition fields together use $a + c = 9$ bits in both cases. Compute $n_H$ and $n_V$.

**Step 1.** Horizontal:

$$
n_H = 28 + 9 = 37 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2.** Vertical: each group has $S/g = 28/4 = 7$ signals. Bits per group = $\lceil \log_2 7 \rceil = 3$.

**Step 3.**

$$
n_V = 4 \times 3 + 9 = 12 + 9 = 21 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4.** Compression ratio:

$$
\rho = \frac{21}{37} \times 100\% \approx 56.8\%
$$

**Answer:** $n_H = 37$ bits, $n_V = 21$ bits, saving $\approx 43.2\%$ of CM capacity with vertical encoding.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Format

| Design Goal | Recommended Format | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum speed | **Horizontal** | No decoder delay |
| Minimize CM size | **Vertical** | Fewer bits per microinstruction |
| Easy to write microcode | **Vertical** | Human-friendly mnemonics |
| Maximum parallel micro-operations per cycle | **Horizontal** | Multiple signals active simultaneously |
| Compatibility with CISC | Either (often hybrid) | Trade-off; most CISC use hybrid |
| Compatibility with RISC pipelines | **Hardwired** | Microprogramming overhead too high |
| Emulation of foreign ISA | **Horizontal / Hybrid** | Field translation flexibility |
| Real-time / embedded | **Hardwired / Horizontal** | Determinism and speed |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Microprogrammed Control Unit

The figure below shows the complete block architecture of a microprogrammed control unit. All signal names are uppercase alphanumeric only, in line with the Mermaid safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IR[Instruction Register] --> MAP[Mapping Logic PLA]
    MAP --> CAR[Control Address Register]
    MUXSEL[Branch MUX Select Logic] --> MUX[Address MUX]
    CAR --> INC[Incrementer CAR Plus 1]
    INC --> MUX
    CDR[Address Field of CDR] --> MUX
    SBRREG[Subroutine Register SBR] --> MUX
    MUX --> CAR
    CAR --> CM[Control Memory ROM]
    CM --> CDR[Control Data Register]
    CDR --> DEC[Field Decoder]
    DEC --> SIG[Control Signals to Datapath]
    CC[External Condition Flags] --> MUXSEL
    CDR --> MUXSEL
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

1. **IR → Mapping Logic → CAR**: The opcode bits of the currently fetched machine instruction are routed through a PLA or simple combinational network to obtain the *starting* micro-address of the corresponding microprogram. This is loaded into the CAR.
2. **CAR → Control Memory**: The CAR provides the address of the next microinstruction to read from CM.
3. **CM → CDR**: The microinstruction read from CM is latched into the CDR at the clock edge.
4. **CDR → Decoder → Control Signals**: The functional fields of the microinstruction are decoded (for vertical) or directly routed (for horizontal) to drive the datapath gates and register enable lines.
5. **CDR → Branch MUX Select**: The branch field of the microinstruction, combined with external condition flags (CC), selects one of the MUX inputs.
6. **MUX → CAR**: The selected next address is clocked into the CAR for the next cycle.

## 4.2 Microinstruction Format Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MI[Microinstruction Word] --> F1[Function Field F1]
    MI --> F2[Function Field F2]
    MI --> F3[Function Field F3]
    MI --> CD[Condition Field CD]
    MI --> BR[Branch Field BR]
    MI --> AD[Address Field AD]
    F1 --> DEC1[Decoder 1]
    F2 --> DEC2[Decoder 2]
    F3 --> DEC3[Decoder 3]
    CD --> CONLOG[Condition Logic]
    BR --> SELOG[Branch Select Logic]
    AD --> NXTADDR[Next Address Bus]
    CONLOG --> SELOG
    CC2[Condition Flags] --> CONLOG
    SELOG --> MUXADDR[Address MUX]
    SELOG --> AD
```

## 4.3 Microprogram Sequencing Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start New Machine Instruction] --> B[Read Opcode from IR]
    B --> C[Mapping Logic Maps Opcode to Start Address]
    C --> D[Load Start Address into CAR]
    D --> E[Fetch Microinstruction from CM into CDR]
    E --> F[Decode Control Fields and Generate Control Signals]
    F --> G[Datapath Performs Microoperation]
    G --> H{End Bit Set}
    H -- No --> I[Compute Next Address from Branch Field CD Flags and AD]
    I --> D
    H -- Yes --> J[Return to Step A for Next Machine Instruction]
    J --> B
```

## 4.4 Wilkes Control — Diagonal Matrix Representation

The original 1951 Wilkes microprogrammed control organized the CM as a **2D matrix** of diodes, with rows representing microinstruction words and two halves of the word representing the *control signals* (left half) and the *next address* (right half). For each row $i$, the next address is computed as:

$$
A_{next} = \left( b_1 \cdot a_1 \right) \lor \left( b_2 \cdot a_2 \right) \lor \cdots \lor \left( b_n \cdot a_n \right) \lor \left( C \right)
$$

where $b_j$ are the branch-selection bits in the microinstruction, $a_j$ are the corresponding matrix columns (forming the next address), and $C$ is the incrementer output (default next address = row+1).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROW1[Microinstruction Row i] --> COLA[Control Signal Half Word]
    ROW1 --> COLB[Next Address Half Word]
    COLB --> BO[Branch Decoder]
    BO --> NXTADDR2[Next Address Selection Matrix]
    INC2[Incrementer Row i Plus 1] --> NXTADDR2
    NXTADDR2 --> ROWSEL[Select Row i Plus 1 for Next Cycle]
    COLA --> CSIG[Control Signal Bus to Datapath]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Wilkes design is conceptually equivalent to a ROM with two halves: one half drives the datapath (control signals), the other half drives the address sequencer. This is the historical seed of every modern microprogrammed control unit.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define microprogrammed control unit. List any two advantages and one disadvantage compared to hardwired control.**

**Model Answer:**

A microprogrammed control unit is a control unit in which the control logic is implemented as a sequence of microinstructions stored in a **Control Memory (CM)**, with each machine instruction interpreted by fetching and executing a microprogram from CM.

**Advantages:**
1. **Design is systematic and modular** — control logic is stored in memory rather than realized by ad-hoc gate networks, simplifying the design of complex CPUs.
2. **Easy to modify / patch** — changing the microcode (in EPROM or writable CM) is enough to fix bugs or add instructions, without rewiring hardware.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Slower** than hardwired control because each clock cycle (or multiple cycles) requires a CM access, adding ROM-access delay to the critical path.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "it is more flexible" but forget to say *why* — a complete answer must mention that flexibility comes from the **stored nature of microinstructions in a rewritable CM**. Marks are lost on half-baked advantages.

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare horizontal and vertical microinstruction formats in three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Horizontal | Vertical |
|---|---|---|
| Word length | Long (one bit per control signal) | Short (encoded) |
| Encoding | Direct / unencoded | Highly encoded via decoders |
| Parallel micro-operations per cycle | Many | Usually one |
| CM size | Larger | Smaller |
| Speed of decoding | Fast (no decoder) | Slower (decoder delay) |
| Microprogram length | Shorter | Longer |

Any three clearly-stated points (e.g., word length, encoding, speed) fetch full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 3 — Internal Choice Q]** *(i) Explain the organization of a microprogrammed control unit with a neat block diagram. (ii) Design the microprogram for the fetch routine of a basic single-address computer.

#### (a) Block Diagram & Explanation [7 Marks]

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**1. Opcode Mapping (1 mark):** The **Mapping Logic** block (typically a PLA or a small ROM) accepts the opcode from the IR and produces the starting micro-address for the corresponding microprogram. This is loaded into the **Control Address Register (CAR)**.

**2. Control Memory (1 mark):** The CM is a high-speed ROM (or Writable Control Store) containing all microprograms. The output width of CM is the microinstruction length.

**3. Control Data Register (CDR) (1 mark):** The microinstruction read from CM is latched into CDR, which holds it for the entire micro-cycle.

**4. Decoder (1 mark):** The function fields of the microinstruction are decoded into individual control signals that drive the datapath (register selects, ALU function, memory R/W, etc.).

**5. Next-Address Logic (1.5 marks):** Composed of an incrementer, a multiplexer, and a subroutine register (SBR). The MUX selects between `CAR+1`, the address field of the current microinstruction, or the SBR contents, based on the `BR` (branch) field and condition flags.

**6. Sequencing (1 mark):** After each micro-cycle, the selected next address is clocked into CAR, and the next microinstruction is fetched. The cycle continues until an `END` microinstruction is reached, after which control returns to fetch the next machine instruction.

**7. Feedback (0.5 mark):** External condition flags (Z, N, C, V) and status signals from the datapath feed back into the next-address logic to enable conditional microbranches.

#### (b) Microprogram for the Fetch Routine [7 Marks]

The **fetch routine** is the universal microprogram executed at the start of every machine instruction cycle. It performs:
- `AR ← PC` (send PC to Address Register)
- `DR ← M[AR]` (memory read into Data Register)
- `IR ← DR` (transfer opcode to Instruction Register)
- `PC ← PC + 1` (increment PC)

**Symbolic microprogram (5 microinstructions typical, in vertical/assembler style):**

| Address | Micro-operation | CD | BR | AD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `AR ← PC` | — | Next | — |
| 1 | `DR ← M[AR], Read` | — | Next | — |
| 2 | `AR ← DR[0..11], DR ← IR` | — | Next | — |
| 3 | `PC ← PC + 1` | — | Next | — |
| 4 | `Map CAR ← PLA(IR[12..14])` | — | Jump to MAP output | — |

**Encoding (binary, 20-bit microinstruction example):**

| Addr | $F_3$ | $F_2$ | $F_1$ | CD | BR | AD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 00000 | 100 | 000 | 110 | 00 | 00 | 00000 |
| 00001 | 010 | 000 | 000 | 00 | 00 | 00000 |
| 00010 | 011 | 110 | 000 | 00 | 00 | 00000 |
| 00011 | 100 | 001 | 101 | 00 | 00 | 00000 |
| 00100 | 111 | 000 | 000 | 00 | 11 | xxxxx |

(The last microinstruction's AD field is replaced by the mapping PLA output.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls:** (1) Forgetting that the `Read` signal of memory must be asserted during the `DR ← M[AR]` step. (2) Forgetting to increment PC *before* the microprogram jumps to the mapped routine (otherwise, the next fetch will re-read the same opcode). (3) Writing `IR ← M[AR]` directly without going through DR — most textbook designs use DR as a buffer to free the memory bus for the next micro-operation.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 3 — Internal Choice Q]** *(a) Explain the concept of microinstruction sequencing with a block diagram of the next-address generation logic. (b) A control memory has 1024 words, each of 24 bits. Find the address bits, control bits, and the address of the microprogram for opcode `110` if mapping is done with 4 leading zeros.

#### (a) Microinstruction Sequencing — Next-Address Logic [7 Marks]

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**1. The Sequencing Problem (1 mark):** Once a microinstruction is being executed, the control unit must compute the *next* address to fetch. Three cases exist: (i) sequential, (ii) conditional/unconditional branch, (iii) subroutine call/return.

**2. Default: Incrementer (1 mark):** The simplest next address is `CAR + 1`. A binary incrementer adds 1 to the current CAR contents, producing the next sequential address.

**3. Conditional / Unconditional Branch (2 marks):** The microinstruction contains an address field $AD$. A MUX selects between `CAR+1` and $AD$ based on the `BR` field and condition flags. The `CD` (condition) field tests flags such as `Z`, `N`, `C`. The MUX's select line is:

$$
S = (BR = \text{JUMP}) \land (CC = \text{TRUE})
$$

**4. Subroutine Call / Return (1.5 marks):** A call saves `CAR+1` into the **Subroutine Register (SBR)** and jumps to $AD$. A return loads `CAR ← SBR`. The SBR stack can be implemented as a small RAM (LIFO) for nested subroutines.

**5. Initial Fetch Address (1 mark):** At the start of every machine instruction, the **Mapping PLA** overrides the MUX to load the starting address of the appropriate microprogram (computed from the opcode).

**6. Block diagram (0.5 mark):** All five components — Incrementer, SBR, Mapping PLA, MUX, CAR — form a feedback loop. The MUX select is driven by a combinational block that takes the `BR` and `CD` fields from CDR plus external condition flags.

#### (b) Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Given:** CM has 1024 words × 24 bits per word. Opcode = `110` (3 bits). Mapping is "opcode with 4 leading zeros" (interpreted as opcode placed in the 4 most significant bits of CAR, with the remaining bits zero).

> **Note on interpretation:** "4 leading zeros" can mean two different things. The most common KTU convention is: the CAR is wider than 3 bits, and the opcode `110` is placed in the upper 3 bits with the next **higher bit being 0** (4 leading zeros means: bits 7, 6, 5, 4 = 0; bits 3, 2, 1 = opcode 110; bit 0 = 0). However, the more common interpretation is that "4 leading zeros" pads the opcode to occupy the lower 3 bits of an 8-bit address with the upper 4 bits = 0. We will use the latter, which is the standard KTU solution.

**Step 1. Address bits (k):**

$$
k = \log_2 1024 = 10 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2. Control bits (function + condition + branch):** The microinstruction has 24 bits. Of these, the address field consumes some bits.

If we allocate 10 bits to the address field (one per CM location), then:

$$
\text{Control bits} = 24 - 10 = 14 \text{ bits}
$$

These 14 control bits are split among function fields, condition, and branch.

**Step 3. Microprogram starting address for opcode `110`:**

Method 1 — Opcode placed in the upper 3 bits of the 10-bit address, with the lower 7 bits = 0:

$$
A_{start} = 110 \, 0000000_2 = 768_{10}
$$

The microprogram occupies addresses 768 through 895 ($768 + 2^7 - 1$).

**Answer:**
- Address bits: $k = 10$
- Control bits: $14$
- Start address of microprogram: $A_{start} = 1100000000_2 = 768_{10}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse "leading zeros" with "trailing zeros." Re-read the question — "4 leading zeros" almost always means the opcode is shifted to the *right*, with zeros on the *left* (MSB side). **Examiner's Pitfall 2:** Many forget to subtract address bits from total microinstruction length to find the *control* bit count. **Examiner's Pitfall 3:** Some students compute `1024 × 24 = 24576` bits and present that as "address bits" — this is the *CM capacity*, not the address bit count. Read the question stem carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the last 24 hours before the KTU ESE.** Tick each item mentally.

- [ ] **Definition:** Microprogrammed control = control logic stored as microinstructions in a ROM called Control Memory (CM).
- [ ] **Key inventor:** Maurice Wilkes (1951, Cambridge).
- [ ] **Three primary blocks:** Control Memory (ROM), CAR, CDR.
- [ ] **Mapping function:** Opcode → Start micro-address via a small PLA / ROM.
- [ ] **Microinstruction fields:** $F_1, F_2, F_3$ (function), `CD` (condition), `BR` (branch), `AD` (address).
- [ ] **Default sequencing:** `CAR ← CAR + 1` (incrementer).
- [ ] **Conditional branch:** $A_{next} = A_{AD}$ if condition is true; else $A_{next} = \text{CAR}+1$.
- [ ] **Subroutine:** Save return in SBR, jump to subroutine. Restore from SBR on return.
- [ ] **Horizontal vs Vertical:** Horizontal = 1 bit per signal, long word, fast; Vertical = encoded, short word, compact, slow.
- [ ] **Wilkes Control:** 2D diode matrix; one half drives datapath, other half drives next-address.
- [ ] **CM size formula:** $C_{CM} = 2^{k} \times n$ bits, where $k$ = address bits, $n$ = word length.
- [ ] **Address bit formula:** $k = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$, where $N$ = number of microinstructions.
- [ ] **Advantages over hardwired:** flexibility, regularity, ease of modification, systematic design.
- [ ] **Disadvantages over hardwired:** slower (ROM access delay), more memory, more sequencing logic.
- [ ] **End-bit:** A special control field bit that signals the end of a microprogram.
- [ ] **Mapping convention:** Opcode occupies the **most significant** bits of CAR; trailing bits are 0.
- [ ] **Total microprogram storage allocation per opcode:** $2^{(k-m)}$ microinstructions, where $m$ = opcode width.
- [ ] **Remember the fetch routine's 4 micro-operations:** `AR←PC`, `DR←M[AR]`, `IR←DR[op]`, `PC←PC+1`.

> **Final Word:** In KTU ESE Module 3, marks for Microprogrammed Control are typically split as **2–3 marks** for a short answer (definition / comparison) and **7–14 marks** for a long answer combining a block diagram with a numerical design problem. Practice drawing the block diagram from memory and solving at least 3–4 CM-size and mapping-address numericals before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microinstructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microinstructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microinstruction (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **microinstruction** is a binary-encoded control word stored in **Control Memory (CM)** that specifies a single, indivisible set of **micro-operations** to be executed during one **clock cycle (micro-cycle)** of the processor, along with the address of the next microinstruction to be fetched.

It is the **smallest, atomic unit of a microprogram**, the lowest level of control granularity in a microprogrammed control unit (MPCU). Each bit (or group of encoded bits) within a microinstruction directly drives one or more control signals of the datapath.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Relationship in Microprogrammed Control:**
> - **Micro-operation** → an elementary register-transfer action (e.g., `R2 ← R1 + R2`)
> - **Microinstruction** → binary word that activates one set of micro-operations
> - **Microprogram** → ordered sequence of microinstructions implementing a macro-instruction
> - **Macro-instruction** → the user-visible machine instruction (e.g., `ADD R1, R2`)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **musical conductor's score sheet**:
- The **full orchestra** = the processor datapath (ALU, registers, buses, memory)
- Each **musician's action** at a specific beat = a **micro-operation** (e.g., "violin plays note C")
- A **single line of the score** that tells *which* musicians play *what* at one beat = a **microinstruction**
- The **entire score** for one symphony movement = a **microprogram**
- A **complete concert program** (symphony, concerto, overture) = the full **control memory** repertoire

Just as the score is read beat-by-beat by the conductor, the control unit **fetches** microinstructions from control memory **cycle-by-cycle** to orchestrate the datapath.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Micro-cycle time (T)**: typically **100 ns to 500 ns** in modern MPCUs, with advanced designs reaching sub-**10 ns**.
- **Control Memory word size (microinstruction width)**: ranges from **20 bits (vertical)** to **≥ 100 bits (horizontal)**.
- **Control Memory depth**: typically **2K to 4K words** (each word = one microinstruction).
- **Address field width**: log₂(depth) — e.g., for **4096 words** ⇒ **12 address bits**.
- **Conditional branch decision latency**: must be **≤ 1 micro-cycle** for pipelined microsequencing.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners expect you to **always quote units** (ns, bits, words) and clearly distinguish between *control memory* (ROM/PLA-like) and *main memory* (RAM).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Microinstruction Format – Structural Anatomy

A microinstruction is partitioned into **functional fields**. The standard KTU-traced architecture splits the microinstruction into:

| Field Group | Sub-field | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Field** | F1, F2, F3, …, Fn | Activate specific micro-operations on datapath |
| **Condition Field** | CD (Condition), BR (Branch) | Select branch condition for next address |
| **Address Field** | AD | Address of next microinstruction to fetch |
| **Auxiliary Field** | MAP, CD (select bits) | Support microprogram branching & mapping |

The generic bit-layout is:

$$ \text{Microinstruction Word} = \underbrace{C_1 C_2 \ldots C_k}_{\text{Control Field}} \;|\; \underbrace{CD_1 CD_2}_{\text{Condition}} \;|\; \underbrace{BR}_{\text{Branch}} \;|\; \underbrace{AD_1 AD_2 \ldots AD_n}_{\text{Next Address}} $$

### 2.2 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step

1. **Fetch** the microinstruction whose address is held in the **Control Address Register (CAR)** from Control Memory.
2. **Decode** the Control Field bits; each bit/encoded group asserts a corresponding control signal in the datapath.
3. **Execute** the corresponding micro-operations (register transfers, ALU action, memory R/W) during the current micro-cycle.
4. **Compute Next Address** using the CD, BR, and AD fields combined with status flags (e.g., `Z`, `C`, `N` from CC).
5. **Load** the computed next address into **CAR** at the end of the cycle.
6. **Repeat** indefinitely (until a `HALT` or interrupt microinstruction is reached).

### 2.3 Microinstruction Encoding Strategies (THE most-tested KTU concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three canonical encoding schemes** — KTU frequently tests comparison tables here.

| Strategy | Bits per Control Signal | Decoder Logic | Speed | Word Width |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct / Horizontal** | 1 bit per signal (unencoded) | None — direct wire | **Fastest** | **Widest** (≥ 100 bits) |
| **Indirect / Vertical** | log₂(N) bits (heavily encoded) | Required — multi-level | **Slower** | **Narrowest** (≤ 24 bits) |
| **Field-encoded (Hybrid)** | Bits per group, multiple groups | One decoder per group | **Balanced** | **Moderate** (32–64 bits) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Control Memory depth from address bits | $D = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = address field width in bits |
| 2 | Address field width from depth | $n = \log_2 D$ | bits |
| 3 | Vertical microinstruction width | $W_v = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | $N$ = number of distinct micro-operations |
| 4 | Horizontal microinstruction width | $W_h = N$ | one bit per micro-operation |
| 5 | Microprogram execution time | $T_{exec} = k \times t_{cycle}$ | $k$ = # microinstructions, $t_{cycle}$ = micro-cycle time |
| 6 | Total control memory size | $S = D \times W$ | bits (= $D \times W / 8$ bytes) |
| 7 | Encoding efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{W_v}{W_h}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le \eta < 1$ |
| 8 | Branch decision delay budget | $t_{branch} \le t_{cycle} - t_{setup}$ | ns, $t_{setup}$ = next-address settling time |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule:** In all rows above, do **not** write `|x|` for absolute value — use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to preserve the markdown table.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microprogrammed control** is the foundation of **CISC processors** (e.g., Intel x86 microcode ROM).
- Modern **FPGA bitstreams** are effectively *horizontal microinstructions* for configurable logic blocks.
- **Microcode patching** is how manufacturers fix silicon bugs in shipped processors (e.g., the famous Pentium FDIV bug fix).
- **ASIC verification** uses microinstruction-level tracing to debug datapath timing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Microinstruction Width for *k* Encoding Groups

**Given:** A processor requires $N$ distinct micro-operations, partitioned into $g$ mutually exclusive groups of sizes $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_g$ such that:

$$N = n_1 + n_2 + \cdots + n_g$$

**Find:** The total microinstruction word width $W$ under field-encoding.

**Derivation (Step-by-step):**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Width of group } i &= \lceil \log_2 n_i \rceil &&\text{(binary encoding within group)} \\
\text{Step 2: Total control-field width } &= \sum_{i=1}^{g} \lceil \log_2 n_i \rceil &&\text{(sum of group widths)} \\
\text{Step 3: Add address + condition fields} &= n_{addr} + n_{cond} &&\text{(constant overhead)} \\
\text{Step 4: Total word width } W &= \sum_{i=1}^{g} \lceil \log_2 n_i \rceil + n_{addr} + n_{cond} &&\text{(final expression)} \\
\end{aligned} $$

This formula is the **basis of KTU's "calculate the word size" numerical problems**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Control Memory Sizing for an *n*-bit Instruction Set

**Given:** A CPU supports $I$ macro-instructions, with average microinstruction count $\bar{k}$ per macro-instruction, and microinstruction width $W$ bits.

**Find:** Total control memory capacity $S$ in bits and bytes.

**Derivation:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Number of unique microinstructions} &= I \times \bar{k} &&\text{(worst-case count)} \\
\text{Step 2: Depth of control memory} &= D = I \times \bar{k} &&\text{(rounded up to power of 2)} \\
\text{Step 3: Address bits needed} &= n = \lceil \log_2 D \rceil &&\text{(exact address field width)} \\
\text{Step 4: Total capacity (bits)} &= S_{bits} = D \times W &&\text{(depth × width)} \\
\text{Step 5: Total capacity (bytes)} &= S_{bytes} = \dfrac{D \times W}{8} &&\text{(convert bits → bytes)} \\
\end{aligned} $$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A processor has **8 micro-operations grouped into three exclusive groups** of sizes 2, 3, and 3. Find:
(a) Control field width under field-encoding.
(b) Width if fully horizontal.
(c) Width if fully vertical.
(d) Encoding efficiency of field-encoding vs horizontal.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) – Field-encoding:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
W_1 &= \lceil \log_2 2 \rceil = 1 \text{ bit} \\
W_2 &= \lceil \log_2 3 \rceil = 2 \text{ bits} \\
W_3 &= \lceil \log_2 3 \rceil = 2 \text{ bits} \\
W_{field} &= 1 + 2 + 2 = \boxed{5 \text{ bits}} \\
\end{aligned} $$
