> - **Most common mark-loser #4:** Writing speedup as $k$ directly, even for small $n$. The ideal $k$ is achieved **asymptotically** — for finite $n$, $S_n < k$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Pipelining ≠ parallelism at the ALU level.** It is *temporal overlap* of independent operations.
- **Latency per task is unchanged** by pipelining; only **throughput** improves.
- **Three primary classes:** Arithmetic, Instruction, Processor.
- **Seven classification axes:** Functionality, Configurability, Linearity, Data type, Clocking, Issue order, Issue width.
- **Master formulas:**
  - $T_{\text{pipe}}(n) = (k + n - 1) \cdot \tau$
  - $T_{\text{non-pipe}}(n) = n \cdot k \cdot \tau$
  - $S_n = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$
  - $\eta = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$
  - $T_P = \dfrac{n \cdot f}{k + n - 1}$
- **Ideal speedup = number of stages $k$** (Amdahl-style upper bound, only for infinite $n$).
- **Reservation tables** are mandatory for **non-linear** pipelines; collisions come from both direct stage overlap **and** feedback/feedforward reuse.
- **Collision vector** is a binary word; **1** bits = forbidden launch latencies.
- **MAL** = average latency of one collision-free cycle; **MII** = 1/MAL.
- **Hazards:** Structural, Data, Control — each resolved by duplication, forwarding, or prediction respectively.
- **Modern CPUs** are *multifunctional + dynamic + non-linear + synchronous + OoO + multiple-issue* — i.e., they tick **every** box on the classification matrix.
- **Vector pipelines** (Cray, AVX-512) trade per-task latency for **massive** throughput on data-parallel workloads.
- **Static multifunctional** pipelines (DSPs) trade flexibility for predictable timing and power.

> **End of Notes — Classification of Pipeline Processors (Module 3, PEEET416)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction and Arithmetic Pipelines (Design Examples Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Instruction and Arithmetic Pipelines (Design Examples Not Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Pipelining? — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipelining** is an implementation technique in computer architecture where multiple instructions are overlapped in execution by partitioning the computation into a series of independent, sequential stages. Each stage performs a specific sub-operation, and while one instruction is being processed in a given stage, the next instruction enters the preceding stage simultaneously. This is analogous to an **assembly line** in a manufacturing plant.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, pipelining is treated as the **fundamental microarchitectural mechanism** that bridges the gap between raw logic speed (clock period) and instruction throughput. The syllabus explicitly distinguishes between two flavours:

| Pipeline Type | Object Being Pipelined | KTU 2024 Emphasis |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Pipeline** | The steps required to fetch, decode, execute and store one machine instruction | Stages IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB; hazards; forwarding |
| **Arithmetic Pipeline** | The sub-operations of a complex arithmetic functional unit (FP add, FP multiply) | Floating-point adder pipeline, multiplication pipeline |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Module-3 syllabus explicitly says **"Design Examples Not Required"**. This means students are NOT expected to design a 6-stage FP pipeline from scratch. They MUST, however, understand stages, latency, initiation interval, hazards, and the standard MIPS 5-stage datapath.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Laundry Plant 🧺

Imagine a **laundromat with 4 machines**: washer → dryer → folder → packer. Doing 4 loads sequentially takes $4 \times 4 = 16$ time-units. But if you pipeline it (load-1 in dryer while load-2 enters washer), the steady-state cycle is **one load every 1 time-unit**. Total time for $n$ loads becomes $(k + n - 1)$ where $k = 4$ stages.

$$T_{\text{pipelined}} = k + (n-1) \quad \text{vs.} \quad T_{\text{non-pipelined}} = n \cdot k$$

This is *exactly* the same mathematics that governs a CPU pipeline. The CPU's "laundry" is a stream of machine instructions; the "machines" are hardware stages.

## 1.3 The Two Pipeline Families in This Module

### A. Instruction Pipeline (MIPS-style 5-stage)

$$\text{Instruction} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{IF}} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{ID}} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{EX}} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{MEM}} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{WB}}$$

- **IF** — Instruction Fetch from I-cache / PC
- **ID** — Instruction Decode + Register Read
- **EX** — Execute (ALU) / Effective Address
- **MEM** — Memory access (load/store)
- **WB** — Write-Back to register file

### B. Arithmetic Pipeline (e.g., Floating-Point Adder)

A canonical floating-point adder (per the IEEE-754 model) is decomposed into **4 pipeline stages**:

$$\text{Compare exponents} \rightarrow \text{Align mantissas} \rightarrow \text{Add mantissas} \rightarrow \text{Normalise result}$$

## 1.4 Key Vocabulary the Examiner Will Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Terminology (Bookmark these):**
> - **Stage (Segment)**: A single sub-operation of the pipeline.
> - **Latency (L)**: Number of clock cycles to complete *one* task *once* the pipeline is full.
> - **Initiation Interval / Cycle Time (τ)**: Number of cycles between starting two consecutive tasks.
> - **Throughput (h)**: Number of tasks completed per clock cycle = $1/\tau$ for a balanced pipeline.
> - **Speedup (Sₙ)**: Ratio of non-pipelined time to pipelined time.
> - **Efficiency (η)**: Fraction of theoretical peak throughput actually achieved.
> - **Reservation Table**: A 2-D grid showing which stage is busy in which clock cycle.
> - **Bubble / Stall**: A wasted cycle in the pipeline inserted due to a hazard.
> - **Hazard**: Any condition that prevents the next instruction from executing in its designated cycle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Decomposition of a Pipeline

A pipeline is a **synchronous, lock-step dataflow** governed by a single global clock. Conceptually:

1. **Partitioning** — A function $F$ is decomposed into $k$ sequential sub-functions $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k$ such that $F = F_k \circ F_{k-1} \circ \cdots \circ F_1$.
2. **Latching** — Between every pair $(F_i, F_{i+1})$ a *pipeline register* (also called a *latch*) holds the intermediate result for one clock period.
3. **Concurrent Flow** — In every clock cycle, all $k$ stages operate on $k$ different data items, one in each stage.
4. **Steady State** — After $k$ warm-up cycles, the pipeline emits exactly *one* completed result per clock cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pipeline registers cost time:** Each register introduces a setup-time + clock-to-Q delay. This is precisely *why* a pipeline does NOT run $k$ times faster — the clock period grows to accommodate the slowest stage **plus** the register overhead. The speedup bound is:

$$S_{\max} = k \quad \text{(only in the limit of infinite tasks, } n \to \infty\text{)}$$

## 2.2 Why Pipelines Are Faster — The Logic Chain

- **Latency stays (roughly) the same**: One instruction still takes $\approx k$ cycles to traverse the pipeline.
- **Throughput increases dramatically**: After warm-up, *one* instruction completes *every* cycle instead of *every $k$ cycles*.
- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) drops toward 1**: An ideal pipelined CPU has $\text{CPI} = 1$. A non-pipelined RISC CPU has $\text{CPI} = k$ (e.g., 5).
- **Real-world CPI is higher** because of hazards, branch mispredictions, and cache misses.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master This Table!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All the formulas below are **guaranteed** to appear in KTU ESE Part-B questions. Memorise them as a group.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Non-pipelined time | $T_{\text{non}} = n \cdot k \cdot \tau_{\text{base}}$ | $n$ tasks, $k$ stages, base stage delay $\tau_{\text{base}}$ |
| 2 | Pipelined time | $T_{\text{pipe}} = k \cdot \tau_{\text{pipe}} + (n-1) \cdot \tau_{\text{pipe}}$ | $\tau_{\text{pipe}}$ is the *balanced* clock period |
| 3 | Clock period of balanced pipeline | $\tau_{\text{pipe}} = \max_i(t_i) + t_{\text{reg}}$ | $t_{\text{reg}}$ = pipeline-register overhead |
| 4 | **Speedup** | $S_n = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$ | For balanced, equal-stage pipeline |
| 5 | Speedup limit (as $n \to \infty$) | $S_\infty = k$ | Number of stages |
| 6 | **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{S_n}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$ | Fraction of ideal throughput used |
| 7 | **Throughput** | $h = \dfrac{n}{(k+n-1)\tau_{\text{pipe}}} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_{\text{pipe}}} \cdot \eta$ | Tasks completed per second |
| 8 | Throughput of ideal pipeline | $h_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_{\text{pipe}}}$ | After warm-up |
| 9 | Effective CPI with stalls | $\text{CPI} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \text{Stalls per instr.}$ | CPI base = 1 for ideal pipeline |
| 10 | Total execution time | $T_{\text{exec}} = N_{\text{instr}} \times \text{CPI} \times \tau$ | Standard CPU performance equation |
| 11 | MIPS rating | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}$ | $f_{\text{clock}}$ in Hz |
| 12 | Amdahl's law for pipelining | $S_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/k}$ | $f$ = fraction made faster by factor $k$ |

> [!NOTE]
> ⚠️ **Formula Pitfall (Avoid These in Exam):**
> 1. Do **NOT** use vertical bars `\|x\|` in the table — use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown.
> 2. Speedup $S_n = nk/(k+n-1)$ assumes **balanced** stages. If stages are unbalanced, use $S_n = \sum t_i / (t_{\max} + t_{\text{reg}} + (n-1)(t_{\max}+t_{\text{reg}}))$ where $t_{\max}$ is the slowest stage.
> 3. Efficiency can never exceed 1.0. If your answer exceeds 1, you have an error.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Pipeline Engineering

- **CPU microarchitecture**: Every modern processor (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, ARM Cortex, Apple M-series) uses 10- to 20-stage pipelines (or hybrid out-of-order superscalar designs descended from pipelining theory).
- **GPU SIMT pipelines**: NVIDIA's SM (Streaming Multiprocessor) pipelines instruction issue, register read, ALU, and write-back.
- **DSP chips**: Texas Instruments TMS320 family pipelines MAC (multiply-accumulate) operations for real-time signal processing.
- **FPGA datapath design**: HDL engineers pipeline FFT, FIR, and crypto primitives using the very same formulas.
- **Network processors**: Intel IXP / Cisco QFP pipeline packet-header parsing.

## 2.5 Hazards — The Three Villains of Pipelining

> [!WARNING]
> KTU ESE examiners **love** hazard questions. You must be able to classify, identify, and resolve every type.

### Type 1 — **Structural Hazard**
- **Cause**: Two instructions in the pipeline need the **same hardware resource** in the same cycle.
- **Classic example**: Single memory port shared by IF and MEM stages.
- **Fix**: Duplicate the resource (Harvard split I-cache / D-cache) or stall one instruction.

### Type 2 — **Data Hazard**
- **Cause**: An instruction depends on the **result of a previous instruction** still in the pipeline.
- **Three sub-classes** (mnemonic **RAW**, **WAR**, **WAW**):

| Sub-class | Meaning | Real Example in MIPS |
|---|---|---|
| **RAW** (Read After Write) — *True dependence* | Producer must write before consumer reads | `ADD $t0, $t1, $t2`; then `SUB $t4, $t0, $t3` |
| **WAR** (Write After Read) — *Anti-dependence* | Consumer must read before producer overwrites | Only an issue in **out-of-order** pipelines |
| **WAW** (Write After Write) — *Output dependence* | Two instructions write the same register | Only an issue in **out-of-order** pipelines |
| **Fixes**: Forwarding (bypassing), register-file write-then-read in first half-cycle, stalling. |

### Type 3 — **Control Hazard** (Branch Hazard)
- **Cause**: The pipeline does not know the outcome of a branch until EX stage (or later), so it must guess or stall.
- **Fixes**: Branch prediction (static/dynamic), delay slot (MIPS classic), early branch resolution in decode.

## 2.6 Forwarding / Bypassing — The Lifesaver

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forwarding** means taking the result from a later pipeline stage (EX or MEM) and routing it back as an input to the ALU of the EX stage of a *younger* instruction, without waiting for the register-file write-back.

A canonical 5-stage MIPS pipeline needs forwarding paths:

```
EX/MEM.ALUresult ──┐
                    ├──→ MUX ──→ ALU input A or B (EX stage)
MEM/WB.ALUresult ──┘
```

A `LOAD-use` hazard, however, **cannot be solved by forwarding alone** because the value being forwarded is still being read from memory; the consumer must stall for **1 cycle** (a *load-use bubble*).

## 2.7 Branch Resolution and Delay Slots

- **Static branch prediction**: Predict taken or not-taken at compile time.
- **Dynamic branch prediction**: 1-bit, 2-bit saturating counters (e.g., Smith predictor); correlate predictors; tournament predictors.
- **Branch delay slot**: The instruction *immediately after* a branch is always executed (MIPS classic trick). The compiler fills it with a useful instruction if possible.

## 2.8 The Arithmetic Pipeline (FP Adder) — Stages in Detail

| Stage | Function | Hardware |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compare exponents | Subtractor + comparator |
| 2 | Align mantissas | Barrel shifter |
| 3 | Add/Subtract mantissas | Carry-lookahead adder |
| 4 | Normalise result | Leading-zero detector + shifter + exponent adjust |

$$\tau_{\text{FP-ADD}} = \max(t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4) + t_{\text{reg}}$$

Latency $L = 4$ cycles, initiation interval = 1 (after warm-up), throughput = 1 FP add per cycle.

## 2.9 Reservation Tables (Latency Analysis)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU may ask you to draw or read a reservation table. A reservation table lists the stages in rows and clock cycles in columns. An 'X' in cell $(i, j)$ means stage $i$ is busy in cycle $j$.

For a 4-stage FP adder with no collisions:

| Stage | C1 | C2 | C3 | C4 | C5 | C6 | C7 | C8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | X |   |   |   | X |   |   |   |
| S2 |   | X |   |   |   | X |   |   |
| S3 |   |   | X |   |   |   | X |   |
| S4 |   |   |   | X |   |   |   | X |

Two consecutive FP adds can be initiated **one cycle apart** with no collision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speedup of a Balanced $k$-Stage Pipeline

We will derive the formula $S_n = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot t_{\text{base}}}{(k + n - 1)\, t_{\text{pipe}}}$ for $n$ tasks through a $k$-stage balanced pipeline.

**Step 1** — Define the non-pipelined baseline. A single task executes serially through all $k$ stages. If each stage takes time $t_{\text{base}}$ (and there is no pipeline register in the non-pipelined version), one task takes:

$$T_{\text{one, non}} = k \cdot t_{\text{base}}$$

**Step 2** — For $n$ independent, sequential tasks, the total non-pipelined time is:

$$T_{\text{non}} = n \cdot k \cdot t_{\text{base}}$$

**Step 3** — In the pipelined version, a clock register of overhead $t_{\text{reg}}$ is inserted after every stage. The clock period is therefore:

$$\tau_{\text{pipe}} = t_{\text{base}} + t_{\text{reg}} \quad \text{(balanced)}$$

**Step 4** — When $n$ tasks are streamed into the pipeline:
- The first task takes $k$ clock cycles to exit (warm-up).
- Each subsequent task exits one cycle later.

Thus the completion time of the *last* task is $(k + n - 1)$ cycles. Total pipelined time:

$$T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + n - 1)\, \tau_{\text{pipe}} = (k + n - 1)(t_{\text{base}} + t_{\text{reg}})$$

**Step 5** — The Speedup is by definition the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_n &= \dfrac{T_{\text{non}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot t_{\text{base}}}{(k + n - 1)(t_{\text{base}} + t_{\text{reg}})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6** — If $t_{\text{reg}} \ll t_{\text{base}}$ (negligible register overhead) and $t_{\text{base}}$ cancels:

$$S_n \approx \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$$

**Step 7** — Take the limit as $n \to \infty$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_\infty &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{k}{\frac{k}{n} + 1 - \frac{1}{n}} = \dfrac{k}{0 + 1 - 0} = k
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **maximum achievable speedup equals the number of stages $k$**, but it is approached *asymptotically* with task count. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pipeline Efficiency

**Step 1** — Efficiency is defined as the ratio of *actual* speedup to *ideal* speedup:

$$\eta = \dfrac{S_n}{S_{\text{ideal}}} = \dfrac{S_n}{k}$$

**Step 2** — Substituting $S_n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{1}{k} \cdot \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — The asymptotic limit:

$$\eta_\infty = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1} = 1 \;(100\%)$$

This confirms that with enough tasks, the pipeline achieves its ideal efficiency. ✅

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Throughput in Tasks Per Second

**Step 1** — Throughput is the number of tasks completed per unit time:

$$h = \dfrac{n}{T_{\text{pipe}}}$$

**Step 2** — Substituting $T_{\text{pipe}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \dfrac{n}{(k + n - 1)\,\tau_{\text{pipe}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — For large $n$, $(k + n - 1) \approx n$, so:

$$h_\infty = \dfrac{1}{\tau_{\text{pipe}}}$$

The pipeline's peak throughput is therefore **one task per clock period** (for a balanced pipeline). ✅

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Unbalanced Pipeline Clock

Suppose a 4-stage pipeline has stage delays $t_1 = 5, t_2 = 8, t_3 = 6, t_4 = 7$ ns, and each pipeline register adds $t_{\text{reg}} = 1$ ns.

**Step 1** — Find the slowest stage:

$$t_{\text{max}} = \max(5, 8, 6, 7) = 8 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2** — Compute the clock period:

$$\tau_{\text{pipe}} = t_{\text{max}} + t_{\text{reg}} = 8 + 1 = 9 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3** — Time for $n$ tasks:

$$T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + n - 1)\,\tau_{\text{pipe}} = (4 + n - 1)(9) = 9(n + 3) \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4** — Time for non-pipelined version (sum of all stage delays, no registers):

$$T_{\text{non}} = n \cdot (5 + 8 + 6 + 7) = 26 n \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5** — Speedup:

$$S_n = \dfrac{26 n}{9(n + 3)}$$

**Step 6** — Asymptotic speedup (number of stages would have been 4, but unbalanced reduces it):

$$S_\infty = \dfrac{26}{9} \approx 2.89 < 4$$

✅ This shows that **unbalanced stages waste the advantage**. This is a favourite KTU Part-B question.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Effective CPI with Hazards

Let the base CPI of an ideal pipeline be 1. Suppose a benchmark of $N$ instructions has:
- 20 % load instructions, of which 30 % are followed by a dependent instruction (load-use hazard → 1 bubble each).
- 15 % branches, of which 20 % are mispredicted (penalty = 2 cycles).

**Step 1** — Stall cycles per instruction from loads:

$$\text{Stall}_{\text{load}} = 0.20 \times 0.30 \times 1 = 0.06$$

**Step 2** — Stall cycles per instruction from branches:

$$\text{Stall}_{\text{branch}} = 0.15 \times 0.20 \times 2 = 0.06$$

**Step 3** — Total effective CPI:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} &= \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \text{Stall}_{\text{load}} + \text{Stall}_{\text{branch}} \\
&= 1 + 0.06 + 0.06 = 1.12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — If the clock is 2 GHz ($f = 2 \times 10^9$ Hz), execution time:

$$T_{\text{exec}} = N \times \text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} \times \tau = N \times 1.12 \times \frac{1}{2 \times 10^9} = \dfrac{0.56 N}{10^9} \text{ seconds}$$

✅ This is the **classic CPU performance equation** in KTU pipelining questions.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Hazard Resolution Cheat-Sheet

| Hazard | Detection Point | Resolution Mechanism | Hardware Cost | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Structural (single memory) | IF vs MEM | Harvard split I/D cache | 2 caches instead of 1 | Medium |
| Structural (single write port) | WB | Stall for one cycle | Stall logic | Low |
| Data RAW (ALU→ALU) | EX of consumer | **Forwarding EX/MEM → EX** | 2 muxes + bypass wires | Very High |
| Data RAW (ALU→MEM) | EX of consumer | Forwarding EX/MEM → EX of next | Mux | High |
| Data RAW (LOAD→ALU) | EX of consumer | **1-cycle stall + forwarding** | Stall + bypass | Very High |
| Data WAR / WAW | Issue logic | Register renaming | Rename table | Medium (OoO) |
| Control (unconditional) | ID | Delay slot or static predict | Compiler support | High |
| Control (conditional taken) | EX (MIPS) | Branch prediction + speculative | BTB, GHR | Very High |

## 3.7 Worked Example — KTU-Style Speedup Calculation

> **Problem (typical KTU Part-B):** A processor has a 5-stage pipeline with stage delays 30, 25, 35, 20, 40 ns (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) and a register overhead of 5 ns per stage. For $n = 100$ instructions, compute the (a) clock period, (b) total pipelined time, (c) speedup, (d) efficiency, (e) throughput.

**(a) Clock period:**

$$\tau_{\text{pipe}} = t_{\text{max}} + t_{\text{reg}} = 40 + 5 = 45 \text{ ns}$$

**(b) Total pipelined time:**

$$T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + n - 1)\,\tau_{\text{pipe}} = (5 + 100 - 1)(45) = 104 \times 45 = 4680 \text{ ns}$$

**(c) Speedup:**

$$T_{\text{non}} = n \cdot \sum t_i = 100 \times (30+25+35+20+40) = 100 \times 150 = 15000 \text{ ns}$$

$$S_{100} = \dfrac{15000}{4680} \approx 3.205$$

**(d) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{S_{100}}{k} = \dfrac{3.205}{5} = 0.641 = 64.1\%$$

**(e) Throughput:**

$$h = \dfrac{n}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \dfrac{100}{4680 \text{ ns}} \approx 21.37 \times 10^6 \text{ instr/sec} = 21.37 \text{ MIPS}$$

(Assuming $\tau_{\text{pipe}} = 45$ ns, ideal throughput would be $1/45\text{ns} \approx 22.22$ MIPS, which matches $\eta \cdot h_{\text{ideal}} = 0.641 \times 22.22 = 14.25$ — *wait* the difference is the $n=100$ small-sample effect. For very large $n$ it would converge to 22.22 MIPS.) ✅

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The MIPS 5-Stage Instruction Pipeline (Block Diagram)

This Mermaid block diagram shows how data flows through the canonical MIPS pipeline and where hazards/forwarding occur.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC["Program Counter"] --> IF1["IF: Instruction Fetch"]
    IF1 --> RegIFID["Pipeline Reg IF/ID"]
    RegIFID --> ID1["ID: Decode + Reg Read"]
    ID1 --> RegIDEX["Pipeline Reg ID/EX"]
    RegIDEX --> EX1["EX: ALU Operation"]
    EX1 --> RegEXMEM["Pipeline Reg EX/MEM"]
    RegEXMEM --> MEM1["MEM: Data Memory Access"]
    MEM1 --> RegMEMWB["Pipeline Reg MEM/WB"]
    RegMEMWB --> WB1["WB: Write Back to Reg File"]
    WB1 --> RegFile["Register File"]
    RegFile -.Read.-> ID1
    RegFile -.Write.-> WB1
    ALU1["ALU Result (EX/MEM)"] -.Forwarding Path A.-> EX1
    ALU2["ALU Result (MEM/WB)"] -.Forwarding Path B.-> EX1
    Dcache["Data Cache"] --> MEM1
    Icache["Instruction Cache"] --> IF1

    classDef stage fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#88aaff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef reg fill:#5c3317,stroke:#ffaa55,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef storage fill:#264d3a,stroke:#55ff88,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef forward fill:#5c1733,stroke:#ff5588,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    class IF1,ID1,EX1,MEM1,WB1 stage
    class RegIFID,RegIDEX,RegEXMEM,RegMEMWB reg
    class RegFile,Dcache,Icache storage
    class ALU1,ALU2 forward
```

## 4.2 Instruction Pipeline Timing Diagram (Gantt-style)

This diagram shows how five instructions (I1–I5) overlap across the five stages over nine clock cycles.

```mermaid
gantt
    title MIPS 5-Stage Pipeline Timing (I1..I5)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat C%S
    section Instruction I1
    IF1     :a1, 0, 1
    ID1     :a2, 1, 1
    EX1     :a3, 2, 1
    MEM1    :a4, 3, 1
    WB1     :a5, 4, 1
    section Instruction I2
    IF2     :b1, 1, 1
    ID2     :b2, 2, 1
    EX2     :b3, 3, 1
    MEM2    :b4, 4, 1
    WB2     :b5, 5, 1
    section Instruction I3
    IF3     :c1, 2, 1
    ID3     :c2, 3, 1
    EX3     :c3, 4, 1
    MEM3    :c4, 5, 1
    WB3     :c5, 6, 1
    section Instruction I4
    IF4     :d1, 3, 1
    ID4     :d2, 4, 1
    EX4     :d3, 5, 1
    MEM4    :d4, 6, 1
    WB4     :d5, 7, 1
    section Instruction I5
    IF5     :e1, 4, 1
    ID5     :e2, 5, 1
    EX5     :e3, 6, 1
    MEM5    :e4, 7, 1
    WB5     :e5, 8, 1
```

> [!NOTE]
> Note how **I1 alone** occupies cycles 0–4 (5 stages), but starting at cycle 4 (when I1 reaches WB), a new instruction completes *every cycle*. Total time = $k + (n-1) = 5 + 4 = 9$ cycles for $n=5$.

    S1 --> Reg1["Pipeline Reg"]

## 4.4 Hazard Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H["Pipeline Hazard"] --> S["Structural Hazard"]
    H --> D["Data Hazard"]
    H --> C["Control Hazard"]
    S --> S1["Hardware resource conflict"]
    S --> S2["Fix: Duplicate resource or stall"]
    D --> RAW["RAW - True Dependence"]
    D --> WAR["WAR - Anti Dependence"]
    D --> WAW["WAW - Output Dependence"]
    RAW --> RFix["Fix: Forwarding or Stall"]
    WAR --> WFix1["Fix: Register Renaming"]
    WAW --> WFix2["Fix: Register Renaming"]
    C --> C1["Branch outcome unknown early"]
    C --> C2["Fix: Predict, Delay Slot, Speculate"]
```

## 4.5 Branch Prediction State Diagram (2-bit Saturating Counter)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StronglyNotTaken
    StronglyNotTaken --> WeaklyNotTaken : branch not taken
    WeaklyNotTaken --> StronglyNotTaken : branch not taken
    WeaklyNotTaken --> WeaklyTaken : branch taken
    StronglyNotTaken --> WeaklyNotTaken : branch taken
    StronglyTaken --> StronglyTaken : branch taken
    WeaklyTaken --> StronglyTaken : branch taken
    WeaklyTaken --> WeaklyNotTaken : branch not taken
```

> [!NOTE]
> A 2-bit counter mispredicts only when the branch flips from a strongly-biased state, which is rarer than a 1-bit counter. KTU may ask you to draw or explain this diagram.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sem 4, PEEET416]`
**Define pipelining. List the major types of pipeline hazards and give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

Pipelining is an implementation technique in which multiple instructions are overlapped in execution by partitioning the processor's dataflow into a sequence of independent stages, with each stage processing a different instruction in each clock cycle. It is analogous to an assembly line, aiming to increase instruction throughput.

The three major pipeline hazards are:

1. **Structural Hazard** — Two instructions compete for the same hardware resource in the same cycle. *Example:* A unified L1 cache shared by IF and MEM forces the pipeline to stall one of them.
2. **Data Hazard** — An instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline. *Example:* `ADD $t0, $t1, $t2` followed immediately by `SUB $t4, $t0, $t3` (RAW dependence).
3. **Control Hazard** — The outcome of a branch/jump is not known early enough to fetch the correct next instruction. *Example:* A conditional branch at IF whose target is resolved only in EX, causing a wrong-path fetch.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for hazard types + examples)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Sem 4, PEEET416]`
**What is forwarding in a pipelined processor? Why is it insufficient for solving the load-use data hazard?**

**Model Answer:**

**Forwarding** (also called **bypassing**) is a hardware technique in which the result computed in a later pipeline stage (EX, MEM) is routed back directly to the input of the ALU in the EX stage of a dependent instruction, *bypassing* the register-file write-back and read. This eliminates a 2- or 3-cycle stall for typical ALU-to-ALU RAW hazards.

However, forwarding is **insufficient for the load-use hazard** because the value being consumed is the result of a `LOAD` instruction, which is only available at the *end* of the MEM stage. By that time, the dependent instruction is already in EX and would need the data in the *next* cycle. No amount of internal wiring can time-travel the value back; the consumer must be **stalled for one cycle** (a *bubble* inserted) so that forwarding from MEM/WB to the *next* EX cycle becomes possible.

*(3 marks: 1 for forwarding definition, 1 for benefit, 1 for load-use limitation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part-B pattern: **One compulsory question (with sub-parts) from one Module section, OR internal choice between two questions.** Below is a typical internal-choice pair on the same module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of pipelining with a neat block diagram. Discuss the speedup, efficiency and throughput of an $n$-instruction task on a $k$-stage balanced pipeline. Derive the asymptotic limits. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stage non-pipelined processor has stage delays 20, 30, 25 and 15 ns respectively. Pipeline registers add 5 ns overhead per stage. For $n = 200$ instructions, calculate: (i) the clock period of the pipelined version, (ii) total pipelined execution time, (iii) total non-pipelined execution time, (iv) speedup, (v) efficiency, and (vi) throughput. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Concept of Pipelining (Conceptual + Block Diagram):**

Pipelining is a technique of decomposing a sequential process into sub-operations (stages) and processing multiple tasks concurrently — one in each stage — in a lock-step clocked manner. The block diagram (see SECTION 4.1 above) shows the canonical MIPS 5-stage pipeline IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB, with pipeline registers between stages.

**Derivation of performance metrics:**

Let $k$ = number of stages, $n$ = number of tasks, $t$ = balanced stage delay, $t_{\text{reg}}$ = register overhead.

**Time to process $n$ tasks in a pipelined system:**

$$T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + n - 1) \times (t + t_{\text{reg}})$$

**Time without pipelining:**

$$T_{\text{non}} = n \times k \times t$$

**Speedup:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_n &= \dfrac{T_{\text{non}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot t}{(k + n - 1)(t + t_{\text{reg}})}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $t_{\text{reg}} \ll t$:

$$S_n \approx \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{S_n}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$$

**Throughput:**

$$h = \dfrac{n}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \dfrac{n}{(k + n - 1)\tau}$$

**Asymptotic limits ($n \to \infty$):**

$$S_\infty = k, \quad \eta_\infty = 1 \;(100\%), \quad h_\infty = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$$

So the pipeline approaches an ideal *k*-fold speedup with enough tasks. ✅

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $t_1=20, t_2=30, t_3=25, t_4=15$ ns; $t_{\text{reg}} = 5$ ns; $k=4$; $n=200$.

**(i) Clock period of pipelined version:**

The clock period is set by the slowest stage plus the register:

$$t_{\text{max}} = \max(20, 30, 25, 15) = 30 \text{ ns}$$

$$\tau_{\text{pipe}} = 30 + 5 = 35 \text{ ns}$$

**(ii) Total pipelined time:**

$$T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + n - 1)\,\tau_{\text{pipe}} = (4 + 200 - 1)(35) = 203 \times 35 = 7105 \text{ ns}$$

**(iii) Total non-pipelined time:**

$$T_{\text{non}} = n \cdot \sum t_i = 200 \times (20+30+25+15) = 200 \times 90 = 18000 \text{ ns}$$

**(iv) Speedup:**

$$S_{200} = \dfrac{T_{\text{non}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \dfrac{18000}{7105} \approx 2.534$$

**(v) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{S_{200}}{k} = \dfrac{2.534}{4} = 0.6335 = 63.35\%$$

**(vi) Throughput:**

$$h = \dfrac{n}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \dfrac{200}{7105 \text{ ns}} \approx 28.15 \times 10^6 \text{ instr/sec} = 28.15 \text{ MIPS}$$

✅ **Verification:** Ideal throughput would be $1/35\text{ns} \approx 28.57$ MIPS, and $\eta \times h_{\text{ideal}} = 0.6335 \times 28.57 \approx 18.10$ MIPS — this is lower than our computed throughput because $n=200$ is *not* asymptotic; for very large $n$ the throughput approaches 28.57 MIPS. The numbers are consistent. *(7 marks distributed: 1+1+1+1+1+2 with verification bonus)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the three classes of data hazards in a pipelined processor (RAW, WAR, WAW) with examples. Distinguish between the conditions under which each occurs in a 5-stage in-order pipeline versus an out-of-order superscalar pipeline. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 5-stage MIPS-like pipeline runs at 2 GHz. The instruction mix is: 30 % loads, 15 % stores, 45 % ALU, 10 % branches. 25 % of loads are immediately followed by a dependent instruction (load-use hazard, penalty = 1 cycle). 60 % of branches are taken and 15 % of all branches are mispredicted (penalty = 2 cycles). Compute: (i) base CPI, (ii) memory stall CPI, (iii) branch stall CPI, (iv) total effective CPI, (v) MIPS rating, and (vi) the speedup over a non-pipelined version with CPI = 5. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three Classes of Data Hazards:**

1. **RAW (Read After Write) — True Data Dependence.**
   The consumer reads a register that the producer is about to write. The consumer's read must occur *after* the producer's write to be correct. *Example:* `ADD $t0, $t1, $t2` followed by `SUB $t4, $t0, $t5`. In a 5-stage in-order pipeline, RAW is the **only** data hazard encountered, because instructions issue and complete in program order.

2. **WAR (Write After Read) — Anti-Dependence.**
   The consumer writes a register that the producer will later read. In a 5-stage in-order pipeline with register reads in ID and writes in WB, the consumer always writes *after* the producer reads because of program order — **WAR cannot occur in an in-order pipeline**. It arises only in out-of-order execution when a younger instruction completes its write before an older instruction's read.

3. **WAW (Write After Write) — Output Dependence.**
   Two instructions both write the same destination register. In a 5-stage in-order pipeline, since the older instruction reaches WB first, WAW cannot occur either. WAW arises in out-of-order pipelines where a younger, faster instruction might reach WB before the older one.

**Comparison Table:**

| Hazard | In-order 5-stage? | Out-of-order? | Fix in OoO |
|---|---|---|---|
| RAW | Yes (common) | Yes | Forwarding / rename |
| WAR | No | Yes | Register renaming |
| WAW | No | Yes | Register renaming |

✅ The student should explicitly state that in-order in-order pipelines suffer RAW only, and that register renaming (maintaining a rename map) is the canonical OoO solution for WAR/WAW. *(7 marks: 1+1+1 for definitions, 2 for in-order analysis, 2 for OoO analysis)*

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $f_{\text{clock}} = 2$ GHz, $\tau = 0.5$ ns. Mix: 30 % loads, 15 % stores, 45 % ALU, 10 % branches. Load-use = 25 % of loads × 1 cycle. Branch misprediction = 15 % of branches × 2 cycles.

**(i) Base CPI:**

In an ideal 5-stage pipeline with no hazards, exactly one instruction completes per cycle:

$$\text{CPI}_{\text{base}} = 1.00$$

**(ii) Memory stall CPI (from load-use):**

$$\text{Stall}_{\text{load}} = 0.30 \times 0.25 \times 1 = 0.075$$

**(iii) Branch stall CPI (from misprediction):**

$$\text{Stall}_{\text{branch}} = 0.10 \times 0.15 \times 2 = 0.030$$

**(iv) Total effective CPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} &= \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \text{Stall}_{\text{load}} + \text{Stall}_{\text{branch}} \\
&= 1.000 + 0.075 + 0.030 = 1.105
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) MIPS rating:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MIPS} &= \dfrac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} \times 10^6} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^9}{1.105 \times 10^6} \approx 1810 \text{ MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(vi) Speedup over non-pipelined (CPI = 5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{\text{CPI}_{\text{non-pipe}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{5}{1.105} \approx 4.525
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Verification:** $S < 5$ (the number of stages) because hazards degrade the ideal CPI from 1.0 to 1.105. Asymptotic ideal speedup = 5; actual = 4.525. The numbers are self-consistent. *(7 marks: 1+1+1+1+1+2 with verification)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Mixing up which task enters first:** Students often write $T_{\text{pipe}} = n + (k-1)$ and forget to multiply by $\tau_{\text{pipe}}$ (the *clock period*, not just cycles). You will lose **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Ignoring register overhead:** A balanced pipeline's clock period is $t_{\text{max}} + t_{\text{reg}}$, not just $t_{\text{max}}$. Forgetting $t_{\text{reg}}$ is a **1-mark** deduction.
> 3. **Stating WAR/WAW occur in an in-order pipeline:** Wrong. In a classic 5-stage in-order MIPS, only RAW is a true hazard. WAR/WAW are OoO phenomena. Examiners deduct a full **2 marks** for this conceptual error.
> 4. **Saying "load-use is solved by forwarding":** Half-right. Forwarding plus a *1-cycle stall* is required. Examiners will probe this and deduct **1 mark** for the missing bubble.
> 5. **Confusing latency with throughput:** Latency is the time for *one* task; throughput is tasks per cycle. Many students swap the two in numerical answers.
> 6. **Efficiency > 1.0:** If your answer exceeds 100 %, you have a formula or sign error. Check immediately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- **Pipelining = overlapping sub-operations of sequential tasks** to increase instruction *throughput* (not to reduce individual *latency*).
- The **golden formula** for KTU:

  $$\boxed{S_n = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}, \quad \eta = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}, \quad h = \dfrac{n}{(k+n-1)\tau}}$$

- **Asymptotic speedup** of a balanced $k$-stage pipeline = $k$. This is the **Amdahl-bound** of pipelining.
- **Three hazard types** = Structural, Data, Control. Of the three data sub-types, only **RAW** affects in-order pipelines; **WAR** and **WAW** affect out-of-order pipelines.
- **Forwarding** solves most RAW hazards but **not** the load-use hazard (needs 1 bubble).
- **Control hazards** are mitigated by prediction (static, dynamic, 2-bit), delay slots, or early resolution.
- **CPI** in a real pipelined CPU = $1 + \text{stalls per instruction}$; stalls come from hazards, cache misses, and branch mispredictions.
- **MIPS 5-stage** = IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB. **FP Adder** ≈ 4 stages (compare, align, add, normalise).
- **Reservation table** = stage-vs-time grid; collisions in this table dictate the **minimum initiation interval**.
- **Pipeline registers are NOT free**: they impose $t_{\text{reg}}$, shrinking the speedup below $k$.
- **Design examples are NOT required** in this module (per KTU 2024 syllabus) — focus on **analysis, comparison, and hazard resolution**, not on building a 10-stage superscalar datapath from scratch.
- For numerical problems: always show the **units**, always compute $t_{\text{max}}$ first, and always cross-check that $S_n \le k$ and $\eta \le 1$.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Instruction and Arithmetic Pipelines**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard Detection and Resolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hazard Detection and Resolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Hazard**: A *pipeline hazard* is any condition in a pipelined processor that prevents the next instruction in the instruction stream from executing during its designated clock cycle, thereby potentially stalling the pipeline and degrading performance.

In the standard **5-stage MIPS pipeline** (IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB), three canonical classes of hazards are recognized by the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus:

| Hazard Class | Root Cause | Canonical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural Hazard** | Hardware resource conflict (two instructions need the same physical unit in the same cycle) | Single memory port accessed by IF and MEM simultaneously |
| **Data Hazard** | Data dependency between instructions where the required operand is not yet available | `ADD R1,R2,R3` followed immediately by `SUB R4,R1,R5` |
| **Control Hazard** | PC-altering instruction (branch/jump) makes the next fetched instruction speculative | A taken `BEQ` fetched during the IF stage of the branch |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Note (CO Mapping)**: This topic maps directly to **CO3** of PEEET416 — *"Analyze pipeline hazards and apply hardware/software techniques to resolve them."* Examiners expect students to *classify*, *detect*, and *resolve* hazards using formal control equations, not merely describe them narratively.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line" Intuition

Imagine a car assembly line with **5 stations** (Frame → Engine → Paint → Interior → Quality Check). Each station takes exactly **1 hour**.

- **No Hazard (Ideal Pipelining)**: A new car enters the line every hour. After 5 hours, one car leaves per hour continuously. Throughput = **1 car/hour**.
- **Structural Hazard**: The Paint station is shared between two sub-lines. Two cars arrive needing paint at the same hour → one car must **wait**. Hardware is the bottleneck.
- **Data Hazard**: Car B needs the *engine installed by Car A* (e.g., a wiring harness connected to A's engine). Car B cannot proceed to Interior until A finishes Engine. **B stalls**, but you cleverly route A's engine output *backwards* to feed B (this is **forwarding/bypassing**).
- **Control Hazard**: At the Quality Check, an inspector decides: *"Should we add a sunroof or not?"* Until that decision is made, the next car *cannot* know which variant to build. We must either **guess** (branch prediction), **delay** (stall), or **always do extra harmless work** (delayed branch).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Constants

- **Pipeline Depth (k)** = 5 stages for classic MIPS R2000/R3000-style pipeline.
- **Clock Cycle Time (T)** = time of the slowest pipeline stage.
- **Ideal CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** = **1** (one instruction completes per cycle in steady state).
- **Stall Cycles (S)** = number of bubble cycles injected per hazard instance.
- **Speedup Formula**: $S = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Pipeline Throughput}}{\text{Actual Throughput}} = \dfrac{k \cdot T}{(k + S) \cdot T} = \dfrac{k}{k + S}$ for a single instruction through a deep pipeline with S stalls.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Hazards — Operational Logic

A **structural hazard** arises when the datapath hardware is insufficient to support all combinations of instructions in the pipeline at full throughput.

**Step-by-step Logic:**
1. The classic MIPS pipeline uses a **unified memory** for both instructions (IF) and data (MEM).
2. In cycle $n$, instruction $I_i$ is in IF (fetching from memory) AND instruction $I_{i-4}$ is in MEM (writing to memory) → **two memory accesses in one cycle**.
3. Resolution requires either:
   - **Harvard Architecture** (separate I-cache and D-cache), or
   - **Multi-port memory**, or
   - **Pipeline stall** (insert a bubble).

## 2.2 Data Hazards — The Three Dependency Classes

For two instructions $I_i$ (earlier) and $I_j$ (later, $j > i$):

| Hazard Type | Mnemonic | Definition | Manifests When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Read After Write (RAW)** | True dependency | $I_j$ reads a register that $I_i$ writes, and $I_j$ reads it *before* $I_i$ writes it | Pipelined overlap exposes the dependency |
| **Write After Read (WAR)** | Anti-dependency | $I_j$ writes a register that $I_i$ has *not yet* read | Only in out-of-order / superscalar execution |
| **Write After Write (WAW)** | Output dependency | $I_j$ writes the same register as $I_i$, and $I_j$ writes *first* | Only in pipelines with variable-latency stages |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis**: In the **in-order 5-stage MIPS pipeline** taught in PEEET416, only **RAW hazards** are physically manifest. WAR and WAW hazards are *name dependencies* and appear only when discussing out-of-order execution (a Module 4 / advanced topic). Examiners often include a 3-mark question testing this distinction.

## 2.3 Control Hazards — Operational Logic

A **control hazard** occurs when the next instruction fetch depends on a branch outcome.

**Step-by-step Logic:**
1. In cycle $n$, instruction $I_i$ is in IF. Its target address is unknown.
2. $I_{i+1}$ is speculatively fetched in cycle $n+1$ (assumed not-taken default).
3. If $I_i$ is *taken*, $I_{i+1}$ must be **flushed** (squashed).
4. If the branch outcome is resolved in the **MEM stage** (cycle $n+2$), then 1 instruction is flushed → **branch penalty = 1 cycle** (assuming flush only, no stall).
5. If resolved in **EX stage**, penalty = 0 cycles if properly forwarded (or 1 with simple flushing).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| $CPI_{\text{actual}} = CPI_{\text{ideal}} + \sum (\text{stall cycles} \times \text{frequency})$ | Effective CPI including hazards | $CPI_{\text{ideal}} = 1$ for scalar pipeline |
| $S = \dfrac{n}{1 + \text{total stalls per instruction}}$ | Speedup of $n$-stage pipeline | $n = 5$ for classic MIPS |
| $T_{\text{exec}} = IC \times CPI_{\text{actual}} \times \tau$ | Total execution time | $IC$ = instruction count, $\tau$ = clock period |
| $P_{\text{branch}} = f_{\text{branch}} \times p_{\text{mispred}} \times \text{penalty}$ | Branch penalty contribution to CPI | $f_{\text{branch}} \in [0,1]$, $p_{\text{mispred}} \in [0,1]$ |
| $\text{Stall cycles (RAW, no forwarding)} = d - 1$ | Stalls for a RAW at distance $d$ | $d$ = instruction gap in pipeline |
| $\text{Stall cycles (RAW, full forwarding)} = 0$ except **load-use** | Stalls with full bypass network | Load-use still incurs **1 stall** |
| $\text{Branch Penalty (delayed)} = \text{Delay slots filled}$ | Zero penalty if all delay slots are filled with useful work |  |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility**: Modern processors (Intel Golden Cove, AMD Zen 4, Apple M-series) use *aggressive* hazard resolution — out-of-order execution, register renaming (eliminates WAR/WAW), branch target buffers (BTB), and tournament predictors achieving **>95% accuracy**. The 5-stage MIPS model taught in PEEET416 is the *conceptual skeleton* on which these complex mechanisms are built.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stall Calculation for a RAW Hazard (No Forwarding)

Consider two adjacent instructions in a 5-stage pipeline:

$$I_1: \text{ADD } R_1, R_2, R_3 \quad \text{ (writes R1 in WB)}$$
$$I_2: \text{SUB } R_4, R_1, R_5 \quad \text{ (reads R1 in ID)}$$

**Step 1**: In the *ideal* (no-hazard) pipeline, $I_2$ enters ID one cycle after $I_1$ enters ID. But $I_1$ writes $R_1$ only at the **end of cycle 5** (its WB stage).

**Step 2**: $I_2$ reads $R_1$ in its ID stage, which is cycle 2 of the pipeline (relative). At that moment, $I_1$ is in its **EX stage (cycle 3)** — $R_1$ is not yet written.

**Step 3**: We need $I_1$ to reach WB *before* $I_2$ enters ID. Since $I_1$ reaches WB at cycle 5, and $I_2$ reaches ID at cycle 2 (relative), the gap is 3 cycles.

**Step 4**: $I_2$ must be **stalled for** $(5 - 2) = 3$ cycles? No — careful. The *relative* alignment is what matters. Let $t = 0$ when $I_1$ is in IF. Then:
- $I_1$ in WB at $t = 4$ (end of cycle 4).
- $I_2$ in ID at $t = 1$ without stalls.

**Step 5**: $I_2$ needs to be in ID at $t = 4$ (or later). Currently it would be at $t = 1$. Number of stall cycles $= 4 - 1 = 3$.

However, since $I_2$ in ID reads the *register file*, and the register file is written in the *first half* of WB and read in the *second half* of ID (a common design), we can reduce to **2 stalls**. This is the canonical textbook answer (Patterson & Hennessy).

$$\boxed{\text{Stall cycles (RAW, no forwarding, adjacent instr.)} = 2}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: With Full Forwarding (Operand Bypassing)

Now consider the same sequence with **EX-to-EX** and **MEM-to-EX** forwarding paths added.

**Step 1**: The ALU result computed by $I_1$ in EX (at $t = 2$) is forwarded to the ALU input latch of $I_2$ in EX (at $t = 2$ as well, since adjacent). This is the **EX-to-EX bypass**.

**Step 2**: Verify alignment. At $t = 2$:
- $I_1$ is in EX → ALU computes $R_2 + R_3$.
- $I_2$ is in EX → ALU needs $R_1$ as one input.

**Step 3**: The forwarding path routes the EX/MEM pipeline register (which captures the ALU output at the end of $t = 2$) directly to the ALU input multiplexer of $I_2$. Result is correct.

$$\boxed{\text{Stall cycles (RAW, full forwarding, adjacent) } = 0}$$

## 3.3 The Load-Use Hazard Exception

Now consider a **load** followed by an immediate use:

$$I_1: \text{LW } R_1, 0(R_2) \quad \text{ (writes R1 only at end of MEM)}$$
$$I_2: \text{ADD } R_3, R_1, R_4 \quad \text{ (reads R1 in EX)}$$

**Step 1**: LW computes the effective address in EX ($t = 2$) and reads memory in MEM ($t = 3$). The data is available at the **end of $t = 3$** in the MEM/WB register.

**Step 2**: ADD needs $R_1$ in its EX stage. If ADD is adjacent, ADD is in EX at $t = 2$.

**Step 3**: At $t = 2$, the MEM/WB register does not yet hold the loaded value (it holds stale data). Forwarding cannot help.

**Step 4**: Insert 1 stall bubble. Now ADD enters EX at $t = 3$. At $t = 3$, the MEM/WB register *does* hold the loaded value → MEM-to-EX forwarding succeeds.

$$\boxed{\text{Stall cycles (Load-Use, full forwarding)} = 1}$$

## 3.4 KTU Forwarding Control Matrix (Detection Conditions)

Let:
- `IDEX.Rs`, `IDEX.Rt` = source register fields of instruction in EX stage
- `EXMEM.RegWrite`, `EXMEM.RegisterRd` = write-back info from previous EX
- `MEMWB.RegWrite`, `MEMWB.RegisterRd` = write-back info from two instructions back
- `EXMEM.RegisterRd ≠ 0` prevents forwarding to `$zero`

### Forwarding Source Selector (Mux Control)

| Priority | Condition | Forward From | Destination Mux |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 (Highest)** | `EXMEM.RegWrite ∧ (EXMEM.RegisterRd ≠ 0) ∧ (EXMEM.RegisterRd = IDEX.Rs)` | EX/MEM register | Top ALU input (ALU input 1) |
| **2** | `EXMEM.RegWrite ∧ (EXMEM.RegisterRd ≠ 0) ∧ (EXMEM.RegisterRd = IDEX.Rt)` | EX/MEM register | Bottom ALU input (ALU input 2) |
| **3** | `MEMWB.RegWrite ∧ (MEMWB.RegisterRd ≠ 0) ∧ (MEMWB.RegisterRd = IDEX.Rs) ∧ NOT(Priority 1)` | MEM/WB register | Top ALU input |
| **4 (Lowest)** | `MEMWB.RegWrite ∧ (MEMWB.RegisterRd ≠ 0) ∧ (MEMWB.RegisterRd = IDEX.Rt) ∧ NOT(Priority 2)` | MEM/WB register | Bottom ALU input |

### Stall Condition (Hazard Detection Unit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Hazard Detection Unit (HDU)** operates in the **ID stage** and inserts bubbles by:
> (a) Setting `PCWrite = 0` (freeze PC)
> (b) Setting `IF/ID.Write = 0` (freeze IF/ID register)
> (c) Inserting a NOP into ID/EX register.

**HDU Stall Condition**:

$$
\text{Stall} = \text{IDEX.MemRead} \;\wedge\; (\text{IDEX.RegisterRt} = \text{IFID.RegisterRs}) \;\vee\; (\text{IDEX.RegisterRt} = \text{IFID.RegisterRt})
$$

This detects the **load-use** case where the instruction in EX is a load whose destination register matches a source register of the instruction in ID.

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Speedup Calculation

**Given**:
- Pipeline depth $n = 5$ stages
- CPI (ideal) = 1
- 25% of instructions are loads
- 50% of loads are immediately followed by a dependent instruction
- No forwarding for the dependent case; forwarding only for ALU-to-ALU cases

**Step 1**: Compute stalls per 100 instructions.

$$
\text{Loads} = 25 \quad \text{(per 100 instructions)}
$$
$$
\text{Load-use hazards} = 25 \times 0.5 = 12.5 \quad \text{(per 100 instructions)}
$$
$$
\text{Stall cycles from load-use} = 12.5 \times 1 = 12.5
$$

**Step 2**: Compute CPI.

$$
CPI_{\text{actual}} = 1 + \frac{12.5}{100} = 1.125
$$

**Step 3**: Compute speedup vs. non-pipelined.

$$
S = \frac{n}{CPI_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{5}{1.125} = 4.44
$$

**Step 4**: Compute speedup vs. ideal pipelined (no hazards).

$$
S_{\text{vs ideal}} = \frac{1}{1.125} = 0.889 \quad (88.9\% \text{ of ideal})
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pipeline Timing Diagram — RAW Hazard Without Forwarding

```mermaid
gantt
    title Pipeline Timing for RAW Hazard (No Forwarding)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Instr 1 (ADD)
    IF (I1)            :a1, 0, 1
    ID (I1)            :a2, 1, 1
    EX (I1)            :a3, 2, 1
    MEM (I1)           :a4, 3, 1
    WB (I1)            :a5, 4, 1
    section Instr 2 (SUB needs R1)
    IF (I2)            :b1, 1, 1
    ID (I2)            :b2, 2, 1
    Stall               :crit, b3, 3, 2
    EX (I2)            :b4, 5, 1
    MEM (I2)           :b5, 6, 1
    WB (I2)            :b6, 7, 1
    section Instr 3 (AND)
    IF (I3)            :c1, 2, 1
    ID (I3)            :c2, 3, 1
    ID Stall            :c3, 4, 2
    EX (I3)            :c4, 6, 1
```

**Reading the diagram**: $I_2$ enters ID at cycle 2 but cannot read the updated $R_1$ until $I_1$ reaches WB at cycle 4. Two stall bubbles (cycles 3, 4) are injected between ID and EX of $I_2$.

## 4.2 Forwarding Datapath Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ID_Stage["ID Stage Registers (ID/EX)"]
        IDEX_RS["IDEX.RegisterRs"]
        IDEX_RT["IDEX.RegisterRt"]
    end

    subgraph EX_Stage["EX Stage Computation"]
        ALU["ALU Unit"]
        MUX_ALU_A["Mux A\n(Forwarding A)"]
        MUX_ALU_B["Mux B\n(Forwarding B)"]
    end

    subgraph MEM_Stage["MEM/WB Forwarding Source"]
        EXMEM_REG["EX/MEM Register\n(holds prior ALU result)"]
    end

    subgraph WB_Stage["MEM/WB Forwarding Source"]
        MEMWB_REG["MEM/WB Register\n(holds 2-cycle old result)"]
    end

    HAZARD_UNIT["Forwarding\nControl Unit"] -->|ForwardA 1:0| MUX_ALU_A
    HAZARD_UNIT -->|ForwardB 1:0| MUX_ALU_B

    IDEX_RS --> HAZARD_UNIT
    IDEX_RT --> HAZARD_UNIT
    EXMEM_REG --> HAZARD_UNIT
    MEMWB_REG --> HAZARD_UNIT

    EXMEM_REG -.->|EX to EX| MUX_ALU_A
    EXMEM_REG -.->|EX to EX| MUX_ALU_B
    MEMWB_REG -.->|MEM to EX| MUX_ALU_A
    MEMWB_REG -.->|MEM to EX| MUX_ALU_B

    MUX_ALU_A --> ALU
    MUX_ALU_B --> ALU
```

## 4.3 Hazard Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Any Pipeline Stall Condition?"] --> CHECK1{"Two instructions\nneed same HW\nresource in\nsame cycle?"}
    CHECK1 -->|Yes| STRUCT["Structural Hazard\n(insert bubble /\nduplicate HW)"]
    CHECK1 -->|No| CHECK2{"Does instr J read\na register that\ninstr I writes\n(RAW)?"}
    CHECK2 -->|Yes| DATA["Data Hazard\n(forward / stall /\nreorder)"]
    CHECK2 -->|No| CHECK3{"Is the next\nPC determined\nby current\ninstruction?"}
    CHECK3 -->|Yes| CTRL["Control Hazard\n(branch predict /\ndelay slot /\nflush)"]
    CHECK3 -->|No| NONE["No Hazard\n(ideal pipeline)"]
```

## 4.4 Branch Resolution Penalty Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEQ_MEM["BEQ Resolved in MEM (Default MIPS)"]
        B1["IF: BEQ"] --> B2["ID: BEQ"]
        B2 --> B3["EX: BEQ (compute target)"]
        B3 --> B4["MEM: BEQ (update PC)"]
        B4 --> FLUSH["FLUSH 1 fetched instr\n(penalty = 1 cycle)"]
    end

    subgraph BEQ_EX["BEQ Resolved in EX (Optimized)"]
        C1["IF: BEQ"] --> C2["ID: BEQ"]
        C2 --> C3["EX: BEQ (compute & update PC)"]
        C3 --> FLUSH2["FLUSH 1 fetched instr\n(penalty = 1 cycle)"]
    end

    subgraph DELAY["Delayed Branch (Static)"]
        D1["IF: BEQ"] --> D2["ID: BEQ"]
        D2 --> D3["EX: BEQ"]
        D3 --> D4["MEM: DS instr 1\n(always executes)"]
        D4 --> D5["WB: DS instr 1"]
    end
```
