Main program resumes. **Part (b) — Vector Address Calculation for RST 5** **Step 1:** Apply the formula $V_{RST}(n) = 8 \times n$. $$ V_{RST}(5) = 8 \times 5 = 40_{10} $$ **Step 2:** Convert $40_{10}$ to hexadecimal. $$ 40_{10} = 2 \times 16 + 8 = 28_{H} $$ $$ \boxed{V_{RST}(5) = 0028H} $$ **Step 3:** The CPU executes the following sequence: 1. Saves PC of next instruction on stack. 2. Saves PSW on stack. 3. Loads PC with `0028H`. 4. Jumps to memory location `0028H` and executes the ISR stored there. 5. On `RETI`, restores PSW and PC; returns to main program. **Other vector addresses:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{RST}(0) &= 8 \times 0 = 00_{H} = 0000H \\ V_{RST}(3) &= 8 \times 3 = 24_{H} = 0024H \\ V_{RST}(7) &= 8 \times 7 = 56_{H} = 0038H \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** (a) **[7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of a **daisy-chain priority interrupt** system. Derive the worst-case acknowledgment latency for a chain of $N = 6$ devices, each with a gate propagation delay of $t_g = 10$ ns. Comment on the scalability of this scheme. (b) **[7 Marks]** Design a **parallel priority interrupt** system for 4 interrupt sources using a priority encoder. Construct the complete truth table, derive the Boolean output equations, and show how the encoded output is used to vector the CPU to the correct ISR. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Daisy-Chain Priority Interrupt** **Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 above).** **Operation:** The INTA signal from the CPU enters Device 1 (highest priority). If Device 1 has an active IRQ, it absorbs the INTA and places its vector on the data bus. If not, it forwards the INTA to Device 2, and so on. The first device in the chain with an active request captures the INTA. **Derivation of Worst-Case Latency:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{prop,\,max} &= N \cdot t_g \\ &= 6 \times 10 \text{ ns} \\ &= 60 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Scalability Comment:** The latency scales **linearly** with $N$. For a chain of 64 devices, the propagation delay alone becomes $640$ ns, which is unacceptable for high-speed systems. The daisy-chain scheme is therefore preferred only for **small, fixed-priority** applications. **Part (b) — Parallel Priority Interrupt Design** **Step 1:** Define the 4 interrupt sources: $I_0, I_1, I_2, I_3$ with priority $I_3 > I_2 > I_1 > I_0$. **Step 2:** Determine encoder output width: $n = \lceil \log_2(4+1) \rceil = 3$ bits (2-bit code $A_1A_0$ + 1-bit VALID). **Step 3:** Truth table (already derived in Section 3.2 — refer to it). **Step 4:** Boolean equations: $$ \begin{aligned} A_1 &= I_2 + I_3 \\ A_0 &= I_3 + I_1 \cdot \overline{I_2} \cdot \overline{I_3} \\ VALID &= I_0 + I_1 + I_2 + I_3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Vectoring mechanism. The 3-bit output `VALID A1 A0` is fed to the CPU's data bus during the INTA cycle. The CPU uses this code as an offset into the IVT to fetch the corresponding ISR address: $$ \text{ISR Address} = \text{IVT Base} + (A_1 A_0) \times W_{ptr} $$ For example, if `IVT Base = 2000H` and `W_ptr = 4 bytes`, then source $I_2$ (encoded as `10`) vectors to `2000H + 2 × 4 = 2008H`. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Definition Anchor:** Vectored interrupt = hardware provides the ISR address; non-vectored = CPU must poll. - **Six Phases of Interrupt Handling:** Request → Recognition → Save Context → Acquire Vector → Execute ISR → Return. - **8085 RST Vector Formula:** $V_{RST}(n) = 8 \times n$ (hexadecimal result). - **IVT in 8085:** Spans `0000H` to `003FH`, 8 entries of 8 bytes each. - **RST 7.5** is the highest-priority hardware vectored interrupt on the 8085 (after `TRAP`); vector at `003CH`. - **`TRAP`** is the only **non-maskable** hardware interrupt on the 8085; vector at `0024H`. - **Daisy-Chain Pros:** Simple, expandable. **Cons:** Fixed priority, linear latency growth. - **Parallel Priority Pros:** Constant latency, reconfigurable. **Cons:** Higher hardware cost (encoder). - **Encoder Bit Width:** $n = \lceil \log_2(N+1) \rceil$ for $N$ interrupt sources. - **Latency Components:** $t_L = t_{instr} + t_{save} + t_{vector}$. - **Context Save:** Always push PC and PSW (minimum 3 bytes on 8085). - **Return Instruction:** Always use the dedicated `RETI` (not generic `RET`) so that the interrupt enable state is correctly restored. - **Vectored ≠ Maskable:** These are **independent** properties of an interrupt. - **Real-World Parallel:** The Intel 8259A PIC and modern ARM NVIC are quintessential examples of parallel priority vectored interrupt controllers. - **Edge vs Level Triggering:** Vectored interrupts may be edge-triggered (latched) or level-triggered (sustained). Misconfiguration causes lost or spurious interrupts. - **Nesting:** Nested vectored interrupts require stack-based context saving and re-entrant ISRs. - **KTU Frequent Mistakes:** Confusing "vector" with "vector address"; misquoting RST vector locations; drawing daisy chains without showing INTA propagation. ---

topics.title: Servicing of Multiple Input/Output Devices - Polling and Daisy Chaining Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Servicing of Multiple Input/Output Devices - Polling and Daisy Chaining Schemes # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Fundamental Problem: Who Gets the CPU's Attention? In any computer system, the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** is a single, finite resource. However, it is surrounded by multiple peripheral devices (Keyboard, Mouse, Disk Controller, Printer, Scanner, Network Card, etc.) that operate asynchronously and frequently demand service. The central challenge of **Input/Output Organization** is: *"When multiple I/O devices simultaneously raise service requests, how does the CPU identify which device to service first?"* The two classical hardware/software schemes that resolve this conflict are: 1. **Polling (Programmed Priority Scheme)** — A *software-driven* sequential interrogation method. 2. **Daisy Chaining (Hardware Priority Scheme)** — A *hardware-wired* parallel priority resolution method. > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Polling):** > Polling is a *software-controlled* priority determination technique in which the CPU periodically and sequentially interrogates (checks) each I/O device through a dedicated status register to ascertain which device is requesting service. The device physically closest to the CPU in the polling sequence is assigned the **highest priority**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Daisy Chaining):** > Daisy Chaining is a *hardware-controlled* parallel priority interrupt scheme in which multiple I/O devices are connected in a serial cascade (chain). The device **physically closest to the CPU** receives the highest priority. Priority is resolved by a combinational hardware circuit using a wired-OR / wired-AND logic gate, eliminating CPU software overhead. --- ## 1.2 💡 Intuitive Real-World Analogies ### 🧑‍🏫 Analogy for Polling: The Teacher's Roll Call Imagine a class teacher with **40 students**, all wanting to ask a question. Instead of letting the loudest one speak (interrupt chaos), the teacher follows a **fixed roll-call list**: "Ravi? No. Anita? No. Kiran? Yes, what is your question?" The teacher walks through every name, one by one, until a request is found. *Students late in the list (low priority) suffer the most.* This is exactly how polling works — the **CPU acts as the teacher**, and each **I/O device is a student** in a hard-coded order. ### ✈️ Analogy for Daisy Chaining: The Airport Boarding Gate At an airport boarding gate, **First Class** → **Business Class** → **Economy Class** passengers board. The hardware is a **physical counter and gate operator** at the gate (not a software app). No matter how many people are in each queue, the gate is wired so that *only the highest-tier passenger* standing at the front is allowed to board first. The moment a higher-priority passenger arrives, the lower-priority boarding is paused. This mirrors the **hardware priority encoder** of daisy chaining — the logic gate decides *instantaneously* who is serviced. --- ## 1.3 Why These Two Schemes? (KTU Context) | Aspect | Polling | Daisy Chaining | |---|---|---| | **Resolution Method** | Software (CPU cycle) | Hardware (Logic Gate) | | **CPU Overhead** | **High** (CPU is busy checking) | **Low** (CPU is free until interrupted) | | **Response Time (Worst Case)** | Poor for low-priority devices | Bounded and fair (hardware-enforced) | | **Scalability** | Limited (linear in N) | Limited (chain propagation delay) | | **KTU 2024 Weightage** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Frequently asked) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Frequently asked) | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** In Module 4, questions are frequently framed as *"Compare and contrast Polling vs. Daisy Chaining"* or *"Design a daisy chain priority system for N devices."* Always draw the schematic — marks are reserved for **diagrams**. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Polling Scheme — Operational Deep Dive ### 2.1.1 Architecture In a polled system, the system designer assigns a **unique address** to each I/O device's status register. All devices share a common data bus, but each has a unique *device select* line activated by an address decoder. ### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. The CPU **initializes** the polling routine — a software loop stored in memory. 2. A pointer (or index register) starts at the address of the **highest-priority device**. 3. The CPU issues a **READ** command to the device's status register. 4. The status bit is checked: a logic `1` indicates the device requires service. 5. If `0`, the pointer **increments** to the next device address, and Step 3 is repeated. 6. If `1`, the CPU **branches** to the device's Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). 7. After servicing, the CPU **resumes** the polling loop from where it left off. ### 2.1.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step - **Why sequential?** Because polling is implemented in software, the CPU has no parallel "view" of all devices — it must ask one at a time. - **Why fixed order?** The order is hard-coded in the firmware/BIOS, so the *first device queried* is implicitly the highest priority. - **Why is overhead high?** In a system with N devices, the CPU wastes up to **N clock cycles** per polling cycle *even when no device needs service*. --- ## 2.2 Daisy Chaining Scheme — Operational Deep Dive ### 2.2.1 Architecture Each I/O device has an **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** line and a **Priority In (PI)** and **Priority Out (PO)** line. Devices are daisy-chained: the **PO of the higher-priority device feeds the PI of the next lower-priority device**. The chain terminates at a **Combinational Priority Encoder** (or a single AND/OR gate logic) that interfaces with the CPU's interrupt pin. ### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. All devices that need service assert their **IRQ line** simultaneously. 2. The daisy chain logic checks the **closest device to the CPU** first. 3. If the closest device is requesting service, it **blocks** the propagation of the signal to downstream devices. 4. The CPU receives the **highest-priority interrupt** and acknowledges (INTA) it. 5. The CPU branches to the ISR corresponding to that specific device. 6. After servicing, the CPU returns to the main program and the chain logic is re-evaluated. ### 2.2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step - **Why a chain?** Because we want **hardware parallelism** — multiple requests can be evaluated simultaneously by combinational logic, not sequentially by software. - **Why the AND gate?** The chain is essentially a **priority arbitration circuit** using cascading AND gates, where `PO_i = PI_i AND (NOT IRQ_i)`. A device requesting service pulls its PO low, masking all downstream devices. - **Why is CPU overhead low?** The CPU is not involved in *deciding* the priority — the hardware does it in **one gate delay**. --- ## 2.3 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet) | Parameter | Symbol | Polling Formula | Daisy Chain Formula | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | Number of I/O devices | $N$ | — | — | devices | | **Worst-case polling time per cycle** | $T_{poll}$ | $T_{poll} = N \times t_{check}$ | Not applicable | seconds | | Time to service one device | $t_{service}$ | $t_{service}$ | $t_{service}$ | seconds | | **Worst-case latency for device $i$** | $L_i$ | $L_i = (i-1) \cdot t_{check} + t_{service}$ | $L_i \approx t_{gate} + t_{service}$ | seconds | | CPU overhead per poll cycle | $O_{cpu}$ | $O_{cpu} = N \cdot C_{instr}$ | $O_{cpu} = 0$ (hardware-driven) | instruction cycles | | Gate propagation delay (chain) | $t_{gate}$ | Not applicable | $t_{gate} = (N-1) \cdot t_{AND}$ | seconds | | Effective priority of device $i$ | $P_i$ | $P_i \propto \frac{1}{i}$ (first = highest) | $P_i \propto \frac{1}{i}$ (first = highest) | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Notations for the Table Above:** > - $t_{check}$ = Time taken by CPU to read and test one device's status register. > - $C_{instr}$ = Number of clock cycles per instruction. > - $t_{AND}$ = Propagation delay of a single AND gate (~ 2–10 ns in TTL). > - $\vert x \vert$ notation is used for **absolute magnitude** when needed. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Polling in Practice:** Used in **embedded systems** (microcontrollers like 8051) where deterministic, low-jitter response is needed, and the number of devices is small (typically 4–8). - **Daisy Chaining in Practice:** Found in **legacy 8259A Programmable Interrupt Controllers**, **VME bus arbitration**, and **daisy-chained JTAG interfaces** in digital electronics testing. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Worst-Case Latency in a Polled System Consider a system with $N$ I/O devices arranged in decreasing priority order: $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_N$. Device $D_1$ is the highest priority and is checked first. **Goal:** Find the maximum time delay experienced by device $D_i$ between the moment it raises a service request and the moment the CPU actually begins servicing it. **Step 1 — Define the variables:** - Let $t_{check}$ be the constant time taken by the CPU to read and test the status register of one device. - Let $t_{service}$ be the time taken by the CPU to execute the device's Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). **Step 2 — Position of device $D_i$ in the polling list:** - $D_1$ is at position 1, $D_2$ at position 2, ..., $D_i$ at position $i$. **Step 3 — Worst-case arrival time:** - In the absolute worst case, device $D_i$ raises its request **just after** the CPU has finished checking it and moved on. The CPU has to complete a full cycle of checking the remaining $N - i$ lower-priority devices and then loop back to the top. **Step 4 — Worst-case latency formulation:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_i^{poll} &= \underbrace{(N - i) \cdot t_{check}}_{\text{Time to finish current cycle}} \;+\; \underbrace{i \cdot t_{check}}_{\text{Time to reach device } i \text{ in next cycle}} \;+\; t_{service} \\ L_i^{poll} &= N \cdot t_{check} + t_{service} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** Notice that the worst-case latency for **every** device in a pure polling scheme is essentially the same: $N \cdot t_{check} + t_{service}$. This means that adding more devices penalizes *all* devices equally — the system becomes sluggish overall. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Worst-Case Latency in a Daisy-Chained System **Step 1 — Define the variables:** - Let $t_{AND}$ be the propagation delay of a single AND gate in the daisy chain. - Let $t_{service}$ be the ISR execution time. **Step 2 — Hardware signal propagation:** In a chain of $N$ devices, the worst-case path the priority signal must travel is from the last device $D_N$ back through all $N-1$ AND gates to the CPU's interrupt pin. **Step 3 — Worst-case latency formulation:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_i^{daisy} &= \underbrace{(N-1) \cdot t_{AND}}_{\text{Gate propagation through chain}} \;+\; t_{gate,\text{enc}} \;+\; t_{service} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** Since $t_{AND}$ is on the order of nanoseconds (typically $\approx 5$ ns for TTL logic), even for $N = 16$ devices, the propagation delay is only $(16-1) \cdot 5 \text{ ns} = 75$ ns — orders of magnitude faster than a CPU software polling loop, which involves instruction fetch-decode-execute cycles measured in **microseconds**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: CPU Time Saving (Efficiency Metric) This is a high-yield KTU numerical problem. The CPU time saved by switching from polling to interrupt-driven (daisy chain) servicing in one second is: **Step 1 — CPU time spent on polling per second:** $$ T_{cpu,poll} = f_{poll} \cdot N \cdot C_{instr} \cdot T_{clk} $$ Where: - $f_{poll}$ = Polling frequency (polls per second) - $N$ = Number of devices - $C_{instr}$ = Clock cycles per polling instruction - $T_{clk}$ = Clock period **Step 2 — CPU time spent in daisy chain mode per second:** $$ T_{cpu,daisy} = N \cdot f_{int} \cdot t_{ISR} \cdot f_{cpu} $$ Where $f_{int}$ is the average interrupt rate and $t_{ISR}$ is the ISR duration. **Step 3 — Net CPU time saved:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T_{cpu} &= T_{cpu,poll} - T_{cpu,daisy} \\ \Delta T_{cpu} &= f_{poll} \cdot N \cdot C_{instr} \cdot T_{clk} - N \cdot f_{int} \cdot t_{ISR} \cdot f_{cpu} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Efficiency percentage:** $$ \eta_{saved} = \frac{\Delta T_{cpu}}{T_{cpu,poll}} \times 100\% $$ --- ## 3.4 📊 Comparative Analytical Matrix | Performance Metric | Polling | Daisy Chaining | Winner | |---|---|---|---| | Hardware cost | **Low** (no extra logic) | High (priority encoder, gates) | Polling | | Software complexity | **High** (polling loop) | Low (ISR table) | Daisy Chain | | Determinism (Real-time) | **High** (bounded) | Medium (interrupt latency varies) | Polling | | CPU utilization for I/O | **Poor** (always checking) | Excellent (event-driven) | Daisy Chain | | Priority flexibility | **Easy** (change software) | Hard (rewire hardware) | Polling | | Suitability for $N > 32$ devices | Poor | Poor (gate delay) | Tie | | Power consumption (idle) | Higher (CPU active) | Lower (CPU sleeps) | Daisy Chain | # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Polling Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CPU] -->|Address Bus| B[Address Decoder] B -->|Device Select DS1| C1[Device D1
Highest Priority] B -->|Device Select DS2| C2[Device D2] B -->|Device Select DS3| C3[Device D3] B -->|Device Select DSN| CN[Device DN
Lowest Priority] C1 -->|Status Bit on Data Bus| A C2 -->|Status Bit on Data Bus| A C3 -->|Status Bit on Data Bus| A CN -->|Status Bit on Data Bus| A ``` **Functional Description:** The CPU sequentially enables each Device Select line via the address decoder, reads the corresponding status register, and checks the bit pattern to determine if service is needed. Priority is implicit in the order of the polling list. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Daisy Chain Priority Interrupt Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CPU] -->|INTA Acknowledge| H[Priority Encoder
Combinational Logic] H -->|IRQ to CPU| A H -->|PI Highest| D1[Device D1
Highest Priority] D1 -->|PO| D2[Device D2] D2 -->|PO| D3[Device D3] D3 -->|PO| DN[Device DN
Lowest Priority] DN -->|Ground / End of Chain| Z((End)) D1 -.->|IRQ1| H D2 -.->|IRQ2| H D3 -.->|IRQ3| H DN -.->|IRQN| H ``` **Functional Description:** Each device has an **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** output to the priority encoder and a **Priority In / Priority Out (PI/PO)** daisy-chain link. A device requesting service pulls its PO line low, masking all downstream devices. The CPU receives a single, pre-resolved highest-priority interrupt. --- ## 4.3 Logic Gate Internals of a Single Daisy Chain Stage ```mermaid flowchart TD A[PI from Higher Priority Device] --> AND[AND Gate] B[IRQ from THIS Device] --> INVERTER[NOT Gate] INVERTER --> AND AND --> C[PO to Lower Priority Device] D[IRQ from THIS Device] --> CPU[OR with other IRQs to CPU] ``` **Logic Equation:** $$ PO_i = PI_i \cdot \overline{IRQ_i} $$ When device $i$ raises its IRQ, $IRQ_i = 1$, so $\overline{IRQ_i} = 0$, forcing $PO_i = 0$, which masks all lower-priority devices regardless of $PI_i$. --- ## 4.4 Combined Comparative Flow — Decision Process ```mermaid flowchart TD START([Multiple I/O Devices Raise Request]) --> Q1{Resolution Method?} Q1 -->|Software| POLL[Polling Scheme] Q1 -->|Hardware| DAISY[Daisy Chaining Scheme] POLL --> P1[CPU checks device 1 status] P1 -->|Needs service| P2[Service device 1] P1 -->|No service| P3[CPU checks device 2 status] P3 -->|Needs service| P4[Service device 2] P3 -->|No service| P5[Continue loop until end] P5 --> P6[Loop back to device 1] DAISY --> D1[All IRQs asserted simultaneously] D1 --> D2[Closest device to CPU wins] D2 --> D3[CPU receives highest priority IRQ] D3 --> D4[CPU executes ISR for winning device] ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **Define the term *Polling* in the context of multiple I/O device servicing. State one major disadvantage.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Polling is a *software-controlled* priority determination technique in which the CPU periodically and sequentially interrogates each I/O device's status register to identify which device requires service. The device checked first in the polling sequence is implicitly assigned the highest priority. **Major Disadvantage (1 Mark):** Polling introduces significant CPU overhead because the CPU wastes clock cycles checking devices even when no request is pending. In systems with many I/O devices, the worst-case response time for low-priority devices becomes unacceptably large, as the CPU must exhaustively scan the entire list before looping back. --- ### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **What is the role of the Priority-In (PI) and Priority-Out (PO) lines in a daisy-chained interrupt system?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** In a daisy-chained interrupt system, the **Priority-In (PI)** line of a device is fed by the **Priority-Out (PO)** of the next higher-priority device in the chain. The logic equation governing each stage is: $$ PO_i = PI_i \cdot \overline{IRQ_i} $$ When a device raises its Interrupt Request ($IRQ_i = 1$), it forces its PO line LOW, which propagates downstream and **masks all lower-priority devices**, ensuring only the highest-priority request reaches the CPU. This hardware-based cascading logic eliminates the need for CPU software to determine priority. --- ## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to draw the **PI/PO daisy chain** structure in daisy-chaining answers. KTU examiners allocate **at least 3 marks** specifically for the diagram. *Always draw it.* > - **Common Mistake #2:** In polling derivations, students omit the term $t_{service}$ (ISR execution time) in the worst-case latency formula. The full equation is $L_i = N \cdot t_{check} + t_{service}$, NOT just $N \cdot t_{check}$. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Students confuse the **direction** of the daisy chain. Remember: the device *closest to the CPU* has the *highest* priority — the chain flows *away* from the CPU, not toward it. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Explain the **Polling** scheme for servicing multiple I/O devices with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for the **worst-case latency** experienced by the $i^{th}$ device in a system of $N$ devices. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A computer system services 8 I/O devices using polling. The CPU takes $2 \mu s$ to check each device's status register and $40 \mu s$ to service a request. Calculate: (i) The worst-case latency for device 5. (ii) The percentage of CPU time spent on polling if the system polls at 10 kHz and the clock period is 100 ns. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Step 1 — Definition of Polling (2 Marks):** Polling is a software-driven priority resolution technique where the CPU sequentially interrogates each I/O device's status register. The first device in the polling sequence is implicitly the highest priority. *(See Section 1 for formal definition.)* **Step 2 — Block Diagram (2 Marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 — CPU → Address Decoder → Devices $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_N$ with status bits returned on the data bus.)* **Step 3 — Derivation of Worst-Case Latency (3 Marks):** Let $t_{check}$ = time to check one device, $t_{service}$ = ISR time. The $i^{th}$ device in a system of $N$ devices has the worst case when it raises its request *just after* the CPU has finished checking it. The CPU must complete the remaining $N - i$ checks, then loop back through $i$ checks, and finally execute the ISR: $$ \begin{aligned} L_i^{poll} &= (N - i) \cdot t_{check} + i \cdot t_{check} + t_{service} \\ L_i^{poll} &= N \cdot t_{check} + t_{service} \end{aligned} $$ Note that this is **independent of $i$** — every device suffers the same worst-case latency in a pure polled system. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Given Data:** - $N = 8$ devices - $t_{check} = 2 \mu s$ - $t_{service} = 40 \mu s$ - $f_{poll} = 10 \text{ kHz}$ - $T_{clk} = 100 \text{ ns}$ **Part (i) — Worst-case latency for device 5 (3 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_5^{poll} &= N \cdot t_{check} + t_{service} \\ L_5^{poll} &= 8 \times 2 \mu s + 40 \mu s \\ L_5^{poll} &= 16 \mu s + 40 \mu s \\ L_5^{poll} &= 56 \mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Part (ii) — Percentage of CPU time spent on polling (4 Marks):** The CPU performs $N$ status checks per polling cycle, and polling happens at $f_{poll}$ cycles per second: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{poll,total} &= f_{poll} \cdot N \cdot t_{check} \\ T_{poll,total} &= 10 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz} \times 8 \times 2 \mu s \\ T_{poll,total} &= 80{,}000 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\ T_{poll,total} &= 0.16 \text{ s per second} \\ \therefore \text{CPU Utilization for Polling} &= \frac{0.16}{1} \times 100\% = 16\% \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** 16% of the CPU's time is consumed by polling overhead. In a 1-second window, the CPU wastes 160 ms simply checking for I/O requests. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **(a)** Describe the **Daisy Chaining** scheme for priority interrupt handling with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of the priority encoder and the PI/PO chain. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A daisy-chain system has 6 I/O devices. The AND gate propagation delay is 5 ns, and the ISR takes 25 $\mu s$. Compare the worst-case latencies of the polling and daisy-chain schemes if $t_{check} = 1.5 \mu s$ for polling. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Step 1 — Definition (1 Mark):** Daisy chaining is a *hardware-driven* parallel priority resolution scheme where devices are connected in a serial cascade, and priority is determined by physical proximity to the CPU. The device closest to the CPU has the highest priority. **Step 2 — Block Diagram (3 Marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 — CPU ↔ Priority Encoder ↔ Daisy chain of devices with PI/PO lines cascading and IRQ lines going to the encoder.)* **Step 3 — Role of the Priority Encoder (2 Marks):** The priority encoder is a combinational logic block that receives individual IRQ lines from all devices and generates a single **interrupt vector** or address for the CPU. It encodes the highest-priority active request into a binary code, allowing the CPU to directly jump to the correct ISR without software arbitration. **Step 4 — Role of the PI/PO Chain (1 Mark):** The PI/PO chain acts as a hardware "masking" mechanism. The logic $PO_i = PI_i \cdot \overline{IRQ_i}$ ensures that once a high-priority device asserts its IRQ, it forces its PO low, which cascades down and disables all lower-priority devices from interrupting — even if their IRQs are also active. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Given Data:** - $N = 6$ devices - $t_{AND} = 5 \text{ ns}$ - $t_{service} = 25 \mu s$ - $t_{check} = 1.5 \mu s$ (for polling) **Part 1 — Worst-case latency in Polling (3 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{poll} &= N \cdot t_{check} + t_{service} \\ L_{poll} &= 6 \times 1.5 \mu s + 25 \mu s \\ L_{poll} &= 9 \mu s + 25 \mu s \\ L_{poll} &= 34 \mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Part 2 — Worst-case latency in Daisy Chaining (3 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{daisy} &= (N - 1) \cdot t_{AND} + t_{service} \\ L_{daisy} &= 5 \times 5 \text{ ns} + 25 \mu s \\ L_{daisy} &= 25 \text{ ns} + 25 \mu s \\ L_{daisy} &= 25{,}025 \text{ ns} = 25.025 \mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Part 3 — Comparison and Conclusion (1 Mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta L &= L_{poll} - L_{daisy} \\ \Delta L &= 34 \mu s - 25.025 \mu s \\ \Delta L &= 8.975 \mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Daisy chaining reduces the worst-case latency by approximately **26.4%** compared to polling, and the gap widens dramatically as $N$ increases. For real-time systems, daisy chaining is the preferred choice. --- ## 5.3 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **🧠 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - ✅ **Polling = Software Priority.** CPU sequentially checks devices. First checked = highest priority. - ✅ **Daisy Chain = Hardware Priority.** Closest device to CPU = highest priority. PI/PO lines mask downstream devices. - ✅ **Core Logic Equation:** $PO_i = PI_i \cdot \overline{IRQ_i}$ — memorize this for the exam. - ✅ **Worst-case Polling Latency:** $L_i = N \cdot t_{check} + t_{service}$ — *independent of device position*. - ✅ **Worst-case Daisy Chain Latency:** $L_i = (N-1) \cdot t_{AND} + t_{service}$ — grows linearly but in *nanoseconds*. - ✅ **Polling Pros:** Low hardware cost, deterministic, simple to re-prioritize via software. - ✅ **Polling Cons:** High CPU overhead, poor worst-case latency, inefficient for many devices. - ✅ **Daisy Chain Pros:** Near-zero CPU overhead, fast hardware arbitration, scalable priority. - ✅ **Daisy Chain Cons:** Fixed hardware priority (hard to change), gate propagation delay limits very large $N$. - ✅ **Real-World Examples:** Polling → 8051 microcontroller, simple UART loops. Daisy Chain → 8259A PIC, VME bus, JTAG. - ✅ **Always draw the PI/PO daisy chain diagram** — it's worth **3+ marks** in KTU ESE answers. - ✅ **Watch for the $\vert x \vert$ notation** in table cells — KTU formula tables use it for absolute value.

topics.title: Direct Memory Accessing (DMA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET416 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Direct Memory Accessing (DMA)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a data transfer technique in which a dedicated hardware unit, called the **DMA Controller (DMAC)**, takes control of the system bus to transfer a block of data directly between an I/O device and main memory **without continuous intervention by the CPU**. The CPU is involved only at the beginning (to set up the transfer) and at the end (to receive an interrupt).

The CPU temporarily grants bus mastership to the DMA controller using bus request and bus grant handshake lines, allowing high-speed I/O devices (such as disks, tapes, and network interfaces) to exchange data with memory at speeds approaching the memory's native bandwidth.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Courier" Model

Imagine a busy office manager (the **CPU**) who normally handles every small office task — fetching coffee, sending letters, accepting deliveries. Two alternatives exist:

| Strategy | Analogy | Computer Equivalent |
|----------|---------|---------------------|
| **Programmed I/O** | Manager personally walks to the post office for every single letter | CPU polls/executes every byte transfer |
| **Interrupt-driven I/O** | Bell rings every time a letter arrives; manager handles each one personally | CPU interrupted for every word |
| **DMA** | A dedicated office courier is hired; manager signs a one-time work order, the courier shuttles 1000 letters, and rings the bell only when finished | DMAC transfers the whole block, interrupts CPU at the end |

This is exactly what DMA does — it **offloads bulk transfer work** from the CPU to a specialized hardware assistant, freeing the processor to do useful computation.

## 1.3 Why DMA is Necessary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without DMA, every byte of I/O data must travel through the CPU's internal registers. This creates a **bottleneck** for high-speed devices. Three traditional I/O mechanisms are inadequate for bulk transfer:
>
> 1. **Programmed I/O** — CPU wastes cycles in a polling loop (CPU-bound).
> 2. **Interrupt-driven I/O** — each word generates an interrupt; for a 4096-byte block at 1 µs/word the CPU gets 4096 interrupts (interrupt saturation).
> 3. **DMA** — the entire block is transferred with **only one interrupt** at completion.

## 1.4 Key Terminology and Bus Signals

- **DMAC** — DMA Controller, the dedicated IC that performs transfers.
- **HOLD / HLDA** (in 8086) or **BR / BG** (bus request / bus grant, generic) — handshake pair to surrender bus control.
- **DREQ** — DMA Request from I/O device to DMAC.
- **DACK** — DMA Acknowledge from DMAC to I/O device.
- **MAR** — Memory Address Register (inside DMAC).
- **WC / CAR** — Word Count / Current Address Register.
- **Block size**: typically **up to 64 KB** in older 8-bit DMACs and **up to 16 MB or 4 GB** in modern 32/64-bit DMACs.

## 1.5 Section 1 Recap (Why this matters for KTU)

Direct Memory Access is the standard mechanism by which disk controllers, network cards, sound cards, and GPUs exchange data with RAM. KTU Module 4 expects you to know **the architecture of the DMAC, the three transfer modes, and the time calculations** for each mode.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Inside the DMA Controller

The DMA Controller is essentially a **mini-processor specialized for memory-to-peripheral data movement**. It contains four key registers per channel, as shown below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Registers inside a typical DMAC (per channel):**
> 1. **MAR (Memory Address Register)** — Holds the starting address of the memory block.
> 2. **WC (Word Count Register)** — Holds the number of words/bytes to transfer; auto-decremented.
> 3. **IAR / DAR (I/O Address Register)** — Holds the address of the I/O device.
> 4. **CR (Control Register)** — Holds transfer mode, direction (read/write), and start/stop bits.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Flow of a DMA Transfer

The DMA transfer proceeds through a precise sequence:

1. **CPU programs the DMAC**: The CPU writes the starting memory address into MAR, the transfer count into WC, the I/O address into IAR, and sets the transfer mode/direction in CR. This is done via normal I/O instructions on the DMAC's internal registers.
2. **DMAC issues bus request**: The DMAC asserts the **HOLD/BR** line to the CPU, requesting control of the system bus.
3. **CPU acknowledges**: After completing the current bus cycle, the CPU asserts **HLDA/BG**, floats its address, data, and control lines (enters a high-impedance state), and effectively steps aside.
4. **DMAC becomes bus master**: The DMAC places the memory address on the address bus, asserts the appropriate **MEMRD/MEMWR** and **IOR/IOW** control signals, and performs the actual data transfer in one of three modes.
5. **Word transferred**: One word moves between memory and the I/O device. The DMAC auto-increments **MAR** and auto-decrements **WC**.
6. **Repeat** until WC = 0 (transfer complete).
7. **DMAC releases the bus**: HOLD is de-asserted, CPU regains bus mastership, and DMAC raises an **interrupt** to inform the CPU that the transfer is done.

## 2.3 The Three DMA Transfer Modes (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU repeatedly asks students to compare these three modes. Memorize the trade-off between **throughput** and **CPU idle time**.

### Mode A — Burst Mode (Block Transfer Mode)

- The DMAC takes control of the bus for the **entire duration** of the block transfer.
- The CPU is **completely frozen (HALTED)** for the whole duration.
- **Fastest** mode because there is no bus arbitration overhead between words.
- **Worst** for CPU utilization.

### Mode B — Cycle Stealing Mode

- The DMAC transfers **one word at a time**, returning the bus to the CPU between words.
- For each word, the DMAC **steals one memory cycle** from the CPU.
- The CPU continues executing its program but is occasionally paused.
- **Slower** than burst mode (due to repeated arbitration), but the CPU remains partially productive.

### Mode C — Transparent Mode

- The DMAC performs a transfer **only when the CPU is not using the bus** (e.g., during an instruction fetch/decode of the next instruction, or while the CPU is performing an internal operation that does not need the bus).
- **Slowest** mode, but the CPU is never paused — it runs at full speed.
- Useful only when the CPU has idle bus cycles to spare (rare in modern systems).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the complete formula set you need for any KTU numerical on DMA. Master these four equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Total transfer time — **Burst Mode** | $T_{burst} = T_{setup} + n \cdot t_{mem}$ | $T_{setup}$ = bus acquisition overhead (constant); $n$ = number of words; $t_{mem}$ = memory cycle time |
| 2 | Total transfer time — **Cycle Stealing** | $T_{steal} = n \cdot (t_{mem} + t_{arb})$ | $t_{arb}$ = arbitration / handshake overhead per word |
| 3 | Total transfer time — **Transparent** | $T_{trans} = n \cdot (t_{mem} + t_{wait})$ | $t_{wait}$ = average idle time CPU leaves the bus; very small or zero |
| 4 | Effective data rate | $R = \dfrac{n \cdot W_{word}}{T_{total}}$ | $W_{word}$ = word width in bytes; result in bytes/second |
| 5 | CPU idle fraction (Burst) | $f_{idle} = \dfrac{T_{burst}}{T_{total\ program}} \cdot 100\%$ | Often used in long-answer questions |
| 6 | Percentage slowdown due to cycle stealing | $\Delta = \left(1 - \dfrac{t_{mem}}{t_{mem}+t_{arb}}\right) \cdot 100\%$ | Indicates how much slower stealing is vs. ideal |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

DMA is the **backbone of every high-performance I/O subsystem**:
- **Hard disk controllers (SATA/NVMe)** use DMA to move 4 KB–2 MB chunks.
- **Network interface cards (NICs)** use bus-mastering DMA to place packets directly into kernel buffers.
- **GPUs** use DMA engines to upload textures and read back rendered frames.
- **Audio DSPs** use double-buffered DMA to sustain uninterrupted playback.
- **Embedded SoCs (ARM, RISC-V)** integrate multiple DMAC channels for sensor and display pipelines.

A solid understanding of DMA transfer modes is essential for systems engineers designing real-time firmware and for anyone preparing for KTU's Computer Organization examinations.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Burst Mode Transfer Time (Model KTU Numerical)

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A hard-disk controller uses DMA in burst mode to transfer a block of **n = 2048 words** to memory. The memory cycle time is $t_{mem} = 200\text{ ns}$. The bus-acquisition handshake takes $T_{setup} = 400\text{ ns}$. Compute the total transfer time and the effective data rate if the word size is 4 bytes.

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant formula.**

This is burst mode, so we use Equation (1) from the cheat sheet:

$$T_{burst} = T_{setup} + n \cdot t_{mem}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$T_{burst} = 400\text{ ns} + 2048 \times 200\text{ ns}$$

Compute the multiplication first:

$$2048 \times 200 = 409{,}600\text{ ns}$$

Add the setup time:

$$T_{burst} = 400 + 409{,}600 = 410{,}000\text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to microseconds for cleaner presentation.**

$$T_{burst} = 410{,}000\text{ ns} = 410\text{ µs}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the effective data rate using Equation (4).**

$$R = \dfrac{n \cdot W_{word}}{T_{total}} = \dfrac{2048 \times 4\text{ bytes}}{410\text{ µs}}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$2048 \times 4 = 8192\text{ bytes}$$

Convert the denominator to seconds:

$$410\text{ µs} = 410 \times 10^{-6}\text{ s}$$

Substitute:

$$R = \dfrac{8192}{410 \times 10^{-6}}\text{ bytes/s}$$

**Step 5 — Final numerical evaluation.**

$$R = \dfrac{8192}{0.000410} = 19{,}980{,}487.8\text{ bytes/s} \approx 19.98\text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 6 — Result statement.**

> **Answer:** $T_{burst} = 410\text{ µs}$ and the effective data rate is approximately **$19.98\text{ MB/s}$**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cycle Stealing Mode Transfer Time (Comparison Question)

> **Problem:** For the same 2048-word transfer in Example 1, recompute the transfer time assuming **cycle-stealing mode** with per-word arbitration overhead $t_{arb} = 50\text{ ns}$. Also compute the percentage slowdown versus burst mode.

**Step 1 — Apply Equation (2).**

$$T_{steal} = n \cdot (t_{mem} + t_{arb})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$T_{steal} = 2048 \times (200 + 50)\text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Inner sum.**

$$t_{mem} + t_{arb} = 250\text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply.**

$$T_{steal} = 2048 \times 250 = 512{,}000\text{ ns} = 512\text{ µs}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the percentage slowdown versus burst mode.**

$$\Delta = \dfrac{T_{steal} - T_{burst}}{T_{burst}} \times 100\%$$

$$\Delta = \dfrac{512 - 410}{410} \times 100\%$$

$$\Delta = \dfrac{102}{410} \times 100\% = 24.88\%$$

**Step 6 — Result statement.**

> **Answer:** $T_{steal} = 512\text{ µs}$, which is **24.88% slower** than burst mode. The trade-off is that the CPU remains partially active during cycle stealing.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Multi-Mode Comparison Table

> **Problem:** For a block of 1024 words, $t_{mem} = 100\text{ ns}$, $T_{setup} = 200\text{ ns}$, $t_{arb} = 30\text{ ns}$, and transparent-mode wait $t_{wait} = 0$ (assume ideal). Compute the transfer time for all three modes.

**Step 1 — Burst mode.**

$$T_{burst} = 200 + 1024 \times 100 = 200 + 102{,}400 = 102{,}600\text{ ns} \approx 102.6\text{ µs}$$

**Step 2 — Cycle stealing mode.**

$$T_{steal} = 1024 \times (100 + 30) = 1024 \times 130 = 133{,}120\text{ ns} \approx 133.12\text{ µs}$$

**Step 3 — Transparent mode (with $t_{wait} = 0$).**

$$T_{trans} = 1024 \times (100 + 0) = 102{,}400\text{ ns} = 102.4\text{ µs}$$

**Step 4 — Present as an Analytical Matrix.**

| Transfer Mode | Formula Used | Numerical Result | CPU Status | Relative Speed |
|---------------|--------------|------------------|------------|----------------|
| Burst | $T_{setup} + n \cdot t_{mem}$ | **102.6 µs** | **HALTED** | **Fastest** |
| Cycle Stealing | $n \cdot (t_{mem} + t_{arb})$ | **133.12 µs** | **Active but paused** | Medium (29.8% slower) |
| Transparent | $n \cdot (t_{mem} + t_{wait})$ | **102.4 µs** (ideal) | **Always running** | Best CPU usage; depends on $t_{wait}$ |

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Why Burst Mode is Theoretically Fastest

Starting from the general per-word transfer cost $C$:

$$C_{burst} = t_{mem} \quad (\text{no per-word overhead, because bus is held throughout})$$

$$C_{steal} = t_{mem} + t_{arb} \quad (\text{bus returned and re-requested for each word})$$

$$C_{trans} = t_{mem} + t_{wait} \quad (\text{wait time until CPU releases bus})$$

For any $t_{arb} > 0$ and $t_{wait} \geq 0$:

$$C_{burst} \leq C_{trans} \leq C_{steal}$$

Therefore, multiplying by $n$ (and adding the one-time $T_{setup}$ in burst):

$$T_{burst} = T_{setup} + n \cdot C_{burst}$$

$$T_{steal} = n \cdot C_{steal}$$

$$T_{trans} = n \cdot C_{trans}$$

For sufficiently large $n$ (which is the case for real I/O blocks, typically 512 bytes to 4 MB), the one-time $T_{setup}$ is negligible and **burst mode is asymptotically the fastest**.

---

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    cpuNode <-- normal instructions --> dmacNode
    dmacNode -- DREQ DACK --> ioNode

    cpuNode <-- program fetch data --> memNode

```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    marNode["MAR MEMORY ADDRESS REGISTER"]
    wcNode["WC WORD COUNT REGISTER"]
    iarNode["IAR I/O ADDRESS REGISTER"]
    crNode["CR CONTROL REGISTER MODE DIR START"]
    arbNode["BUS ARBITRATION UNIT HOLD HLDA"]
    intrNode["INTERRUPT GENERATOR TO CPU"]

    ctrlNode --- marNode
    ctrlNode --- wcNode
    ctrlNode --- iarNode
    ctrlNode --- crNode
    ctrlNode --- arbNode
    ctrlNode --- intrNode
    marNode -- incremented per transfer --> ctrlNode

```

## 4.3 Operational Flowchart of a Complete DMA Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["Start CPU initiates DMA transfer"]
    setupNode["CPU writes MAR WC IAR CR into DMAC"]
    dreqNode["I/O device asserts DREQ"]
    holdNode["DMAC asserts HOLD to CPU"]
    hldaNode["CPU completes current cycle asserts HLDA floats bus"]
    transferNode["DMAC transfers one word and updates MAR WC"]
    checkNode{"WC equals 0"}
    releaseNode["DMAC deasserts HOLD releases bus"]
    intrNode["DMAC sends interrupt to CPU"]
    resumeNode["CPU resumes normal execution"]
    loopNode["Wait for next DREQ or next cycle"]

    startNode --> setupNode
    setupNode --> dreqNode
    dreqNode --> holdNode
    holdNode --> hldaNode
    hldaNode --> transferNode
    transferNode --> checkNode
    checkNode -- No --> loopNode
    loopNode --> transferNode
    checkNode -- Yes --> releaseNode
    releaseNode --> intrNode
    intrNode --> resumeNode
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Direct Memory Access (DMA). Why is it preferred over interrupt-driven I/O for block transfers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Direct Memory Access is a data transfer technique in which a dedicated hardware unit called the **DMA Controller (DMAC)** transfers a block of data directly between an I/O device and main memory, with CPU intervention only at the start and end of the transfer. (2 marks)

DMA is preferred over interrupt-driven I/O for block transfers because interrupt-driven I/O generates **one interrupt per word**, causing severe interrupt overhead and CPU saturation. DMA generates only **one interrupt at the end of the entire block**, drastically reducing overhead and freeing the CPU to perform other useful computation during the transfer. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the three transfer modes of DMA and state one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mode | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| **Burst Mode** | Fastest transfer — no per-word arbitration | CPU is fully halted during the transfer |
| **Cycle Stealing** | CPU remains partially active; better CPU utilization | Slower than burst because each word needs bus arbitration |
| **Transparent Mode** | CPU is never paused; full CPU throughput | Slowest mode; usable only when CPU has idle bus cycles |

(3 marks — 1 mark per row, advantage + disadvantage combined)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Examiner Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Internal choice is provided**, meaning the student answers either OR group.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**A.** Describe the internal architecture of a DMA controller with a neat block diagram. Explain the operation of DMA in **burst mode** with a timing diagram. **(14 marks — split as 7 + 7)**

#### Sub-part (a) — DMA Controller Architecture (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

A typical DMA controller contains the following registers and units per channel: **(7 marks — distribute as below)**

1. **Memory Address Register (MAR)** — 16/32-bit register holding the starting memory address; auto-incremented after every word transfer.
2. **Word Count Register (WC / CAR)** — Holds the number of words/bytes to be transferred; auto-decremented; transfer stops when WC = 0.
3. **I/O Address Register (IAR / DAR)** — Holds the address of the peripheral device.
4. **Control Register (CR)** — Holds transfer mode (burst/cycle steal/transparent), direction (read/write), and a START/STOP bit.
5. **Bus Arbitration Unit** — Issues HOLD to CPU, receives HLDA, and acquires/releases the system bus.
6. **Interrupt Generator** — Sends an interrupt to the CPU when WC reaches 0.

**Block diagram (already shown in Section 4.2 of these notes).**

**Operation summary:** The CPU programs the four registers, then the I/O device asserts DREQ, the DMAC requests the bus, transfers words, and finally interrupts the CPU.

*(Full marks for: register list = 3, arbitration = 2, interrupt mechanism = 1, working description = 1.)*

#### Sub-part (b) — Burst Mode Operation with Timing (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

In **burst (block) mode**, the DMAC takes complete control of the system bus for the entire duration of the block transfer.

**Step-by-step timing description:**

1. The CPU programs MAR = 2000H, WC = 1024, IAR = device address, CR = burst + read mode.
2. The peripheral asserts DREQ.
3. The DMAC asserts HOLD to the CPU.
4. The CPU finishes its current instruction, then asserts **HLDA** and floats its bus drivers (becomes high-impedance).
5. The DMAC places address 2000H on the address bus and asserts MEMRD + IOW to read one word from memory and write it to the device.
6. DMAC increments MAR to 2001H, decrements WC to 1023.
7. The DMAC repeats step 5–6 for the next word without releasing the bus.
8. When WC = 0, DMAC deasserts HOLD; the CPU regains bus control and DMAC raises an interrupt.

**Timing diagram (textual):**

```
   CPU:    [EXEC]----HALTED FOR 1024 CYCLES----[EXEC]
   HOLD:   _____|^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^|____
   HLDA:   _____|^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^|____
   MEMRD:  _____|_|__|__|__|__|__|__...__|__|____
   DATA:   XXXXXX [W1] [W2] [W3] [W4] ... [W1024] XXXXX
   INTR:   ________________________________|^^^^^^^^
```

*(Full marks for: 7-step sequence = 4 marks, timing diagram = 2 marks, register update behaviour = 1 mark.)*

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks — OR Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**B.** With neat block diagrams, explain the **three transfer modes of DMA**. A system uses DMA to transfer 4096 words of 32-bit data from an I/O device to memory. Memory cycle time = 200 ns, bus-acquisition setup time = 500 ns, per-word arbitration overhead in cycle-stealing = 40 ns. Calculate the total transfer time and the effective data rate in **cycle-stealing mode** and **burst mode**. **(14 marks — split as 7 + 7)**

#### Sub-part (a) — Three Transfer Modes Explanation (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The three transfer modes are **(approx. 2.3 marks each)**:

1. **Burst Mode** — DMAC takes the bus once and transfers the entire block without releasing it. The CPU is **fully halted** during the transfer. Maximum throughput, zero CPU activity.
2. **Cycle Stealing Mode** — DMAC transfers **one word per memory cycle** and then releases the bus back to the CPU. CPU executes between steals. Slower due to repeated arbitration, but CPU remains partially active.
3. **Transparent Mode** — DMAC performs the transfer **only during CPU idle bus cycles** (e.g., during an instruction's internal execution phase). The CPU is never interrupted or paused. Slowest mode but best CPU throughput.

**Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.4 of these notes for the visual summary).

#### Sub-part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $n = 4096$ words, $W_{word} = 32\text{ bits} = 4\text{ bytes}$, $t_{mem} = 200\text{ ns}$, $T_{setup} = 500\text{ ns}$, $t_{arb} = 40\text{ ns}$.

**Step 1 — Burst mode total transfer time (Equation 1).**

$$T_{burst} = T_{setup} + n \cdot t_{mem} = 500 + 4096 \times 200$$

Compute:

$$4096 \times 200 = 819{,}200\text{ ns}$$

$$T_{burst} = 500 + 819{,}200 = 819{,}700\text{ ns} = 819.7\text{ µs}$$

**Step 2 — Burst mode effective data rate (Equation 4).**

$$R_{burst} = \dfrac{4096 \times 4}{819.7 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{16{,}384}{0.0008197}$$

$$R_{burst} \approx 19{,}985{,}116\text{ bytes/s} \approx 19.99\text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 3 — Cycle-stealing total transfer time (Equation 2).**

$$T_{steal} = n \cdot (t_{mem} + t_{arb}) = 4096 \times (200 + 40) = 4096 \times 240 = 983{,}040\text{ ns} = 983.04\text{ µs}$$

**Step 4 — Cycle-stealing effective data rate.**

$$R_{steal} = \dfrac{16{,}384}{0.00098304} \approx 16{,}667{,}000\text{ bytes/s} \approx 16.67\text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 5 — Final comparison statement.**

> **Result:** Burst mode finishes in **819.7 µs** at **19.99 MB/s**, while cycle-stealing mode takes **983.04 µs** (≈ **19.95% slower**) at **16.67 MB/s**. Burst mode is faster, but the CPU is completely idle during the entire transfer.

*(Full marks distribution: Burst time = 1.5, Burst rate = 1.5, Steal time = 1.5, Steal rate = 1.5, Comparison statement = 1.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $T_{setup}$ in burst mode.** Many students write $T_{burst} = n \cdot t_{mem}$ only and lose **1 mark**.
> 2. **Mixing up the formulas.** Cycle stealing needs the per-word overhead $t_{arb}$ **added to $t_{mem}$**, not multiplied. Burst mode has **no per-word overhead** at all.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversions.** The result is in nanoseconds; you must convert to µs or seconds before computing the data rate in bytes/s. Losing this costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Confusing DREQ/DACK with HOLD/HLDA.** DREQ/DACK is between the **device and DMAC**; HOLD/HLDA is between the **DMAC and CPU**. Mixing them is a **favourite KTU trap** worth 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the timing diagram.** Even a textual one-line timing diagram earns 2 marks; skipping it entirely can cost 2–3 marks in burst-mode questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> 📌 **High-Density Revision Block — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **DMA = bulk transfer between I/O and memory, with CPU uninvolved during the transfer.**
- **DMAC has 4 registers per channel:** MAR, WC, IAR, CR.
- **Two handshake pairs:** DREQ/DACK (device ↔ DMAC) and HOLD/HLDA (DMAC ↔ CPU).
- **MAR is auto-incremented, WC is auto-decremented** after every transfer.
- **One interrupt at the end of the block** — not per word.
- **Three modes:**
  - **Burst** → fastest, CPU HALTED.
  - **Cycle Stealing** → one word at a time, CPU partially active.
  - **Transparent** → only during CPU idle cycles, CPU always active.
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $T_{burst} = T_{setup} + n \cdot t_{mem}$
  - $T_{steal} = n \cdot (t_{mem} + t_{arb})$
  - $T_{trans} = n \cdot (t_{mem} + t_{wait})$
  - $R = \dfrac{n \cdot W_{word}}{T_{total}}$
- **Burst mode is asymptotically fastest** for large $n$ (one-time $T_{setup}$ is amortized).
- **Cycle stealing is preferred in real systems** because the CPU is never fully halted.
- **Transparent mode is rare in practice** because modern CPUs rarely have idle bus cycles.
- **Real-world DMA users:** Disk controllers, NICs, GPUs, audio DSPs, embedded sensors.
- **Common KTU mistake trap:** DREQ/DACK and HOLD/HLDA are *different* signals used at *different* levels — do not interchange them in diagrams.
